Title: Annual Research Review: New frontiers in developmental neuropharmacology: can long-term therapeutic effects of drugs be optimized through carefully timed early intervention?
Abstract: Recent advances in developmental neuroscience suggest that early pharmacological interventions may have enduring effects on neural development that extend beyond immediate symptom relief. This review examines emerging evidence for critical periods in neurodevelopment where therapeutic drug administration could optimize long-term outcomes in pediatric psychiatric and neurological conditions. We conducted a comprehensive review of preclinical and clinical studies published between 2005-2011 investigating the developmental timing of psychoactive drug interventions, focusing on antidepressants, stimulants, antipsychotics, and mood stabilizers. Particular attention was given to studies examining neuroplasticity markers, synaptic development, and long-term behavioral outcomes following early versus delayed treatment initiation. Converging evidence from animal models demonstrates that pharmacological interventions during specific developmental windows can permanently alter neural circuitry through effects on synaptogenesis, myelination, and neurotransmitter system maturation. Clinical data, while more limited, suggest that early intervention with certain medications may confer greater therapeutic benefits and potentially reduce long-term medication requirements compared to delayed treatment approaches. However, concerning findings also indicate that some drug classes may interfere with normal developmental processes when administered during vulnerable periods, potentially leading to adverse long-term consequences. These findings highlight the critical importance of developmental timing in pediatric neuropharmacology and suggest that optimizing therapeutic outcomes requires careful consideration of both the benefits and risks of early intervention. Future research should prioritize identifying specific critical periods for different drug classes and developing biomarkers to guide personalized timing of therapeutic interventions in developing populations.

---

Title: Neuropsychiatric aspects of Parkinson disease psychopharmacology: Insights from circuit dynamics.
Abstract: Neuropsychiatric manifestations in Parkinson disease (PD) represent a complex therapeutic challenge, with current pharmacological interventions often producing paradoxical effects on motor and non-motor symptoms. Recent advances in circuit-based neuroimaging and electrophysiological techniques have revealed distinct patterns of dysfunction within cortico-basal ganglia-thalamic loops that underlie these psychiatric comorbidities. This review synthesizes emerging evidence linking dopaminergic medication effects to specific neural circuit alterations in PD patients with depression, anxiety, psychosis, and impulse control disorders. We analyzed data from 847 PD patients across 23 neuroimaging studies published between 2015-2018, examining correlations between pharmacological interventions and circuit-specific functional connectivity patterns. Dopamine replacement therapy demonstrated differential effects on limbic versus associative circuits, with levodopa preferentially normalizing motor circuit dysfunction while potentially exacerbating ventral striatal hyperdopaminergia associated with impulse control disorders. Dopamine agonists showed stronger correlations with altered activity in orbitofrontal-striatal circuits (r=0.73, p<0.001) compared to levodopa (r=0.42, p<0.05). Antipsychotic medications, while effectively treating PD psychosis, produced significant reductions in striatal dopamine D2 receptor availability and compensatory increases in prefrontal-parietal network connectivity. Deep brain stimulation of the subthalamic nucleus demonstrated complex effects on emotional processing circuits, with high-frequency stimulation modulating amygdala-prefrontal connectivity patterns. These findings suggest that circuit-specific approaches to PD neuropsychiatric symptoms may optimize therapeutic outcomes while minimizing adverse effects, supporting the development of personalized pharmacological strategies based on individual circuit dysfunction profiles.

---

Title: Pharmacological enhancement of memory and executive functioning in laboratory animals.
Abstract: Investigating how different pharmacological compounds may enhance learning, memory, and higher-order cognitive functions in laboratory animals is the first critical step toward the development of cognitive enhancers that may be used to ameliorate impairments in these functions in patients suffering from neuropsychiatric disorders. Rather than focus on one aspect of cognition, or class of drug, in this review we provide a broad overview of how distinct classes of pharmacological compounds may enhance different types of memory and executive functioning, particularly those mediated by the prefrontal cortex. These include recognition memory, attention, working memory, and different components of behavioral flexibility. A key emphasis is placed on comparing and contrasting the effects of certain drugs on different cognitive and mnemonic functions, highlighting methodological issues associated with this type of research, tasks used to investigate these functions, and avenues for future research. Viewed collectively, studies of the neuropharmacological basis of cognition in rodents and non-human primates have identified targets that will hopefully open new avenues for the treatment of cognitive disabilities in persons affected by mental disorders.

---

Title: Neuropharmacology of attention.
Abstract: Early philosophers and psychologists defined and began to describe attention. Beginning in the 1950's, numerous models of attention were developed. This corresponded with an increased understanding of pharmacological approaches to manipulate neurotransmitter systems. The present review focuses on the knowledge that has been gained about these neurotransmitter systems with respect to attentional processing, with emphasis on the functions mediated within the medial prefrontal cortex. Additionally, the use of pharmacotherapies to treat psychiatric conditions characterized by attentional dysfunction are discussed. Future directions include developing a more comprehensive understanding of the neural mechanisms underlying attentional processing and novel pharmacotherapeutic targets for conditions characterized by aberrant attentional processing.

---

Title: Rat Ultrasonic Vocalizations and Behavioral Neuropharmacology: From the Screening of Drugs to the Study of Disease.
Abstract: Several lines of evidence indicate that rats emit ultrasonic vocalizations (USVs) in response to a wide range of stimuli that are capable of producing either euphoric (positive) or dysphoric (negative) emotional states. On these bases, recordings of USVs are extensively used in preclinical studies of affect, motivation, and social behavior. Rat USVs are sensitive to the effects of certain classes of psychoactive drugs, suggesting that emission of rat USVs can have relevance not only to neurobiology, but also to neuropharmacology and psychopharmacology. This review summarizes three types of rat USVs, namely 40-kHz USVs emitted by pups, 22-kHz USVs and 50-kHz USVs emitted by young and adult animals, and relevance of these vocalizations to neuropharmacological studies. Attention will be focused on the issues of how rat USVs can be used to evaluate the pharmacological properties of different classes of drugs, and how rat USVs can be combined with other behavioral models used in neuropharmacology. The strengths and limitations of experimental paradigms based on the evaluation of rat USVs will also be discussed.

---

Title: Exploring chemical space for drug discovery using the chemical universe database.
Abstract: Herein we review our recent efforts in searching for bioactive ligands by enumeration and virtual screening of the unknown chemical space of small molecules. Enumeration from first principles shows that almost all small molecules (>99.9%) have never been synthesized and are still available to be prepared and tested. We discuss open access sources of molecules, the classification and representation of chemical space using molecular quantum numbers (MQN), its exhaustive enumeration in form of the chemical universe generated databases (GDB), and examples of using these databases for prospective drug discovery. MQN-searchable GDB, PubChem, and DrugBank are freely accessible at www.gdb.unibe.ch.

---

Title: Applying small quantities of multiple compounds to defined locations of in vitro brain slices.
Abstract: Local application of pharmacological agents to specific brain regions in slice preparations has traditionally been limited by drug diffusion and imprecise targeting. We developed a novel microinjection system for simultaneous delivery of multiple compounds to defined anatomical locations within acute brain slices. The apparatus consists of borosilicate micropipettes (tip diameter 2-5 μm) mounted on independent micromanipulators, connected to a pressure-controlled delivery system capable of ejecting 10-100 picoliter volumes. Adult rat hippocampal slices (400 μm) were prepared using standard techniques and maintained in artificial cerebrospinal fluid. Fluorescent dyes (rhodamine, FITSC) were used to validate spatial precision and diffusion characteristics. We tested the system by applying GABA (100 μM), bicuculline (20 μM), and glutamate (50 μM) to CA1 pyramidal cell dendrites while recording from soma via whole-cell patch clamp. Confocal microscopy confirmed drug application within 50 μm radius of target sites with minimal cross-contamination between injection sites separated by >200 μm. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated rapid onset of pharmacological effects (200-500 ms) with duration proportional to injection volume. Sequential application of antagonists effectively blocked agonist responses, confirming maintained drug potency. This method enables precise spatiotemporal control over multiple drug applications in brain slices, facilitating investigation of localized neurotransmitter actions and receptor interactions. The system provides superior spatial resolution compared to bath application while maintaining tissue viability and allowing reversible pharmacological manipulations essential for mechanistic studies of synaptic transmission and plasticity.

---

Title: Dementia: the neurochemical basis of putative transmitter orientated therapy.
Abstract: The progressive cognitive decline characteristic of dementia syndromes has been increasingly linked to dysfunction of specific neurotransmitter systems, particularly cholinergic, noradrenergic, and serotonergic pathways. This review examines the neurochemical alterations underlying dementia and evaluates the therapeutic potential of transmitter-specific interventions. We analyzed postmortem brain tissue from 45 patients with Alzheimer's disease, 23 patients with multi-infarct dementia, and 32 age-matched controls using high-performance liquid chromatography to quantify neurotransmitter levels and enzymatic activities. Additionally, we assessed the efficacy of cholinesterase inhibitors, noradrenergic modulators, and serotonergic agents in 127 dementia patients across multiple clinical sites over 12 months. Alzheimer's disease patients showed marked reductions in cortical choline acetyltransferase activity (68% decrease, p<0.001) and acetylcholine levels (72% decrease, p<0.001) compared to controls. Norepinephrine concentrations were reduced by 45% (p<0.01), while serotonin levels decreased by 38% (p<0.05). Multi-infarct dementia exhibited similar but less pronounced deficits. Clinical trials demonstrated that physostigmine and tetrahydroaminoacridine produced modest but significant improvements in cognitive test scores (15-22% improvement, p<0.05), while combined cholinergic-noradrenergic therapy showed enhanced efficacy. These findings support the neurochemical basis of dementia and suggest that rational pharmacological interventions targeting specific transmitter deficits may offer therapeutic benefits. However, the modest clinical improvements indicate the need for more selective agents and combination therapies addressing multiple neurotransmitter systems simultaneously.

---

Title: Targeted gene expression in dopamine and serotonin neurons of the mouse brain.
Abstract: We used a knock-in strategy to generate two lines of mice expressing Cre recombinase under the transcriptional control of the dopamine transporter promoter (DAT-cre mice) or the serotonin transporter promoter (SERT-cre mice). In DAT-cre mice, immunocytochemical staining of adult brains for the dopamine-synthetic enzyme tyrosine hydroxylase and for Cre recombinase revealed that virtually all dopaminergic neurons in the ventral midbrain expressed Cre. Crossing DAT-cre mice with ROSA26-stop-lacZ or ROSA26-stop-YFP reporter mice revealed a near perfect correlation between staining for tyrosine hydroxylase and beta-galactosidase or YFP. YFP-labeled fluorescent dopaminergic neurons could be readily identified in live slices. Crossing SERT-cre mice with the ROSA26-stop-lacZ or ROSA26-stop-YFP reporter mice similarly revealed a near perfect correlation between staining for serotonin-synthetic enzyme tryptophan hydroxylase and beta-galactosidase or YFP. Additional Cre expression in the thalamus and cortex was observed, reflecting the known pattern of transient SERT expression during early postnatal development. These findings suggest a general strategy of using neurotransmitter transporter promoters to drive selective Cre expression and thus control mutations in specific neurotransmitter systems. Crossed with fluorescent-gene reporters, this strategy tags neurons by neurotransmitter status, providing new tools for electrophysiology and imaging.

Title: Contemporary approaches to pharmacotherapy in Tourette's syndrome and obsessive-compulsive disorder.
Abstract: Contemporary approaches used in the pharmacological treatment of children and adolescents with Tourette's syndrome or obsessive-compulsive disorder. Tourette's syndrome (TS) and obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) now are recognized as more common than previously believed. Moreover, there is accumulating evidence that these two disorders may share common neurobiological underpinnings. Nonetheless, pharmacologic approaches to treatment may be quite different. Using recently published information, this paper reviews current treatment for TS and OCD. There have been major advances in the understanding of the pathophysiology of OCD and TS. These advances provides guidance for the treatment of children and adolescents with these disorders. However, additional research is needed to refine current treatment approaches.

Title: Promising avenues of therapeutics for bipolar illness.
Abstract: Bipolar disorder affects approximately 1-2% of the global population and represents a leading cause of disability worldwide, yet current pharmacological treatments remain suboptimal for many patients. Mood stabilizers such as lithium, valproate, and carbamazepine provide therapeutic benefit but are associated with significant side effects and limited efficacy in treatment-resistant cases. This review examines emerging therapeutic approaches that target novel neurobiological pathways implicated in bipolar disorder pathophysiology. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies published between 2000-2008, focusing on glutamatergic modulators, neuroprotective agents, and circadian rhythm regulators. Glutamate NMDA receptor antagonists, including ketamine and memantine, demonstrate rapid antimanic and antidepressant effects in preliminary trials, with response rates of 60-75% within 72 hours. Neuroprotective compounds such as riluzole and N-acetylcysteine show promise in stabilizing mood episodes through modulation of glial function and oxidative stress pathways. Additionally, melatonin receptor agonists and light therapy interventions targeting circadian dysregulation yield significant improvements in sleep architecture and mood stability. Neuroimaging studies reveal that these novel interventions normalize aberrant activity in prefrontal-limbic circuits implicated in emotional regulation. These findings suggest that therapeutics targeting glutamatergic neurotransmission, cellular resilience mechanisms, and biological rhythm disturbances represent promising avenues for bipolar disorder treatment. Future research should prioritize large-scale randomized controlled trials to establish efficacy and safety profiles of these innovative approaches, potentially revolutionizing bipolar disorder management through precision medicine strategies.

Title: I Believe I Can Fly!: Use of Drosophila as a Model Organism in Neuropsychopharmacology Research.
Abstract: The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster has emerged as a powerful model organism for neuropsychopharmacological research, offering unique advantages including genetic tractability, conserved neurotransmitter systems, and behavioral paradigms relevant to human psychiatric disorders. This review synthesizes current applications of Drosophila in drug discovery and mechanism elucidation across major neuropsychiatric conditions. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of Drosophila-based studies published between 2010-2016, focusing on models of addiction, depression, anxiety, schizophrenia, and cognitive disorders. Our analysis reveals that Drosophila exhibits conserved responses to major classes of psychoactive compounds, including dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic modulators. Notably, fly models have successfully recapitulated key features of human drug addiction, including sensitization, tolerance, and withdrawal behaviors following exposure to cocaine, alcohol, and nicotine. Sleep and circadian rhythm studies in flies have provided crucial insights into the neurobiological basis of mood disorders and identified novel therapeutic targets. Furthermore, Drosophila models of neurodevelopmental disorders have facilitated the discovery of small molecule compounds that rescue behavioral and synaptic deficits. High-throughput screening approaches using fly models have identified several lead compounds currently advancing through mammalian preclinical testing. These findings demonstrate that Drosophila serves as an invaluable bridge between molecular mechanisms and mammalian studies in neuropsychopharmacology. The genetic precision, cost-effectiveness, and translational relevance of fly models position Drosophila as an essential component of the modern drug discovery pipeline for neuropsychiatric therapeutics.

---

Title: Trigeminal nerve responses in the rat elicited by chemical stimulation of the tongue.
Abstract: Epithelial and neural mechanisms underlying trigeminal chemoreception were investigated by recording lingual nerve responses to chemical stimulation of the tongue. The chloride salts, NaCl, KCl, NH4Cl, and CaCl2, each elicited distinctly different, integrated whole-nerve responses (thresholds, 0.5-2.0 M). Incubation of the tongue with lanthanum (2.5 mM), which reduces the permeability of epithelial tight junctions, reversibly attenuated these salts responses. It did not affect neural responses to mechanical and thermal stimuli. Incubation with other established transport inhibitors--amiloride, tetra-ethyl ammonium or tetrodotoxin, had no effect on the salt responses. Acetic and hydrochloric acids (thresholds, 0.1-1.0 M), and MgCl2 and BaCl2 (greater than or equal to 0.5 M), also elicited distinctive responses. Other salts, MgSO4, Na isethionate and LaCl3 (greater than or equal to 1.0 M), and also ethanol (4 M), capsaicin (100 mM), nicotine (29.6 mM) and dextrose (0.5-2.5 M), did not elicit responses. These results indicate that ions of selected salts can diffuse through the tight junctions of the lingual epithelium to activate the trigeminal nerve, and suggest that both the cation and the anion may be important in determining if the nerves are activated and the wave-form of the responses.

Title: Grafted dopamine neurons: Morphology, neurochemistry, and electrophysiology.
Abstract: Grafting of dopamine-rich tissue to counteract the symptoms in Parkinson's disease became a promising tool for future treatment. This article discusses how to improve the functional outcome with respect to graft outgrowth and functions of dopamine release and electrophysiological responses to graft implantation in the host brain striatal target. It has been documented that a subpopulation of the dopamine neurons innervates the host brain in a target-specific manner, while some of the grafted dopamine neurons never project to the host striatum. Neurochemical studies have demonstrated that the graft-induced outgrowth synthesize, store, metabolize and release dopamine and possibly other neurotransmitters such as 5-HT. Furthermore, the released dopamine affects the dopamine-depleted brain in areas that are larger than the graft-derived nerve fibers reach. While stem cells will most likely be the future source of cells to be used in grafting, it is important to find the guiding cues for how to reinnervate the dopamine-depleted striatum in a proper way with respect to the dopamine subpopulations of A9 and A10 to efficiently treat the motor abnormalities seen in Parkinson's disease.

Title: Human Brain Neuropharmacology: A Platform for Translational Neuroscience.
Abstract: The translational gap between preclinical neuropharmacological research and clinical outcomes remains a significant challenge in drug development for neuropsychiatric disorders. Traditional animal models, while valuable, often fail to recapitulate the complexity of human neural circuits and their responses to pharmacological interventions. We developed an integrated human brain neuropharmacology platform combining ex vivo human brain tissue analysis, induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived neural networks, and advanced neuroimaging techniques to bridge this translational divide. The platform incorporates post-mortem human brain samples from neurologically healthy donors (n=45) and patients with major depressive disorder, schizophrenia, and Alzheimer's disease (n=78), alongside patient-derived iPSC lines differentiated into cortical and subcortical neuronal subtypes. We employed high-throughput receptor binding assays, electrophysiological recordings, and calcium imaging to evaluate drug responses across multiple scales. Validation studies using 15 FDA-approved psychotropic medications demonstrated strong concordance between our platform predictions and known clinical efficacy profiles (r=0.82, p<0.001). Notably, the platform identified previously unrecognized off-target effects for three antipsychotic agents and predicted optimal dosing ranges that correlated with clinical therapeutic windows. Integration with functional MRI data from healthy volunteers (n=32) further validated network-level drug effects. This human brain neuropharmacology platform represents a paradigm shift toward human-centric drug discovery, offering unprecedented opportunities to accelerate the development of more effective neurotherapeutics while reducing reliance on animal models and improving clinical translation rates.

Title: Bioelectromagnetic applications for multiple sclerosis.
Abstract: There are EM effects on biology that are potentially both harmful and beneficial. We have reviewed applications of EM fields that are relevant to MS. It is possible that EM fields could be developed into a reproducible therapy for both symptom management and long-term care for MS. The long-term care for MS would have to include beneficial changes in the immune system and in nerve regeneration.

Title: Frontal-subcortical neuronal circuits and clinical neuropsychiatry: an update.
Abstract: Frontal-subcortical circuits form the principal network, which mediate motor activity and behavior in humans. Five parallel frontal-subcortical circuits link the specific areas of the frontal cortex to the striatum, basal ganglia and thalamus. These frontal-subcortical circuits originate from the supplementary motor area, frontal eye field, dorsolateral prefrontal region, lateral orbitofrontal region and anterior cingulate portion of the frontal cortex. The open afferent and efferent connections to the frontal-subcortical circuits mediate coordination between functionally similar areas of the brain. Specific chemoarchitecture and multiple neurotransmitter interactions modulate the functional activity of each circuit. Dorsolateral prefrontal circuit lesions cause executive dysfunction, orbitofrontal circuit lesions lead to personality changes characterized by disinhibition and anterior cingulate circuit lesions present with apathy. The neurobiological correlates of neuropsychiatric disorders including depression, obsessive-compulsive disorder, schizophrenia and substance abuse, imply involvement of frontal-subcortical circuits.

---

Title: Mercury, selenium and neurochemical biomarkers in different brain regions of migrating common loons from Lake Erie, Canada.
Abstract: Mercury (Hg) contamination in aquatic ecosystems poses significant neurological risks to piscivorous birds, while selenium (Se) may provide neuroprotective effects through antagonistic interactions. Common loons (Gavia immer) are particularly vulnerable due to their high trophic position and reliance on fish consumption. This study investigated regional brain distribution of Hg and Se, and associated neurochemical alterations in migrating loons from Lake Erie. Brain tissues from 24 adult loons collected during fall migration (2009-2010) were analyzed for total Hg and Se concentrations in cerebrum, cerebellum, and brainstem using cold vapor atomic absorption spectrophotometry and hydride generation atomic absorption spectrophotometry, respectively. Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity, dopamine, serotonin, and their metabolites were quantified using spectrophotometric and HPLC methods. Mercury concentrations ranged from 0.89-4.2 μg/g wet weight, with highest levels in cerebellum, followed by cerebrum and brainstem. Selenium concentrations (0.34-1.1 μg/g wet weight) showed similar regional patterns and were positively correlated with Hg (r=0.72, p<0.001). Significant decreases in AChE activity were observed in cerebrum (23% reduction) and cerebellum (31% reduction) relative to control values. Dopamine concentrations were reduced by 18% in cerebrum, while serotonin metabolite ratios indicated altered neurotransmitter turnover. Se:Hg molar ratios below 1.0 in 67% of individuals suggested inadequate Se protection. These findings demonstrate region-specific neurotoxic effects of Hg exposure in loons, with potential implications for motor function and behavior during critical migration periods.

Title: Serotonin receptors of type 6 (5-HT6): what can we expect from them?
Abstract: Serotonin 5-HT6 receptors represent one of the most recently discovered members of the serotonin receptor family and have emerged as promising therapeutic targets for cognitive disorders and neuropsychiatric conditions. Despite their restricted distribution primarily in the central nervous system, particularly in brain regions associated with learning and memory such as the hippocampus, cortex, and striatum, their precise physiological roles remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding 5-HT6 receptor pharmacology, distribution, and therapeutic potential based on preclinical and early clinical findings through 2011. 5-HT6 receptors are positively coupled to adenylyl cyclase and exhibit high constitutive activity, distinguishing them from other serotonin receptor subtypes. Accumulating evidence suggests these receptors modulate cholinergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic neurotransmission, positioning them as important regulators of cognitive function. Selective 5-HT6 receptor antagonists, including SB-271046, SB-399885, and Ro 04-6790, have demonstrated procognitive effects in various animal models of learning and memory, including novel object recognition, Morris water maze, and attentional set-shifting tasks. Additionally, these compounds show promise in models of Alzheimer's disease, schizophrenia, and obesity. Early clinical trials with 5-HT6 antagonists have yielded mixed but encouraging results in cognitive enhancement and weight reduction. The unique pharmacological profile and therapeutic potential of 5-HT6 receptors suggest they may offer novel approaches for treating cognitive impairment associated with aging, dementia, and psychiatric disorders, warranting continued investigation and development of selective modulators.

Title: Operant self-administration models for testing the neuropharmacological basis of ethanol consumption in rats.
Abstract: Operant self-administration paradigms represent the gold standard for investigating the neurobiological mechanisms underlying ethanol reinforcement and consumption patterns. These models provide translational validity by allowing laboratory animals to voluntarily consume ethanol through learned behaviors that parallel human drinking patterns. The current protocol describes established methods for training rats to self-administer ethanol using operant conditioning chambers, enabling systematic evaluation of pharmacological interventions on ethanol-seeking and consumption behaviors. Male Wistar rats (n=48) were trained to lever-press for 10% ethanol solutions using a fixed-ratio 1 schedule with concurrent water availability. Following acquisition, animals underwent pharmacological challenges with the μ-opioid receptor antagonist naltrexone (0.1-3.0 mg/kg, s.c.), the dopamine D2/D3 receptor antagonist raclopride (0.01-0.1 mg/kg, i.p.), and the GABAB receptor agonist baclofen (1.0-5.0 mg/kg, i.p.) administered 30 minutes prior to test sessions. Naltrexone dose-dependently reduced ethanol self-administration (F=12.4, p<0.001) with significant effects observed at 1.0 and 3.0 mg/kg doses without affecting water consumption. Raclopride similarly decreased ethanol lever-pressing (F=8.7, p<0.01) at 0.1 mg/kg. Baclofen produced mixed effects, reducing consumption at 2.5 mg/kg but causing motor impairment at higher doses. These findings demonstrate the utility of operant self-administration models for characterizing neurotransmitter systems mediating ethanol reinforcement, particularly opioidergic and dopaminergic pathways. This methodology provides a robust platform for screening potential pharmacotherapeutics for alcohol use disorders and investigating the neurochemical basis of ethanol reward.

Title: Academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry.
Abstract: The relationship between academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry has become increasingly complex, raising concerns about potential conflicts of interest that may influence research integrity and clinical practice. This study examined the extent and nature of financial relationships between academic psychiatrists and pharmaceutical companies, and their potential impact on research outcomes and clinical recommendations. We conducted a comprehensive survey of 847 academic psychiatrists at 25 major medical institutions across the United States, supplemented by analysis of pharmaceutical company disclosure reports and published research from 2003-2005. Additionally, we performed a systematic review of 312 randomized controlled trials in major psychiatry journals to assess correlations between industry funding and reported outcomes. Our findings revealed that 78% of academic psychiatrists reported some form of financial relationship with pharmaceutical companies, including consulting fees (45%), research grants (52%), speakers' bureau participation (31%), and equity holdings (12%). Industry-funded studies were significantly more likely to report favorable outcomes for the sponsor's drug compared to independently funded research (OR = 3.4, 95% CI: 2.1-5.6, p < 0.001). Furthermore, 23% of department chairs reported that industry relationships influenced faculty hiring and promotion decisions. These results demonstrate widespread financial entanglements between academic psychiatry and pharmaceutical companies that may compromise scientific objectivity and patient care. Academic medical centers must develop more stringent conflict-of-interest policies and transparency requirements to preserve the integrity of psychiatric research and maintain public trust in the profession.

Title: Curcumin ameliorates reserpine-induced pain-depression dyad: behavioural, biochemical, neurochemical and molecular evidences.
Abstract: Pain and depression frequently co-occur in clinical practice, sharing common neurobiological pathways involving monoaminergic dysfunction and neuroinflammation. Reserpine, a monoamine-depleting agent, has been shown to induce both nociceptive hypersensitivity and depressive-like behaviors in rodents, providing a valuable model for studying pain-depression comorbidity. Curcumin, the active constituent of turmeric, possesses anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and monoamine-modulating properties. The present study investigated the therapeutic potential of curcumin in ameliorating reserpine-induced pain-depression dyad in male Wistar rats. Animals received reserpine (1 mg/kg, s.c.) for three consecutive days, followed by curcumin treatment (25, 50, or 100 mg/kg, p.o.) for 14 days. Behavioral assessments included thermal hyperalgesia (hot plate test), mechanical allodynia (von Frey filaments), and depressive-like behaviors (forced swim test, sucrose preference). Biochemical analyses measured oxidative stress markers, pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), and monoamine levels (5-HT, DA, NE) in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Molecular studies examined BDNF, p-CREB, and NF-κB expression. Reserpine administration induced significant thermal hyperalgesia, mechanical allodynia, increased immobility time, and anhedonia. Curcumin dose-dependently reversed these behavioral alterations. Biochemically, curcumin restored depleted monoamine levels, reduced oxidative stress markers and pro-inflammatory cytokines, while upregulating BDNF and p-CREB expression and suppressing NF-κB activation. These findings suggest that curcumin effectively ameliorates reserpine-induced pain-depression comorbidity through multiple mechanisms involving monoaminergic restoration, anti-inflammatory actions, and neuroprotective effects, supporting its potential as a therapeutic intervention for pain-depression dyad.

Title: Tobacco smoking and nicotine neuropsychopharmacology: some future research directions.
Abstract: Tobacco smoking remains one of the most prevalent forms of drug dependence worldwide, yet fundamental questions regarding nicotine's neuropsychopharmacological mechanisms continue to limit therapeutic interventions. While nicotinic acetylcholine receptors have been established as primary targets for nicotine's reinforcing effects, the complex interactions between receptor subtypes, regional brain distribution, and behavioral outcomes require systematic investigation. This review synthesizes current knowledge of nicotine neuropharmacology and proposes critical research directions for the next decade. Key areas requiring investigation include: (1) characterization of neuronal nicotinic receptor subunit diversity and their differential roles in dependence processes, (2) examination of nicotine's effects on dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic neurotransmission in reward circuits, (3) development of animal models that better recapitulate human smoking patterns and individual vulnerability factors, and (4) identification of genetic polymorphisms influencing nicotine metabolism and receptor function. Emerging molecular techniques, including receptor subtype-selective ligands, transgenic mouse models, and in vivo microdialysis, offer unprecedented opportunities to dissect nicotine's neurobiological effects. Additionally, neuroimaging approaches in human subjects may bridge findings from animal studies to clinical populations. Future research should prioritize understanding how chronic nicotine exposure produces long-lasting neuroadaptations that maintain tobacco dependence. Such investigations will be essential for developing more effective pharmacotherapies and identifying individuals at highest risk for tobacco addiction, ultimately reducing the substantial public health burden of smoking-related diseases.

Title: Name that neurotransmitter: using music to teach psychopharmacology concepts.
Abstract: Background: Teaching psychopharmacology concepts to nursing students presents significant pedagogical challenges, particularly in helping students memorize neurotransmitter functions, pathways, and associated medications. Traditional lecture-based approaches often result in poor retention and difficulty applying concepts to clinical practice. Music-based learning strategies have demonstrated efficacy in various educational contexts but have not been systematically evaluated for psychopharmacology education.

Methods: We implemented a novel educational intervention called "Name that Neurotransmitter" with 84 undergraduate nursing students enrolled in a psychopharmacology course. Students were randomly assigned to either a traditional lecture group (n=42) or a music-enhanced learning group (n=42). The intervention group participated in sessions where neurotransmitter functions, synthesis pathways, and drug mechanisms were taught through original songs set to familiar melodies. Pre- and post-intervention assessments measured knowledge retention using a validated 50-item psychopharmacology examination. Student satisfaction and perceived learning effectiveness were evaluated using Likert-scale surveys.

Results: Students in the music-enhanced group demonstrated significantly higher post-test scores compared to controls (82.3 ± 8.1 vs. 74.6 ± 9.4, p<0.001). Retention testing at 4 weeks showed sustained knowledge improvement in the intervention group (79.1 ± 7.8 vs. 71.2 ± 8.9, p<0.01). Music group participants reported higher engagement levels (4.6 ± 0.5 vs. 3.2 ± 0.7, p<0.001) and increased confidence in applying psychopharmacology concepts clinically.

Conclusions: Music-based teaching strategies significantly enhance learning and retention of complex psychopharmacology concepts among nursing students, suggesting broader applications for neuroscience education in healthcare curricula.

Title: Laboratory of receptor pharmacology.
Abstract: The Laboratory of Receptor Pharmacology studied the GABAB and muscarinic m2/m3 receptors present in primary cultures of rat cerebellar granule cells. From 1985 to 1992, multiple scientists showed the GABAB receptor had, at least, two affinity states, the low affinity receptor inhibiting adenylyl cyclase and the higher affinity receptor increasing intracellular calcium from ryanodine sensitive calcium stores. Muscarinic m2 receptors inhibit adenylyl cyclase and would undergo desensitization using, in part, similar mechanism of desensitization identified for the beta-adrenergic receptor coupled to adenylyl cyclase. Muscarinic m3 receptors increased intracellular calcium levels via inositol 3-phosphate-sensitive intracellular calcium stores.

---

Title: Pitfalls in presenting and interpreting clinical trial data.
Abstract: Information generated by a clinical trial, when conveyed to health professionals and prospective patients, is affected by the original design of the trial and by the manner in which the results are presented. One problem in study design is the management of comparison groups in randomized assignments. When a comparison group is treated with an accepted standard compound, the chosen standard drug may be one that is associated with more side effects and complications than later modifications of the standard. Inadequate dosing of the comparison group can inflate the relative effect size of the experimental compound. Choosing a standard with a verifiable dose reference range can avoid this pitfall. In reporting results, relative score changes on a rating scale are meaningless without reference to an absolute value reflecting a clinically relevant degree of remission. The validity of rating instruments chosen must be judged in the context of the specific population to which it is applied. In the reporting of effects, the emphasis on significance of differences may obscure the critical distinction between statistical significance and clinical relevance, and graphs can appear to overstate a change over time by truncating the ordinate axis.

---

Title: New therapeutic developments in chronic migraine.
Abstract: Chronic migraine is a common cause of chronic daily headache, which is often refractory to standard treatment. New research has increased our understanding of this disorder and its treatment. This review focuses on recent clinical trials and advances in our understanding of migraine pathophysiology. Migraine research has traditionally focused on the more common episodic form of the disorder, but recent clinical trials have started to focus on chronic migraine or chronic daily headache. Topiramate, onabotulinum toxin type A, gabapentin, petasites and tizanidine are among the agents that appear to be effective in the treatment of chronic migraine. New acute medications including an inhaled form of dihydroergotamine will soon be available and neuromodulatory procedures such as occipital nerve stimulation may be effective for the most disabled patients. In the past few years, other studies have shed light on potential risk factors for chronic migraine such as medication-overuse headache, temporomandibular disorders, obstructive sleep apnea and obesity. This review explains advances in the treatment of chronic migraine, a common disorder seen in neurological practice. These new advances in preventive treatment and a better understanding of its risk factors will allow clinicians to better identify individuals at greatest risk and prevent the development of chronic migraine.

---

Title: Determination of high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) and choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) activity in the same population of cultured cells.
Abstract: Cholinergic neurotransmission relies on the coordinated function of high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) and choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) activity, yet technical limitations have prevented simultaneous measurement of these parameters in individual cell populations. We developed a novel dual-assay protocol to assess both HACU and ChAT activity sequentially in the same cultured cholinergic cells, enabling direct correlation of these functionally related processes. Primary septal cholinergic neurons were cultured from embryonic day 18 rat fetuses and maintained for 14-21 days in vitro. HACU was measured using [³H]choline uptake assays with hemicholinium-3 inhibition to confirm specificity, followed by cell harvesting and ChAT activity determination using [¹⁴C]acetyl-CoA as substrate. Muscarinic receptor modulation was examined using carbachol and atropine treatments. The dual-assay protocol successfully quantified both parameters without methodological interference. Baseline HACU rates averaged 45.2 ± 3.8 pmol/min/mg protein, while ChAT activity measured 12.7 ± 1.9 nmol/h/mg protein. Carbachol treatment (100 μM) significantly increased both HACU (168% of control) and ChAT activity (142% of control), effects blocked by atropine pretreatment. Strong positive correlation was observed between HACU and ChAT activity across individual culture wells (r = 0.78, p < 0.001). This methodology provides a valuable tool for investigating cholinergic function at the cellular level and demonstrates coordinated regulation of choline uptake and acetylcholine synthesis mechanisms. The approach will facilitate studies of cholinergic dysfunction in neurodegenerative diseases and therapeutic drug development.

Title: Current evaluation of the millennium phytomedicine- ginseng (II): Collected chemical entities, modern pharmacology, and clinical applications emanated from traditional Chinese medicine.
Abstract: This review, a sequel to part 1 in the series, collects about 107 chemical entities separated from the roots, leaves and flower buds of Panax ginseng, quinquefolius and notoginseng, and categorizes these entities into about 18 groups based on their structural similarity. The bioactivities of these chemical entities are described. The 'Yin and Yang' theory and the fundamentals of the 'five elements' applied to the traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) are concisely introduced to help readers understand how ginseng balances the dynamic equilibrium of human physiological processes from the TCM perspectives. This paper concerns the observation and experimental investigation of biological activities of ginseng used in the TCM of past and present cultures. The current biological findings of ginseng and its medical applications are narrated and critically discussed, including 1) its antihyperglycemic effect that may benefit type II diabetics; in vitro and in vivo studies demonstrated protection of ginseng on beta-cells and obese diabetic mouse models. The related clinical trial results are stated. 2) its aphrodisiac effect and cardiovascular effect that partially attribute to ginseng's bioactivity on nitric oxide (NO); 3) its cognitive effect and neuropharmacological effect that are intensively tested in various rat models using purified ginsenosides and show a hope to treat Parkinson's disease (PD); 4) its uses as an adjuvant or immunotherapeutic agent to enhance immune activity, appetite and life quality of cancer patients during their chemotherapy and radiation. Although the apoptotic effect of ginsenosides, especially Rh2, Rg3 and Compound K, on various tumor cells has been shown via different pathways, their clinical effectiveness remains to be tested. This paper also updates the antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-apoptotic and immune-stimulatory activities of ginseng, its ingredients and commercial products, as well as common side effects of ginseng mainly due to its overdose, and its pharmacokinetics.

---

Title: Characterization of the Neurochemical and Behavioral Effects of Solriamfetol (JZP-110), a Selective Dopamine and Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitor.
Abstract: Excessive sleepiness (ES) is associated with several sleep disorders, including narcolepsy and obstructive sleep apnea (OSA). A role for monoaminergic systems in treating these conditions is highlighted by the clinical use of US Food and Drug Administration-approved drugs that act on these systems, such as dextroamphetamine, methylphenidate, modafinil, and armodafinil. Solriamfetol (JZP-110) is a wake-promoting agent that is currently being evaluated to treat ES in patients with narcolepsy or OSA. Clinical and preclinical data suggest that the wake-promoting effects of solriamfetol differ from medications such as modafinil and amphetamine. The goal of the current studies was to characterize the mechanism of action of solriamfetol at monoamine transporters using in vitro and in vivo assays. Results indicate that solriamfetol has dual reuptake inhibition activity at dopamine (DA; IC50 = 2.9 μM) and norepinephrine (NE; IC50 = 4.4 μM) transporters, and this activity is associated in vivo with increased extracellular concentration of DA and NE as measured by microdialysis. Solriamfetol has negligible functional activity at the serotonin transporter (IC50 > 100 μM). Moreover, the wake-promoting effects of solriamfetol are probably owing to activity at DA and NE transporters rather than other neurotransmitter systems, such as histamine or orexin. The dual activity of solriamfetol at DA and NE transporters and the lack of significant monoamine-releasing properties of solriamfetol might explain the differences in the in vivo effects of solriamfetol compared with modafinil or amphetamine. Taken together, these data suggest that solriamfetol may offer an important advancement in the treatment of ES in patients with narcolepsy or OSA.

---

Title: Amino acid neurochemistry of the vertebrate retina.
Abstract: The vertebrate retina contains a diverse array of amino acid neurotransmitters and neuromodulators that mediate synaptic transmission between photoreceptors, bipolar cells, horizontal cells, amacrine cells, and ganglion cells. While glutamate and GABA have been extensively characterized as the primary excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters respectively, the functional roles of glycine, taurine, aspartate, and other amino acids remain incompletely understood. We employed high-performance liquid chromatography, immunocytochemistry, and electrophysiological recordings to comprehensively map amino acid distribution and function across retinal layers in cat, rabbit, and goldfish preparations. Glutamate immunoreactivity was predominantly localized to photoreceptor terminals and ON-bipolar cells, with tissue concentrations reaching 8-12 mM in outer plexiform layer. GABA was concentrated in horizontal cells and a subset of amacrine cells, showing species-specific distribution patterns. Glycine demonstrated robust immunostaining in narrow-field amacrine cells and exhibited co-localization with GABA in 23% of GABAergic neurons. Taurine, present at unexpectedly high concentrations (15-20 mM), showed uniform distribution across all retinal layers with particular enrichment in Müller cells. Electrophysiological analysis revealed that glycine mediates crossover inhibition in OFF-pathway ganglion cells, while taurine modulates calcium homeostasis and exhibits neuroprotective properties under ischemic conditions. These findings demonstrate that amino acid neurotransmission in the vertebrate retina involves complex, overlapping systems that extend beyond classical glutamatergic and GABAergic pathways, suggesting novel therapeutic targets for retinal degenerative diseases.

Title: Neuropharmacological effects of Nigerian honey in mice.
Abstract: Honey is a natural sweet substance that bees produce by transforming flower nectar or other sweet secretions of plants. It has widespread use in traditional medicine in various parts of the world. It has been reported to assist in building the entire central nervous system. The beneficial effects of honey have been attributed to the possible polyphenolic contents and some other constituents. The geographical locations and the sources of plant nectars may contribute to the effects of honey samples. Thus, we evaluated the neuropharmacological effects of six samples of honey (10%, 20% and 40%(V)/v, p.o.) from three geographical locations of Nigeria using the following behavioral models: Novelty-induced behaviors (NIB), learning and memory, pentobarbital-induced hypnosis, anxiolytic, anticonvulsant, analgesic and antidepressant models in mice. The results showed that honey significantly (p< 0.05) decreased locomotion and rearing behaviors in NIB and amphetamine-induced locomotor activity when compared to the control group. Exploratory behavior was significantly increased in both holeboard and elevated plus maze but had no significant effect on spatial working memory. Honey sample from Umudike has significant hypnotic and anticonvulsant effects. The antinociceptive models (hot plate and tail flick tests) showed that the honey samples significantly increased the pain reaction time and naloxone blocked these central antinociceptive effects. The force swimming test showed that only the Idanre (ID) honey sample had antidepressant effect. In conclusion, some of these honey samples have central inhibitory property, anxiolytic, antinociceptive, anticonvulsant and antidepressant effects, thus may be used as nutraceutic. It can also be inferred that some of these effects are probably mediated through dopaminergic and opioidergic systems.

Title: S(-)Propranolol as a discriminative stimulus and its comparison to the stimulus effects of cocaine in rats.
Abstract: The β-adrenergic antagonist propranolol exists as two enantiomers with distinct pharmacological profiles, with S(-)propranolol demonstrating membrane-stabilizing properties independent of β-blockade. Previous studies suggest that propranolol's discriminative stimulus properties may involve non-adrenergic mechanisms. The present study investigated whether S(-)propranolol could serve as a discriminative stimulus in rats and examined potential cross-generalization with cocaine, given both compounds' membrane-stabilizing effects. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were trained to discriminate S(-)propranolol (10 mg/kg, IP) from saline in a two-lever operant conditioning paradigm using a FR10 schedule reinforced with food pellets. Following acquisition, generalization tests were conducted with various doses of S(-)propranolol (1-20 mg/kg), racemic propranolol (2.5-15 mg/kg), R(+)propranolol (5-20 mg/kg), and cocaine (2.5-20 mg/kg). Rats successfully acquired the S(-)propranolol discrimination within 45±8 sessions. Dose-response analysis revealed full generalization to the training dose occurred at 7.5-10 mg/kg S(-)propranolol (ED50 = 4.2 mg/kg). Racemic propranolol produced partial generalization (maximum 65% drug-lever responding), while R(+)propranolol showed minimal generalization (<25%). Notably, cocaine demonstrated significant cross-generalization with S(-)propranolol, producing dose-dependent increases in drug-lever responding (ED50 = 8.7 mg/kg) with maximum generalization of 78% at 15 mg/kg. These findings suggest that S(-)propranolol's discriminative stimulus effects are mediated by mechanisms distinct from β-adrenergic blockade and share common interoceptive properties with cocaine, likely involving sodium channel blockade or other membrane-stabilizing actions.

Title: A Mobile Health Solution Complementing Psychopharmacology-Supported Smoking Cessation: Randomized Controlled Trial.
Abstract: Smoking cessation is a persistent leading public health challenge. Mobile health (mHealth) solutions are emerging to improve smoking cessation treatments. Previous approaches have proposed supporting cessation with tailored motivational messages. Some managed to provide short-term improvements in smoking cessation. Yet, these approaches were either static in terms of personalization or human-based nonscalable solutions. Additionally, long-term effects were neither presented nor assessed in combination with existing psychopharmacological therapies. This study aimed to analyze the long-term efficacy of a mobile app supporting psychopharmacological therapy for smoking cessation and complementarily assess the involved innovative technology. A 12-month, randomized, open-label, parallel-group trial comparing smoking cessation rates was performed at Virgen del Rocío University Hospital in Seville (Spain). Smokers were randomly allocated to a control group (CG) receiving usual care (psychopharmacological treatment, n=120) or an intervention group (IG) receiving psychopharmacological treatment and using a mobile app providing artificial intelligence-generated and tailored smoking cessation support messages (n=120). The secondary objectives were to analyze health-related quality of life and monitor healthy lifestyle and physical exercise habits. Safety was assessed according to the presence of adverse events related to the pharmacological therapy. Per-protocol and intention-to-treat analyses were performed. Incomplete data and multinomial regression analyses were performed to assess the variables influencing participant cessation probability. The technical solution was assessed according to the precision of the tailored motivational smoking cessation messages and user engagement. Cessation and no cessation subgroups were compared using t tests. A voluntary satisfaction questionnaire was administered at the end of the intervention to all participants who completed the trial. In the IG, abstinence was 2.75 times higher (adjusted OR 3.45, P=.01) in the per-protocol analysis and 2.15 times higher (adjusted OR 3.13, P=.002) in the intention-to-treat analysis. Lost data analysis and multinomial logistic models showed different patterns in participants who dropped out. Regarding safety, 14 of 120 (11.7%) IG participants and 13 of 120 (10.8%) CG participants had 19 and 23 adverse events, respectively (P=.84). None of the clinical secondary objective measures showed relevant differences between the groups. The system was able to learn and tailor messages for improved effectiveness in supporting smoking cessation but was unable to reduce the time between a message being sent and opened. In either case, there was no relevant difference between the cessation and no cessation subgroups. However, a significant difference was found in system engagement at 6 months (P=.04) but not in all subsequent months. High system appreciation was reported at the end of the study. The proposed mHealth solution complementing psychopharmacological therapy showed greater efficacy for achieving 1-year tobacco abstinence as compared with psychopharmacological therapy alone. It provides a basis for artificial intelligence-based future approaches. ClinicalTrials.gov NCT03553173; https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03553173. RR2-10.2196/12464.

Title: The Influence of Prolonged Acetylsalicylic Acid Supplementation-Induced Gastritis on the Neurochemistry of the Sympathetic Neurons Supplying Prepyloric Region of the Porcine Stomach.
Abstract: This experiment was designed to establish the localization and neurochemical phenotyping of sympathetic neurons supplying prepyloric area of the porcine stomach in a physiological state and during acetylsalicylic acid (ASA) induced gastritis. In order to localize the sympathetic perikarya the stomachs of both control and acetylsalicylic acid treated (ASA group) animals were injected with neuronal retrograde tracer Fast Blue (FB). Seven days post FB injection, animals were divided into a control and ASA supplementation group. The ASA group was given 100 mg/kg of b.w. ASA orally for 21 days. On the 28th day all pigs were euthanized with gradual overdose of anesthetic. Then fourteen-micrometer-thick cryostat sections were processed for routine double-labeling immunofluorescence, using primary antisera directed towards tyrosine hydroxylase (TH), dopamine β-hydroxylase (DβH), neuropeptide Y (NPY), galanin (GAL), neuronal nitric oxide synthase (nNOS), leu 5-enkephalin (LENK), cocaine- and amphetamine- regulated transcript peptide (CART), calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), substance P (SP) and vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP). The data obtained in this study indicate that postganglionic sympathetic nerve fibers supplying prepyloric area of the porcine stomach originate from the coeliac-cranial mesenteric ganglion complex (CCMG). In control animals, the FB-labelled neurons expressed TH (94.85 ± 1.01%), DβH (97.10 ± 0.97%), NPY (46.88 ± 2.53%) and GAL (8.40 ± 0.53%). In ASA group, TH- and DβH- positive nerve cells were reduced (85.78 ± 2.65% and 88.82 ± 1.63% respectively). Moreover, ASA- induced gastritis resulted in increased expression of NPY (76.59 ± 3.02%) and GAL (26.45 ± 2.75%) as well as the novo-synthesis of nNOS (6.13 ± 1.11%) and LENK (4.77 ± 0.42%) in traced CCMG neurons. Additionally, a network of CART-, CGRP-, SP-, VIP-, LENK-, nNOS- immunoreactive (IR) nerve fibers encircling the FB-positive perikarya were observed in both intact and ASA-treated animals. The results of this study indicate involvement of these neuropeptides in the development or presumably counteraction of gastric inflammation.

Title: The Psychopharmacology of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Preclinical Roadmap.
Abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) affects 1-3% of the global population and is characterized by intrusive thoughts and repetitive behaviors mediated by dysregulated cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuits. Despite decades of clinical research, current pharmacotherapies provide adequate symptom relief for only 40-60% of patients, highlighting an urgent need for novel therapeutic targets. This review synthesizes emerging preclinical evidence to identify promising pharmacological avenues for OCD treatment. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of rodent models exhibiting OCD-like phenotypes, including genetic knockout models, pharmacological induction paradigms, and behavioral paradigms such as marble burying and schedule-induced polydipsia. Key findings demonstrate that beyond traditional serotonergic mechanisms, glutamatergic dysfunction plays a central role in OCD pathophysiology, with NMDA receptor hypofunction and altered mGluR5 signaling consistently observed across multiple model systems. Preclinical studies reveal therapeutic potential for glutamate modulators including memantine, ketamine, and mGluR2/3 agonists, which normalize compulsive behaviors and restore CSTC circuit function. Additionally, emerging evidence supports roles for GABAergic interneuron dysfunction, altered dopaminergic signaling in the nucleus accumbens, and neuroinflammatory processes involving microglia activation. Novel targets showing preclinical efficacy include sigma-1 receptor modulators, adenosine A2A receptor antagonists, and histone deacetylase inhibitors. Integration of optogenetic and chemogenetic approaches has further elucidated circuit-specific mechanisms and identified the orbitofrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex as critical nodes for therapeutic intervention. These preclinical findings provide a roadmap for developing next-generation OCD pharmacotherapies targeting glutamate, GABA, and neuromodulatory systems beyond serotonin.

Title: Neuropharmacological profile of novel and selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists: WAY-181187 and WAY-208466.
Abstract: The 5-HT6 receptor has emerged as a promising therapeutic target for cognitive enhancement and treatment of neuropsychiatric disorders. We characterized the neuropharmacological profiles of two novel selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists, WAY-181187 and WAY-208466, using in vitro binding assays, functional studies, and behavioral paradigms. Both compounds demonstrated high selectivity for 5-HT6 receptors with Ki values of 4.2 nM (WAY-181187) and 2.8 nM (WAY-208466) and >100-fold selectivity over other serotonin receptor subtypes. Functional assays using HEK293 cells stably expressing human 5-HT6 receptors confirmed full agonist activity, with EC50 values of 8.1 nM and 5.3 nM for WAY-181187 and WAY-208466, respectively. Both compounds stimulated cAMP accumulation and showed excellent brain penetration following systemic administration in rats. In behavioral studies, acute administration of WAY-181187 (3-30 mg/kg, p.o.) and WAY-208466 (1-10 mg/kg, p.o.) dose-dependently enhanced performance in the novel object recognition task and reversed scopolamine-induced deficits in spatial working memory. The cognitive-enhancing effects were blocked by the selective 5-HT6 antagonist SB-271046, confirming receptor-mediated mechanisms. Neither compound produced significant effects on locomotor activity, anxiety-related behaviors, or feeding at cognitively active doses. These findings demonstrate that WAY-181187 and WAY-208466 are potent, selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists with procognitive properties, supporting the therapeutic potential of 5-HT6 receptor activation for treating cognitive impairments associated with Alzheimer's disease and schizophrenia.

Title: [The neurochemistry and neuropharmacology of frontotemporal dementia].
Abstract: To review the neurochemical features and therapeutic options for frontotemporal dementia (FTD). The main neurochemical alterations in FTD are the serotoninergic and dopamine depletion. In contrast with Alzheimer's and diffuse Lewy bodies disease, there are not significant alterations of the cholinergic system. Cerebral perfusion and glucose metabolism studies usually show hypoperfusion or hypometabolism, with predominant involvement of temporal and frontal cortices. There have been described some alterations related with oxidative stress and apoptosis, although its pathogenetic role in FTD is not well known. Treatment of FTD is not well established, because there are only a few studies with some drugs. The most studied drugs are serotonin reuptake inhibitors, however, despite the well-known serotoninergic deficiency described in FTD, the results are not conclusive. The main neurochemical alterations of FTD are serotoninergic and dopaminergic deficiencies. The treatment is not well established, although it should be theoretically ideal to use drugs which modulate these neurotransmitter systems.

Title: Current perspectives on the pathophysiology of schizophrenia, depression, and anxiety disorders.
Abstract: This article reviews the rapidly changing concepts related to the pathophysiology of major psychiatric disorders. The current era is an exciting one for psychiatric research and the rapidity with which advances are being made is a source of hope to patients with these disorders and for society.

Title: Neurobiology and neuroimmunology of Tourette's syndrome: an update.
Abstract: Tourette's syndrome (TS) is a complex neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by chronic motor and vocal tics, with emerging evidence implicating both neurodevelopmental abnormalities and immune dysfunction in its pathogenesis. Recent neurobiological investigations have expanded our understanding of the cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuits underlying tic generation, while neuroimmunological studies suggest autoimmune mechanisms may contribute to symptom onset and exacerbation in susceptible individuals. We conducted a comprehensive review of recent neuroimaging, post-mortem, and immunological studies published between 1995-2003, analyzing data from over 2,400 TS patients and 1,800 controls across multiple research centers. Structural and functional neuroimaging studies consistently demonstrate altered dopaminergic neurotransmission within the caudate nucleus and putamen, with increased D2 receptor binding density correlating with tic severity (r=0.67, p<0.001). Post-mortem analyses reveal decreased interneuron populations in the striatum, particularly parvalbumin-positive GABAergic cells, suggesting disinhibition of motor programs. Immunological investigations demonstrate elevated anti-neuronal antibodies in 34% of TS patients versus 8% of controls (p<0.0001), with molecular mimicry between Group A streptococcal antigens and basal ganglia proteins. Cytokine profiling reveals increased IL-12 and TNF-α levels during symptom exacerbations. These findings support a multifactorial model wherein genetic susceptibility, environmental triggers, and immune dysregulation converge to disrupt CSTC circuit function. The identification of specific neuroimmune biomarkers may facilitate early diagnosis and guide development of targeted immunomodulatory therapies for TS management.

---

Title: Can the mechanisms of aluminum neurotoxicity be integrated into a unified scheme?
Abstract: Regardless of the host, the route of administration, or the speciation, aluminum is a potent neurotoxicant. In the young adult or developmentally mature host, the neuronal response to Al exposure can be dichotomized on morphological grounds. In one, intraneuronal neurofilamentous aggregates are formed, whereas in the other, significant neurochemical and neurophysiological perturbations are induced without neurofilamentous aggregate formation. Evidence is presented that the induction of neurofilamentous aggregates is a consequence of alterations in the posttranslational processing of neurofilament (NF), particularly with regard to phosphorylation state. Although Al has been reported to impact on gene expression, this does not appear to be critical to the induction of cytoskeletal pathology. In hosts responding to Al exposure without the induction of cytoskeletal pathology, impairments in glucose utilization, agonist-stimulated inositol phosphate accumulation, free radical-mediated cytotoxicity, lipid peroxidation, reduced cholinergic function, and altered protein phosphorylation have been described. The extent to which these neurochemical modifications correlate with the induction of a characteristic neurobehavioral state is unknown. In addition to these paradigms, Al is toxic in the immediate postnatal interval. Whether unique mechanisms of toxicity are involved during development remains to be determined. In this article, the mechanisms of Al neurotoxicity are reviewed and recommendations are put forth with regard to future research. Primary among these is the determination of the molecular site of Al toxicity, and whether this is based on Al substitution for divalent metals in a number of biological processes. Encompassed within this is the need to further understand the genesis of host- and developmental-specific responses.

---

Title: In vivo monitoring of extracellular glutamate in the brain with a microsensor.
Abstract: Dysregulation of glutamate neurotransmission has been implicated in numerous neurological disorders including stroke, epilepsy, and neurodegenerative diseases. Current methods for measuring extracellular glutamate in vivo, such as microdialysis, suffer from poor temporal resolution and tissue damage. We developed a novel glutamate-selective microsensor for real-time monitoring of extracellular glutamate concentrations in the living brain. The microsensor consists of a platinum microelectrode (25 μm diameter) coated with glutamate oxidase and poly(o-phenylenediamine) to confer selectivity and minimize interferents. The sensor was calibrated in vitro and demonstrated linear response to glutamate (10 μM to 1 mM, r² = 0.98) with minimal interference from ascorbic acid, dopamine, and GABA. Response time was < 2 seconds with detection limit of 5 μM. In anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8), microsensors were stereotaxically implanted into the striatum and hippocampus. Baseline glutamate levels were 12.3 ± 2.1 μM in striatum and 8.7 ± 1.8 μM in hippocampus. Local application of potassium chloride (100 mM) produced rapid glutamate increases of 340 ± 45% in striatum and 280 ± 38% in hippocampus, returning to baseline within 5 minutes. Focal ischemia induced by middle cerebral artery occlusion resulted in sustained glutamate elevation (peak: 850 ± 120% of baseline) in the ischemic penumbra. This microsensor technology provides unprecedented temporal resolution for monitoring glutamate dynamics and offers valuable insights into excitotoxic mechanisms underlying neurological pathology.

---

Title: Characterization of rat behavior in the elevated plus-maze using a directed graph.
Abstract: The elevated plus-maze is a device widely used to assess rodent anxiety under the effect of several treatments, including pharmacological agents. The animal is placed at the center of the apparatus, which consists of two open arms and two arms enclosed by walls, and the number of entries and duration of stay in each arm are measured for a 5-min exposure period. The effect of an anxiolytic drug is to increase the percentage of time spent and number of entries into the open arms. In this work, we propose a new measure of anxiety levels in the rat submitted to the elevated plus-maze. We represented the spatial structure of the elevated plus-maze in terms of a directed graph and studied the statistics of the rat's transitions between the nodes of the graph. By counting the number of times each transition is made and ordering them in descending frequency we represented the rat's behavior in a rank-frequency plot. Our results suggest that the curves obtained under different pharmacological conditions can be well fitted by a power law with an exponent sensitive to both the drug type and the dose used.

Abstract: Three main directions of neuropharmacology developing at the S. V. Anichkov Department of Pharmacology of the Institute of Experimental Medicine, include the pharmacology of neurogenic damages of visceral organs, the pharmacology of memory disorders and the psychoneuropharmacology. The evolution of the outlook and the main achievements are described. The perspectives are analysed.

Title: Neuropsychiatric aspects of Parkinson disease psychopharmacology: Insights from circuit dynamics.
Abstract: Parkinson disease (PD) is a neurodegenerative disorder with a complex pathophysiology characterized by the progressive loss of dopaminergic neurons within the substantia nigra. Persons with PD experience several motoric and neuropsychiatric symptoms. Neuropsychiatric features of PD include depression, anxiety, psychosis, impulse control disorders, and apathy. In this chapter, we will utilize the National Institutes of Mental Health Research Domain Criteria (RDoC) to frame and integrate observations from two prevailing disease constructions: neurotransmitter anomalies and circuit physiology. When there is available evidence, we posit how unified translational observations may have clinical relevance and postulate importance outside of PD. Finally, we review the limited evidence available for pharmacologic management of these symptoms.

Title: A pre-clinical study showing how dopaminergic drugs administered during pre-exposure can impair or facilitate latent inhibition.
Abstract: It has been suggested that, in classical conditioning, dopamine (DA) codes for (a) attention to the conditioned stimulus (CS) or (b) the intensity of the unconditioned stimulus. To clarify the role of DA in pre-clinical classical conditioning studies. An existing model of classical conditioning presented by Schmajuk, Lam, and Gray (J Exp Psychol Anim Behav Process 22:321-349, 1996) suggests that DA cells in the ventral midbrain area code for the attentionally modulated internal representation of the CS. It is assumed that this representation is increased by dopaminergic agonists and decreased by dopaminergic antagonists. Computer simulations with the model describe the effect of nicotine and haloperidol on latent inhibition. Simulations replicate experimental results demonstrating that both nicotine and haloperidol affect latent inhibition when administered during the pre-exposure phase. In addition, the model reproduces data showing that administration of nicotine or haloperidol results in the impairment or facilitation of latent inhibition depending on the duration of CS or the number of CSs. The model demonstrates that pre-clinical experimental results, including cell activity and pharmacological data, are consistent with an attentional role for DA in classical conditioning.

Title: Differential neuropsychopharmacological influences of naturally occurring tropane alkaloids anisodamine versus scopolamine.
Abstract: Tropane alkaloids derived from Solanaceae plants have been extensively studied for their anticholinergic properties, yet differential neuropsychopharmacological effects among structurally similar compounds remain poorly characterized. This study compared the behavioral and neurochemical effects of anisodamine, a naturally occurring tropane alkaloid from Anisodus tanguticus, with scopolamine in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received intraperitoneal injections of anisodamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg), scopolamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg), or saline vehicle. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity monitoring, Morris water maze performance, and prepulse inhibition testing. Neurochemical analyses measured acetylcholine, dopamine, and serotonin levels in hippocampus, striatum, and prefrontal cortex using HPLC. Both compounds dose-dependently impaired spatial learning and memory consolidation, with scopolamine producing more severe deficits at equivalent doses. Anisodamine induced moderate hyperlocomotion at higher doses, whereas scopolamine caused initial hypoactivity followed by delayed hyperactivity. Prepulse inhibition was disrupted by both alkaloids, but anisodamine showed less pronounced effects. Neurochemically, scopolamine produced greater acetylcholine depletion in hippocampus and prefrontal cortex compared to anisodamine. Interestingly, anisodamine selectively increased striatal dopamine levels, an effect not observed with scopolamine. These findings suggest that despite structural similarity, anisodamine exhibits a distinct neuropsychopharmacological profile characterized by milder cognitive impairment and unique dopaminergic interactions, potentially offering therapeutic advantages over conventional anticholinergic agents in certain clinical applications.

Title: Validation of a mechanical apparatus for the measurement of avian walking.
Abstract: Recent research has demonstrated that avian species may show an enhanced locomotor response to repeated drug exposure, a phenomenon called sensitization. Further research in this domain is warranted as such results not only establish the generality of previous findings, but may lend to a better understanding of sensitization in general. At present, there are no well-studied measurement devices for automating the measurement of bird movement. The present research evaluated a mechanical device comprised of moveable floor panels to assess its suitability. Evaluations of the device were made over the course of repeated cocaine administration. The results showed that there was high correspondence between the apparatus' responses and human observers' responses to movement by pigeons. The apparatus, furthermore, provided useful data on spatial orientation that revealed individual differences in the response to cocaine beyond those available from a collapsed measure of overall output. In sum, this recording strategy appears a viable instrument for the measurement of avian locomotion.
