Title: MR spectroscopy in diagnosis and neurological decision-making.
Abstract: One of the most prolific chemical and anatomical imaging techniques of recent decades, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), includes the ability to noninvasively assess neurochemical changes with magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS). Practical concerns are paramount in applying MRS, such as what the manufacturer provides with a routine MRI scanner, what methods are well tolerated by patients, and what has proved most diagnostically productive over a 25 year span of preliminary exploration of the technology. In this review, the authors explain the technical and neurochemical aspects of MRS and critically discuss its clinical neuroimaging applications.

---

Title: Pharmacotherapy of mental illness--a historical analysis.
Abstract: The treatment of mental illness has evolved dramatically over the past century, with major paradigm shifts occurring in our understanding of neurobiological mechanisms and therapeutic approaches. This historical analysis examines the development of psychopharmacological interventions from the pre-pharmacological era through contemporary practice. We conducted a comprehensive review of psychiatric treatment modalities from 1900-2000, analyzing primary literature, clinical trial data, and epidemiological studies to trace the evolution of mental health pharmacotherapy. The pre-1950 era was characterized by non-specific treatments including barbiturates, insulin coma therapy, and early electroconvulsive approaches. The discovery of chlorpromazine in 1952 marked the beginning of modern psychopharmacology, leading to the development of phenothiazines and the first effective antipsychotic medications. The 1950s-1960s witnessed the introduction of tricyclic antidepressants and benzodiazepines, fundamentally altering treatment approaches for mood and anxiety disorders. The 1980s-1990s brought selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and atypical antipsychotics, offering improved efficacy and tolerability profiles. Our analysis reveals three distinct phases: the empirical period (1900-1950), characterized by trial-and-error approaches; the serendipitous discovery phase (1950-1980), marked by accidental findings leading to major therapeutic breakthroughs; and the rational drug design era (1980-present), driven by advancing neurobiological understanding. This historical perspective demonstrates the critical importance of continued neuroscience research in developing more effective and targeted psychiatric medications, while highlighting persistent challenges in treatment-resistant conditions and the need for personalized therapeutic approaches.

---

Title: Neuropharmacology of brain-stimulation-evoked aggression.
Abstract: Evidence is reviewed concerning the brain areas and neurotransmitters involved in aggressive behavior in the cat and rodent. In the cat, two distinct neural circuits involving the hypothalamus and PAG subserve two different kinds of aggression: defensive rage and predatory (quiet-biting) attack. The roles played by the neurotransmitters serotonin, GABA, glutamate, opioids, cholecystokinin, substance P, norepinephrine, dopamine, and acetylcholine in the modulation and expression of aggression are discussed. For the rat, a single area, largely coincident with the intermediate hypothalamic area, is crucial for the expression of attack; variations in the rat attack response in natural settings are due largely to environmental variables. Experimental evidence emphasizing the roles of serotonin and GABA in modulating hypothalamically evoked attack in the rat is discussed. It is concluded that significant progress has been made concerning our knowledge of the circuitry underlying the neural basis of aggression. Although new and important insights have been made concerning neurotransmitter regulation of aggressive behavior, wide gaps in our knowledge remain.

Title: Probing the neurochemical correlates of motivation and decision making.
Abstract: Online electrochemical detection techniques are the state-of-the-art for evaluating chemical communication in the brain underlying motivated behavior and decision making. In this Viewpoint, we discuss avenues for future technological development, as well as the requirement for increasingly sophisticated and interdisciplinary behavioral analysis.

Title: Time-dependent behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic dysregulation in female C57BL/6 mice caused by chronic high-fat diet intake.
Abstract: Obesity and metabolic dysfunction are increasingly recognized as risk factors for neuropsychiatric disorders, yet the temporal relationship between diet-induced metabolic changes and behavioral alterations remains poorly understood, particularly in females. This study investigated time-dependent effects of chronic high-fat diet (HFD) consumption on behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic parameters in female C57BL/6 mice. Eight-week-old female mice were fed either standard chow (10% fat) or HFD (60% fat) for 2, 8, or 16 weeks (n=12-15/group). Behavioral assessments included open field test, elevated plus maze, forced swim test, and novel object recognition. Neurochemical analyses measured monoamine levels in prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and striatum using HPLC. Metabolic parameters included body weight, glucose tolerance, insulin sensitivity, and serum leptin levels. HFD mice showed progressive weight gain and glucose intolerance by 2 weeks, with insulin resistance developing by 8 weeks. Anxiety-like behavior emerged at 8 weeks, accompanied by reduced dopamine and serotonin levels in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Depressive-like behavior and cognitive impairment were evident only after 16 weeks of HFD exposure, coinciding with further reductions in hippocampal BDNF expression and elevated neuroinflammatory markers. Serum leptin levels were elevated throughout all time points but showed diminished central leptin sensitivity after 8 weeks. These findings demonstrate that chronic HFD consumption in female mice produces sequential metabolic dysfunction, anxiety, and finally depression with cognitive deficits, suggesting distinct temporal windows for intervention strategies targeting diet-induced neuropsychiatric complications.

Title: Neuropsychopharmacology and the genetics of schizophrenia: a history of the diagnosis of schizophrenia.
Abstract: Development of the diagnostic concept of schizophrenia (dementia praecox) is traced from the fourth edition of [Kraepelin, E., 1893. Ein Kurzes Lehrbuch der Psychiatrie. 4 Aufl. Barth, Lepzig] textbook to the DSM-IV [American Psychiatric Association, 1994. Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders. Fourth ed. American Psychiatric Association, Washington, 273-316]. The differences between the criteria used by Bleuler [Bleuler, E., 1911. Dementia Praecox oder Gruppe der Schizophrenien. Deuticke, Leipzig] and Schneider [Fortschr. Neurol. Psychiatr. 25 (1957) 487] in the diagnosis of schizophrenia are discussed. The nosologic contributions of Kleist [Klin. Wochenschr. 2 (1923) 962] and Leonhard [Leonhard, K., 1957. Aufteilung der endogenen Psychosen. Akademie, Berlin]--which split schizophrenia into two major classes of disease with several forms and subforms--are outlined. Epidemiological findings--which show wide variations in the prevalence of schizophrenia in the general population and in the admission rate of schizophrenics to psychiatric clinics--are presented. Findings in genetic studies are reviewed with special reference to family, twin, and adoption studies which have raised the possibility that heredity plays an important role in the etiology of schizophrenia; mathematical analyses are examined which have ruled out monogenic transmission, as well as molecular genetic investigations indicating that the schizophrenic population is genetically heterogeneous. The relevance of findings with endophenotypes to the genetics of schizophrenia is questioned. The history of pharmacotherapy, neuropharmacology, and psychopharmacology of schizophrenia is outlined. Attention is focused on findings which indicate that the schizophrenic population is pharmacologically heterogenous. It is emphasized that neuropsychopharmacology, through its unique capability of linking the effects of psychotropic drugs to brain structures--encoded by genes which have been identified--offers a pioneering methodology for bridging the gap between the genes and psychiatric nosology. It is pointed out that for the detection of subpopulations within schizophrenia, clinical investigations with antipsychotic drugs have to proceed beyond the demonstration of therapeutic efficacy to the identification of treatment-responsive form(s) of illness. Early findings by Fish [L'Encephale 53 (1964) 245] are presented which indicate that affect-laden paraphrenia, one of the three forms of unsystematic schizophrenia in Leonhard [Leonhard, K., 1957. Aufteilung der endogenen Psychosen. Akademie, Berlin] classification, is the treatment-responsive subpopulation within schizophrenia for typical antipsychotic drugs. It is suggested that if the findings of Fish [L'Encephale 53 (1964) 245] could be verified, affect-laden paraphrenia would qualify for molecular genetic research. Another possible subpopulation that might qualify for genetic research is systematic hebephrenia, one of the three forms of systematic hebephrenia. The paper concludes that resolving the heterogeneity of the schizophrenic population would open up a new perspective for genetic research and for the pharmacotherapy of the different illnesses covered up for a century by the diagnostic label of schizophrenia.

---

Title: Deviance detection in auditory subcortical structures: what can we learn from neurochemistry and neural connectivity?
Abstract: A remarkable ability of animals that is critical for survival is to detect and respond to to unexpected stimuli in an ever-changing world. Auditory neurons that show stimulus-specific adaptation (SSA), i.e., a decrease in their response to frequently occurring stimuli while maintaining responsiveness when different stimuli are presented, might participate in the coding of deviance occurrence. Traditionally, deviance detection is measured by the mismatch negativity (MMN) potential in studies of evoked local field potentials. We present a review of the state-of-the-art of SSA in auditory subcortical nuclei, i.e., the inferior colliculus and medial geniculate body of the thalamus, and link the differential receptor distribution and neural connectivity of those regions in which extreme SSA has been found. Furthermore, we review both SSA and MMN-like responses in auditory and non-auditory areas that exhibit multimodal sensitivities that we suggest conform to a distributed network encoding for deviance detection. The understanding of the neurochemistry and response similarities across these different regions will contribute to a better understanding of the neural mechanism underlying deviance detection.

Title: Motor control: exploring the neurochemistry of subliminal inhibition.
Abstract: A new study links individual differences in unconsciously triggered motor control to variability in GABA neurotransmitter concentration in the supplementary motor area of the human brain.

Title: The 20th scientific conference of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology.
Abstract: The 20th Scientific Conference of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology convened in Key Biscayne, Florida from April 2-5, 2014, bringing together 312 researchers from 28 countries to address critical advances in neuroimmune interactions and therapeutic interventions. This milestone conference featured 89 oral presentations, 156 poster sessions, and 4 keynote addresses focusing on emerging paradigms in neuroinflammation, blood-brain barrier dynamics, and immune-mediated neurological disorders. Major themes included novel therapeutic targets for multiple sclerosis, HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders, and neurodegenerative diseases. Significant attention was devoted to recent discoveries regarding microglial activation states, astrocyte-immune cell crosstalk, and the role of peripheral immune cells in CNS pathology. Breakthrough presentations highlighted the therapeutic potential of selective immunomodulators, including fingolimod analogs and novel complement inhibitors, in treating neuroinflammatory conditions. Data presented demonstrated promising preclinical results for targeting specific cytokine pathways, particularly IL-17 and TNF-α signaling cascades, in animal models of neurodegeneration. Clinical trial updates revealed encouraging outcomes for several investigational compounds, including a Phase II study of a CCR5 antagonist for HIV-associated dementia showing significant cognitive improvements. The conference emphasized the growing recognition of bidirectional brain-immune communication and its implications for drug development. These proceedings underscore the rapid evolution of neuroimmune pharmacology as a distinct therapeutic discipline and highlight promising avenues for translating mechanistic insights into clinical interventions for debilitating neurological conditions affecting millions worldwide.

Title: Succi nervorum: a brief history of neurochemistry.
Abstract: The study of neurochemistry has evolved dramatically since the early anatomical descriptions of nervous tissue, yet the historical development of our understanding of neural chemical processes remains poorly documented in contemporary literature. This review examines the progression of neurochemical research from the initial observations of "succi nervorum" (nerve juices) in the 18th century through the establishment of modern neurotransmitter theory. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of primary historical sources, including original manuscripts, laboratory notebooks, and published communications from 1750 to 1970, supplemented by bibliometric analysis of key publications in neurochemistry. Our investigation reveals three distinct phases in neurochemical discovery: the pre-electrical period (1750-1850) characterized by humoral theories of nerve function, the electrophysiological era (1850-1920) dominated by purely electrical models of neural transmission, and the modern chemical transmission period (1920-1970) establishing neurotransmitter mechanisms. Critical developments included du Bois-Reymond's early speculation about chemical transmission in 1877, Elliott's recognition of sympathetic nerve-adrenergic relationships in 1904, and Dale's definitive acetylcholine studies beginning in 1914. The transition from vague concepts of "nerve essences" to precise molecular mechanisms required fundamental advances in analytical chemistry, electrophysiology, and pharmacology. Understanding this historical progression illuminates current challenges in neuropsychopharmacology and provides context for emerging fields such as neuroproteomics and metabolomics, suggesting that historical perspective remains essential for interpreting contemporary neurochemical research paradigms.

---

Title: Endogenous nociceptin/orphanin FQ (N/OFQ) contributes to haloperidol-induced changes of nigral amino acid transmission and parkinsonism: a combined microdialysis and behavioral study in naïve and nociceptin/orphanin FQ receptor knockout mice.
Abstract: The contribution of endogenous nociceptin/orphanin FQ (N/OFQ) to neuroleptic-induced parkinsonism has been evaluated in haloperidol-treated mice. Pharmacological blockade of N/OFQ receptors (NOP) via systemic administration of 1-[(3R,4R)-1-cyclooctylmethyl-3-hydroxymethyl-4-piperidyl]-3-ethyl-1,3-dihydro-2H benzimidazol-2-one (J-113397, 0.01-10 mg/kg i.p.) or central injection of [Nphe(1),Arg(14),Lys(15)]N/OFQ-NH(2) (UFP-101, 10 nmol i.c.v.) attenuated (0.8 mg/kg) haloperidol-induced motor deficits as evaluated by a battery of behavioral tests providing complementary information on motor parameters: the bar, drag and rotarod tests. A combined neurochemical and behavioral approach was then used to investigate whether the substantia nigra reticulata could be involved in antiakinetic actions of J-113397. Microdialysis combined to the bar test revealed that haloperidol (0.3 and 0.8 mg/kg i.p.) caused a dose-dependent and prolonged elevation of immobility time (i.e. akinesia) which was associated with an increase in nigral glutamate and a reduction in GABA release. Conversely, J-113397 (1 mg/kg) alone reduced glutamate and elevated nigral GABA release, and when challenged against haloperidol, counteracted its behavioral and neurochemical effects. Microdialysis coupled to behavioral testing also demonstrated that NOP receptor knockout mice were resistant to haloperidol (0.3 mg/kg) compared to wild-type mice, lack of response being associated with a reversal of glutamate release facilitation into inhibition and no change in nigral GABA release. This study provides pharmacological and genetic evidence that endogenous N/OFQ contributes to haloperidol-induced akinesia and changes of amino acid transmission in mice. Moreover, it confirms the view that NOP receptor antagonists are capable of reversing akinesia across species and genotypes and may prove effective in relieving neuroleptic-induced parkinsonism.

---

Title: From systems biology to dynamical neuropharmacology: proposal for a new methodology.
Abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches have primarily relied on reductionist methodologies that examine isolated molecular targets, often failing to capture the complex, dynamic interactions within neural networks. The emergence of systems biology presents an unprecedented opportunity to revolutionize drug discovery and therapeutic intervention in neurological disorders by integrating multi-scale biological data and network dynamics. We propose a novel methodological framework termed "dynamical neuropharmacology" that combines high-throughput genomic and proteomic data with computational modeling to predict drug effects on neural circuit function. Our approach utilizes differential equation-based models to simulate temporal dynamics of neurotransmitter systems, incorporating feedback loops, crosstalk between signaling pathways, and emergent network properties. We applied this methodology to analyze the effects of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) on hippocampal-prefrontal circuits using integrated datasets from microarray expression profiles, protein-protein interaction networks, and electrophysiological recordings from rat brain slices. The dynamical models successfully predicted biphasic dose-response relationships and identified previously unrecognized off-target effects involving GABAergic interneurons. Validation experiments confirmed 78% of model predictions regarding synaptic plasticity changes following chronic SSRI treatment. Our results demonstrate that dynamical neuropharmacology can reveal non-intuitive drug mechanisms and predict therapeutic windows more accurately than conventional approaches. This systems-level methodology offers a paradigm shift toward personalized neurotherapeutics and may accelerate the development of more effective treatments for psychiatric and neurological disorders by leveraging the inherent complexity of neural systems rather than oversimplifying them.

---

Title: Proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy detection of neurotransmitters in dorsomedial medulla correlate with spontaneous baroreceptor reflex function.
Abstract: The dorsomedial medulla (DMM) contains critical cardiovascular regulatory neurons that modulate baroreceptor reflex sensitivity, but the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this control remain poorly understood. We hypothesized that GABA and glutamate concentrations in the DMM would correlate with spontaneous baroreceptor reflex function in conscious rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) were instrumented with arterial catheters for continuous blood pressure monitoring. Spontaneous baroreceptor sensitivity (BRS) was calculated using sequence analysis of systolic pressure and pulse interval over 2-hour recording periods. Animals were then anesthetized and subjected to localized proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (¹H-MRS) using a 7.0 Tesla scanner with a 2×2×2 mm³ voxel positioned in the DMM. GABA and glutamate concentrations were quantified relative to creatine using LCModel analysis. BRS ranged from 0.8 to 3.2 ms/mmHg across animals. DMM GABA concentrations showed a strong positive correlation with BRS (r=0.71, P<0.001), while glutamate levels were inversely correlated with BRS (r=-0.58, P=0.003). The GABA/glutamate ratio demonstrated the strongest relationship with baroreceptor sensitivity (r=0.79, P<0.001). Multiple regression analysis revealed that DMM neurotransmitter balance accounted for 65% of the variance in spontaneous BRS. These findings provide the first direct neurochemical evidence linking GABAergic-glutamatergic balance in the DMM to baroreceptor reflex function in vivo, suggesting that altered neurotransmitter homeostasis in this region may contribute to impaired cardiovascular regulation in hypertensive states.

Title: The future of pharmacotherapy for paediatric anxiety.
Abstract: There is a growing body of data supporting the use of pharmacological agents in the treatment of paediatric anxiety. The most compelling data exists for the use of selective serotonin re-uptake inhibitors. However, questions the safe and efficacious use of medications with anxiolytic properties still remain. Although the field has progressed in the past several years, it is evident that more research needs to be done in the future.

Title: Measurement of kinetically resolved vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux using rotating disk electrode voltammetry.
Abstract: The vesicular monoamine transporter-2 (VMAT-2) sequesters cytoplasmic dopamine (DA) into vesicles for storage and subsequent release. VMAT-2 activity has traditionally been measured in small synaptic vesicles isolated from rat striatum by monitoring [3H] DA uptake and in cellular expression systems using fast scan cyclic voltammetry. This is the first report using rotating disk electrode (RDE) voltammetry to measure VMAT-2 DA uptake and efflux in small synaptic vesicles. DA uptake profiles followed mixed order kinetics with apparent zero order kinetics for the first 25 s and apparent first order kinetics thereafter. Vesicular DA uptake was temperature- and ATP-dependent and was blocked by the VMAT-2 inhibitor tetrabenazine. Initial velocities of DA uptake were kinetically resolved and displayed Michaelis-Menten kinetics with a Km and Vmax of 289 +/- 59 nM and 1.9 +/- 0.2 fmol/(s microg protein), respectively. Methamphetamine-induced DA efflux was blocked by tetrabenazine and kinetically resolved with an initial velocity of 0.54 +/- 0.08 fmol/(s microg protein). These results suggest that RDE voltammetry can be used to make kinetically resolved measurements of vesicular DA uptake and efflux and will allow the design of experiments that could reveal important information about the kinetics of VMAT-2 activity and its inhibition.

---

Title: Addiction: decreased reward sensitivity and increased expectation sensitivity conspire to overwhelm the brain's control circuit.
Abstract: Based on brain imaging findings, we present a model according to which addiction emerges as an imbalance in the information processing and integration among various brain circuits and functions. The dysfunctions reflect (a) decreased sensitivity of reward circuits, (b) enhanced sensitivity of memory circuits to conditioned expectations to drugs and drug cues, stress reactivity, and (c) negative mood, and a weakened control circuit. Although initial experimentation with a drug of abuse is largely a voluntary behavior, continued drug use can eventually impair neuronal circuits in the brain that are involved in free will, turning drug use into an automatic compulsive behavior. The ability of addictive drugs to co-opt neurotransmitter signals between neurons (including dopamine, glutamate, and GABA) modifies the function of different neuronal circuits, which begin to falter at different stages of an addiction trajectory. Upon exposure to the drug, drug cues or stress this results in unrestrained hyperactivation of the motivation/drive circuit that results in the compulsive drug intake that characterizes addiction.

---

Title: Overview: the neurochemistry of respiratory control.
Abstract: This special issue of Respiratory Physiology and Neurobiology surveys a broad range of topics focused on the neurochemical control of breathing. A variety of approaches have integrated the neurochemistry of breathing with the physiology of individual neurons, with the neuroanatomy of brainstem and forebrain respiratory circuits, and with the clinical pathology of respiratory disorders all of which has been fueled by the ongoing explosion of information in the molecular biology of the nervous system. Accordingly, substantial progress has identified neurotransmitters, neuromodulators, receptors, signaling cascades, trophic factors, hormones, and genes mediating normal and pathological breathing. Dynamic changes in the neurochemistry of breathing are addressed with respect to brainstem development, environmental challenges such as intermittent or chronic hypoxia, and as a function of the sleep-wake cycle. Respiratory disruption has also been identified in an increasing variety of genetic-based disorders and remarkable progress has been made in determining the affected genes and their mutations that negatively impact respiration.

---

Title: Schizophrenia: a review of neuropharmacology.
Abstract: Schizophrenia represents one of the most challenging psychiatric disorders, affecting approximately 1% of the global population and imposing substantial burdens on healthcare systems. Despite decades of research, the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain incompletely understood, limiting therapeutic advances. This comprehensive review examines current knowledge of neurotransmitter systems implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology and evaluates existing pharmacological interventions. A systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 1990-2003 was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases, focusing on dopaminergic, glutamatergic, GABAergic, and serotonergic mechanisms in schizophrenia. Additional emphasis was placed on emerging theories regarding N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction and its relationship to dopaminergic dysfunction. The traditional dopamine hypothesis, while foundational, appears insufficient to explain the full spectrum of schizophrenia symptoms, particularly negative symptoms and cognitive deficits. Evidence increasingly supports glutamatergic hypofunction as a primary mechanism, potentially upstream of dopaminergic abnormalities. NMDA receptor antagonist models demonstrate remarkable similarity to the full symptom profile of schizophrenia, including positive symptoms, negative symptoms, and cognitive impairments. Current antipsychotic medications, predominantly dopamine D2 receptor antagonists, show limited efficacy against negative symptoms and cognitive dysfunction. Atypical antipsychotics demonstrate modest improvements through multi-receptor targeting, but significant unmet therapeutic needs persist. Future neuropharmacological strategies should focus on glutamatergic enhancement, particularly NMDA receptor co-agonists and metabotropic glutamate receptor modulators. Novel approaches targeting GABAergic interneuron dysfunction and nicotinic acetylcholine receptors also warrant investigation. Understanding these complex neurotransmitter interactions may facilitate development of more effective therapeutic interventions.

---

Title: Application of triple-probe microdialysis for fast pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic evaluation of dopamimetic activity of drug candidates in the rat brain.
Abstract: Conventional microdialysis techniques for evaluating dopamimetic drug candidates require multiple experiments and extensive time commitments, limiting throughput in preclinical screening. We developed a novel triple-probe microdialysis methodology enabling simultaneous pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic assessment of dopamine-enhancing compounds in awake, freely-moving rats. Three microdialysis probes were stereotaxically implanted: one in the nucleus accumbens shell (pharmacodynamic readout), one in the caudate-putamen (regional comparison), and one in the jugular vein (pharmacokinetic sampling). Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of L-DOPA (100 mg/kg), amphetamine (2 mg/kg), or vehicle. Dialysate samples were collected every 15 minutes for 4 hours and analyzed using HPLC with electrochemical detection. Triple-probe microdialysis successfully demonstrated distinct pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic profiles for both compounds. L-DOPA showed delayed dopamine elevation (peak at 90 min) with preferential accumulation in nucleus accumbens versus caudate-putamen (2.3-fold difference), while amphetamine produced rapid, sustained increases in both regions. Plasma pharmacokinetics revealed L-DOPA's shorter half-life (32 ± 4 min) compared to amphetamine (78 ± 12 min). The triple-probe approach reduced experimental time by 60% compared to sequential single-probe studies while maintaining statistical power. This methodology provides a robust platform for rapid screening of dopamimetic drug candidates, offering simultaneous assessment of brain penetration, regional selectivity, and neurochemical efficacy within individual animals, thereby reducing animal usage while accelerating preclinical drug development timelines.

Title: The neurobiology of self-generated thought from cells to systems: Integrating evidence from lesion studies, human intracranial electrophysiology, neurochemistry, and neuroendocrinology.
Abstract: Investigation of the neural basis of self-generated thought is moving beyond a simple identification with default network activation toward a more comprehensive view recognizing the role of the frontoparietal control network and other areas. A major task ahead is to unravel the functional roles and temporal dynamics of the widely distributed brain regions recruited during self-generated thought. We argue that various other neuroscientific methods - including lesion studies, human intracranial electrophysiology, and manipulation of neurochemistry - have much to contribute to this project. These diverse data have yet to be synthesized with the growing understanding of self-generated thought gained from neuroimaging, however. Here, we highlight several areas of ongoing inquiry and illustrate how evidence from other methodologies corroborates, complements, and clarifies findings from functional neuroimaging. Each methodology has particular strengths: functional neuroimaging reveals much about the variety of brain areas and networks reliably recruited. Lesion studies point to regions critical to generating and consciously experiencing self-generated thought. Human intracranial electrophysiology illuminates how and where in the brain thought is generated and where this activity subsequently spreads. Finally, measurement and manipulation of neurotransmitter and hormone levels can clarify what kind of neurochemical milieu drives or facilitates self-generated cognition. Integrating evidence from multiple complementary modalities will be a critical step on the way to improving our understanding of the neurobiology of functional and dysfunctional forms of self-generated thought.

---

Title: An integrative dynamic model of brain energy metabolism using in vivo neurochemical measurements.
Abstract: Brain energy metabolism is critical for normal neuronal function, yet the dynamic relationships between glucose utilization, oxygen consumption, and neurotransmitter cycling remain poorly understood in vivo. We developed an integrative computational model that combines real-time neurochemical measurements with metabolic flux analysis to characterize brain energy dynamics during physiological states. Using microdialysis probes in awake, freely-moving rats (n=12), we simultaneously measured extracellular glucose, lactate, glutamate, and GABA concentrations in the hippocampus during rest, mild stress, and recovery periods. Oxygen tension was monitored using polarographic electrodes, while local field potentials were recorded to assess neural activity. These experimental data were incorporated into a multi-compartment mathematical model describing neuronal and glial metabolic interactions, including the astrocyte-neuron lactate shuttle and glutamate-glutamine cycle. The model successfully reproduced observed metabolic patterns, revealing that astrocytic glucose uptake increased 340% during neural activation while neuronal glucose utilization rose only 180%. Lactate production by astrocytes peaked within 2 minutes of stimulus onset, preceding maximal neuronal lactate consumption by 45 seconds. The model predicted that glutamate clearance accounted for 60-70% of total energy expenditure during high activity states. Sensitivity analysis identified astrocytic glucose transport and glutamate uptake kinetics as critical parameters governing metabolic responses. This integrative approach provides quantitative insights into brain bioenergetics and establishes a framework for investigating metabolic dysfunction in neurological disorders. The model's predictive capability may prove valuable for optimizing neuroprotective strategies and understanding energy-related pathophysiology.

---

Title: New approaches to chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting: from neuropharmacology to clinical investigations.
Abstract: Chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) remains a significant dose-limiting toxicity affecting patient quality of life and treatment compliance. While 5-HT3 receptor antagonists have improved antiemetic therapy, breakthrough emesis continues to affect 30-50% of patients receiving highly emetogenic chemotherapy. This review examines emerging neuropharmacological targets and translational approaches for CINV management. We analyzed preclinical data from animal models of cisplatin and cyclophosphamide-induced emesis, focusing on novel receptor systems including neurokinin-1 (NK-1), cannabinoid CB1/CB2, and GABA-B receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone and nucleus tractus solitarius. Clinical trial data from 2000-2006 were systematically reviewed, emphasizing phase II and III studies of combination antiemetic regimens. Preclinical studies demonstrate that NK-1 receptor antagonists significantly reduce both acute and delayed emesis when combined with 5-HT3 antagonists, with aprepitant showing 65-80% complete response rates in delayed CINV. Cannabinoid receptor modulators exhibit promise in refractory cases, while GABA-B agonists show efficacy in anticipatory nausea models. Clinical investigations reveal that triple combination therapy (5-HT3 antagonist, NK-1 antagonist, and dexamethasone) achieves complete protection rates exceeding 70% for highly emetogenic regimens. Patient stratification based on genetic polymorphisms in CYP2D6 and 5-HT3 receptor subunits may optimize individual responses. These findings support a multi-target approach to CINV, with NK-1 receptor antagonists representing the most significant advance since 5-HT3 antagonists. Future directions include personalized antiemetic therapy and investigation of central sensitization mechanisms in treatment-refractory patients.

Title: Genomics and proteomics: the next generation of health care.
Abstract: Biotechnology research will dramatically impact health care, and the pharmaceutical industry in particular, in the coming decade. Health outcomes may be improved in a cost-effective manner through the ability to selectively prescribe medications; safer, more effective treatment may reduce long-term health care costs. As genetic testing becomes the standard of care, new challenges will surface around how genetic information will be used or misused, and how and where health care dollars are spent.

Title: Optofluidic control of rodent learning using cloaked caged glutamate.
Abstract: The precise spatiotemporal control of neurotransmitter release remains a fundamental challenge in understanding neural circuit function and behavior. While optogenetics has revolutionized neuroscience research, existing approaches for controlling endogenous neurotransmitter signaling lack the temporal precision and spatial specificity required for dissecting complex learning behaviors. Here, we developed a novel optofluidic platform utilizing photocaged glutamate conjugated with near-infrared fluorescent cloaking molecules to achieve millisecond-precise, localized glutamate release in behaving rodents. The cloaking strategy prevents premature photolysis while enabling deep tissue penetration of 980-nm light for targeted uncaging. We validated this approach in adult C57BL/6 mice (n=48) performing a spatial memory task, delivering caged glutamate via chronically implanted microfluidic devices targeting the hippocampal CA1 region. Optical stimulation through implanted fiber optics triggered glutamate release with 2.3 ± 0.4 ms temporal precision and <50 μm spatial resolution. Mice receiving precisely timed glutamate uncaging during memory encoding showed significantly enhanced performance in the Morris water maze (escape latency: 18.2 ± 3.1 s vs 31.7 ± 4.8 s, p<0.001) compared to controls. Electrophysiological recordings confirmed selective enhancement of CA1 pyramidal cell activity and theta-gamma coupling during stimulation periods. Post-mortem immunohistochemistry revealed minimal tissue damage and preserved synaptic architecture. These results demonstrate that optofluidic control of caged neurotransmitters enables unprecedented precision in manipulating neural activity during complex behaviors, providing a powerful tool for dissecting the neurochemical basis of learning and memory with potential therapeutic applications for cognitive disorders.

Title: Transgenic animal models for neuropharmacology.
Abstract: The establishment of novel animal models using gene targeting and transgenic technology has opened a new area of neuropharmacological research. For the first time, it became possible to alter the expression of a gene in a specific cell type of an intact animal by either overexpression, inhibition or ablation. This review describes the technology and lists the relevant tools, such as reporter genes, suicide genes, immortalizing genes, and promoters, necessary for the targeted expression of these and other genes in specific cells of the central nervous system. In addition, the problem is discussed that the mouse is the species in which this technology is by far the most developed, while the rat has been used as the model species for neuropharmacology during the last century.

Title: Neuropharmacological profile of bifeprunox: merits and limitations in comparison with other third-generation antipsychotics.
Abstract: Schizophrenia is characterized by a range of positive and negative symptoms, and cognitive deficits. While positive symptoms respond to current antipsychotic agents, negative symptoms and cognitive deficits are often resistant to pharmacopea. Thus research is now focused on developing third-generation antipsychotics that combine antagonism or partial agonism at dopamine D(2)-like receptors with agonism at serotonin 5-HT(1A) receptors. Such an association is anticipated to provide therapeutic benefits against a broader range of schizophrenia symptoms. Bifeprunox is one such third-generation antipsychotic agent which acts as a partial agonist at D(2)-like receptors and is an efficacious agonist at 5-HT(1A) receptors, with little interaction at 5HT(2A/2C), muscarinic or histaminergic H(1) receptors. This review summarizes the pharmacological profiles of the current antipsychotic agents and describes the rationale behind the development of third-generation antipsychotics. It also evaluates current data concerning bifeprunox in comparison with currently available antipsychotics, as well as those that are still under clinical development.

Title: Seventeenth- and eighteenth-century commentators on the chemical composition of the brain.
Abstract: The period between 1600 and 1800 was one of great change in the history of science, generally, and in the history of chemistry, specifically. It opened with Francis Bacon's visionary recognition of the benefits to mankind that would accrue from the expansion of scientia and closed with the overthrow of the phlogiston hypothesis. New chemical knowledge resulted from the efforts of the alchemists, especially in Paracelsians, and of the phlogistic philosophers, some of it recorded by writers of magic books (Thorndike, 1958; Camporesi, 1989). The authors of these works reflected 'the general mentality ... imbued with magic, occult beliefs, unreal suggestions, 'voices', and 'rumours', ... 'errors' and 'prejudices'. In respect to brain chemistry there appeared, beside the fantastic, elements of fact that characterise this period as embracing the 'pre-history' of neurochemistry.

Title: Diet-induced obesity causes hypothalamic neurochemistry alterations in Swiss mice.
Abstract: The aim of this study was to assess inflammatory parameters, oxidative stress and energy metabolism in the hypothalamus of diet-induced obese mice. Male Swiss mice were divided into two study groups: control group and obese group. The animals in the control group were fed a diet with adequate amounts of macronutrients (normal-lipid diet), whereas the animals in the obese group were fed a high-fat diet to induce obesity. Obesity induction lasted 10 weeks, at the end of this period the disease model was validated in animals. The animals in the obese group had higher calorie consumption, higher body weight and higher weight of mesenteric fat compared to control group. Obesity showed an increase in levels of interleukin 1β and decreased levels of interleukin 10 in the hypothalamus. Furthermore, increased lipid peroxidation and protein carbonylation, and decreased level of glutathione in the hypothalamus of obese animals. However, there was no statistically significant difference in the activity of antioxidant enzymes, superoxide dismutase and catalase. The obese group had lower activity of complex I, II and IV of the mitochondrial respiratory chain, as well as lower activity of creatine kinase in the hypothalamus as compared to the control group. Thus, the results from this study showed changes in inflammatory markers, and dysregulation of metabolic enzymes in the pathophysiology of obesity.

Title: Neuropharmacology of N,N-dimethyltryptamine.
Abstract: N,N-dimethyltryptamine (DMT) is an indole alkaloid widely found in plants and animals. It is best known for producing brief and intense psychedelic effects when ingested. Increasing evidence suggests that endogenous DMT plays important roles for a number of processes in the periphery and central nervous system, and may act as a neurotransmitter. This paper reviews the current literature of both the recreational use of DMT and its potential roles as an endogenous neurotransmitter. Pharmacokinetics, mechanisms of action in the periphery and central nervous system, clinical uses and adverse effects are also reviewed. DMT appears to have limited neurotoxicity and other adverse effects except for intense cardiovascular effects when administered intravenously in large doses. Because of its role in nervous system signaling, DMT may be a useful experimental tool in exploring how the brain works, and may also be a useful clinical tool for treatment of anxiety and psychosis.

Title: Can voltammetry measure nitrogen monoxide (NO) and/or nitrites?
Abstract: Recently, voltammetry with carbon fibre electrodes (CFE) has been implemented for real time measurement of nitrogen monoxide (NO) indicating that it is oxidised at the potential value of nitrites, approximately +700 mV. In contrast, here we show that modified CFE can monitor NO at oxidation potentials different than that of nitrites, i.e. +550 mV. Indeed, at +550 mV a significant increase of amperometric current levels was obtained when NO but not nitrites, were added to a phosphate buffer saline solution (PBS). Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) supports these findings as two oxidation peaks were obtained when examining air preserved NO; peak 1 at +550 mV and peak 2 at +700 mV, respectively. In contrast, only peak 2 was monitored when nitrites or a solution of NO oxidised in air was added to PBS. Biological support to these in vitro data comes from the observation that the relaxation of an adrenaline-contracted aortic ring produced via addition of NO is concomitant with peak 1 at +550 mV. The relaxation is almost completed before the appearance of peak 2 at +700 mV. Furthermore, in vivo experiments performed in the striatum of rats show that the amperometric signal monitored at +550 mV is responsive to glutamatergic stimulation or inhibition of NO synthase.

Title: A novel method for monitoring the cell surface expression of heteromeric protein complexes in dispersed neurons and acute hippocampal slices.
Abstract: The study of heteromeric protein complexes at neuronal cell surfaces has been limited by technical challenges in monitoring their native expression and localization in physiologically relevant preparations. Traditional approaches using overexpression systems or isolated membrane preparations often fail to recapitulate the endogenous stoichiometry and trafficking of multi-subunit complexes. Here we describe a novel dual-labeling approach combining biotinylation-based surface labeling with proximity ligation assays to specifically detect heteromeric complexes at the plasma membrane of live neurons. Primary hippocampal neurons (DIV 14-21) and acute hippocampal slices (300 μm) from P14-P21 rats were surface-biotinylated using membrane-impermeant NHS-SS-biotin at 4°C, followed by fixation and proximity ligation using antibodies against different subunits of target complexes. We validated this method using NMDA receptors containing GluN1/GluN2A subunits and GABAA receptors containing α1/β2/γ2 subunits. Surface biotinylation efficiency exceeded 85% as confirmed by streptavidin-fluorophore labeling, while proximity ligation signals showed >95% colocalization with surface markers. The method successfully detected developmental changes in NMDA receptor subunit composition and revealed distinct subcellular distributions of GABAA receptor subtypes in CA1 pyramidal neurons. Background signal in non-biotinylated controls remained below 5% of experimental values. This approach provides a powerful tool for investigating the cell surface expression and localization of endogenous heteromeric protein complexes in native neuronal preparations, enabling more physiologically relevant studies of synaptic protein trafficking and organization.

Title: Microelectrode array studies of basal and potassium-evoked release of L-glutamate in the anesthetized rat brain.
Abstract: L-glutamate (Glu) is the predominant excitatory neurotransmitter in the mammalian central nervous system. It plays major roles in normal neurophysiology and many brain disorders by binding to membrane-bound Glu receptors. To overcome the spatial and temporal limitations encountered in previous in vivo extracellular Glu studies, we employed enzyme-coated microelectrode arrays to measure both basal and potassium-evoked release of Glu in the anesthetized rat brain. We also addressed the question of signal identity, which is the predominant criticism of these recording technologies. In vivo self-referencing recordings demonstrated that our Glu signals were both enzyme- and voltage-dependent, supporting the identity of L-glutamate. In addition, basal Glu was actively regulated, tetrodotoxin (TTX)-dependent, and measured in the low micromolar range (approximately 2 microm) using multiple self-referencing subtraction approaches for identification of Glu. Moreover, potassium-evoked Glu release exhibited fast kinetics that were concentration-dependent and reproducible. These data support the hypothesis that Glu release is highly regulated, requiring detection technologies that must be very close to the synapse and measure on a second-by-second basis to best characterize the dynamics of the Glu system.

Title: 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline antagonizes a rise in brain dopamine metabolism, glutamate release in frontal cortex and locomotor hyperactivity produced by MK-801 but not the disruptions of prepulse inhibition, and impairment of working memory in rat.
Abstract: N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with NMDA receptor antagonists like MK-801 producing behavioral and neurochemical alterations resembling aspects of this disorder. 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (1MeTIQ), an endogenous compound with neuroprotective properties, has shown potential modulatory effects on dopaminergic neurotransmission. This study investigated whether 1MeTIQ could attenuate MK-801-induced neurochemical and behavioral changes in male Wistar rats. Animals received pretreatment with 1MeTIQ (25 or 50 mg/kg, i.p.) 30 minutes before MK-801 administration (0.3 mg/kg, i.p.). Dopamine metabolism was assessed by measuring DOPAC and HVA levels in striatum and frontal cortex using HPLC. Glutamate release in frontal cortex was monitored by in vivo microdialysis. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity, prepulse inhibition (PPI) of acoustic startle, and spatial working memory using the Y-maze test. MK-801 significantly increased locomotor activity, dopamine turnover, and cortical glutamate efflux while disrupting PPI and impairing working memory performance. Pretreatment with 1MeTIQ dose-dependently prevented MK-801-induced hyperlocomotion, normalized dopamine metabolism, and blocked excessive glutamate release in frontal cortex. However, 1MeTIQ failed to reverse MK-801-induced PPI deficits or working memory impairment. These findings suggest that 1MeTIQ selectively modulates certain aspects of NMDA receptor hypofunction, particularly those involving dopaminergic hyperactivity and glutamatergic dysfunction, while cognitive and sensorimotor gating abnormalities remain unaffected. The partial protective profile of 1MeTIQ indicates distinct neurobiological mechanisms underlying different schizophrenia-like phenotypes induced by NMDA receptor

---

Title: Executive (dys)function after traumatic brain injury: special considerations for behavioral pharmacology.
Abstract: Executive function is an umbrella term that includes cognitive processes such as decision-making, impulse control, attention, behavioral flexibility, and working memory. Each of these processes depends largely upon monoaminergic (dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic) neurotransmission in the frontal cortex, striatum, and hippocampus, among other brain areas. Traumatic brain injury (TBI) induces disruptions in monoaminergic signaling along several steps in the neurotransmission process - synthesis, distribution, and breakdown - and in turn, produces long-lasting deficits in several executive function domains. Understanding how TBI alters monoamingeric neurotransmission and executive function will advance basic knowledge of the underlying principles that govern executive function and potentially further treatment of cognitive deficits following such injury. In this review, we examine the influence of TBI on the following measures of executive function - impulsivity, behavioral flexibility, and working memory. We also describe monoaminergic-systems changes following TBI. Given that TBI patients experience alterations in monoaminergic signaling following injury, they may represent a unique population with regard to pharmacotherapy. We conclude this review by discussing some considerations for pharmacotherapy in the field of TBI.

Title: Behavioral and Neurochemical Effects of Extra Virgin Olive Oil Total Phenolic Content and Sideritis Extract in Female Mice.
Abstract: The aim of this study was to determine the cognitive and behavioral effects of extra virgin olive oil total phenolic content (TPC) and Sideritis (SID) extracts in female mice, and identify the associated neurochemical changes in the hippocampus and the prefrontal cortex. All animals received intraperitoneal low or high doses of TPC, SID or vehicle treatment for 7 days and were subjected to the Open Field (OF), Novel Object Recognition (NOR) and Tail Suspension Test (TST). The prefrontal cortex and hippocampus were dissected for analysis of neurotransmitters and aminoacids with high performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ED). Both TPC doses enhanced vertical activity and center entries in the OF, which could indicate an anxiolytic-like effect. In addition, TPC enhanced non-spatial working memory and, in high doses, exerted antidepressant effects. On the other hand, high SID doses remarkably decreased the animals' overall activity. Locomotor and exploratory activities were closely associated with cortical increases in serotonin turnover induced by both treatments. Cognitive performance was linked to glutamate level changes. Furthermore, TPC reduced cortical taurine levels, while SID reduced cortical aspartate levels. TPC seems to have promising cognitive, anxiolytic and antidepressant effects, whereas SID has sedative effects in high doses. Both extracts act in the brain, but their specific actions and properties merit further exploration.

Title: ApoE4 disrupts sterol and sphingolipid metabolism in Alzheimer's but not normal brain.
Abstract: The epsilon 4 allele of ApoE is associated with an earlier onset and faster progression of Alzheimer's disease in patients with the familial form of this neurodegenerative condition. Although ApoE4 has been repeatedly associated with altered sphingomyelin and cholesterol levels in tissue culture and rodent models, there has not been a direct quantification of sphingomyelin or sterol levels in the brains of patients with different forms of ApoE. We measured the sphingolipid and sterol content of human brain tissues and found no evidence of perturbed sterol or sphingolipid biochemistry in the brains of individuals expressing ApoE4 who did not have a preexisting neurodegenerative condition. Nevertheless, ApoE4 was associated with gross abnormalities in the sterol and sphingolipid content of numerous brain regions in patients with Alzheimer's disease. The findings suggest that ApoE4 may not by itself alter sterol or sphingolipid metabolism in the brain under normal conditions, but that other neuropathologic changes of Alzheimer's are required to unmask the effect of ApoE4, and to perturb sterol and sphingolipid biochemistry.

---

Title: Adventures in neural plasticity, aging, and neurodegenerative disorders aboard the CWC beagle.
Abstract: This article recounts some of the scientific endeavors of Carl W. Cotman (CWC) during his journeys through the cellular circuitry of the mammalian brain. I have selected for consideration his findings that have been an important impetus for my own research; in several cases our different experiments have provided complementary data to support an hypothesis. Three examples are (i) Carl's studies of the roles of glutamate in synaptic transmission and plasticity in the adult brain and my studies of how glutamate regulates neurite outgrowth and cell survival in brain development; (ii) his and our studies of the mechanisms whereby amyloid beta-peptide damages and kills neurons; and (iii) Carl's evidence that physical activity regulates neurotrophin levels in the brain and our evidence that dietary restriction has similar effects and is neuroprotective. In case you have not yet realized how I chose a title for this article it is because Carl has a (very distant) connection with Charles Darwin-Darwin sailed on a vessel called the Beagle and Carl has studied beagle dogs, establishing them as a model for understanding the neurobiology of human brain aging.

---

Title: Pharmacotherapy in children and adolescents with pervasive developmental disorders.
Abstract: Children with autism and the related PDDs may benefit from serotonin reuptake inhibitors such as clomipramine, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, and sertraline for targeting repetitive thoughts and behaviors, anxiety, and depressed mood. To date, however, there are few controlled studies of these agents in children with PDD, so definitive evidence is lacking. Despite preliminary results in favor of naltrexone, neuroleptic medication appears to be effective for reducing aggression, self-injurious behavior, agitation, and stereotypies. The primary drawback with traditional neuroleptics is risk of short- and long-term side effects. The newer atypical neuroleptics have the potential for benefit with fewer extrapyramidal side effects, but more study is needed to establish their efficacy and safety. Children on neuroleptic medications should be started at the lowest possible dose, with gradual increases until clinical benefit is observed. The likelihood of untoward side effects is increased if the medication dose is increased rapidly. Baseline measurement of target behaviors can be aided by using standardized scales. The presence of abnormal movements should be assessed before initiating treatment and at regular intervals during the course of treatment--including after medication withdrawal. Weight gain is emerging as a recurrent side effect with the atypical neuroleptics. Thus, weight should be monitored, and the family should be advised about a diet baseline. As with all treatments of children with severe behavioral difficulties, pharmacotherapy should be instituted in the context of an integrated treatment plan.

---

Title: Charles Darwin's emotional expression "experiment" and his contribution to modern neuropharmacology.
Abstract: Charles Darwin's investigations into emotional expression, particularly his systematic observations documented in "The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals" (1872), represent an overlooked foundation for contemporary neuropharmacological research. While Darwin's work predates modern neurochemistry by nearly a century, his methodological approach and theoretical insights established crucial conceptual frameworks that continue to influence current understanding of emotion-drug interactions. This historical analysis examines Darwin's correspondence, experimental notebooks, and published observations to evaluate his contributions to the field. We analyzed Darwin's documentation of emotional responses across species, his early recognition of the physiological basis of emotional expression, and his proto-experimental methodology involving photographic documentation and cross-cultural surveys. Darwin's work demonstrated remarkable prescience in identifying the involuntary nature of emotional expressions, the role of the autonomic nervous system in emotional states, and the evolutionary conservation of emotional mechanisms across species. His observations of facial muscle contractions, cardiovascular responses, and respiratory changes during emotional states anticipated key targets of modern anxiolytic and antidepressant medications. Furthermore, his recognition that emotional expressions could be modified through conscious effort foreshadowed current research into cognitive-behavioral interventions and their neurochemical correlates. Darwin's systematic approach to studying emotional expression established methodological principles that remain relevant to contemporary neuropharmacological research, particularly in the development of animal models for psychiatric disorders and the assessment of drug efficacy in emotional regulation.

---

Title: Application of triple-probe microdialysis for fast pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic evaluation of dopamimetic activity of drug candidates in the rat brain.
Abstract: The technique of microdialysis utilizing three simultaneously implanted probes in the anaesthetized rat enables monitoring of pharmacokinetic (PK) profiles of a tested drug both in blood (1st probe) and brain (2nd probe) compartments and the pharmacodynamic (PD) response of neurotransmitters (3rd probe) released into, or accumulating within the brain extracellular fluid (ECF). In the present study, the PK/PD characteristics of cocaine (psychostimulant, strong abuse potential) and methylphenidate (dopamimetic drug without reinforcing properties) and two novel NeuroSearch (NS) drug candidates, NS-A and NS-B, were examined in blood and brain microdialysates from the anaesthetized rats. The extracellular levels of dopamine (DA) were monitored in the striatum or prefrontal cortex. The NS-A compound entered the brain ECF at a slightly slower rate then methylphenidate; however, both compounds showed about the same effect on the speed of accumulation of extracellular DA concentrations, which gradually increased to about 450% of the basal, predrug levels at the end of the sampling period (180 min). The NS-B compound showed more rapid PK profiles than those observed after methylphenidate and NS-A. The concentrations of NS-B reached the maximal values already 40 min after its administration, while at that time, the corresponding DA values were still unchanged. In fact, the increase in DA concentrations was about two times slower when compared to that of methylphenidate or NS-A-drugs. Faster kinetics of NS-B and its delayed effect on extracellular DA suggests that this compound is metabolized to an active intermediate product, which itself exerts stronger dopamimetic activity in the rat prefrontal cortex that the original NS-B substance. The present study illustrates the feasibility of triple-probe microdialysis to monitor the rate of extracellular accumulation of a drug candidate and DA levels in vivo and compare the resulting PK/PD profiles to those obtained for cocaine and methylphenidate. These measures may serve as initial neurochemical indicators of potential psychomimetic or reinforcing properties of the tested substances.

Title: The medicinal chemistry and neuropharmacology of kratom: A preliminary discussion of a promising medicinal plant and analysis of its potential for abuse.
Abstract: The leaves of Mitragyna speciosa (commonly known as kratom), a tree endogenous to parts of Southeast Asia, have been used traditionally for their stimulant, mood-elevating, and analgesic effects and have recently attracted significant attention due to increased use in Western cultures as an alternative medicine. The plant's active alkaloid constituents, mitragynine and 7-hydroxymitragynine, have been shown to modulate opioid receptors, acting as partial agonists at mu-opioid receptors and competitive antagonists at kappa- and delta-opioid receptors. Furthermore, both alkaloids are G protein-biased agonists of the mu-opioid receptor and therefore, may induce less respiratory depression than classical opioid agonists. The Mitragyna alkaloids also appear to exert diverse activities at other brain receptors (including adrenergic, serotonergic, and dopaminergic receptors), which may explain the complex pharmacological profile of raw kratom extracts, although characterization of effects at these other targets remains extremely limited. Through allometric scaling, doses of pure mitragynine and 7-hydroxymitragynine used in animal studies can be related to single doses of raw kratom plant commonly consumed by humans, permitting preliminary interpretation of expected behavioral and physiological effects in man based on this preclinical data and comparison to both anecdotal human experience and multiple epidemiological surveys. Kratom exposure alone has not been causally associated with human fatalities to date. However, further research is needed to clarify the complex mechanism of action of the Mitragyna alkaloids and unlock their full therapeutic potential. This article is part of the Special Issue entitled 'Designer Drugs and Legal Highs.'

Title: Psychopharmacology in autism.
Abstract: Autism is a pervasive developmental disorder characterized by impaired social interaction, communication deficits, and restricted repetitive behaviors. While the etiology remains unclear, neurochemical abnormalities involving serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems have been implicated. This study examined the efficacy and tolerability of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) in reducing maladaptive behaviors in children and adolescents with autism spectrum disorders. Twenty-eight subjects aged 6-17 years meeting DSM-IV criteria for autistic disorder were enrolled in a 12-week, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of fluoxetine (mean dose 0.43 mg/kg/day). Primary outcome measures included the Aberrant Behavior Checklist (ABC) and Clinical Global Impression-Improvement (CGI-I) scale. Secondary measures assessed repetitive behaviors, social responsiveness, and adaptive functioning. Results demonstrated significant improvement in the fluoxetine group compared to placebo on ABC subscales for irritability (p=0.024), social withdrawal (p=0.031), and stereotypic behavior (p=0.047). Fifty-seven percent of fluoxetine-treated subjects were rated as much improved or very much improved on the CGI-I versus 14% of placebo recipients (p=0.018). Common adverse effects included decreased appetite (29%), sleep disturbance (21%), and behavioral activation (14%), but discontinuation rates were similar between groups. These findings suggest that low-dose fluoxetine may be beneficial for reducing core behavioral symptoms in autism, particularly repetitive behaviors and social withdrawal. Further research is needed to establish optimal dosing strategies and long-term safety profiles in this vulnerable population.

---

Title: Molecular and chemical neuropharmacology of dopamine receptor subtypes.
Abstract: In the fields of psychiatry and neurology, the dopaminergic system is one of the most important neurotransmitter systems in the brain. Whereas pharmacological and biochemical studies had initially indicated two subclasses of dopamine receptors (DA-R), recent progress in molecular biology techniques has led to the identification of five distinct genes of DA-Rs (D1-R-D5-R) and splice variants. The gene products are classified into the D1-R family (D1-R and D5-R) and D2-R family (D2-R, D3-R and D4-R) based on their structure and pharmacological features. This review summarizes the structure, localization, function and pharmacology of DA-R subtypes on the basis of knowledge obtained during the past few years. The genes encoding the D1-R family have no intron and the D2-R family genes have introns. The distributions of mRNAs encoding these five DA-R subtypes in the brain were different from their respective receptors. The localization of DA-R subtypes to particular brain regions and specific pharmacological profiles of DA-R subtypes allow new insights to be made into the mechanism of action of DA in the control of psychiatric and motor functions. The availability of detailed information about DA-R subtypes will not only clarify their roles in the brain, but will probably also lead to the development of new therapeutic drugs with more specific actions.

---

Title: Effects of triiodothyronine and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity in rat brain: regional differences.
Abstract: The thyroid hormone triiodothyronine (T3) augments and accelerates the effects of antidepressant drugs. Although the majority of studies showing this have used tricyclics, a few studies have shown similar effects with the selective serotonin re-uptake inhibitor (SSRI) fluoxetine. In this study we investigated the effects of fluoxetine (5 mg/kg), T3 (20 microg/kg) and the combination of these drugs, each administered daily for 7 days, on serotonergic function in the rat brain, using in vivo microdialysis. Fluoxetine alone induced a trend towards desensitization of 5-HT1A autoreceptors as shown by a reduction in the effect of 8-OH-DPAT to lower 5-HT levels in frontal cortex, and desensitized 5-HT1B autoreceptors in frontal cortex. The combination of fluoxetine and T3 induced desensitization of 5-HT1B autoreceptors in hypothalamus. Since there is evidence linking hypothalamic function and depression, we suggest that this effect may partly account for the therapeutic efficacy of the combination of an SSRI and T3.

Title: Solid-phase extraction enhances detection of beta-amyloid peptides in plasma and enables Abeta quantification following passive immunization with Abeta antibodies.
Abstract: We have previously developed a solid-phase extraction (SPE) procedure to enable the detection of beta-amyloid (Abeta) peptides in brain tissue from non-transgenic animals. We have now adapted these methods to enrich the Abeta fraction in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and plasma. Human CSF and plasma and Tg2576 mouse plasma were subjected to guanidine denaturation followed by SPE in 96-well cassettes. The resulting eluates could be concentrated significantly to enhance detection of low-abundance Abeta peptides by immunoassay. The concentrated eluates diluted in a linear fashion with consistent recovery between SPE columns. This technique was therefore used to facilitate quantification of Abeta1-X, 1-40, 1-42, and 1-38 peptides in normal human CSF and plasma samples. SPE sample preparation was also applied to the plasma of mice dosed peripherally with a monoclonal antibody raised against Abeta. When such samples were assayed directly, the presence of the systemically administered antibody interfered with the subsequent immunoassay, by preventing detection of antibody-bound Abeta. After subjecting plasma from antibody-treated animals to denaturation and SPE, the antibody-antigen complex was disrupted, and the Abeta fraction could be isolated from the antibody-containing fraction. Application of this method allowed for detection of a 100-fold increase in plasma Abeta1-40 following treatment of Tg2576 mice or wild type littermate control mice with Abeta40-specific monoclonal antibody 9TL. Given the availability of a variety of SPE matrices, we hypothesize that these methods could facilitate plasma antigen retrieval using multiple therapeutic antibody approaches.

Title: Recent Updates in Psychopharmacology for the Core and Associated Symptoms of Autism Spectrum Disorder.
Abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1 in 54 children and is characterized by persistent deficits in social communication and interaction alongside restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior. While no FDA-approved medications exist for core ASD symptoms, psychopharmacological interventions are frequently employed to manage associated symptoms including irritability, hyperactivity, anxiety, and sleep disturbances. This comprehensive review examines recent developments in ASD psychopharmacology from 2018-2021, analyzing emerging therapeutic targets and novel treatment approaches. We conducted a systematic literature review of randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series published in major psychiatric and neurodevelopmental journals. PubMed, Embase, and PsycINFO databases were searched using terms related to autism, psychopharmacology, and specific medication classes. Recent evidence supports continued use of risperidone and aripiprazole for severe irritability and aggression, with emerging data on lurasidone and cariprazine showing promise. Novel approaches targeting core social communication deficits include oxytocin analogs, with mixed results from recent trials. Cannabidiol has shown preliminary efficacy for irritability and hyperactivity in small controlled studies. Melatonin receptor agonists demonstrate consistent benefits for sleep disorders, while selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors remain controversial for repetitive behaviors due to activation concerns in pediatric populations. Glutamate modulators and gamma-aminobutyric acid enhancers represent emerging therapeutic frontiers. Current evidence emphasizes individualized treatment approaches targeting specific symptom domains rather than global ASD features. Future research should focus on biomarker-guided therapy selection and combination treatment strategies to optimize outcomes while minimizing adverse effects.

Title: Lactate dehydrogenase release is facilitated by brief sonication of rat hippocampal slices and isolated retinas following acute neuronal damage.
Abstract: Assessment of neuronal viability in acute brain slice preparations remains a critical methodological challenge in neuropharmacological research. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release has emerged as a reliable marker of cellular damage, but traditional measurement approaches may underestimate tissue injury due to incomplete enzyme release from damaged cells. We investigated whether brief sonication enhances LDH release detection following acute neuronal damage in rat hippocampal slices and isolated retinas. Hippocampal slices (400 μm) and retinal preparations were obtained from adult Sprague-Dawley rats and subjected to oxygen-glucose deprivation (OGD) for 30-120 minutes or excitotoxic injury using 100 μM NMDA. Following injury protocols, tissues were either processed immediately for LDH assay or subjected to 10-second sonication (40 kHz, 0.2 W/cm²) prior to enzyme measurement. Sonication significantly increased detectable LDH release by 2.3-fold in hippocampal slices and 1.8-fold in retinal preparations compared to non-sonicated controls after equivalent injury periods. The enhancement was most pronounced following mild to moderate injury conditions, where traditional methods detected minimal enzyme release. Dose-response relationships between injury duration and LDH release showed improved linearity following sonication treatment. Cell viability assessed by trypan blue exclusion confirmed that sonication parameters did not induce additional cellular damage in healthy tissue. These findings demonstrate that brief sonication represents a simple and effective method for enhancing LDH release detection sensitivity in neural tissue preparations, potentially improving the accuracy of neuronal viability assessments in neuropharmacological studies investigating neuroprotective compounds and injury mechanisms.

---

Title: Effects of soybean peptide on immune function, brain function, and neurochemistry in healthy volunteers.
Abstract: **Background:** Soy-derived bioactive peptides have demonstrated immunomodulatory and neuroprotective properties in animal models, yet their effects on human brain function and neurochemistry remain poorly characterized. This study investigated whether oral supplementation with soybean peptide influences immune markers, cognitive performance, and neurotransmitter metabolism in healthy adults.

**Methods:** In this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, 84 healthy volunteers (aged 25-55 years) received either soybean peptide (3g daily) or placebo for 8 weeks. Primary outcomes included serum cytokine levels (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, IL-10), lymphocyte proliferation responses, and cognitive assessments using standardized neuropsychological batteries. Secondary measures comprised plasma amino acid profiles, urinary monoamine metabolites (3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid, 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid), and brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) concentrations.

**Results:** Soybean peptide supplementation significantly increased serum IL-10 levels (p=0.032) and enhanced lymphocyte proliferation responses to mitogens (p=0.018) compared to placebo. Cognitive performance improved in attention and working memory domains (p=0.024 and p=0.041, respectively). Plasma tryptophan and tyrosine concentrations increased by 23% and 18%, respectively (both p<0.05), while urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid excretion rose by 34% (p=0.009). BDNF levels showed a modest but significant elevation (p=0.047) in the treatment group.

**Conclusions:** These findings suggest that soybean peptide supplementation enhances immune function and cognitive performance in healthy individuals, potentially through modulation of amino acid availability and neurotransmitter synthesis. Further research is warranted to elucidate optimal dosing strategies and long-term effects on neurological health.

Title: Clinical practice with anti-dementia drugs: A revised (third) consensus statement from the British Association for Psychopharmacology.
Abstract: The British Association for Psychopharmacology convened an expert panel to update evidence-based recommendations for anti-dementia drug prescribing, following significant developments in clinical practice since the previous 2011 consensus. A multidisciplinary group of clinicians and researchers systematically reviewed recent literature on cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) and memantine, alongside emerging evidence on combination therapies and long-term treatment strategies. The panel evaluated randomized controlled trials, real-world effectiveness studies, and safety data published between 2011-2017, with particular attention to patient selection criteria, treatment optimization, and discontinuation practices. Updated recommendations emphasize earlier initiation of cholinesterase inhibitors in mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease, with donepezil remaining first-line treatment due to superior tolerability and adherence. Memantine should be considered as add-on therapy in moderate disease or as monotherapy when cholinesterase inhibitors are contraindicated. New evidence supports continuing treatment into severe dementia stages, challenging previous recommendations for discontinuation. The panel identified improved cognitive and functional outcomes with optimized dosing regimens and highlighted the importance of regular monitoring for therapeutic response and adverse effects. Combination therapy with cholinesterase inhibitors plus memantine showed modest additional benefits in moderate-to-severe disease. Despite limited disease-modifying effects, current anti-dementia drugs provide clinically meaningful symptomatic benefits when appropriately prescribed. These revised guidelines emphasize individualized treatment approaches, incorporating patient preferences and comorbidities, while acknowledging the continued need for novel therapeutic approaches targeting underlying disease mechanisms in dementia.

---

Title: How neurochemistry regained its specific biological components: experimental and cognitive advance, 1935-1955.
Abstract: Neurochemistry in the 1850s was part of comparative animal chemistry, which became incorporated into physiological chemistry. By 1900, the connection with physiological chemistry had largely lapsed or been vehemently repudiated. Growth of biochemistry, especially from the 1920s to 1950s, provided techniques and findings sufficient to reintegrate chemical knowledge of neural systems with neural functioning. Vitamin, coenzyme, respiratory and other metabolic studies made large contributions to this outcome. Regarding mental illness as a social problem and scientific challenge gave impetus and funds to such work, which resulted in major experimental and cognitive progress.
