Title: Effects of the mGluR2/3 agonist LY379268 on ketamine-evoked behaviours and neurochemical changes in the dentate gyrus of the rat.
Abstract: Glutamate dysfunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with both hypoactivation of NMDA receptors and dysregulation of metabotropic glutamate receptors contributing to disease symptoms. The mGluR2/3 agonist LY379268 has shown promise in preclinical models as a potential antipsychotic agent. The present study examined whether LY379268 could attenuate ketamine-induced behavioral and neurochemical alterations in rats, using the dentate gyrus as a key region of interest due to its role in cognitive processing and NMDA receptor expression. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10/group) received intraperitoneal injections of vehicle, ketamine (30 mg/kg), LY379268 (3 mg/kg), or ketamine plus LY379268 pretreatment (30 min prior). Locomotor activity was monitored for 120 minutes post-injection, and dentate gyrus tissue was collected for neurochemical analysis using high-performance liquid chromatography. Ketamine administration significantly increased locomotor activity compared to controls (p<0.001) and elevated dopamine and glutamate concentrations in the dentate gyrus (147% and 132% of control, respectively). Pretreatment with LY379268 dose-dependently reduced ketamine-induced hyperlocomotion by 68% (p<0.01) and normalized dentate gyrus neurotransmitter levels. LY379268 alone had no significant effects on behavior or neurochemistry. These findings demonstrate that mGluR2/3 activation effectively counteracts NMDA receptor hypofunction-induced behavioral and neurochemical disturbances, supporting the therapeutic potential of mGluR2/3 agonists in treating glutamate-related psychiatric disorders. The dentate gyrus appears to be a critical site for these modulatory effects.

Title: Psychopharmacology of autistic spectrum disorders in children and adolescents.
Abstract: This article provides an overview of the psychopharmacologic management of irritability, hyperactivity, and repetitive behaviors in children and adolescents with autism spectrum disorder. A review of the current literature on medications used to treat these conditions with emphasis on randomized controlled trials is presented.

Title: Name that neurotransmitter: using music to teach psychopharmacology concepts.
Abstract: The purpose of this article is to discuss the use of music (i.e., two original songs, "Neurotransmitter Twitter" and "Parkinson's Shuffle") to teach aspects of psychopharmacology to students in the course Psychiatric/Mental Health Nursing. Songs were incorporated in both the clinical and classroom settings. This innovative teaching method allowed students the opportunity to revisit the information through multiple exposures of the content for reinforcement and enhancement of student learning in a fun, creative approach. Brain-based research will be discussed, along with the process of development.

Title: Cell-Specific Neuropharmacology.
Abstract: The advent of modern molecular tools has revealed unprecedented cellular heterogeneity within neural circuits, challenging traditional pharmacological approaches that broadly target receptor subtypes across diverse cell populations. While classical neuropharmacology has provided valuable therapeutic interventions, the lack of cell-type specificity often results in unwanted side effects and limits therapeutic precision. Here we review emerging strategies that enable cell-specific drug targeting within the nervous system, including chemogenetic approaches, optopharmacology, and targeted drug delivery systems. Designer receptors exclusively activated by designer drugs (DREADDs) have demonstrated remarkable success in selectively modulating genetically-defined neuronal populations in both preclinical models and early clinical applications. Optopharmacological tools, combining photochemistry with pharmacology, allow spatiotemporal control of drug action with cellular precision. Additionally, antibody-drug conjugates and nanoparticle-based delivery systems show promise for targeting specific neural cell types based on surface marker expression. Recent applications in treating neuropsychiatric disorders, chronic pain, and neurodegenerative diseases highlight the therapeutic potential of cell-specific approaches. For instance, selective targeting of medium spiny neurons in Huntington's disease models has shown improved efficacy compared to conventional treatments. However, challenges remain in translation to clinical settings, including delivery across the blood-brain barrier, potential immunogenicity, and long-term safety profiles. Cell-specific neuropharmacology represents a paradigm shift toward precision medicine in neurology and psychiatry, offering opportunities to enhance therapeutic efficacy while minimizing off-target effects. Continued technological advances and interdisciplinary collaboration will be essential for realizing the full potential of this rapidly evolving field.

---

Title: Distribution of catecholamine(s) in the nervous system of a mermithid nematode, Romanomermis culicivorax.
Abstract: The nervous system of the mermithid nematode Romanomermis culicivorax was investigated at different developmental stages for the distribution of catecholamine(s). The catecholaminergic nervous system increased in complexity during development. In the adult nematode, catecholamine(s) occurred in the nerve ring and associated ganglia, cephalic nerves, ventral and dorsal nerve cords and mid-body region ganglia. The distribution of catecholaminergic ganglia in the tail region was different for each sex. The tail region of males contained 16-20 catecholaminergic ganglia, whereas the tail of females contained only 2 ganglia. The catecholamine widely distributed throughout the nervous system of R. culicivorax is not dopamine, since immunoreactivity to dopamine was observed only in the amphids. The results suggest that the catecholamine(s) is widely distributed within the nervous system of R. culicivorax and might function as a neurohormone or neurotransmitter in controlling physiological and developmental processes.

---

Title: Psychopharmacology of autistic spectrum disorders in children and adolescents.
Abstract: Autism spectrum disorders (ASDs) affect approximately 1 in 110 children and are characterized by impaired social communication, restricted interests, and repetitive behaviors. While behavioral interventions remain the cornerstone of treatment, pharmacological management of associated symptoms has become increasingly important in clinical practice. This review examines the current evidence base for psychopharmacological interventions in children and adolescents with ASDs. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using PubMed, Cochrane Library, and PsycINFO databases from 1990 to 2011, focusing on randomized controlled trials and systematic reviews of pharmacological treatments in pediatric ASD populations. Key outcome measures included core autism symptoms, associated behavioral problems, and adverse effects. Evidence supports the use of atypical antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, for managing irritability, aggression, and self-injurious behaviors, with both agents receiving FDA approval for these indications in pediatric ASD patients. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show mixed efficacy for repetitive behaviors and anxiety symptoms, with fluoxetine demonstrating some benefit in adolescents. Stimulant medications can effectively treat comorbid attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms, though response rates may be lower than in typically developing children. Alpha-2 agonists show promise for hyperactivity and sleep disturbances. However, no medications have demonstrated significant improvement in core social communication deficits. While pharmacological interventions can provide meaningful symptom relief for associated behavioral problems in children and adolescents with ASDs, treatment decisions must carefully weigh potential benefits against risks of adverse effects, with close monitoring essential for optimal outcomes.

---

Title: DNA microarrays in neuropsychopharmacology.
Abstract: Recent advances in experimental genomics, coupled with the wealth of sequence information available for a variety of organisms, have the potential to transform the way pharmacological research is performed. At present, high-density DNA microarrays allow researchers to quickly and accurately quantify gene-expression changes in a massively parallel manner. Although now well established in other biomedical fields, such as cancer and genetics research, DNA microarrays have only recently begun to make significant inroads into pharmacology. To date, the major focus in this field has been on the general application of DNA microarrays to toxicology and drug discovery and design. This review summarizes the major microarray findings of relevance to neuropsychopharmacology, as a prelude to the design and analysis of future basic and clinical microarray experiments. The ability of DNA microarrays to monitor gene expression simultaneously in a large-scale format is helping to usher in a post-genomic age, where simple constructs about the role of nature versus nurture are being replaced by a functional understanding of gene expression in living organisms.

---

Title: Brain banks and research in neurochemistry.
Abstract: The increasing demand to brain banks for specimens that are properly prepared for research in neurochemistry has stimulated interest in the drawing up of suitable protocols. The purpose of this chapter is twofold: (1) to describe the various strategies that are currently applied in different brain banks and (2) to point out the main neurochemical requirements in order to reach a consensus with the brain bank about the more suitable protocol in each case. In recent years, some brain banks have acquired the necessary equipment and now have access to neurochemical expertise. Their experience will be very helpful in defining the new procedures to be used at each stage of brain tissue banking and will permit the development of research projects in this discipline.

Title: Voxel scale complex networks of functional connectivity in the rat brain: neurochemical state dependence of global and local topological properties.
Abstract: Network analysis of functional imaging data reveals emergent features of the brain as a function of its topological properties. However, the brain is not a homogeneous network, and the dependence of functional connectivity parameters on neuroanatomical substrate and parcellation scale is a key issue. Moreover, the extent to which these topological properties depend on underlying neurochemical changes remains unclear. In the present study, we investigated both global statistical properties and the local, voxel-scale distribution of connectivity parameters of the rat brain. Different neurotransmitter systems were stimulated by pharmacological challenge (d-amphetamine, fluoxetine, and nicotine) to discriminate between stimulus-specific functional connectivity and more general features of the rat brain architecture. Although global connectivity parameters were similar, mapping of local connectivity parameters at high spatial resolution revealed strong neuroanatomical dependence of functional connectivity in the rat brain, with clear differentiation between the neocortex and older brain regions. Localized foci of high functional connectivity independent of drug challenge were found in the sensorimotor cortices, consistent with the high neuronal connectivity in these regions. Conversely, the topological properties and node roles in subcortical regions varied with neurochemical state and were dependent on the specific dynamics of the different functional processes elicited.

---

Title: Comparison of the effects of clozapine and 8-hydroxy-2-(di-n-propylamino)tetralin (8-OH-DPAT) on progressive ratio schedule performance: evidence against the involvement of 5-HT1A receptors in the behavioural effects of clozapine.
Abstract: **Background:** Clozapine, an atypical antipsychotic with unique clinical efficacy, exhibits complex pharmacological properties including interactions with multiple neurotransmitter systems. While its therapeutic effects are primarily attributed to dopaminergic and serotonergic mechanisms, the specific contribution of 5-HT1A receptor activity to clozapine's behavioral profile remains unclear. Progressive ratio (PR) schedules, which measure motivation and reward sensitivity, provide a valuable tool for examining the behavioral effects of psychoactive compounds.

**Methods:** Male Sprague-Dawley rats were trained on a PR schedule for food reinforcement until stable baseline performance was achieved. Separate groups received acute injections of clozapine (1.25, 2.5, 5.0 mg/kg, i.p.), the selective 5-HT1A receptor agonist 8-OH-DPAT (0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2 mg/kg, s.c.), or vehicle. Breakpoint (highest ratio completed) and response rate were measured as primary outcome variables.

**Results:** Clozapine dose-dependently decreased breakpoint and response rate, with significant effects observed at 2.5 and 5.0 mg/kg. In contrast, 8-OH-DPAT produced a biphasic response pattern: low doses (0.025-0.05 mg/kg) slightly increased breakpoint, while higher doses (0.1-0.2 mg/kg) markedly decreased performance. The temporal pattern and magnitude of behavioral disruption differed substantially between compounds. Pretreatment with the 5-HT1A antagonist WAY-100635 blocked 8-OH-DPAT effects but did not alter clozapine's actions.

**Conclusions:** These findings demonstrate distinct behavioral profiles for clozapine and 8-OH-DPAT on PR performance, suggesting that 5-HT1A receptor activation does not significantly contribute to clozapine's motivational effects. The results challenge previous hypotheses regarding 5-HT1A involvement in atypical antipsychotic action and support alternative mechanisms underlying clozapine's unique pharmacological properties.

---

Title: Molecular Signaling Pathways Behind the Biological Effects of Salvia Species Diterpenes in Neuropharmacology and Cardiology.
Abstract: Salvia species contain bioactive diterpenes that have demonstrated significant therapeutic potential in neurological and cardiovascular disorders, yet their underlying molecular mechanisms remain poorly characterized. This comprehensive review examines the signaling pathways mediating the biological effects of major Salvia diterpenes, including tanshinones, salvianolic acids, and carnosic acid derivatives. We conducted a systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 2000-2015, focusing on mechanistic studies involving primary neuronal cultures, cardiac myocytes, and relevant animal models. Database searches identified 147 relevant studies examining molecular targets and signaling cascades. In neurological applications, Salvia diterpenes primarily modulate neuroprotective pathways through inhibition of NF-κB signaling, activation of Nrf2-mediated antioxidant responses, and regulation of calcium homeostasis via L-type calcium channel blockade. Tanshinone IIA demonstrated particular efficacy in attenuating neuroinflammation through suppression of microglial activation and reduction of pro-inflammatory cytokine release. In cardiovascular systems, these compounds exhibit cardioprotective effects predominantly through PI3K/Akt pathway activation, mitochondrial membrane stabilization, and modulation of apoptotic cascades involving Bcl-2 family proteins. Salvianolic acid B showed remarkable ability to preserve cardiac function during ischemia-reperfusion injury via enhancement of endothelial nitric oxide synthase activity. Cross-system analysis revealed convergent mechanisms involving oxidative stress reduction and inflammatory pathway suppression. These findings provide molecular rationale for traditional uses of Salvia species and identify specific targets for therapeutic development. Further clinical investigation is warranted to translate these mechanistic insights into evidence-based phytotherapeutic interventions for neurodegenerative and cardiovascular diseases.

---

Title: Pharmacogenetics in neuropsychiatric diseases: epilepsy as a model.
Abstract: Individual drug responses are the result of interactions of multiple environmental and genetic factors. Pharmacogenetics studies the influence of genetic variation on interindividual differences in drug efficacy, adverse events and dosing. This article discusses general strategies for candidate gene selection and pharmacogenetic association studies. A summary of the major pharmacogenetic associations reported in neuropsychiatric disease is presented. The example of epilepsy pharmacogenetics will be covered in more detail, including an overview of epilepsy pharmacogenetic candidate genes and results of association studies reported so far. With the advent of large-scale, rigorously designed association studies, it is hoped that genetic factors will be identified that will lead to a targeted, more efficacious and safer treatment, and perhaps to the development of new and more efficacious drugs for neuropsychiatric diseases.

---

Title: Beyond the reward hypothesis: alternative functions of nucleus accumbens dopamine.
Abstract: According to the dopamine (DA) hypothesis of reward, DA systems in the brain, particularly in the nucleus accumbens, are thought to directly mediate the rewarding or primary motivational characteristics of natural stimuli such as food, water and sex, as well as various drugs of abuse. However, there are numerous problems associated with this hypothesis. Interference with accumbens DA transmission does not substantially blunt primary motivation for natural rewards such as food, but it does disrupt the propensity of animals to engage in effortful responding to obtain food. Electrophysiological and voltammetric studies indicate that novel stimuli, conditioned stimuli that predict reward, and instrumental behaviors that deliver natural rewards all act to stimulate DA activity. Accumbens DA acts as a modulator of several functions related to motivated behavior, and can influence normal and pathological cognitive function, activational aspects of motivation, anergia or psychomotor slowing in depression, the impact of conditioned stimuli, plasticity and a variety of sensorimotor functions.

---

Title: Neurochemistry of Anesthetic States.
Abstract: The molecular mechanisms underlying anesthetic-induced unconsciousness remain incompletely understood, hindering the development of safer anesthetic agents and improved monitoring techniques. While previous studies have characterized individual neurotransmitter systems during anesthesia, comprehensive neurochemical profiling across multiple brain regions has been limited by methodological constraints. Here, we present a novel enzymatic approach combining high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) and optimized tissue extraction protocols to simultaneously quantify monoamines, amino acids, and acetylcholine in discrete brain regions during various anesthetic states. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were anesthetized with propofol, sevoflurane, or ketamine at equipotent doses, with conscious controls receiving saline. Brain regions including prefrontal cortex, thalamus, hippocampus, and brainstem were rapidly extracted and processed using our modified enzymatic digestion protocol. Propofol administration significantly decreased dopamine (67% reduction, p<0.001) and norepinephrine (45% reduction, p<0.01) in the prefrontal cortex while increasing GABA levels by 34% (p<0.05). Sevoflurane produced similar but less pronounced changes, whereas ketamine uniquely elevated glutamate concentrations in the hippocampus (28% increase, p<0.01) while reducing thalamic acetylcholine by 52% (p<0.001). Regional variations in neurochemical responses correlated with known anesthetic binding sites and consciousness-related neural circuits. This enzymatic approach provides enhanced sensitivity and reproducibility for studying anesthetic neurochemistry, revealing agent-specific patterns that may inform mechanism-based anesthetic development and perioperative monitoring strategies.

---

Title: Neurochemical insights into the radiation protection of astronauts: Distinction between low- and moderate-LET radiation components.
Abstract: Radiation protection of astronauts remains an ongoing challenge in preparation of deep space exploratory missions. Exposure to space radiation consisting of multiple radiation components is associated with a significant risk of experiencing central nervous system (CNS) detriments, potentially influencing the crew operational decisions. Developing of countermeasures protecting CNS from the deleterious exposure requires understanding the mechanistic nature of cognitive impairments induced by different components of space radiation. The current study was designed to identify differences in neurochemical modifications caused by exposure to low- and moderate-LET radiations and to elucidate a distinction between the observed outcomes. We exposed rats to accelerated protons (170 MeV; 0.5 keV/μm) or to carbon ions (12C; 500 MeV/u; 10.5 keV/μm) delivered at the same dose of 1 Gy. Neurochemical alterations were evaluated 1, 30, and 90 days after exposure via indices of the monoamine metabolism measured in five brain structures, including prefrontal cortex, hypothalamus, nucleus accumbens, hippocampus and striatum. We obtained the detailed patterns of neurochemical modifications after exposure to the mentioned radiation modalities. Our data show that the enhancement in the radiation LET from relatively low to moderate values leads to different neurochemical outcomes and that a particular effect depends on the irradiated brain structure. We also hypothesized that exposure to the moderate-LET radiations can induce a hyperactivation of feedback neurochemical mechanisms, which blur metabolic deviations and lead to the delayed impairments in brain functions. Based on our findings we discuss possible contribution of the observed changes to behavioural impairments.

---

Title: Roles of transmembrane domain 2 and the first intracellular loop in human noradrenaline transporter function: pharmacological and SCAM analysis.
Abstract: The human noradrenaline transporter (hNET) mediates reuptake of synaptic noradrenaline and is a primary target for antidepressants and psychostimulants. While transmembrane domain 2 (TM2) and the first intracellular loop (ICL1) are predicted to be critical for transporter function based on homology models, their specific roles in substrate recognition and transport remain poorly characterized. To investigate the functional importance of these regions, we employed site-directed mutagenesis combined with substituted cysteine accessibility method (SCAM) analysis in HEK293 cells stably expressing hNET constructs. Individual residues within TM2 (positions 118-142) and ICL1 (positions 143-156) were systematically mutated to cysteine and analyzed for noradrenaline uptake activity, ligand binding affinity, and accessibility to membrane-impermeant sulfhydryl reagents. Cysteine substitution at positions Ile119, Phe124, and Ser128 within TM2 resulted in 60-85% reduction in noradrenaline uptake without affecting surface expression levels. SCAM analysis revealed that residues Ile119 and Ser128 are accessible to MTSEA modification from the extracellular side, suggesting their location near the substrate permeation pathway. In ICL1, mutation of Arg148 and Glu151 significantly impaired both uptake function and desipramine binding affinity (Kd increased 4-8 fold). Methanethiosulfonate accessibility patterns indicated that ICL1 undergoes conformational changes during the transport cycle. These findings demonstrate that TM2 contributes directly to the substrate translocation pathway while ICL1 is essential for proper transporter conformational dynamics, providing new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying noradrenaline transport and antidepressant action.

Title: Corticosterone urinalysis and nicotinic receptor modulation in rats.
Abstract: Chronic stress and nicotine exposure produce complex neuroadaptations that are difficult to assess using invasive sampling methods. Non-invasive biomarkers would facilitate longitudinal studies of stress-nicotine interactions in rodent models. We developed and validated a urinalysis protocol for measuring corticosterone levels and examined the relationship between urinary corticosterone and nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) expression following chronic nicotine treatment. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were housed in metabolic cages and received either saline or nicotine (0.4 mg/kg, s.c.) twice daily for 14 days. Urine samples were collected every 48 hours, and corticosterone concentrations were determined using enzyme immunoassay. Following treatment, brain tissues were processed for α4β2 and α7 nAChR binding using [³H]-epibatidine and [¹²⁵I]-α-bungarotoxin autoradiography, respectively. Urinary corticosterone showed significant positive correlation with plasma levels (r=0.82, p<0.001) and exhibited circadian variation consistent with the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis rhythm. Chronic nicotine treatment elevated mean urinary corticosterone by 34% compared to controls (p<0.01). Nicotine-treated animals displayed upregulated α4β2 nAChR binding in cortical and hippocampal regions (25-40% increase, p<0.05) but unchanged α7 nAChR binding. Importantly, individual differences in urinary corticosterone levels significantly predicted the magnitude of α4β2 upregulation (r=0.71, p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that urinalysis provides a reliable, non-invasive method for monitoring corticosterone in rats and reveals a novel relationship between stress hormone levels and nicotinic receptor plasticity that may inform addiction vulnerability mechanisms.

Title: Neurochemical and physiological correlates of a critical period of respiratory development in the rat.
Abstract: Respiratory control undergoes dramatic reorganization during early postnatal development, with disruptions during critical periods potentially leading to sudden infant death syndrome and other breathing disorders. This study investigated the neurochemical and physiological changes occurring during the critical period of respiratory development in Sprague-Dawley rats between postnatal days 10-14 (P10-P14). We recorded respiratory frequency, tidal volume, and hypoxic ventilatory responses using whole-body plethysmography in conscious pups. Brainstem sections from the pre-Bötzinger complex and rostral ventrolateral medulla were analyzed for neurotransmitter receptor expression using quantitative immunohistochemistry and in situ hybridization. GABA-A and glycine receptor subunit mRNA levels were measured by real-time PCR. Respiratory frequency decreased significantly from P10 to P14 (184±12 to 156±8 breaths/min, p<0.01), while tidal volume increased proportionally with body weight. Hypoxic ventilatory responses showed biphasic changes, with peak sensitivity at P12 followed by depression at P14. GABA-A receptor α1 subunit expression increased 2.3-fold between P10-P14 (p<0.001), while glycine receptor α1 subunits showed more modest increases (1.4-fold, p<0.05). Serotonin 2A receptor density decreased by 35% during this period (p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that the P10-P14 period represents a critical developmental window characterized by enhanced inhibitory neurotransmission and altered chemosensitivity in brainstem respiratory circuits. The coordinated changes in GABAergic inhibition and serotoninergic modulation may contribute to the vulnerability of respiratory control during early postnatal life.

Title: [Current trends in clinical psychiatric research].
Abstract: SCHIZOPHRENIA: The long-standing dopaminergic hypothesis is based on well-established findings, mainly related to a dysfunction of the mesolimbic C2 and D3 receptors, negative signs of prefrontal hypodopaminergia concerning the D2 and D4 receptors. Immune disorders are also involved as well as a possible neurodevelopmental component. Finally genetic factors play an important role. Three monoaminergic systems appear to be implicated: the catecholaminergic, serotaminergic and homovanilic systems. It is not known whether the disorder of the serotaminergic system results from pre-existing fragilization or a symptom-related modification. According to the chronobiology hypothesis, certain biological rhythms (cortisol, temperature, perhaps melatonin) are desynchronized, a disequilibrium which disappears after cure. The fragilization hypothesis is based on the progressive development of a stress-related pathological organization in the hippocampo-hypothalamo-adrenal axis. The development of new psychotropes remains a challenge to meet the unsatisfied demands in psychiatrics. Drugs with rapid action and minimal side effects are needed.

Title: Neuropharmacological properties of Trichosanthes dioica root.
Abstract: Trichosanthes dioica Roxb. (Cucurbitaceae), commonly known as pointed gourd in English, is a dioecious climber grown widely in the Indian subcontinent. Traditionally, this plant has been used in India for several medicinal purposes. The present study aimed to evaluate certain neuropharmacological properties of the hydroalcoholic extract of T. dioica root (TDA) in experimental animal models. TDA (at 100 and 200 mg·kg(-1) body weight, p.o.) was evaluated for anti-nociceptive activity by the acetic acid-induced writhing and tail flick methods. Locomotor depressant activity was measured by means of an actophotometer. Skeletal muscle relaxant effects were evaluated by using a rota-rod apparatus, and the sedative potentiating property by a phenobarbitone-induced sleep potentiation study. The results of the present study revealed significant (P < 0.001) and dose dependent anti-nociceptive, locomotor depressant, muscle relaxant, and sedative potentiating effects of TDA, demonstrating its depressant action on the central nervous system (CNS). From the present study, it can be concluded that T. dioica root possessed prominent anti-nociceptive, as well as depressant, action on the CNS, as manifested by these important neuropharmacological properties in mice.

Title: Neurogenic bladder model for spinal cord injury: spinal cord microdialysis and chronic urodynamics.
Abstract: We describe an animal model to study neurotransmitter changes in parallel with urodynamic testing following Spinal Cord Injury (SCI). Urodynamic access was achieved using a subcutaneously placed 7 French dual lumen portacatheter. Spinal cord injury was induced by weight drop technique onto exposed dura at T8. The L6-S1 detrusor nuclei were localized stereotactically and microdialysis probe placement was confirmed through histologic methods. Chronic urodynamics revealed detrusor hyperreflexia (DH) 14 days following SCI. In vivo microdialysis of spinal cord amino acids was performed using CMA 11 (240 uM) probes in halothane-anesthetized rats at baseline and intervals of 20-30 min following spinal cord injury. Significant increases in the excitatory amino acid glutamate, and the inhibitory amino acids, glycine and taurine, were seen following spinal cord injury. Amino acid levels peaked at approximately 40 min following contusion injury with glycine demonstrating the highest levels of all amino acids measured. This neurogenic rat model provides a useful means of examining the effects of spinal cord injury on bladder function. By utilizing spinal cord microdialysis, one could intervene at the level of the detrusor nuclei to modulate bladder function.

Title: A novel system allowing long-term simultaneous video-electroencephalography recording, drug infusion and blood sampling in rats.
Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) recording and drug administration is commonly used for neurological experiments in rats, but is typically cumbersome due the use of multiple lines. We have developed a unique system, which allows long-term simultaneous video-electroencephalography recording, drug infusion and blood sampling in rats. The vEEG/drug infusion system was designed and tested on two contrasting animal models of epilepsy. Animals were implanted with EEG-electrodes and a jugular vein cannula fixed in a head cap, avoiding an additional cable for tethering. In an acute infusion study (n=16), repeated blood samples were taken after i.v. bolus injection of valproate. In a subset of these rats (n=10), paired blood samples were removed from the jugular vein and the heart after valproate administration. In a chronic infusion study (n=38), heparinised (4IU/h) saline or valproate (42mg/kg/h) was infused continuously for up to 17 days. In the acute study, repeated blood samples showed a decrease in plasma valproate levels over time following bolus injection. In the chronic study, high quality continuous EEG was achieved and 79% of animals were successfully infused throughout the planned infusion period (13-17 days), with 66% of projected blood samples able to be taken during the infusion. There was a high correlation between the jugular vein and cardiac plasma levels of valproate (Spearman test, r=0.69; p<0.05). This system is ideal for pharmacokinetic/dynamic studies and long-term drug infusion where simultaneous EEG and/or frequent blood sampling are desired.

Title: Efficient measurement of endogenous neurotransmitters in small localized regions of central nervous systems in vitro with HPLC.
Abstract: Traditional methods for measuring endogenous neurotransmitters in discrete brain regions have been limited by tissue volume requirements and analytical sensitivity. This study describes an optimized high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system with electrochemical detection for quantifying dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and their metabolites from microdissected brain regions weighing 0.5-2.0 mg. Brain slices (300 μm) from adult male Sprague-Dawley rats were prepared using a vibratome, and specific nuclei including the nucleus accumbens, caudate-putamen, and substantia nigra were microdissected under stereomicroscopic guidance. Tissue samples were homogenized in 0.1 M perchloric acid containing 0.1 mM EDTA and 10 ng/ml dihydroxybenzylamine as internal standard. The HPLC system employed a C18 reverse-phase column with a mobile phase consisting of 75 mM sodium phosphate buffer, 1.7 mM octanesulfonic acid, 25 μM EDTA, and 10% acetonitrile at pH 3.0. Detection limits were 0.5 pg for dopamine, 1.0 pg for norepinephrine, and 0.8 pg for serotonin. Intra-assay coefficient of variation was <5% for all compounds. Regional neurotransmitter distributions obtained were consistent with known neuroanatomical patterns, with dopamine levels highest in striatal regions and serotonin predominant in raphe-projecting areas. This method provides reliable quantification of multiple neurotransmitters from small tissue samples, enabling detailed neurochemical mapping studies and reducing the number of animals required for neurochemical analyses while maintaining analytical precision suitable for pharmacological investigations.

Title: Cannabinoids: reward, dependence, and underlying neurochemical mechanisms--a review of recent preclinical data.
Abstract: Starting with the discovery of an endogenous brain cannabinoid system with specific receptors and endogenous ligands, research in the cannabinoid field has accelerated dramatically over the last 15 years. Cannabis is the most used illicit psychotropic substance in the world but only recently have reliable preclinical models become available for investigating the rewarding and dependence-producing actions of its psychoactive constituent, delta9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC). The goal of this review is to examine the various animal models currently available that are being used to facilitate our understanding of the rewarding and dependence-producing actions of cannabinoids, which are central to their abuse liability, and of the neurochemical mechanisms that may underlie these actions of cannabinoids. Recent demonstrations that strong and persistent intravenous self-administration behavior can be obtained in squirrel monkeys using a range of THC doses that are in agreement with the total intake and the single doses of THC normally self-administered by humans smoking marijuana cigarettes provides a reliable and direct tool for assessing the reinforcing effects of THC that are central to its abuse liability. In addition, recent demonstrations of persistent intravenous self-administration of synthetic cannabinoid CB1 receptor agonists by rats and mice and the development of genetically modified mice lacking specific cannabinoid receptors provide convenient rodent models for exploring underlying neurochemical mechanisms. Repeated demonstrations in rats that THC and synthetic CB1 agonists can induce conditioned place preferences or aversions, depending on details of dose and spacing, can reduce the threshold for intracranial self-stimulation behavior under certain conditions, and can serve as effective discriminative stimuli for operant behavior provide less direct, but more rapidly established, measures for investigating the rewarding effects of cannabinoids. Finally, there have been numerous recent reports of major functional interactions between endogenous cannabinoid, opioid, and dopaminergic neurotransmitter systems in areas such as analgesia, physical dependence and tolerance development, and drug reinforcement or reward. This provides an opportunity to search for drugs with the beneficial therapeutic effects of currently available cannabinoids or opioids but without undesirable adverse effects such as abuse liability.

Title: [Lithium in the psychopharmacology of affective disorders and mechanisms of its effects on cellular physiology].
Abstract: However, despite successful use of lithium in the treatment of affective disorders for almost 40 years, the mechanisms of its therapeutic action are still poorly understood. This review presents and summarizes the current literature about the use of lithium in treatment of affective disorders, as well as its effects on cellular physiology, with a separate description of the effect of this ion on the functioning of nerve tissue and ion-molecular mechanisms. Несмотря на успешное применение лития в лечении аффективных расстройств на протяжении почти 40 лет, механизмы его терапевтического действия до сих пор изучены мало. В обзоре представлены и обобщены имеющиеся на сегодняшний момент данные литературы, касающиеся как применения лития для терапии аффективных расстройств, так и его эффектов на клеточную физиологию, с отдельным описанием влияния данного иона на функционирование нервной ткани и ионно-молекулярные механизмы.

Title: Chapter 12- Brain as an HIV sequestered site: Use of nanoparticles as a therapeutic option.
Abstract: Patients infected with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) are at increased risk to develop neurocognitive problems. HIV crosses the blood-brain barrier (BBB) through a variety of means. Once within the brain tissue, HIV stimulates immunoactivation and inflammation that lead to neuronal loss. This review discusses the pathophysiology of HIV within the brain and the treatment modalities used to prevent neurocognitive problems from developing. Modalities that prevent antiretroviral drugs from crossing into the central nervous system and reducing viral load are also discussed. Finally, since drug penetration across the BBB is reduced, the use of nanoparticles as a treatment modality to increase BBB penetration may be an option worth further exploration.

Title: Gaddum and LSD: the birth and growth of experimental and clinical neuropharmacology research on 5-HT in the UK.
Abstract: The vasoconstrictor substance named serotonin was identified as 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT) by Maurice Rapport in 1949. In 1951, Rapport gave Gaddum samples of 5-HT substance allowing him to develop a bioassay to both detect and measure the amine. Gaddum and colleagues rapidly identified 5-HT in brain and showed that lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) antagonized its action in peripheral tissues. Gaddum accordingly postulated that 5-HT might have a role in mood regulation. This review examines the role of UK scientists in the first 20 years following these major discoveries, discussing their role in developing assays for 5-HT in the CNS, identifying the enzymes involved in the synthesis and metabolism of 5-HT and investigating the effect of drugs on brain 5-HT. It reviews studies on the effects of LSD in humans, including Gaddum's self-administration experiments. It outlines investigations on the role of 5-HT in psychiatric disorders, including studies on the effect of antidepressant drugs on the 5-HT concentration in rodent and human brain, and the attempts to examine 5-HT biochemistry in the brains of patients with depressive illness. It is clear that a rather small group of both preclinical scientists and psychiatrists in the UK made major advances in our understanding of the role of 5-HT in the brain, paving the way for much of the knowledge now taken for granted when discussing ways that 5-HT might be involved in the control of mood and the idea that therapeutic drugs used to alleviate psychiatric illness might alter the function of cerebral 5-HT.

Title: Isolation of a retinal pigment epithelial cell-derived fraction which promotes Müller cell proliferation.
Abstract: Retinal pigment epithelial cells (RPE) secrete a factor(s) in vitro which promotes Müller cell proliferation. To begin the isolation of the active factor, medium conditioned by cultured RPE (RPE-CM) was fractionated by fast protein liquid chromatography. The fractions were tested in an assay for Müller cell proliferation and it was found that one fraction contained biological activity comparable to that of complete RPE-CM. Gel electrophoresis demonstrated that this fraction consisted of proteins with approximate molecular masses of 35-88 kDa. An affinity assay revealed that only an 88-kDa protein in the RPE-CM binds to Müller cells. This protein was also present in the active fraction; therefore, it is the most likely source of the mitogenic activity of RPE-CM.

Title: Schizophrenia.
Abstract: To provide the most effective care for this difficult patient population, it is helpful to remember that patients with schizophrenia have disease-intrinsic limitations that limit their ability to participate in their care. These limitations are symptoms of a disease and not volitional. For the physician to substitute for these deficits, a certain degree of flexibility as well as the willingness to use unorthodox interventions is necessary. Good medical care is as important for the patient with schizophrenia as for any other patient.

Title: Current state of the use of neuroimaging techniques to understand and alter appetite control in humans.
Abstract: It is in the brain where the decision is made what and how much to eat. In the last decades neuroimaging research has contributed extensively to new knowledge about appetite control by revealing the underlying brain processes. Interestingly, there is the fast growing idea of using these methods to develop new treatments for obesity and eating disorders. In this review, we summarize the findings of the importance of the use of neuropharmacology and neuroimaging techniques in understanding and modifying appetite control. Appetite control is a complex interplay between homeostatic, hedonic, and cognitive processes. Administration of the neuropeptides insulin and oxytocin curb food intake and alter brain responses in reward and cognitive control areas. Additionally, these areas can be targeted for neuromodulation or neurofeedback to reduce food cravings and increase self-control to alter food intake. The recent findings reveal the potential of intranasal administration of hormones or modifying appetite control brain networks to reduce food consumption in volunteers with overweight and obesity or individuals with an eating disorder. Although long-term clinical studies are still needed.

Title: Effects of soman (pinacolyl methylphosphonofluoridate) on coronary blood flow and cardiac function in swine.
Abstract: Organophosphorus compounds such as soman pose significant toxicological threats due to their irreversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase, leading to cholinergic crisis. While the neurological and respiratory effects of soman poisoning are well-documented, the direct cardiovascular consequences remain poorly characterized. This study examined the acute effects of soman exposure on coronary hemodynamics and myocardial function in an anesthetized swine model. Twelve Yorkshire swine (25-30 kg) were instrumented with coronary flow probes, left ventricular pressure catheters, and ECG electrodes under pentobarbital anesthesia. Animals received either soman (0.5 × LD50, n=8) or saline control (n=4) via intravenous injection. Coronary blood flow, left ventricular pressure, heart rate, and cardiac output were continuously monitored for 120 minutes post-exposure. Soman administration produced rapid onset of cholinergic signs including bradycardia, hypotension, and cardiac arrhythmias within 5-10 minutes. Coronary blood flow decreased significantly by 35 ± 8% (p<0.01) compared to baseline, while coronary vascular resistance increased 2.3-fold. Left ventricular contractility (dP/dt max) declined by 42 ± 12% (p<0.001), and cardiac output fell to 60% of control values. These hemodynamic changes preceded the onset of severe respiratory distress and were partially reversed by atropine pretreatment in additional animals. The results demonstrate that soman exerts direct cardiotoxic effects independent of respiratory compromise, suggesting that cardiovascular support should be considered as an essential component of organophosphate poisoning treatment protocols.

Title: Neuropsychopharmacological aesthetics: A theoretical consideration of pharmacological approaches to causative brain study in aesthetics and art.
Abstract: Recent developments in neuroaesthetics have heightened the need for causative approaches to more deeply understand the mechanism underlying perception, emotion, and aesthetic experiences. This has recently been the topic for empirical work, employing several causative methods for changing brain activity, as well as comparative assessments of individuals with brain damage or disease. However, one area of study with high potential, and indeed a long history of often nonscientific use in the area of aesthetics and art, employing psychopharmacological chemicals as means of changing brain function, has not been systematically utilized. This chapter reviews the literature on this topic, analyzing neuroendocrinological (neurochemical) approaches and mechanisms that might be used to causatively study the aesthetic brain. We focus on four relevant neuromodulatory systems potentially related to aesthetic experience: the dopaminergic, serotonergic, cannabinoid, and the opioidergic system. We build a bridge to psychopharmacological methods and review drug-induced behavioral and neurobiological consequences. We conclude with a discussion of hypotheses and suggestions for future research.

Title: Neurochemical substrates linked to impulsive and compulsive phenotypes in addiction: A preclinical perspective.
Abstract: Addiction is characterized by a transition from impulsive drug-seeking to compulsive drug-taking behaviors, yet the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this phenotypic shift remain poorly understood. We examined dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic neurotransmitter systems in brain regions implicated in addiction using a well-validated rat self-administration model. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) underwent cocaine self-administration training followed by behavioral phenotyping using delay discounting and compulsivity tasks. Animals were classified as high-impulsive (HI) or low-impulsive (LI) based on delay discounting performance, and high-compulsive (HC) or low-compulsive (LC) based on punishment-resistant responding. Post-mortem neurochemical analysis was performed using high-performance liquid chromatography in the nucleus accumbens, prefrontal cortex, and dorsal striatum. HI animals showed significantly reduced dopamine transporter expression in the nucleus accumbens (p<0.01) and elevated dopamine D2 receptor binding in the prefrontal cortex compared to LI rats. HC animals exhibited decreased serotonin transporter density in the dorsal striatum (p<0.05) and reduced GABA concentrations in the prefrontal cortex relative to LC animals. Importantly, impulsive and compulsive phenotypes were associated with distinct neurochemical profiles, with minimal overlap between groups. Correlation analyses revealed that compulsivity scores negatively correlated with prefrontal GABA levels (r=-0.67, p<0.001), while impulsivity measures correlated with accumbal dopamine transporter expression (r=-0.58, p<0.01). These findings suggest that impulsive and compulsive addiction phenotypes are mediated by dissociable neurochemical substrates, providing potential targets for personalized therapeutic interventions in substance use disorders.

Title: Neurochemical mechanisms and pharmacologic strategies in managing nausea and vomiting related to cyclic vomiting syndrome and other gastrointestinal disorders.
Abstract: Nausea and vomiting are common gastrointestinal complaints which could be triggered by stimuli in both the peripheral and central nervous systems. They may be considered as defense mechanisms when threatening toxins/agents enter the gastrointestinal tract or there is excessive retention of gastrointestinal contents due to obstruction. The pathophysiology of nausea and vomiting is complex and much still remains unknown. Therefore, treatments are restricted or ineffective in many cases. Nausea and vomiting with functional etiologies including cyclic vomiting syndrome are challenging in gastroenterology. In this article, we review potential pathways, neurochemical transmitters, and their receptors which are possibly involved in the pathophysiology of nausea and vomiting including the entity cyclic vomiting syndrome.

Title: Beyond the reward hypothesis: alternative functions of nucleus accumbens dopamine.
Abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAcc) has been predominantly studied within the framework of reward processing and addiction, where dopamine release is viewed as encoding reward prediction error and motivational salience. However, accumulating evidence suggests that NAcc dopamine serves broader computational functions beyond hedonic processing. This review synthesizes recent findings challenging the traditional reward hypothesis and proposes alternative roles for accumbens dopamine signaling. We examined literature from 2000-2005 encompassing electrophysiological recordings, microdialysis studies, and behavioral pharmacology experiments in rodents and non-human primates. Lesion studies utilizing 6-OHDA and pharmacological manipulations with dopamine receptor antagonists were analyzed alongside emerging optogenetic approaches. Our analysis reveals that NAcc dopamine responds robustly to aversive stimuli, uncertainty, and novelty independent of reward value. Dopaminergic signaling in the shell and core subregions exhibits distinct temporal dynamics during action selection, with phasic release preceding motor initiation rather than following reward delivery. Furthermore, dopamine depletion impairs effort-based decision making and behavioral switching without abolishing reward preference. These findings indicate that NAcc dopamine functions as a general motivational signal that energizes behavior and facilitates adaptive responding to environmental demands. Rather than simply encoding "wanting" or reward prediction, accumbens dopamine appears to regulate behavioral activation, attention allocation, and cognitive flexibility. This reconceptualization has important implications for understanding psychiatric disorders characterized by motivational deficits, including depression and schizophrenia, and suggests novel therapeutic targets beyond traditional reward-based frameworks.

---

Title: Zebrafish assessment of cognitive improvement and anxiolysis: filling the gap between in vitro and rodent models for drug development.
Abstract: Traditional drug discovery for neuropsychiatric disorders relies heavily on in vitro assays followed by costly rodent studies, creating a significant gap in translational research. Zebrafish (Danio rerio) have emerged as a promising intermediate model organism, offering vertebrate complexity with high-throughput screening capabilities. This review examines the utility of zebrafish behavioral assays for evaluating cognitive enhancement and anxiolytic properties of novel compounds. We analyzed published studies from 2005-2011 employing standardized zebrafish protocols including novel tank diving, light-dark preference, shoaling behavior, and spatial learning paradigms. Pharmacological validation studies demonstrated that zebrafish exhibit dose-dependent responses to established anxiolytics (diazepam, buspirone) and cognitive enhancers (modafinil, piracetam) consistent with mammalian models. The novel tank test showed 89% concordance with rodent elevated plus-maze results for anxiolytic screening across 23 compounds tested. Cognitive assessment using T-maze and conditioned place preference protocols revealed significant correlations (r=0.76, p<0.001) with Morris water maze performance in corresponding rodent studies. Zebrafish demonstrated superior throughput capacity, requiring 48-72 hours versus 2-4 weeks for equivalent rodent protocols, while maintaining 85% translational predictivity for clinical outcomes. These findings establish zebrafish as a valuable bridge between cellular and mammalian models, offering rapid, cost-effective screening for neurotropic compounds. The high evolutionary conservation of neurotransmitter systems and behavioral responses supports zebrafish integration into tiered drug development strategies for anxiety and cognitive disorders.

Title: A primary culture system for biochemical analyses of neuronal proteins.
Abstract: Low-density cultures of embryonic rat hippocampal neurons have been widely used to investigate localization and function of neuronal proteins using immunocytochemistry and electrophysiology. These cultures provide a relatively homogeneous population of hippocampal pyramidal neurons and interneurons compared to post-natal mixed neuron/glial cultures from hippocampus, cerebral cortex, and cerebellum. However, the limited quantity of neurons and the difficulty in harvesting adequate amounts makes biochemical analyses of endogenous neuronal proteins in these low-density cultured neurons difficult. Here, we provide detailed methods to prepare cultures of embryonic rat hippocampal neurons suitable for biochemical analyses of both endogenously and exogenously expressed proteins. The procedures described here are also suitable for comprehensive studies of expression, localization, post-translational modification, and function of neuronal proteins in the same neuronal culture system.
