Title: The psychopharmacology of pseudobulbar affect.
Abstract: Pseudobulbar affect (PBA) is characterized by uncontrollable emotional episodes disconnected or disproportionate with mood, in association with an array of neurologic conditions. PBA is associated with disruption of descending control of brainstem motor circuitry and dysregulation of serotonergic and glutamatergic function. PBA has been historically under recognized, though advances resulting in more specific diagnostic criteria, validated rating scales, and an approved pharmacotherapy offer opportunities for improved treatment outcomes.

---

Title: Limbic corticostriatal systems and delayed reinforcement.
Abstract: Impulsive choice, one aspect of impulsivity, is characterized by an abnormally high preference for small, immediate rewards over larger delayed rewards, and can be a feature of adolescence, but also attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), addiction, and other neuropsychiatric disorders. Both the serotonin and dopamine neuromodulator systems are implicated in impulsivity; manipulations of these systems affect animal models of impulsive choice, though these effects may depend on the receptor subtype and whether or not the reward is signaled. These systems project to limbic cortical and striatal structures shown to be abnormal in animal models of ADHD. Damage to the nucleus accumbens core (AcbC) causes rats to exhibit impulsive choice. These rats are also hyperactive, but are unimpaired in tests of visuospatial attention; they may therefore represent an animal model of the hyperactive-impulsive subtype of ADHD. Lesions to the anterior cingulate or medial prefrontal cortex, two afferents to the AcbC, do not induce impulsive choice, but lesions of the basolateral amygdala do, while lesions to the orbitofrontal cortex have had opposite effects in different tasks measuring impulsive choice. In theory, impulsive choice may emerge as a result of abnormal processing of the magnitude of rewards, or as a result of a deficit in the effects of delayed reinforcement. Recent evidence suggests that AcbC-lesioned rats perceive reward magnitude normally, but exhibit a selective deficit in learning instrumental responses using delayed reinforcement, suggesting that the AcbC is a reinforcement learning system that mediates the effects of delayed rewards.

---

Title: Microfluidics: a new cosset for neurobiology.
Abstract: Recently, microfluidic systems have shown great potential in the study of molecular and cellular biology. With its excellent properties, such as miniaturization, integration and automation, to name just a few, microfluidics creates new opportunities for the spatial and temporal control of cell growth and environmental stimuli in vitro. In the field of neuroscience, microfluidic devices offer precise control of the microenvironment surrounding individual cells, and the delivery of biochemical or physical cues to neural networks or single neurons. The intent of this review is to outline recent advances in microfluidic-based applications in neurobiology, with emphasis on neuron culture, neuron manipulation, neural stem cell differentiation, neuropharmacology, neuroelectrophysiology, and neuron biosensors. It also aims to stimulate development of microfluidic-based applications in neurobiology by involving scientists from various disciplines, especially neurobiology and microtechnology.

---

Title: The footfault test as a screening tool in the 6-hydroxydopamine rat model of Parkinson's disease.
Abstract: The administration of 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) into the nigrostriatal pathway is a rat model of Parkinson's disease (PD). The footfault test is a behavioural task in which rodents have their motor functions assessed. Here, we observed that unilaterally 6-OHDA-lesioned animals show a context-induced ipsilateral rotational behaviour when placed on the footfault apparatus for 3 min and this may be used as index to detect lesioned animals. Our results showed a sensitivity and specificity of 100% for lesions higher than 94% and 64%, respectively (ROC curve: AUC=0.988). A binary logistic regression model showed an expB=1.116 (95% CI, 1.007-1.236) and C=-9.081+/-4.554 (p=0.046) using the nigral tyrosine hidroxylase immunocontent as standard (each unit represents a 10%-lesion extension). Additionally, the footfault test was more sensitive than apomorphine challenging at 1mg/kg when these tests were carried out days apart and it was less sensitive than methylphenidate at 40 mg/kg (sign test, p<0.05). Therefore, the footfault test may be very useful in the PD animal model for screening animals since it is fast and simple and it does not require a drug to induce rotational activity.

---

Title: Neuropharmacological modulation of cognitive deficits after brain damage.
Abstract: Cognitive impairments following traumatic brain injury, stroke, and neurodegenerative diseases represent a major clinical challenge with limited therapeutic options. Recent advances in understanding the neurochemical basis of cognitive dysfunction have identified potential pharmacological targets for intervention. This review examines current evidence for neuropharmacological approaches to treating post-injury cognitive deficits, focusing on cholinergic, dopaminergic, and glutamatergic systems. We analyzed data from randomized controlled trials and case studies published between 1995-2004 involving patients with acquired brain injury. Cholinesterase inhibitors, particularly donepezil and rivastigmine, demonstrated modest but significant improvements in attention and memory function in 60-70% of patients with traumatic brain injury-related cognitive impairment. Dopaminergic agents including bromocriptine and methylphenidate showed efficacy in enhancing executive function and processing speed, with effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.6. Modafinil emerged as a promising treatment for post-injury fatigue and attention deficits, with response rates approaching 65% in preliminary studies. AMPA receptor potentiators and NMDA receptor modulators showed encouraging results in animal models but remain in early-phase human trials. Combination therapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems demonstrated superior outcomes compared to monotherapy in limited studies. While current pharmacological interventions provide meaningful but moderate cognitive benefits, optimal treatment protocols remain undefined. Future research should focus on biomarker-guided personalized medicine approaches and novel neuroprotective strategies to maximize therapeutic potential in this vulnerable population.

---

Title: [Multilevel neurochemical organization of the brain].
Abstract: Many years' studies in functional neurochemistry have provided a concept of the functionally induced biochemical heterogeneity of neurons and their subcellular components, which is formed during ontogenesis as the functions of the central nervous system are developed. It has been established that it manifests itself at the systemic, cellular, and subcellular levels: (1) between brain formations which constitute the single functional system; (2) between the neurons of various morphofunctional types (associative, efferent, afferent ones) between the neurons and the glia; (3) between individual components (the nucleus and the cytoplasm) of a neuron and its subcellular (synaptosomes, mitochondria, synaptosomal membranes) components. It is concluded that the functionally determined biochemical heterogeneity and morphofunctional features found in animals of various strains may be regarded as one of the ways of compensatory, rehabilitative, and adaptative processes in the central nervous system.

---

Title: Spontaneously hypertensive rat neuroanatomy: applications to pharmacological research.
Abstract: Spontaneously hypertensive rats (SHR), which are normotensive at birth and develop sustained hypertension between 3 and 6 months of age, are the model most extensively investigated for evaluating hypertensive brain damage and its treatment. The time-dependent rise of arterial blood pressure and the occurrence of brain atrophy, loss of nerve cells and glial reaction are shared to some extent with what occurs human hypertensive brain. SHR, therefore, can represent a reasonable model of hypertension-related brain damage. Our main studies on cerebrovascular and brain microanatomical changes occurring in SHR and their sensitivity to pharmacological interventions are summarized.

---

Title: Pharmacological findings on the biochemical bases of memory processes: a general view.
Abstract: Memory formation and consolidation represent complex neurobiological processes involving multiple neurotransmitter systems and intracellular signaling cascades. While considerable progress has been made in identifying key molecular mechanisms underlying learning and memory, the pharmacological interactions between different memory systems remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current pharmacological evidence regarding the biochemical foundations of memory processes, with particular emphasis on synaptic plasticity mechanisms. We analyzed data from over 200 studies published between 1990-2004 examining the effects of various pharmacological agents on memory formation, consolidation, and retrieval in both animal models and human subjects. Key findings demonstrate that memory processes depend critically on cholinergic, glutamatergic, and GABAergic neurotransmission, with the NMDA receptor-mediated signaling cascade playing a central role in long-term potentiation and memory consolidation. Protein kinase A, protein kinase C, and calcium/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II emerge as essential mediators linking neurotransmitter activation to gene transcription and protein synthesis required for long-term memory storage. Additionally, the cAMP response element-binding protein (CREB) pathway appears fundamental for converting short-term synaptic changes into persistent structural modifications. Pharmacological interventions targeting these pathways demonstrate distinct temporal windows for memory enhancement or impairment, suggesting multiple phases of memory consolidation with different molecular requirements. These findings provide a comprehensive framework for understanding memory biochemistry and highlight potential therapeutic targets for memory-related disorders including Alzheimer's disease and age-related cognitive decline.

---

Title: 5-hydroxytryptamine-immunoreactivity in the monogenean parasite, Entobdella soleae.
Abstract: 5-HT-immunoreactivity in Entobdella soleae was found to be extensive throughout both the central and peripheral nervous systems, with the strongest staining occurring in the innervation of the forebody, most notably in the paired cerebral ganglia, pharynx and adhesive pads. In the reproductive system, staining was evident throughout the numerous cell bodies and fibres innervating the musculature of the egg-assembly apparatus. The haptor contained an extensive array of serotoninergic fibres derived from the main longitudinal cords; this array was associated with the haptoral muscles and sclerites, and possibly with the ventral sensory papillae.

---

Title: [The history of CINP between 1958 and 2000].
Abstract: The Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) was established in 1958 as the first international organization dedicated to advancing the scientific understanding of neuropsychopharmacology. This historical review examines the evolution and contributions of CINP during its formative decades from 1958 to 2000, analyzing its role in shaping modern psychopharmacology research and clinical practice. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of CINP archival materials, including congress proceedings, membership records, scientific publications, and correspondence from founding members. Historical documents were obtained from the CINP archives and supplemented with interviews of key figures who participated in the organization's early development. Between 1958 and 2000, CINP organized 22 biennial world congresses across four continents, facilitating international collaboration among 3,847 researchers from 67 countries. The organization played a pivotal role in establishing standardized methodologies for psychopharmacological research, promoting the development of rating scales for psychiatric disorders, and fostering the emergence of biological psychiatry as a distinct discipline. Notable achievements included the creation of task forces on depression, schizophrenia, and anxiety disorders, which produced influential consensus statements that guided clinical practice. CINP also contributed significantly to the training of young investigators through fellowship programs and mentorship initiatives. The period from 1958 to 2000 represents a foundational era for CINP, during which the organization established itself as the premier international forum for neuropsychopharmacology research. CINP's contributions during this period were instrumental in transforming psychopharmacology from an emerging field into a mature scientific discipline with global impact.

---

Title: An investigation of whether there are sex differences in certain behavioural and neurochemical parameters in the rat.
Abstract: In clinical populations, sex differences in disease prevalence, symptoms and outcome have been established. Despite this, female rats are frequently omitted from preclinical research; growing preclinical evidence, however, illustrates meaningful sex differences in behavioural, neurochemical and neuroanatomical endpoints. This review outlines the effects of sex on tests of depression- and anxiety-like symptoms, learning and memory, and responses to stress in rats. In addition, sexual dimorphisms in monoamine neurotransmitter and neurotrophic factor levels, neurogenesis and plasticity, and responsiveness to drugs of abuse are reviewed. Female rats display greater baseline activity levels compared to males, test-specific sex differences also exist in learning and memory protocols as females respond more actively in conditioning paradigms and are somewhat impaired in tests of spatial memory compared to males. Differential baseline and stress-induced hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis responses between male and female rats depend on the nature of the stressor. Females are more responsive to the effects of psychomotor stimulant drugs; sexual dimorphisms in response to psychotropic drugs are likely mediated by neurochemical differences between male and female rats. Differences exist in neurotransmitter activity, transporter and receptor expression between the sexes. Studies of ovariectomised and intact female rats demonstrate a potent impact of elevated estrogen during the estrous cycle on behaviour, neurochemistry, dendritic growth and drug response. Sex differences in baseline behaviours and the methodological procedures employed can influence behavioural pharmacology result interpretation. In addition, the inclusion of both male and female rats in studies investigating neurochemistry and neuromorphology may enhance the validity of drug or rehabilitative treatments.

---

Title: Three-dimensional patterning in biomedicine: Importance and applications in neuropharmacology.
Abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) patterning technologies have emerged as transformative tools in neuropharmacology research, enabling precise spatial control over drug delivery and cellular microenvironments. Traditional two-dimensional culture systems inadequately recapitulate the complex architecture of neural tissues, limiting the translation of pharmacological findings to clinical applications. This review examines recent advances in 3D biomaterial patterning techniques and their applications in neuropharmacological studies. We analyzed peer-reviewed literature from 2010-2018 focusing on 3D printing, microfluidics, hydrogel patterning, and electrospinning approaches for neural drug delivery systems. Key applications include gradient-based drug screening platforms, spatially-controlled release systems for neuroprotective agents, and biomimetic blood-brain barrier models. 3D-printed polylactic acid scaffolds demonstrated sustained release of brain-derived neurotrophic factor over 28 days, while microfluidic devices enabled real-time monitoring of drug penetration across engineered neural barriers. Hydrogel-based systems showed superior cell viability (>85%) compared to conventional cultures (65%) when evaluating neuroprotective compounds. Electrospun nanofiber matrices facilitated directional axonal growth and localized delivery of anti-inflammatory drugs in spinal cord injury models. These technologies significantly improved drug screening accuracy, reduced animal testing requirements, and enhanced our understanding of neuropharmacological mechanisms. Future developments in 4D printing and smart biomaterials promise even greater precision in temporal drug release patterns. 3D patterning represents a paradigm shift in neuropharmacology, offering unprecedented control over drug-tissue interactions and accelerating the development of targeted neurotherapeutics.

---

Title: In-vivo brain dialysis of neurotransmitters.
Abstract: Recent advances in microdialysis technology have provided unprecedented opportunities to monitor neurotransmitter release in the living brain with high temporal resolution. This technique involves the stereotaxic implantation of a dialysis probe with a semipermeable membrane into specific brain regions, allowing continuous sampling of extracellular fluid. We evaluated the efficacy of in-vivo microdialysis for measuring dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and acetylcholine release in the striatum, hippocampus, and prefrontal cortex of freely moving rats. Dialysis probes (2-4 mm membrane length, 20,000 Da molecular weight cutoff) were perfused with artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 1-2 μL/min, and samples were collected at 20-minute intervals for high-performance liquid chromatography analysis. Basal concentrations of dopamine (5-15 nM), norepinephrine (2-8 nM), and serotonin (1-5 nM) were successfully detected, representing approximately 10-20% of actual extracellular levels due to probe recovery limitations. Pharmacological validation experiments demonstrated that amphetamine (2 mg/kg, i.p.) increased striatal dopamine release by 400-600%, while haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg, i.p.) produced a delayed elevation consistent with autoreceptor blockade. Local perfusion of tetrodotoxin abolished neurotransmitter release, confirming the neuronal origin of detected compounds. These findings establish microdialysis as a powerful tool for real-time neurochemical monitoring, offering significant advantages over traditional post-mortem analyses. The technique promises to advance our understanding of neurotransmitter dynamics in behavioral, pharmacological, and pathophysiological studies.

---

Title: Cosmetic psychopharmacology and the President's Council on Bioethics.
Abstract: Advances in neuroscience and biotechnology have heightened the urgency of the debate over "cosmetic psychopharmacology," the use of drugs to enhance mood and temperament in the absence of illness. Beyond Therapy: Biotechnology and the Pursuit of Happiness (2003), the report of the President's Council on Bioethics, has criticized the use of cosmetic psychopharmacology. The Council claimed that cosmetic psychopharmacology will necessarily lead to "severing the link between feelings of happiness and our actions and experiences in the world," but it provided no satisfactory arguments to support this claim and ignored the possibility that cosmetic psychopharmacology might actually enhance the link between happiness and experience. The Council's arguments against cosmetic psychopharmacology depend heavily on the mistaken belief that Prozac and similar antidepressants are mood brighteners in healthy subjects. The empirical evidence, however, clearly indicates that these drugs are not forms of cosmetic psychopharmacology, thus negating much of the Council's arguments. The use of pharmaceutical agents to enhance mood or personality in normal individuals should not be rejected a priori. Instead, the effects of each agent on the individual and on society must be weighed using sound ethical reasoning and the best evidence available.

---

Title: Cocaine increases stimulated dopamine release more in periadolescent than adult rats.
Abstract: The neural mechanisms responsible for the enhanced adolescent vulnerability for initiating drug abuse are unclear. We investigated whether age differences in dopamine neurotransmission could explain cocaine's enhanced psychomotor effects in the periadolescent rat. Electrical stimulation of the medial forebrain bundle of anesthetized post-natal age 28 days (PN28) and PN65 rats elicited dopamine release in caudate nucleus and nucleus accumbens core before and after 15 mg/kg cocaine i.p. Extracellular dopamine concentrations were greater in PN65 than PN28 caudate following 20 and 60 Hz stimulations and in the PN65 nucleus accumbens following 60 Hz stimulations. Cocaine increased dopamine concentrations elicited by 20 Hz stimulations 3-fold in the adult, but almost 9-fold in periadolescent caudate. Dopamine release rate was lower in the periadolescent caudate although total dopamine clearance was similar to that of adults. The periadolescent caudate achieved adult levels of clearance by compensating for a lower V(max) with higher uptake affinity. Tighter regulation of extracellular dopamine by the higher uptake/release ratio in periadolescents led to greater increases after cocaine. In nucleus accumbens, dopamine release and V(max) were lower in periadolescents than adults, but uptake affinity and cocaine effects were similar. Immaturity of dopamine neurotransmission in dorsal striatum may underlie enhanced acute responses to psychostimulants in adolescent rats and suggests a mechanism for the greater vulnerability of adolescent humans to drug addiction.

---

Title: Team Evans and Watkins: Excitatory Amino Acid research at Bristol University 1973-1981.
Abstract: Between 1973 and 1981, the research teams led by Evans and Watkins at Bristol University made seminal contributions to understanding excitatory amino acid neurotransmission, fundamentally shaping modern neuropharmacology. This historical review examines their pioneering work identifying glutamate receptors and establishing the pharmacological framework that underlies contemporary NMDA, AMPA, and kainate receptor classification. The Bristol group's systematic investigation of L-glutamate and L-aspartate as excitatory neurotransmitters began with electrophysiological studies on spinal neurons, demonstrating that these amino acids produced dose-dependent depolarization distinct from other neurotransmitter systems. Their development of selective antagonists, including 2-amino-5-phosphonovalerate (AP5) and 6,7-dinitroquinoxaline-2,3-dione (DNQX), provided crucial pharmacological tools for dissecting glutamatergic transmission. Subsequent binding studies and receptor autoradiography revealed heterogeneous glutamate binding sites throughout the central nervous system, with particularly high densities in hippocampus, cerebral cortex, and cerebellum. The team's characterization of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) as a selective agonist and their discovery of the glycine modulatory site established the foundation for understanding NMDA receptor function in synaptic plasticity, learning, and excitotoxicity. These discoveries directly influenced the development of neuroprotective strategies for stroke and neurodegenerative diseases, while their receptor classification system remains the standard framework for glutamate pharmacology. The Evans-Watkins collaboration exemplifies how systematic neuropharmacological research can transform our understanding of brain function and provide therapeutic targets for neurological disorders.

---

Title: Neuropharmacologic correlates of deglutition: lessons from fictive swallowing.
Abstract: The neurochemical mechanisms underlying deglutition remain poorly understood despite the critical importance of swallowing function. Recent advances in fictive swallowing preparations have provided new opportunities to examine the neuropharmacologic basis of this complex motor behavior. We investigated the role of various neurotransmitter systems in the generation and modulation of fictive swallowing patterns using an in vitro brainstem preparation from adult cats. Fictive swallowing was induced by electrical stimulation of the superior laryngeal nerve while recording from hypoglossal and recurrent laryngeal nerve branches. Bath application of neurotransmitter agonists and antagonists was used to assess their effects on swallowing motor patterns. Serotonin (5-HT) application significantly enhanced the amplitude and duration of fictive swallowing bursts, while the 5-HT2 receptor antagonist ketanserin abolished these facilitatory effects. GABA application dose-dependently suppressed swallowing activity, with complete inhibition occurring at concentrations above 100 μM. The GABAA receptor antagonist bicuculline partially reversed GABA-induced suppression and spontaneously increased swallowing frequency. Dopamine produced biphasic effects, initially facilitating then suppressing swallowing patterns at higher concentrations. Acetylcholine consistently enhanced swallowing activity through muscarinic mechanisms, as atropine blocked these effects while nicotinic antagonists had minimal impact. These findings demonstrate that fictive swallowing is modulated by multiple neurotransmitter systems, with serotonin and acetylcholine providing excitatory drive while GABA exerts inhibitory control. Understanding these neuropharmacologic correlates may inform therapeutic approaches for dysphagia associated with neurologic disorders.

---

Title: [Neuropharmacology yesterday, today and tomorrow].
Abstract: The development of neuropharmacology as a scientific discipline has undergone remarkable transformation since the pioneering investigations of Sechenov and Pavlov. Early neurophysiological studies focused primarily on anatomical localization and basic reflex mechanisms, while contemporary neuropharmacological research emphasizes molecular mechanisms of synaptic transmission and receptor pharmacology. This analytical review examines the evolution of neuropharmacological concepts and methodologies from classical neurophysiology to modern molecular approaches. Historical analysis was conducted through systematic examination of key publications spanning seven decades, from initial studies of autonomic pharmacology to recent investigations of neurotransmitter systems. Classical experiments established fundamental principles of chemical neurotransmission, beginning with acetylcholine at neuromuscular junctions and extending to central monoaminergic pathways. Contemporary research has identified numerous neurotransmitter systems including dopamine, serotonin, GABA, and neuropeptides, elucidating their roles in behavior, cognition, and neurological disorders. Methodological advances in radioligand binding, electrophysiology, and biochemical assays have enabled precise characterization of receptor subtypes and their pharmacological profiles. Current investigations focus on G-protein coupled receptors, ion channels, and intracellular signaling cascades. Future directions will likely emphasize molecular cloning approaches, computer-assisted drug design, and therapeutic applications for neurodegenerative diseases. The integration of classical neurophysiological principles with emerging molecular techniques represents the optimal strategy for advancing neuropharmacological knowledge in service of clinical medicine and our understanding of higher nervous system function.

---

Title: [Atypical antipsychotic drugs].
Abstract: The introduction of atypical antipsychotic drugs has fundamentally altered the treatment landscape for schizophrenia and related psychotic disorders. Unlike conventional neuroleptics that primarily block dopamine D2 receptors, atypical agents demonstrate complex pharmacological profiles involving multiple neurotransmitter systems. We conducted a comprehensive review of the pharmacological mechanisms and clinical efficacy of atypical antipsychotics, focusing on clozapine, risperidone, olanzapine, and quetiapine. Clinical data from 24 controlled studies involving 3,847 patients with schizophrenia were analyzed, comparing efficacy and side effect profiles between atypical and conventional antipsychotics. Atypical agents demonstrated superior efficacy in treating negative symptoms, with 40-60% of patients showing significant improvement compared to 20-35% with haloperidol. Importantly, the incidence of extrapyramidal side effects was markedly reduced: tardive dyskinesia occurred in 0.5-5% of patients treated with atypical drugs versus 15-20% with conventional neuroleptics. The unique receptor binding profiles of atypical antipsychotics, particularly their high 5-HT2A/D2 receptor affinity ratios and interaction with cholinergic, histaminergic, and adrenergic systems, appear to underlie their improved tolerability. However, metabolic side effects including weight gain and glucose dysregulation emerged as new concerns, particularly with olanzapine and clozapine. These findings suggest that atypical antipsychotics represent a significant therapeutic advance, offering improved symptom control with reduced neurological complications, though requiring careful monitoring of metabolic parameters. Their introduction has established new standards for antipsychotic therapy in Dutch clinical practice.

---

Title: Role of GIRK channels on the noradrenergic transmission in vivo: an electrophysiological and neurochemical study on GIRK2 mutant mice.
Abstract: G protein-coupled inwardly rectifying potassium (GIRK) channels play a crucial role in neuronal excitability and neurotransmitter release regulation. While GIRK2 subunits are abundantly expressed in noradrenergic neurons, their specific contribution to norepinephrine transmission remains poorly understood. This study investigated the functional role of GIRK2 channels in noradrenergic neurotransmission using GIRK2 knockout mice and their wild-type littermates. In vivo electrophysiological recordings were performed in the locus coeruleus (LC) of anesthetized mice to assess noradrenergic neuronal firing patterns and responses to α2-adrenoceptor stimulation. Microdialysis experiments measured extracellular norepinephrine levels in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus under basal conditions and following pharmacological challenges. Additionally, high-performance liquid chromatography quantified tissue norepinephrine content in multiple brain regions. GIRK2 knockout mice exhibited significantly increased spontaneous firing rates of LC neurons (2.8 ± 0.3 Hz) compared to wild-types (1.9 ± 0.2 Hz, p<0.01). The inhibitory response to clonidine, an α2-adrenoceptor agonist, was markedly attenuated in knockout animals (32% vs 68% firing rate reduction, p<0.001). Microdialysis revealed elevated basal norepinephrine levels in both prefrontal cortex and hippocampus of GIRK2-deficient mice. Tissue norepinephrine content was reduced by 25-40% across examined brain regions in knockouts, suggesting compensatory downregulation. These findings demonstrate that GIRK2 channels are essential modulators of noradrenergic neurotransmission, critically involved in maintaining physiological firing patterns and enabling proper α2-adrenoceptor-mediated feedback inhibition in norepinephrine neurons.

Title: 3D-QSAR study of corticotropin-releasing factor 1 antagonists and pharmacophore-based drug design.
Abstract: Corticotropin-releasing factor (CRF) is a neuropeptide that falls into the broad spectrum of having neurotransmitter/neurohormonal/neuromodulator activities. The design and synthesis of low molecular weight non-peptide antagonists for the CRF receptors is a very important area of research as they can be employed in the treatment of a wide variety of disorders. To investigate the ligand-receptor binding mode and design novel CRF1 antagonists, both quantitative and qualitative 3D-QSAR analysis have been performed on a data set of CRF(1) antagonists by using HypoGen and HipHopRefine programs of Catalyst software. The training set of HypoGen study included twenty-five structurally diverse CRF(1) antagonists with Ki values ranging from 0.5 nM to 10 microM. The common feature-based 3D-QSAR study used eight highly potent CRF(1) antagonists and four poor antagonistic ligands to generate 3D-pharmacophore models with excluded volumes. The obtained 3D-pharmacophore models from each study served as queries for virtual screening with a 'focused compound library' for novel CRF(1) antagonist development. Pharmacophore models obtained for antagonist binding are useful for CRF related chemical biology and drug design. Strategies and methods employed in this paper are simple and practical for medicinal chemists in drug R&D.

---

Title: Psychopharmacology and pharmacokinetics.
Abstract: The overall physiologic changes associated with aging lead to changes in both pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic actions of many medications. This, in turn, leads to changes in the impact that a wide variety of medications have on older adults when compared to younger, healthy individuals. These pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic variations can cause either lesser or greater than expected responses to medication. Knowledge of the physiologic changes that are expected to occur in the elderly can help to identify medications that may require dose adjustments or that should be avoided, due to increased risk in this population. As a general principle, medications should be started at lower doses in the elderly with slow titration schedules to reduce the risk of adverse effects.

Title: Bibliometric analysis of biomedical publications on SSRI during 1980-2000.
Abstract: We performed a bibliometric study of the scientific publications referring to selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs). The database used was EMBASE: Psychiatry. We applied the principal bibliometric indicators: Price's and Bradford's laws on the increase or dispersion of scientific literature, Lotka's law on the productivity of authors, the participation index (PaI) of countries, the productivity index (PI) of authors, and the collaboration index. By means of manual coding, documents were classified according to type of study and to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fourth Edition (DSM-IV) or nonpsychiatric categories. We analysed 3,622 original documents published between 1980 and 2000. Our results show nonfulfilment of Price's law because production on SSRIs does not grow exponentially (r=0.937 vs. r = 0.946, after linear adjustment). The journal most employed is the Journal of Clinical Psychiatry (Bradford's first zone). The United States is the most productive country (PaI=41.50). The documents were distributed in four groups: experimental pharmacology (8.38%), tolerance and safety (34.94%), clinical efficacy (49.11%), and not specified (7.56%). The drug most studied was fluoxetine (1,745 articles), followed by paroxetine (659). The DSM-IV diagnostic categories most studied were depression (834), obsessive-compulsive disorder (171), and panic disorder (75). The control antidepressants most used in comparative clinical studies were amitriptyline (51) and imipramine (42). The results of the present study show that the SSRIs are not solely antidepressant drugs, but also have a wide range of uses both within the psychiatric sphere (especially in the field of anxiety) and outside it, which explains the considerable scientific production generated in relation to these drugs.

---

Title: CNS Targets for multi-functional drugs in the treatment of Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases.
Abstract: The progressive neurodegenerative nature of Alzheimer's disease (AD) and Parkinson's disease (PD) involves multiple pathological mechanisms including cholinergic dysfunction, oxidative stress, protein aggregation, and neuroinflammation. Current monotherapeutic approaches provide limited clinical benefits, suggesting that multi-target drug strategies may offer superior therapeutic outcomes. This study investigated the potential CNS targets for developing multi-functional compounds that could simultaneously address multiple pathogenic pathways in both diseases. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of shared molecular targets between AD and PD using computational modeling and in vitro screening approaches. Primary neuronal cultures from transgenic mouse models expressing mutant APP/presenilin-1 (AD) and α-synuclein (PD) were treated with a library of 147 small molecules designed to interact with multiple targets including acetylcholinesterase, monoamine oxidase-B, glutamate receptors, and antioxidant pathways. Cell viability, neurite outgrowth, protein aggregation, and mitochondrial function were assessed. Results identified 23 compounds showing significant neuroprotective effects in both disease models. The most promising candidates demonstrated dual acetylcholinesterase/monoamine oxidase-B inhibition coupled with antioxidant properties and reduced β-amyloid and α-synuclein aggregation. Lead compound MT-7741 showed 67% reduction in protein aggregation, 45% improvement in mitochondrial function, and enhanced neurite density compared to controls. These findings support the feasibility of multi-functional drug approaches targeting shared pathogenic mechanisms in AD and PD, potentially offering more effective therapeutic strategies than current single-target interventions for these devastating neurodegenerative disorders.

Title: Psychedelic Psychiatry's Brave New World.
Abstract: The resurgence of psychedelic research has transformed our understanding of psychiatric therapeutics, yet the underlying neural mechanisms remain poorly characterized. Here, we investigated the molecular and circuit-level effects of psilocybin, a 5-HT2A receptor agonist, in treatment-resistant depression using a combination of single-cell RNA sequencing, electrophysiology, and functional connectivity analysis in both mouse models and human subjects. Single-cell profiling revealed that psilocybin induces rapid transcriptional changes in cortical pyramidal neurons, particularly upregulating genes associated with synaptic plasticity including BDNF, Arc, and Egr1. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated increased dendritic spine formation and enhanced long-term potentiation in prefrontal cortex within 24 hours of treatment. Notably, we identified a critical period of heightened neuroplasticity lasting 2-4 weeks post-administration, characterized by elevated mTOR signaling and increased protein synthesis. In a randomized, double-blind clinical trial (n=89), patients receiving psilocybin-assisted therapy showed significant improvements in depression scores (HAM-D reduction of 18.3 ± 3.2 points) compared to placebo controls (4.1 ± 2.8 points, p<0.001). Functional MRI revealed increased connectivity between default mode network regions and decreased amygdala hyperactivity that correlated with therapeutic outcomes. These effects persisted for at least 6 months, suggesting sustained neuroplastic remodeling. Our findings reveal that psychedelics leverage endogenous plasticity mechanisms to enable rapid and durable psychiatric improvements, establishing a neurobiological framework for precision psychedelic medicine.

Title: Neuropsychopharmacologic challenge in biological psychiatry.
Abstract: Neuropsychopharmacologic challenge paradigms have emerged as valuable research tools for investigating the pathophysiology of psychiatric disorders and elucidating central neurotransmitter function in vivo. These approaches utilize selective pharmacologic agents to probe specific neurotransmitter systems while monitoring behavioral, neuroendocrine, and physiologic responses. We reviewed the current status of challenge strategies in biological psychiatry, focusing on serotonergic, dopaminergic, and noradrenergic probe compounds. Data from 127 published studies conducted between 1985-1993 were systematically analyzed to evaluate the clinical utility and methodologic considerations of major challenge paradigms. Fenfluramine challenge, measuring prolactin and cortisol responses as indices of serotonergic function, demonstrated consistent abnormalities in patients with depression, bulimia nervosa, and personality disorders characterized by impulsivity and aggression. Growth hormone responses to clonidine challenge revealed blunted noradrenergic function in depression and panic disorder. Dopaminergic challenge with apomorphine or L-DOPA showed altered growth hormone responses in schizophrenia and mood disorders. Methodologic factors significantly influencing challenge outcomes included subject selection criteria, drug dosing protocols, sampling intervals, and control for confounding variables such as age, gender, and medication status. These findings support the utility of neuropsychopharmacologic challenges as research tools for characterizing neurotransmitter dysfunction in psychiatric populations. However, standardization of protocols and careful attention to methodologic variables are essential for reliable interpretation of results. Future applications may include treatment prediction and monitoring of therapeutic interventions targeting specific neurotransmitter systems.

---

Title: Cerebral microdialysis of interleukin (IL)-1beta and IL-6: extraction efficiency and production in the acute phase after severe traumatic brain injury in rats.
Abstract: As a research tool, cerebral microdialysis might be a useful technique in monitoring the release of cytokines into the extracellular fluid (ECF) following traumatic brain injury (TBI). We established extraction efficiency of Interleukin(IL)-1ss and Interleukin(IL)-6 by an in vitro microdialysis-perfusion system, followed by in vivo determination of the temporal profile of extracellular fluid cytokines after severe TBI in rats. In vitro experiments using a polyether sulfon (PES) microdialysis probe especially developed for recovery of macromolecules such as cytokines, were carried out to establish the extraction efficiency of IL-1ss and IL-6 from artificial cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) with defined IL-1ss and IL-6 concentrations. In vivo experiments in which rats were subjected to TBI or sham and microdialysis samples were collected from the parietal lobe for measurement of cytokines. The extraction efficiency was maximal 6.05% (range, 5.97-6.13%) at 0.5 microl/min(-1) and decreased at higher flow rates. Both cytokines were detectable in the dialysates. Highest IL-1ss levels were found within 200 min, highest IL-6 concentrations were detected at later intervals (200-400 min). No differences were found between the TBI and control groups. Cerebral microdialysis allows measurement of cytokine secretion in the ECF of brain tissue in rats.

---

Title: The neurochemistry and morphology of functionally identified corneal polymodal nociceptors and cold thermoreceptors.
Abstract: It is generally believed that the unencapsulated sensory nerve terminals of modality specific C- and Aδ-neurons lack structural specialization. Here we determined the morphology of functionally defined polymodal receptors and cold thermoreceptors in the guinea pig corneal epithelium. Polymodal receptors and cold thermoreceptors were identified by extracellular recording at the surface of the corneal epithelium. After marking the recording sites, corneas were processed to reveal immunoreactivity for the transient receptor potential channels TRPV1 (transient receptor potential cation channel, subfamily V, member 1) or TPRM8 (transient receptor potential cation channel subfamily M member 8). Polymodal receptor nerve terminals (n = 6) were TRPV1-immunoreactive and derived from an axon that ascended from the sub-basal plexus to the squamous cell layer where it branched into fibers that ran parallel to the corneal surface and terminated with small bulbar endings (ramifying endings). Cold thermoreceptor nerve terminals were TRPM8-immunoreactive (n = 6) and originated from an axon that branched as it ascended through the wing cell and squamous cell layers and terminated with large bulbar endings (complex endings). These findings indicate that modality specific corneal sensory neurons with unencapsulated nerve endings have distinct nerve terminal morphologies that are likely to relate to their function.

---

Title: Epigenetic Neuropharmacology: Drugs Affecting the Epigenome in the Brain.
Abstract: This review explores how different classes of drugs, including those with therapeutic and abuse potential, alter brain functions and behavior via the epigenome. Epigenetics, in its simplest interpretation, is the study of the regulation of a genes' transcriptional potential. The epigenome is established during development but is malleable throughout life by a wide variety of drugs, with both clinical utility and abuse potential. An epigenetic effect can be central to the drug's therapeutic or abuse potential, or it can be independent from the main effect but nevertheless produce beneficial or adverse side effects. Here, I discuss the various epigenetic effects of main pharmacological drug classes, including antidepressants, antiepileptics, and drugs of abuse.

---

Title: Chronic infusion of PCP via osmotic mini-pumps: a new rodent model of cognitive deficit in schizophrenia characterized by impaired attentional set-shifting (ID/ED) performance.
Abstract: Schizophrenia is characterized by persistent cognitive deficits, particularly in executive function and attentional flexibility, which are poorly modeled by acute pharmacological interventions. The N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction hypothesis of schizophrenia suggests that chronic NMDA receptor blockade may better recapitulate the cognitive symptoms observed clinically. We developed a novel rodent model using continuous subcutaneous infusion of phencyclidine (PCP) via osmotic mini-pumps to examine sustained NMDA receptor antagonism effects on cognitive performance. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were implanted with 14-day osmotic pumps delivering either saline or PCP at doses of 2.58, 5.16, or 10.32 mg/kg/day. Following a 7-day recovery period, animals were trained on an intra-dimensional/extra-dimensional (ID/ED) attentional set-shifting task, a rodent analog of the Wisconsin Card Sorting Test sensitive to prefrontal cortical dysfunction. Chronic PCP infusion produced dose-dependent impairments in cognitive flexibility without affecting simple discrimination learning or reversal performance. Animals receiving 5.16 and 10.32 mg/kg/day PCP showed significantly increased trials to criterion and errors specifically during extra-dimensional set-shifting phases (p<0.01), while intra-dimensional shifting remained intact. These deficits emerged after 5-7 days of continuous infusion and persisted throughout the 14-day treatment period. Locomotor activity was minimally affected at the lower doses that produced cognitive impairment. This chronic infusion model provides a novel tool for investigating NMDA receptor hypofunction-mediated cognitive deficits relevant to schizophrenia and may facilitate development of procognitive therapeutic interventions targeting executive dysfunction.

---

Title: An improved approach to steady-state analysis of monoamine oxidases.
Abstract: The search for new monoamine oxidase inhibitors aims to identify potential lead compounds that are more potent and selective than current drugs for use in treating a variety of neuropsychiatric and neurodegenerative conditions. An integral part of this process is a kinetic examination of monoamine oxidases in the presence of the inhibitor, to determine potency and selectivity and to obtain information on mechanism. To date, kinetic data obtained with a probe substrate have been analysed by fitting to the Michaelis-Menten equation which describes a unireactant process in which velocity is related to substrate concentration in a rectangular hyperbolic manner. In this study, we present evidence that monoamine oxidase activity is often not adequately described by this approach. We outline a novel equation strategy that takes account of substrate and inhibitor binding to oxidised and reduced enzyme forms, and quantifies differences between substrates and inhibitors in this regard. When combined with plate reader-based experimental techniques that allow large numbers of substrate and inhibitor concentrations to be used, and the global nonlinear regression facilities of GraphPad Prism software, this straightforward approach allows more appropriate analyses of monoamine oxidase by non-experts than has previously been possible.

---

Title: Use of NAD(P)H and flavoprotein autofluorescence transients to probe neuron and astrocyte responses to synaptic activation.
Abstract: Synaptic stimulation in brain slices is accompanied by changes in tissue autofluorescence, which are a consequence of changes in tissue metabolism. Autofluorescence excited by ultraviolet light has been most extensively studied, and is due to reduced pyridine nucleotides (NADH and NADPH, collectively termed NAD(P)H). Stimulation generates a characteristic compound NAD(P)H response, comprising an initial fluorescence decrease and then an overshooting increase that slowly recovers to baseline levels. Evoked NAD(P)H transients are relatively easy to record, do not require the addition of exogenous indicators and have good signal-noise ratios. These characteristics make NAD(P)H imaging methods very useful for tracking the spread of neuronal activity in complex brain tissues, however the cellular basis of synaptically-evoked autofluorescence transients has been the subject of recent debate. Of particular importance is the question of whether signals are due primarily to changes in neuronal mitochondrial function, and/or whether astrocyte metabolism triggered by glutamate uptake may be a significant contributor to the overshooting NAD(P)H fluorescence increases. This mini-review addresses the subcellular origins of NAD(P)H autofluorescence and the evidence for mitochondrial and glycolytic contributions to compound transients. It is concluded that there is no direct evidence for a contribution to NAD(P)H signals from glycolysis in astrocytes following synaptic glutamate uptake. In contrast, multiple lines of evidence, including from complimentary flavoprotein autofluorescence signals, imply that mitochondrial NADH dynamics in neurons dominate compound evoked NAD(P)H transients. These signals are thus appropriate for studies of mitochondrial function and dysfunction in brain slices, in addition to providing robust maps of postsynaptic neuronal activation following physiological activation.

---

Title: Methodological optimization of applying neuroactive agents for the study of locomotor-like activity in the mudpuppies (Necturus maculatus).
Abstract: The mudpuppy (Necturus maculatus) represents a valuable model for studying fundamental mechanisms of vertebrate locomotion due to its simplified neural circuitry and robust motor patterns. However, standardized protocols for pharmacological manipulation of locomotor networks in this species remain limited. We developed and validated an optimized methodology for applying neuroactive compounds to induce and modulate locomotor-like activity in the isolated mudpuppy brainstem-spinal cord preparation. Adult mudpuppies (n=24, 15-25 cm) were anesthetized and decerebrated, and brainstem-spinal cord preparations were maintained in oxygenated artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 10°C. Locomotor-like rhythmic activity was monitored via extracellular recordings from ventral roots L2-L4. We systematically tested concentration-response relationships for N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA, 25-200 μM), 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT, 10-100 μM), and dopamine (DA, 50-300 μM) applied via bath perfusion. Optimal locomotor rhythm induction was achieved using NMDA (75 μM) combined with 5-HT (25 μM), generating stable alternating burst patterns with cycle periods of 2.8 ± 0.4 seconds and consistent left-right alternation (phase lag: 0.51 ± 0.03). Dopamine dose-dependently increased burst frequency without disrupting coordination. Application order significantly affected response magnitude, with 5-HT pre-treatment enhancing NMDA sensitivity by 35%. Temperature stability (±0.5°C) and gradual drug application (≤10 μM/min) were critical for reproducible results. This standardized protocol provides a reliable platform for investigating spinal locomotor networks and testing pharmacological interventions in an evolutionarily relevant vertebrate model, facilitating comparative studies of motor control mechanisms across species.

---

Title: European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) - 23rd Congress.
Abstract: The 23rd Congress of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) convened in Amsterdam, Netherlands, from August 28 to September 1, 2010, bringing together leading researchers in neuropsychopharmacology to present cutting-edge findings in psychiatric drug development and neuroscience. This comprehensive report summarizes key presentations and symposia focusing on novel therapeutic targets and investigational compounds for major psychiatric disorders. The congress featured 142 oral presentations, 756 poster presentations, and 28 satellite symposiums covering diverse areas including depression, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, anxiety disorders, and addiction medicine. Notable highlights included presentations on next-generation antidepressants targeting the glutamatergic system, including mGluR2/3 agonists and NMDA receptor modulators showing promise in treatment-resistant depression. Significant attention was devoted to novel antipsychotic agents with improved metabolic profiles, particularly partial dopamine D2 receptor agonists and dual-acting compounds modulating both dopaminergic and serotonergic pathways. Innovative approaches in addiction medicine featured presentations on phosphodiesterase inhibitors and GABA-B receptor agonists for alcohol and cocaine dependence. Emerging biomarker strategies for personalized psychiatric medicine were extensively discussed, emphasizing pharmacogenomic approaches and neuroimaging-guided treatment selection. The congress highlighted the industry's shift toward more sophisticated mechanistic approaches in psychiatric drug development, moving beyond traditional monoaminergic targets. These advances represent significant progress in addressing the substantial unmet medical needs in psychiatry, with several investigational compounds expected to enter Phase III trials within the next two years, potentially transforming treatment paradigms for millions of patients worldwide.

---

Title: Acquiring local field potential information from amperometric neurochemical recordings.
Abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) and constant potential amperometry are widely used electrochemical techniques for real-time monitoring of neurotransmitter release in behaving animals. However, these methods typically focus solely on neurochemical signals while discarding potentially valuable electrophysiological information present in the raw current recordings. Here we describe a novel signal processing approach to extract local field potential (LFP) information from amperometric neurochemical recordings using carbon fiber microelectrodes. We developed filtering algorithms to isolate low-frequency components (1-100 Hz) from high-frequency electrochemical noise in amperometric recordings obtained from the nucleus accumbens and dorsal striatum of anesthetized rats during electrical stimulation of dopaminergic pathways. Simultaneous recordings were performed using conventional tungsten microelectrodes for LFP validation. Our approach successfully recovered LFP-like signals that correlated strongly with conventional electrophysiological recordings (r = 0.78 ± 0.12, n = 8 animals). The extracted signals exhibited characteristic LFP features including evoked responses to electrical stimulation and spontaneous oscillatory activity in the theta (4-8 Hz) and gamma (30-80 Hz) frequency bands. Signal-to-noise ratio of recovered LFPs was optimal when amperometric recordings were performed at +0.7 V vs Ag/AgCl reference electrode. This method provides a cost-effective means to simultaneously monitor neurochemical and electrophysiological activity from a single electrode, potentially reducing tissue damage while increasing information yield from individual recording sites. The technique may prove particularly valuable for chronic recording applications where electrode count is limited by surgical constraints or for studying neurochemical-electrophysiological coupling in freely moving animals.

---

Title: New approaches to chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting: from neuropharmacology to clinical investigations.
Abstract: Nausea and vomiting are considered to be among the most distressing consequences of cytotoxic chemotherapies. Currently, there are several novel 5-HT(3) receptor antagonists for the treatment of chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV), including ondansetron, granisetron, and dolasetron. These agents provide significant improvement in the management of acute emesis but are ineffective at preventing delayed emesis. In 2003, a new 5-HT(3) receptor antagonist, palonosetron HCL (Aloxi), was introduced to the U.S. market. Palonosetron was found to be effective in preventing delayed CINV. Indeed, palonosetron was the first and only 5-HT(3) receptor antagonist approved by the FDA for the prevention of both acute and delayed CINV. More recently, studies on the role of substance P in the emetic process led to the development of aprepitant (Emend) for the prevention of delayed emesis in combination with 5-HT(3) receptor antagonists. Despite these major advances, CINV remains uncontrolled in some patients. Current efforts are focused on treating refractory emesis and include both the clinical evaluation of compounds marketed for other indications and the preclinical evaluation of novel molecules targeting other transmitters in the emetic pathway. Ongoing work in pharmacogenomics has postulated several candidate genes that could be involved in emetic sensitivity and responsiveness to antiemetic therapy. Investigations into the pharmacogenomics of CINV may someday be able to aid in the identification of high risk patients and patients unlikely to respond to conventional therapies.

---

Title: Targeted disruption of cocaine-activated nucleus accumbens neurons prevents context-specific sensitization.
Abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAc) plays a critical role in drug addiction, yet the specific neuronal populations mediating different aspects of cocaine-induced plasticity remain poorly defined. Context-specific sensitization, in which repeated cocaine exposure in a particular environment leads to enhanced locomotor responses upon re-exposure to that context, represents a key model of addiction-related learning. Here we used activity-dependent genetic targeting to selectively ablate cocaine-activated NAc neurons and examine their contribution to sensitization. We employed transgenic mice expressing diphtheria toxin receptor under control of the immediate early gene c-fos promoter, allowing targeted elimination of neurons activated during initial cocaine exposure. Mice received cocaine (15 mg/kg, i.p.) or saline in distinct environmental contexts for five consecutive days, followed by diphtheria toxin administration to ablate c-fos-expressing NAc neurons. After a 10-day withdrawal period, animals were challenged with cocaine in both the drug-paired and unpaired contexts. Control mice exhibited robust context-specific sensitization, displaying significantly enhanced locomotor activity when cocaine was administered in the previously drug-paired environment compared to the unpaired context (2.8-fold vs 1.4-fold increase over saline baseline, p<0.001). In contrast, mice with targeted ablation of cocaine-activated NAc neurons showed equivalent, non-sensitized responses in both contexts, with locomotor activity comparable to drug-naive animals. Importantly, acute cocaine responses remained intact, indicating preserved basic drug sensitivity. These findings demonstrate that NAc neurons activated during initial cocaine exposure form a stable cellular engram essential for context-dependent sensitization, providing direct evidence for ensemble-specific encoding of addiction-related memories.

---

Title: Antipsychotic drugs: evolving mechanisms of action with improved therapeutic benefits.
Abstract: One of the conundrums of neuropharmacology is to understand the therapeutic mechanisms of action of antipsychotic drugs. Every drug with antipsychotic activity is a dopamine (DA) D(2)-like receptor antagonist and therefore this function is critical to reducing psychotic symptoms. However, the actions of the archetypal atypical antipsychotic drug clozapine go beyond antipsychotic effects because the drug is efficacious in treating psychotic symptoms that do not respond to drugs mainly directed at antagonizing the DA D(2) receptor, has benefits in cognition and has recently been shown to reduce levels of suicide. A growing understanding of the mechanisms of clozapine and other atypical antipsychotic drugs suggests that both partial and inverse agonism, as well as receptor antagonism, at specific neurotransmitter receptors is required to give full therapeutic benefits. It is, therefore, timely to review the evolving nature of the mechanisms of action of different antipsychotic drugs.

---

Title: The presence of comorbidity in Tourette syndrome increases the need for pharmacological treatment.
Abstract: Tourette syndrome (TS) is a complex neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by motor and vocal tics, with up to 90% of patients presenting comorbid psychiatric conditions including attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), and anxiety disorders. While tic severity alone has traditionally guided pharmacological intervention decisions, the impact of comorbid conditions on treatment necessity remains poorly understood. We conducted a retrospective analysis of 247 pediatric patients (ages 6-17 years) diagnosed with TS at our tertiary care center between 2005-2008. Patients were categorized into TS-only (n=61) and TS-comorbid groups (n=186), with comprehensive assessment using the Yale Global Tic Severity Scale, Children's Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale, ADHD Rating Scale-IV, and Beck Youth Inventories. Treatment decisions and medication utilization were analyzed using logistic regression models controlling for tic severity, age, and socioeconomic status. Patients with TS plus comorbidities were significantly more likely to receive pharmacological intervention than those with TS alone (78.5% vs 41.0%, p<0.001). This association remained significant after controlling for tic severity (OR=3.24, 95% CI: 1.82-5.77, p<0.001). The presence of ADHD (OR=2.89) and OCD symptoms (OR=2.15) were the strongest predictors of medication use. Functional impairment scores were significantly higher in the comorbid group (p<0.001), correlating with increased polypharmacy requirements. These findings suggest that comorbid psychiatric conditions, rather than tic severity alone, primarily drive pharmacological treatment decisions in pediatric TS, highlighting the importance of comprehensive psychiatric evaluation in treatment planning.

---

Title: Neurosurgery for obsessive-compulsive disorder: contemporary approaches.
Abstract: Surgery for psychiatric disorders has a controversial history. Traditionally, procedures were undertaken to physically disconnect or destroy certain areas of the brain thought to constitute critical components of the limbic pathways. The relatively recent advent of the much safer and non-destructive technique known as deep brain stimulation has coincided with a resurgence in interest in psychosurgery. Contemporary approaches to the surgical management of obsessive-compulsive disorder are discussed, together with our current understanding of its pathophysiology.

Title: An improved method for analyzing coenzyme Q homologues and multiple detection of rare biological samples.
Abstract: Coenzyme Q homologues play critical roles in mitochondrial electron transport and neuroprotection, yet their analysis in rare biological samples such as cerebrospinal fluid and small brain tissue specimens remains technically challenging. Traditional HPLC methods suffer from poor sensitivity and require large sample volumes, limiting their utility in clinical neuroscience research. We developed an improved analytical method combining reversed-phase HPLC with electrochemical and UV detection systems to simultaneously quantify CoQ9, CoQ10, and their reduced forms (ubiquinols) in samples as small as 50 μL. The method utilizes a C18 column with methanol-ethanol gradient elution and post-column reduction with sodium borohydride to enhance detection sensitivity. Sample preparation involves protein precipitation with methanol followed by lipid extraction with hexane-isopropanol. We validated this approach using rat brain homogenates, human cerebrospinal fluid, and neuronal cell cultures. The detection limits were 0.5 pmol for CoQ10 and 0.8 pmol for CoQ9, representing a 10-fold improvement over conventional methods. Intra-assay and inter-assay coefficients of variation were below 8% and 12%, respectively. Recovery rates ranged from 87-94% across different sample types. The method successfully detected age-related changes in CoQ levels in mouse brain regions and demonstrated altered CoQ10/CoQ9 ratios in cerebrospinal fluid from patients with neurodegenerative disorders. This enhanced analytical approach provides researchers with a sensitive, reproducible tool for investigating coenzyme Q metabolism in limited clinical samples, facilitating studies of mitochondrial dysfunction in neurological diseases.

---

Title: Determination of the lumped constant for the alpha-methyltryptophan method of estimating the rate of serotonin synthesis.
Abstract: The lumped constant (LC) for the alpha-methyl-L-tryptophan method to convert the brain's uptake of labeled alpha-methyl-L-tryptophan into the regional rate of serotonin synthesis was estimated. The method involved independently estimating the unidirectional uptake constant of the tracer (alpha-[14C]methyl-L-tryptophan) to the tissue and the tracee (tryptophan) (with the addition of a radioactive compound) and calculating their ratio. The LC was estimated from logarithmically transformed data. Similar experiments were performed using rats treated with the drug probenecid, which blocks the efflux of 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (a metabolite of serotonin) from the brain. The experiments using probenecid, corrected for the difference in the levels of plasma free tryptophan (increased in probenecid-treated rats) relative to control experiments, gave an average LC for the rat brain of 0.46 +/- 0.14 (mean +/- SD). This value was not significantly different from the one obtained in controls (0.43 +/- 0.13). In addition, the LC was also calculated using unidirectional uptake constants in the probenecid-treated rats for alpha-methyl-L-tryptophan and L-tryptophan. This LC value was 0.39 +/- 0.10. There was no significant difference between these three LC values. Thus, an average +/- SD LC of 0.42 +/- 0.07 for 28 brain structures investigated in this study was obtained. Statistically the LC obtained in different structures had a variability that could be accounted for by errors in measurements alone. In other words, dispersion in the LC values could be fully accounted for by chance alone. Data confirmed that the LC value did not change when the rate of serotonin synthesis was increased by probenecid treatment. We also showed that the rate of 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid accumulation in probenecid-treated rats was 58 pmol g-1 min-1 (rat brain), which is about twice as much as reported by others for a normal rat. This difference could also be accounted for by the increase in the plasma level of free tryptophan in probenecid-treated rats.

Title: Demonstration and use of nanoliter sampling of in vivo rat vitreous and vitreoretinal interface.
Abstract: An understanding of the chemical microenvironments at different locations on the retina can provide unique insights into retinal neurochemistry and pathology. The anatomical shape and the small volumes available from a spatially restricted volume greatly complicate these types of measurements. The aim of this study was to demonstrate an in vivo sampling system to probe different regions of the rat retina. A low-flow push-pull perfusion probe was developed with concentric fused-silica capillaries. It was designed to fit through a 29-gauge needle for placement in the vitreous and at the vitreoretinal interface of the rat eye. Physiological saline was perfused and withdrawn through outer and inner capillaries, respectively, at flow rates between 10 and 50 nl/min. Samples of 500 nl were collected for amino acid analysis by capillary electrophoresis. Perfusion of a potent and selective inhibitor of the excitatory amino acid transporters was performed through the probe with the tip located 1-2 mm away from the optic nerve head. Ten amino acids were quantified from the perfusates of vitreous and the vitreoretinal interface. Sampling through time showed the use of this system to monitor retinal changes in these amino acids. The infusion of a transport protein antagonist shows a statistically significant increase in the glutamate concentration in collected samples when the probe tip is placed peripheral to but not over the optic nerve head. We demonstrate a new method for following neurochemical changes at the retina with spatial resolution. This in vivo method is widely applicable to the site-specific study of states of normal and dysfunctional retinal neurochemistry.

---

Title: Microdialysis applications in neuroscience.
Abstract: Microdialysis has emerged as a powerful in vivo sampling technique that enables real-time monitoring of neurotransmitters, metabolites, and drug concentrations in discrete brain regions of awake, freely moving animals. This review examines recent applications of cerebral microdialysis in neuroscience research, highlighting methodological advances and novel experimental paradigms developed over the past decade. We analyzed 127 peer-reviewed studies published between 2000-2007 that employed microdialysis to investigate neurotransmitter systems, drug pharmacokinetics, and pathophysiological mechanisms. Major applications included monitoring dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, and glutamate release in response to pharmacological challenges, behavioral paradigms, and disease models. Technological improvements have enhanced temporal resolution to sub-minute sampling intervals and reduced probe size to minimize tissue damage. Novel probe designs incorporating biosensors and online analytical systems have enabled simultaneous measurement of multiple analytes with improved sensitivity. Microdialysis studies have provided critical insights into addiction mechanisms, demonstrating drug-induced alterations in mesolimbic dopamine signaling, and have elucidated neurotransmitter changes in models of Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and stroke. Recent applications in clinical settings show promise for monitoring brain chemistry in neurosurgical patients. Despite limitations including delayed equilibration and potential tissue trauma, microdialysis remains an invaluable tool for investigating dynamic neurochemical processes. Future developments in probe miniaturization, analytical sensitivity, and wireless telemetry systems will likely expand applications in translational neuroscience research and clinical neuropharmacology.

---

Title: Isolation of neuronal chromatin from brain tissue.
Abstract: DNA-protein interactions in mature brain are increasingly recognized as key regulators for behavioral plasticity and neuronal dysfunction in chronic neuropsychiatric disease. However, chromatin assays typically lack single cell resolution, and therefore little is known about chromatin regulation of differentiated neuronal nuclei that reside in brain parenchyma intermingled with various types of non-neuronal cells. Here, we describe a protocol to selectively tag neuronal nuclei from adult brain - either by (anti-NeuN) immunolabeling or transgene-derived histone H2B-GFP fusion protein - for subsequent fluorescence-activated sorting and chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP). To illustrate an example, we compared histone H3 lysine 4 and 9 methylation marks at select gene promoters in neuronal, non-neuronal and unsorted chromatin from mouse forebrain and human cerebral cortex, and provide evidence for neuron-specific histone methylation signatures. With the modifications detailed in this protocol, the method can be used to collect nuclei from specific subtypes of neurons from any brain region for subsequent ChIP with native/un-fixed or crosslinked chromatin preparations. Starting with the harvest of brain tissue, ChIP-ready neuronal nuclei can be obtained within one day.
