Title: [Neurobiochemistry of experimental lesions of the basal nucleus of Meynert].
Abstract: Lesions of the nucleus basalis magnocellularis (of Meynert) in rats induce severe cognitive impairments, in both mnesic and attentional functions. During the last 10 years, many studies tended to establish some relationships between the behavioral and biochemical effects of such lesions. Recent data show that the activity of some peptidergic and glutamaterigic neurons in target areas is modified by cholinergic deafferentation. A new physiological interpretation may be proposed for the associated cognitive deficits.

---

Title: The psychopharmacology of violence: making sensible decisions.
Abstract: Violence represents a complex behavioral phenotype with significant public health implications, yet evidence-based psychopharmacological interventions remain limited and controversial. Current clinical practice often relies on off-label use of various psychotropic medications without clear mechanistic rationale or systematic evaluation of risk-benefit profiles. This review synthesizes existing literature on pharmacological approaches to violence reduction across psychiatric populations and examines neurobiological substrates underlying aggressive behavior to inform clinical decision-making. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of randomized controlled trials and observational studies published between 1990-2013 examining psychopharmacological interventions for violence and aggression. Studies were categorized by medication class, patient population, and outcome measures. Additionally, we reviewed neuroimaging and preclinical studies elucidating neurochemical pathways involved in aggressive behavior, particularly focusing on serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems. Evidence supports modest efficacy for several medication classes in reducing aggressive behavior, including atypical antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, though effect sizes remain small to moderate. Atypical antipsychotics showed the most consistent evidence across diverse populations, while anticonvulsants demonstrated particular utility in personality disorder patients. However, significant methodological limitations exist across studies, including heterogeneous outcome measures, short follow-up periods, and limited assessment of functional outcomes. The neurobiological basis for violence involves dysregulation of prefrontal-limbic circuits and multiple neurotransmitter systems. Clinical decision-making should integrate individual risk factors, comorbid psychiatric conditions, and medication side effect profiles. Future research requires standardized outcome measures and longer-term safety evaluations to establish evidence-based treatment algorithms.

---

Title: The Psychopharmacology of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Preclinical Roadmap.
Abstract: This review evaluates current knowledge about obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), with the goal of providing a roadmap for future directions in research on the psychopharmacology of the disorder. It first addresses issues in the description and diagnosis of OCD, including the structure, measurement, and appropriate description of the disorder and issues of differential diagnosis. Current pharmacotherapies for OCD are then reviewed, including monotherapy with serotonin reuptake inhibitors and augmentation with antipsychotic medication and with psychologic treatment. Neuromodulatory therapies for OCD are also described, including psychosurgery, deep brain stimulation, and noninvasive brain stimulation. Psychotherapies for OCD are then reviewed, focusing on behavior therapy, including exposure and response prevention and cognitive therapy, and the efficacy of these interventions is discussed, touching on issues such as the timing of sessions, the adjunctive role of pharmacotherapy, and the underlying mechanisms. Next, current research on the neurobiology of OCD is examined, including work probing the role of various neurotransmitters and other endogenous processes and etiology as clues to the neurobiological fault that may underlie OCD. A new perspective on preclinical research is advanced, using the Research Domain Criteria to propose an adaptationist viewpoint that regards OCD as the dysfunction of a normal motivational system. A systems-design approach introduces the security motivation system (SMS) theory of OCD as a framework for research. Finally, a new perspective on psychopharmacological research for OCD is advanced, exploring three approaches: boosting infrastructure facilities of the brain, facilitating psychotherapeutic relearning, and targeting specific pathways of the SMS network to fix deficient SMS shut-down processes. SIGNIFICANCE STATEMENT: A significant proportion of patients with obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) do not achieve remission with current treatments, indicating the need for innovations in psychopharmacology for the disorder. OCD may be conceptualized as the dysfunction of a normal, special motivation system that evolved to manage the prospect of potential danger. This perspective, together with a wide-ranging review of the literature, suggests novel directions for psychopharmacological research, including boosting support systems of the brain, facilitating relearning that occurs in psychotherapy, and targeting specific pathways in the brain that provide deficient stopping processes in OCD.

---

Title: Psychotropic Medication Prescribing in Primary Care: Pediatric Chief Resident Perspectives.
Abstract: Evidence-based treatments for mental health concerns include psychopharmacological and psychosocial approaches. Pediatrics organizations indicate psychopharmacology needs to be a component of training. This study investigated the status of training in psychotropic medication prescribing through a national survey of pediatric chief residents. Pediatric chief residents (one per residency program) completed a survey (response rate = 60.2%, 127/211) about their attitudes, knowledge, comfort, practice, and training around prescribing psychotropic medication in primary care. Quantitative data included descriptive statistics and correlational analyses to explore relationships between variables; qualitative data were examined through classical content analysis. Almost half of respondents reported prescribing sometimes or often. Frequency of prescribing varied by mental health condition, with highest frequency for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder. About two-thirds of respondents reported having at least some/average knowledge. About half of respondents reported being uncomfortable with prescribing; respondents were most uncomfortable with prescribing antianxiety, mood stabilizing, and antipsychotic medications, and with discontinuing medication; about half indicated their competence in progress-monitoring needed improvement. Concurrent psychosocial treatment was perceived as very useful, although often inaccessible to patients. Prescribing frequency was related to knowledge, comfort, progress-monitoring competence, and training quality; training quality was related to knowledge and comfort. Over 60% rated their training as not at all or only somewhat adequate. Pediatric chief residents reported having knowledge of psychotropic medication issues, but experience a general discomfort, especially with discontinuing medication, and with medications other than stimulants. Most thought their training needed improvement in terms of comprehensiveness and clinical practice experiences.

---

Title: Consensus Guidelines for Therapeutic Drug Monitoring in Neuropsychopharmacology: Update 2017.
Abstract: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is the quantification and interpretation of drug concentrations in blood to optimize pharmacotherapy. It considers the interindividual variability of pharmacokinetics and thus enables personalized pharmacotherapy. In psychiatry and neurology, patient populations that may particularly benefit from TDM are children and adolescents, pregnant women, elderly patients, individuals with intellectual disabilities, patients with substance abuse disorders, forensic psychiatric patients or patients with known or suspected pharmacokinetic abnormalities. Non-response at therapeutic doses, uncertain drug adherence, suboptimal tolerability, or pharmacokinetic drug-drug interactions are typical indications for TDM. However, the potential benefits of TDM to optimize pharmacotherapy can only be obtained if the method is adequately integrated in the clinical treatment process. To supply treating physicians and laboratories with valid information on TDM, the TDM task force of the Arbeitsgemeinschaft für Neuropsychopharmakologie und Pharmakopsychiatrie (AGNP) issued their first guidelines for TDM in psychiatry in 2004. After an update in 2011, it was time for the next update. Following the new guidelines holds the potential to improve neuropsychopharmacotherapy, accelerate the recovery of many patients, and reduce health care costs.

---

Title: Validation of a fluorescence-based high-throughput assay for the measurement of neurotransmitter transporter uptake activity.
Abstract: Pre-synaptic dopamine, norepinephrine and serotonin transporters (DAT, NET and SERT) terminate synaptic catecholamine transmission through reuptake of released neurotransmitter. Common approaches for studying these transporters involve radiolabeled substrates or inhibitors which, however, have several limitations. In this study we have used a novel neurotransmitter transporter uptake assay kit. The assay employs a fluorescent substrate that mimics the biogenic amine neurotransmitters and is taken up by the cell through the specific transporters, resulting in increased fluorescence intensity. In order to validate the assay, a variety of reference and proprietary neurotransmitter transporter ligands from a number of chemical and pharmacological classes were tested. The ability of these compounds to inhibit the selective transporter-mediated uptake demonstrated a similar rank order of potency and IC(50) values close to those obtained in radiolabeled neurotransmitter uptake assays. The described assay enables monitoring of dynamic transport activity of DAT, NET and SERT and is amenable for high-throughput screening and compound characterization.

---

Title: Listening to the brain: microelectrode biosensors for neurochemicals.
Abstract: Real-time monitoring of neurochemical dynamics in the living brain remains a critical challenge in neuroscience research. Traditional analytical techniques lack the spatial and temporal resolution necessary to capture rapid neurotransmitter fluctuations at synaptic and circuit levels. Here we review recent advances in microelectrode biosensor technology that enable direct, continuous measurement of neurochemicals in vivo with unprecedented precision. Carbon fiber microelectrodes modified with selective enzymes or aptamers have emerged as powerful tools for detecting dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, and glutamate with sub-second temporal resolution and nanomolar sensitivity. Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry coupled with these biosensors allows discrimination between structurally similar compounds while minimizing tissue damage. We examined validation studies demonstrating sensor selectivity, stability, and biocompatibility across multiple species and brain regions. Recent applications include real-time monitoring of dopamine release in reward circuits during behavioral tasks, tracking glutamate dynamics during synaptic plasticity, and measuring neurotransmitter perturbations in disease models. Emerging multiplexed sensor arrays enable simultaneous detection of multiple analytes, providing unprecedented insight into neurochemical network interactions. However, challenges remain in sensor fouling, drift compensation, and standardization across laboratories. Despite these limitations, microelectrode biosensors represent a transformative technology for understanding brain chemistry in health and disease. Future developments integrating wireless telemetry and advanced nanomaterials promise to further revolutionize our ability to decode the brain's chemical language in real-time.

---

Title: Psychopharmacology in autism.
Abstract: Autism is a neurobiological disorder. The core clinical features of autism include impairment in social interaction, impairments in verbal and nonverbal communication, and restricted, repetitive, and stereotyped patterns of behavior, interests, and activities. Autism often has coexisting neuropsychiatric disorders, including seizure disorders, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, affective disorders, anxiety disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and Tourette disorder. No etiology-based treatment modality has been developed to cure individuals with autism. However, comprehensive intervention, including parental counseling, behavior modification, special education in a highly structured environment, sensory integration training, speech therapy, social skill training, and medication, has demonstrated significant treatment effects in many individuals with autism. Findings from preliminary studies of major neurotransmitters and other neurochemical agents strongly suggest that neurochemical factors play a major role in autism. The findings also provide the rationale for psychopharmacotherapy in individuals with autism. This article reviews studies of neurochemical systems and related psychopharmacological research in autism and related neuropsychiatric disorders. Clinical indications for pharmacotherapy are described, and uses of various medications are suggested. This article also discusses new avenues of investigation that may lead to the development of more effective medication treatments in persons with autism.

---

Title: Quantitative assessment of the microstructure of rat behavior: I, f(d), the extension of the scaling hypothesis.
Abstract: Previous studies demonstrated that drug effects on the movement sequences of rats in unconditioned motor activity paradigms can be quantified by scaling measures that describe the average relationship between a variable of interest and an experimental parameter. However, rats engage in a wide variety of geometrically distinct movements that can be influenced differentially by drugs. In this investigation, the extended scaling approach is presented to capture quantitatively the relative contributions of geometrically distinct movement sequences to the overall path structure. The calculation of the spectrum of local spatial scaling exponents, f(d), is based on ensemble methods used in statistical physics. Results of the f(d) analysis confirm that the amount of motor activity is not correlated with the geometrical structure of movement sequences. Changes in the average spatial scaling exponent, d, correspond to shifting the entire f(d) function, and indicate overall changes in path structure. With the extended scaling approach, straight movement sequences are assessed independently from highly circumscribed movements. Thus, the f(d) function identifies drug effects on particular ranges of movement sequences as defined by the geometrical structure of movements. More generally, the f(d) function quantifies the relationship between microscopically recorded variables, in this paradigm consecutive (x,y) locations, and the macroscopic behavioral patterns that constitute the animal's response topography.

---

Title: Brain Serotonergic and Noradrenergic Deficiencies in Behavioral Variant Frontotemporal Dementia Compared to Early-Onset Alzheimer's Disease.
Abstract: Routinely prescribed psychoactive drugs in behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia (FTD) for improvement of (non)cognitive symptoms are primarily based on monoamine replacement or augmentation strategies. These were, however, initially intended to symptomatically treat other degenerative, behavioral, or personality disorders, and thus lack disease specificity. Moreover, current knowledge on brain monoaminergic neurotransmitter deficiencies in this presenile disorder is scarce, particularly with reference to changes in Alzheimer's disease (AD). The latter hence favors neurochemical comparison studies in order to elucidate the monoaminergic underpinnings of FTD compared to early-onset AD, which may contribute to better pharmacotherapy. Therefore, frozen brain samples, i.e., Brodmann area (BA) 6/8/9/10/11/12/22/24/46, amygdala, and hippocampus, of 10 neuropathologically confirmed FTD, AD, and control subjects were analyzed by means of reversed-phase high-performance liquid chromatography. Levels of serotonergic, dopaminergic, and noradrenergic compounds were measured. In nine brain areas, serotonin (5-HT) concentrations were significantly increased in FTD compared to AD patients, while 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid/5-HT ratios were decreased in eight regions, also compared to controls. Furthermore, in all regions, noradrenaline (NA) levels were significantly higher, and 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycol/NA ratios were significantly lower in FTD than in AD and controls. Contrarily, significantly higher dopamine (DA) levels and reduced homovanillic acid/DA ratios were only found in BA12 and BA46. Results indicate that FTD is defined by distinct serotonergic and noradrenergic deficiencies. Additional research regarding the interactions between both monoaminergic networks is required. Similarly, clinical trials investigating the effects of 5-HT1A receptor antagonists or NA-modulating agents, such as α1/2/β1-blockers, seem to have a rationale and should be considered.

---

Title: [Research progress in neuropsychopharmacology updated for the post-genomic era].
Abstract: The completion of the Human Genome Project in 2003 has fundamentally transformed neuropsychopharmacology research, shifting the paradigm from traditional phenotype-driven approaches to genotype-based drug discovery and personalized medicine strategies. This review examines the current state of neuropsychopharmacological research in the post-genomic era, analyzing developments from 2004 to 2009. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of major neuropsychopharmacology publications, focusing on genomics-based drug discovery, pharmacogenomics applications, and novel therapeutic targets identified through genomic approaches. Database searches included PubMed, Web of Science, and Japanese medical databases, encompassing studies on major psychiatric disorders including schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depression, and anxiety disorders. Our analysis reveals significant progress in several key areas: identification of over 150 novel CNS drug targets through genome-wide association studies (GWAS), development of pharmacogenomic testing for antidepressant and antipsychotic medications, and emergence of epigenetic-based therapeutic strategies. Notably, cytochrome P450 genotyping has become clinically relevant for personalizing psychotropic drug dosing, while GWAS have identified new susceptibility loci for psychiatric disorders. However, the translation of genomic discoveries into approved therapeutics remains limited, with only three genomics-derived neuropsychiatric drugs reaching clinical trials by 2009. The post-genomic era has revolutionized our understanding of psychiatric disorders' molecular basis, yet significant challenges remain in bridging genomic knowledge to clinical applications. Future research must focus on functional validation of genomic targets and development of more sophisticated personalized treatment algorithms to fully realize the potential of genomics-based neuropsychopharmacology.

---

Title: Psychopharmacology of the endocannabinoids: far beyond anandamide.
Abstract: The endocannabinoid system has emerged as a critical neuromodulatory network, yet research has predominantly focused on anandamide (AEA) while neglecting other bioactive lipid mediators. This review synthesizes current understanding of the broader endocannabinoid pharmacology beyond classical eCB signaling. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent literature examining 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG), palmitoylethanolamide (PEA), oleoylethanolamide (OEA), and N-arachidonoyldopamine (NADA) across preclinical models of neuropsychiatric disorders. Unlike AEA's predominant CB1 receptor activity, 2-AG demonstrates distinct biosynthetic pathways via diacylglycerol lipase and enhanced synaptic plasticity modulation. PEA and OEA, while lacking direct cannabinoid receptor affinity, exhibit potent anti-inflammatory and feeding regulatory properties through peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-α (PPAR-α) and GPR119 mechanisms. NADA uniquely activates both cannabinoid and vanilloid receptor systems, suggesting novel therapeutic targets. Pharmacokinetic analyses reveal differential enzymatic degradation patterns, with fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) and monoacylglycerol lipase (MAGL) displaying substrate selectivity that impacts tissue-specific endocannabinoid tone. Behavioral studies demonstrate that selective modulation of non-AEA pathways produces distinct anxiolytic, analgesic, and cognitive effects, often with improved therapeutic windows compared to direct CB1 agonism. These findings highlight the endocannabinoid system's remarkable pharmacological diversity and suggest that therapeutic interventions targeting specific eCB metabolic pathways may offer superior clinical outcomes with reduced psychoactive liability compared to traditional cannabinoid approaches.

---

Title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: I. Cellular, electrophysiological, and neurochemical profile in comparison with ropinirole.
Abstract: Dopamine D3 receptors represent promising therapeutic targets for neurological disorders, yet selective pharmacological tools remain limited. This study characterized S32504, a novel naphtoxazine derivative, as a potential D3/D2 receptor agonist and compared its profile to the established antiparkinsonian agent ropinirole. Binding affinity was determined using radioligand displacement assays in CHO cells expressing human D2 and D3 receptors. Functional activity was assessed via cAMP inhibition assays and patch-clamp electrophysiology in primary rat striatal neurons. In vivo neurochemical effects were evaluated using microdialysis in freely moving rats. S32504 displayed high affinity for D3 receptors (Ki = 0.8 nM) with 15-fold selectivity over D2 receptors, compared to ropinirole which showed 3-fold D3 selectivity. In cAMP assays, S32504 acted as a full agonist at D3 receptors (EC50 = 2.1 nM) and partial agonist at D2 receptors. Electrophysiological recordings revealed that S32504 (0.1-10 μM) dose-dependently reduced neuronal firing rates and inhibited spontaneous excitatory postsynaptic currents, effects blocked by the D2/D3 antagonist sulpiride. Microdialysis studies demonstrated that S32504 (0.1-1.0 mg/kg s.c.) decreased extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens more potently than ropinirole, consistent with preferential D3 autoreceptor activation. These findings establish S32504 as a novel D3-preferring agonist with distinct pharmacological properties compared to ropinirole, suggesting potential therapeutic applications in conditions associated with dopaminergic dysfunction where enhanced D3 receptor selectivity may provide clinical advantages.

---

Title: Inside-out neuropharmacology of nicotinic drugs.
Abstract: Upregulation of neuronal nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (AChRs) is a venerable result of chronic exposure to nicotine; but it is one of several consequences of pharmacological chaperoning by nicotine and by some other nicotinic ligands, especially agonists. Nicotinic ligands permeate through cell membranes, bind to immature AChR oligomers, elicit incompletely understood conformational reorganizations, increase the interaction between adjacent AChR subunits, and enhance the maturation process toward stable AChR pentamers. These changes and stabilizations in turn lead to increases in both anterograde and retrograde traffic within the early secretory pathway. In addition to the eventual upregulation of AChRs at the plasma membrane, other effects of pharmacological chaperoning include modifications to endoplasmic reticulum stress and to the unfolded protein response. Because these processes depend on pharmacological chaperoning within intracellular organelles, we group them as "inside-out pharmacology". This term contrasts with the better-known, acute, "outside-in" effects of activating and desensitizing plasma membrane AChRs. We review current knowledge concerning the mechanisms and consequences of inside-out pharmacology. This article is part of the Special Issue entitled 'The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor: From Molecular Biology to Cognition'.

---

Title: The physiology and neurochemistry of self-injurious behavior: a nonhuman primate model.
Abstract: Self-injurious behavior (SIB) is a serious behavioral condition that afflicts millions of individuals in the United States alone. The underlying factors contributing to the development of self-injury in people are poorly understood, and existing treatment strategies for this condition are limited. A low but persistent percentage of socially reared individually housed rhesus monkeys also spontaneously develop SIB. Data obtained from colony records suggest that the risk of developing SIB in socially reared rhesus monkeys is heightened by adverse early experience and subsequent stress exposure. The present review summarizes the physiological and neurochemical findings obtained in this nonhuman primate model of SIB, focusing on monoamine neurotransmitters, neuropeptides, and neuroendocrine systems. The results indicate that monkeys with SIB exhibit long-lasting disturbances in central and peripheral opioid and stress response systems, which lead to increased levels of anxiety. Based on these findings, we propose an integrated developmental-neurochemical hypothesis in which SIB arises from adverse life events in a subset of vulnerable monkeys, is maintained by a persisting dysregulation of several neurochemical and physiological systems, and functions to periodically reduce anxiety when the levels of anxiety become excessive. Implications of this hypothesis for understanding self-injury in patients with borderline personality disorder and members of the general population are discussed.

---

Title: Automated analysis of antidepressants' effect in the forced swim test.
Abstract: The forced swim test (FST) is a commonly used procedure of preclinical screening of drugs for the antidepressant activity. It has high predictive validity for a large group of antidepressant drugs blocking serotonin and noradrenaline reuptakes and improvement of immobility time evaluation in the FST is an important problem of preclinical psychopharmacology. Here a new automated version of the FST was developed. This version includes 4 inventions: (1) transmitted lighting instead of reflected lighting, (2) mouse silhouette tracking, (3) automated choice of immobility threshold and (4) the permutation test of drug's effect. Experiment was carried out on adult males of C57BL/6J and BALB/cJ mouse strains. The mice were treated with tricyclic antidepressant imipramine (15 and 30 mg/kg, i.p.). Mouse was placed in water tank, its movements were recorded by a rater and the silhouette alterations were automatically tracked. The sequence of silhouette alterations was scanned for immobility bouts with a threshold algorithm. Threshold was gradually altered and the value which maximized the difference between control and treated groups was chosen. The immobility values obtained with the procedure were compared with the permutation test. The data obtained with this procedure did not differ from those obtained by the rater. Imipramine dose dependently attenuated immobility time in C57BL/6 mice without any effect on BALB/c mice. The new procedure has been implemented in the EthoStudio software. It provides an objective automated evaluation of immobility time in the FST.

---

Title: Measurement of kinetically resolved vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux using rotating disk electrode voltammetry.
Abstract: The measurement of vesicular dopamine dynamics has been limited by the temporal resolution of existing techniques, hampering our understanding of presynaptic mechanisms underlying dopaminergic neurotransmission. We developed a novel approach using rotating disk electrode voltammetry (RDEV) to achieve kinetically resolved measurements of vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux in isolated synaptic vesicle preparations from rat striatum. Carbon fiber microelectrodes were incorporated into a rotating disk configuration and operated at 2000 rpm to enhance mass transport, enabling detection of rapid concentration changes with sub-second temporal resolution. Vesicular preparations were incubated with varying dopamine concentrations (1-100 μM) in the presence and absence of reserpine (10 μM) or bafilomycin A1 (100 nM) to pharmacologically characterize vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) activity. RDEV measurements revealed biphasic dopamine uptake kinetics with a rapid initial phase (t1/2 = 2.3 ± 0.4 s) followed by a slower equilibration phase (t1/2 = 18.7 ± 3.2 s), consistent with VMAT2-mediated transport. Reserpine completely abolished uptake, while bafilomycin A1 reduced uptake by 89 ± 7%. Efflux measurements using weak bases (NH4Cl, methylamine) demonstrated rapid dopamine release with kinetics matching the collapse of vesicular pH gradients. The technique achieved detection limits of 50 nM dopamine with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 20:1. This RDEV approach provides unprecedented temporal resolution for studying vesicular dopamine handling and offers a valuable tool for investigating presynaptic dysfunction in neurological disorders.

Title: Advances in paediatric neuropsychopharmacology: an overview.
Abstract: This Special Section of Int J Neuropsychopharmacol highlights current progress in paediatric neuropsychopharmacology. Combining critical reviews and, in some cases, new data, specific topics include: biological findings in major depression, sleep dysregulation in depressed youth, cardiovascular and ventilatory dysregulation in panic disorder, paediatric autoimmune neuropsychiatric disorder associated with strep (PANDAS), age of onset as a subtype marker in tic and obsessive--compulsive disorders (OCD), functional and pharmaconeuroanatomy of OCD and the behavioural pharmacokinetics of methylphenidate. In this introductory section, these articles are placed in the context of the state-of-the field and, more specifically, within the framework of recent NIMH initiatives in paediatric neuropsychopharmacology.

Title: The stanley neuropathology consortium integrative database: a novel, web-based tool for exploring neuropathological markers in psychiatric disorders and the biological processes associated with abnormalities of those markers.
Abstract: An integrative database, Stanley Neuropathology Consortium Integrative Database (SNCID) (http://sncid.stanleyresearch.org), has been developed to facilitate psychiatric research. The SNCID includes 1749 neuropathological markers measured in 12 different brain regions in 60 human subjects (15 each schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, depression, and unaffected controls). Genome-wide expression microarray datasets from three independent studies are also included. Statistical analysis tools such as variance analysis, correlation analysis, and functional annotation tools have been integrated into the database. In this report, we first replicate an earlier correlation analysis between genome-wide expression profiles and an abnormal cytoarchitectural marker using the SNCID. We then show the potential for identifying neuropathological markers that are abnormal in subjects with psychiatric disorders. We also identify biological pathways associated with several abnormal neuropathological markers, including those in the dopamine, glutamate, Reelin, and gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA)ergic systems. Data exploration using the SNCID may provide insights into the biological pathways associated with the neurotransmitter abnormalities identified in subjects with major psychiatric disorders.

Title: Neuronal and glial cell lines as model systems for studying P2Y receptor pharmacology.
Abstract: P2Y receptors are G protein-coupled receptors activated by extracellular nucleotides that play crucial roles in neurotransmission, glial cell activation, and neuroinflammation. While primary cell cultures have been traditionally used to study P2Y receptor function, immortalized cell lines offer advantages including reproducibility, genetic homogeneity, and ease of manipulation. We systematically characterized P2Y receptor expression and pharmacological profiles in five commonly used neuronal and glial cell lines: PC12, SH-SY5Y, U87-MG, C6, and 1321N1 astrocytoma cells. RT-PCR analysis revealed differential expression patterns, with PC12 cells predominantly expressing P2Y₁, P2Y₂, and P2Y₁₂ receptors, while U87-MG glioblastoma cells showed high levels of P2Y₂, P2Y₆, and P2Y₁₂. Functional responses were assessed using intracellular calcium mobilization assays and cAMP measurements following stimulation with selective agonists including ADP, UTP, UDP, and 2-MeSADP. SH-SY5Y neuroblastoma cells demonstrated robust calcium responses to ADP (EC₅₀ = 1.2 ± 0.3 μM) and UTP (EC₅₀ = 0.8 ± 0.2 μM), while C6 glioma cells showed preferential sensitivity to UDP stimulation. Pharmacological characterization using selective antagonists confirmed functional receptor subtypes, with suramin effectively blocking P2Y₂-mediated responses and MRS2179 specifically inhibiting P2Y₁ activation. These findings establish a comprehensive pharmacological profile of P2Y receptors in commonly used cell line models, providing valuable tools for investigating purinergic signaling mechanisms in neurodegenerative diseases, glial activation, and drug discovery applications.

Title: Commentary: physician-scientist's frustrations fester.
Abstract: A growing problem of major proportions had been confronting biomedical scientists for many decades. Until solved, this long-neglected problem, the abject failure of the American health care system, presents a gigantic obstacle to the application of the discoveries flowing from neuropsychopharmacological research into deliverable medications utilized by medical practitioners. Although it is recognized that such advances could benefit all of society, both in the United States and globally, progress toward this important goal has not happened. As I noted 5 years ago, 'Unless steps are taken soon to undertake a comprehensive restoration of our system, the profound advances in bio-medical research so rapidly accruing today may never be effectively transformed into meaningful advances in health care for society.' I remain perplexed and frustrated by the reluctance of scientific research societies such as our ACNP to engage their energies and intellect into this most serious issue.

Title: Updating neuropathology and neuropharmacology of monoaminergic systems.
Abstract: This article is part of a themed section on Updating Neuropathology and Neuropharmacology of Monoaminergic Systems. To view the other articles in this section visit http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bph.v173.13/issuetoc.

Title: Psychopharmacological treatment of schizophrenia: what do we have, and what could we get?
Abstract: Antipsychotic drugs for the treatment of schizophrenia arrived in the clinic in the fifties of the previous century and have since been the most effective treatment for patients with this devastating disorder. In spite of the more than half a century of clinical experience, and the introduction of a large number of chemical divers antipsychotic drugs, several recent large, multi-center studies have shown that, although novel (second generation) antipsychotics seem to be tolerated somewhat better (especially in relation to neurological side effects), their therapeutic potential is comparable to that of first generation antipsychotics. Hence there is still an urgent need for better pharmacological tools to treat schizophrenic patients. The current paper reviews the benefits and shortcomings of the currently available drugs, and gives an outlook towards the drugs and targets that are currently being pursued in clinical trials. Given the uncertainty of the drug discovery process and the relatively poor predictive validity of the currently available animal models, it is, at present, impossible to predict which of these drugs will ultimately become available for treating schizophrenic patients.

Title: The Effects of Chronic Amitriptyline on Zebrafish Behavior and Monoamine Neurochemistry.
Abstract: Amitriptyline is a commonly used tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) inhibiting serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake. The exact CNS action of TCAs remains poorly understood, necessitating new screening approaches and novel model organisms. Zebrafish (Danio rerio) are rapidly emerging as a promising tool for pharmacological research of antidepressants, including amitriptyline. Here, we examine the effects of chronic 2-week exposure to 10 and 50 μg/L amitriptyline on zebrafish behavior and monoamine neurotransmitters. Overall, the drug at 50 μg/L evoked pronounced anxiolytic-like effects in the novel tank test (assessed by more time in top, fewer transition and shorter latency to enter the top). Like other TCAs, amitriptyline reduced serotonin turnover, but also significantly elevated whole-brain norepinephrine and dopamine levels. The latter effect was not reported in this model previously, and accompanied higher brain expression of tyrosine hydroxylase (a rate-limiting enzyme of catecholamine biosynthesis), but unaltered expression of dopamine-β-hydroxylase and monoamine oxidase (the enzymes of dopamine metabolism). This response may underlie chronic amitriptyline action on dopamine and norepinephrine neurotransmission, and contribute to the complex CNS profile of this drug observed both clinically and in animal models. Collectively, these findings also confirm the important role of monoamine modulation in the regulation of anxiety-related behavior in zebrafish, and support the utility of this organism as a promising in-vivo model for CNS drug screening.

Title: Laboratory of receptor pharmacology.
Abstract: G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) represent the largest class of membrane proteins targeted by therapeutic agents, yet the molecular mechanisms underlying ligand-receptor interactions and downstream signaling remain incompletely understood. We investigated the pharmacological properties and signaling pathways of dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors using a combination of radioligand binding assays, functional cAMP measurements, and calcium mobilization studies in HEK293 cells stably transfected with human receptor constructs. Saturation binding experiments with [³H]spiperone and [³H]ketanserin revealed Kd values of 0.89 ± 0.12 nM and 2.3 ± 0.4 nM for D2 and 5-HT2A receptors, respectively. Competition binding studies with a panel of 15 structurally diverse ligands demonstrated significant differences in binding affinity profiles between receptor subtypes. Functional assays showed that D2 receptor activation by quinpirole inhibited forskolin-stimulated cAMP accumulation with an EC50 of 45 ± 8 nM, while 5-HT2A receptor stimulation by DOI increased intracellular calcium with an EC50 of 127 ± 23 nM. Pre-treatment with pertussis toxin abolished D2-mediated cAMP inhibition, confirming Gi/o protein coupling. Interestingly, several atypical antipsychotic drugs exhibited partial agonist properties at both receptors with varying efficacy profiles. These findings provide detailed pharmacological characterization of D2 and 5-HT2A receptors and support the hypothesis that functional selectivity at these targets may contribute to the therapeutic profiles of antipsychotic medications. Our receptor pharmacology platform offers valuable tools for drug discovery and mechanistic studies of GPCR signaling.

Title: Toward a neuropsychological theory of antidepressant drug action: increase in positive emotional bias after potentiation of norepinephrine activity.
Abstract: Major depressive disorder is characterized by negative cognitive biases and blunted positive emotional processing, yet the mechanisms by which antidepressants restore normal emotional cognition remain poorly understood. While current theories focus primarily on monoamine receptor changes, emerging evidence suggests that antidepressants may act by modulating emotional information processing biases. This study examined whether acute potentiation of norepinephrine activity influences emotional bias in healthy volunteers using a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design. Twenty-four healthy adults received either reboxetine (4mg) or placebo before completing computerized tasks measuring emotional word recognition, facial expression categorization, and attentional bias toward positive versus negative stimuli. Participants also underwent functional magnetic resonance imaging during an emotional faces paradigm. Compared to placebo, reboxetine administration significantly increased recognition accuracy for positive emotional words (p<0.01) and shifted the threshold for categorizing ambiguous facial expressions toward happy rather than sad interpretations (p<0.05). Attentional bias measures revealed faster orienting toward positive compared to negative images following reboxetine treatment (p<0.05). Neuroimaging data showed increased amygdala-prefrontal coupling during positive emotion processing and reduced amygdala reactivity to negative faces in the reboxetine group. These findings demonstrate that acute norepinephrine enhancement produces measurable shifts toward positive emotional bias in healthy individuals, suggesting that antidepressants may exert therapeutic effects by correcting the negative cognitive biases characteristic of depression rather than simply through direct mood elevation. This neuropsychological model of antidepressant action has important implications for understanding therapeutic mechanisms and optimizing treatment approaches.

Title: Data collection and analysis strategies for phMRI.
Abstract: Although functional MRI traditionally has been applied mainly to study changes in task-induced brain function, evolving acquisition methodologies and improved knowledge of signal mechanisms have increased the utility of this method for studying responses to pharmacological stimuli, a technique often dubbed "phMRI". The proliferation of higher magnetic field strengths and the use of exogenous contrast agent have boosted detection power, a critical factor for successful phMRI due to the restricted ability to average multiple stimuli within subjects. Receptor-based models of neurovascular coupling, including explicit pharmacological models incorporating receptor densities and affinities and data-driven models that incorporate weak biophysical constraints, have demonstrated compelling descriptions of phMRI signal induced by dopaminergic stimuli. This report describes phMRI acquisition and analysis methodologies, with an emphasis on data-driven analyses. As an example application, statistically efficient data-driven regressors were used to describe the biphasic response to the mu-opioid agonist remifentanil, and antagonism using dopaminergic and GABAergic ligands revealed modulation of the mesolimbic pathway. Results illustrate the power of phMRI as well as our incomplete understanding of mechanisms underlying the signal. Future directions are discussed for phMRI acquisitions in human studies, for evolving analysis methodologies, and for interpretative studies using the new generation of simultaneous PET/MRI scanners. This article is part of the Special Issue Section entitled 'Neuroimaging in Neuropharmacology'.

---

Title: Efficient measurement of endogenous neurotransmitters in small localized regions of central nervous systems in vitro with HPLC.
Abstract: High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is widely used to determine neurotransmitter concentrations in the central nervous system (CNS). Finding the optimal methods to sample from CNS tissue poses a challenge for neuroscientists. Here, we describe a method that allows assay of neurotransmitters (or other chemicals) in small regions (down to 180mum in diameter) in in vitro preparations concurrently with electrophysiological recordings. The efficiency for measuring small amounts of chemicals is enhanced by a sample collecting pipette with filter paper at the tip that makes close contact with the target region in CNS tissue. With a wire plunger in the calibrated pipette controlled by a microsyringe pump, there is virtually no dead volume. Samples in a volume of 10muL (taken, e.g., at 2muL/min over 5min) can be injected into a HPLC machine with microbore columns. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this method by measuring acetylcholine (ACh) in the ventral horn and its surrounding areas of the spinal cord in en bloc brainstem-spinal cord preparations. In control conditions, endogenous ACh levels in these regions were detectable. Application of neostigmine (an inhibitor of acetylcholinesterases (AChEs)) increased ACh concentrations, and at the same time, induced tonic/seizure-like activity in efferent motor output recorded from cervical ventral nerve roots. Higher ACh concentrations in the ventral horn were differentiated from nearby regions: the lateral and midline aspects of the ventral spinal cord. In addition, ACh in the preBötzinger Complex (preBötC) and the hypoglossal nucleus in medullary slice preparations can also be measured. Our results indicate that the method proposed in this study can be used to measure neurotransmitters in small and localized CNS regions. Correlation between changes in neurotransmitters in target regions and the neuronal activities can be revealed in vitro. Our data also suggest that there is endogenous ACh release in spinal ventral motor columns at fourth cervical (C4) level that regulates the respiratory-related motor activity.

Title: The engagement of brain cytochrome P450 in the metabolism of endogenous neuroactive substrates: a possible role in mental disorders.
Abstract: Brain cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes have been increasingly recognized as key regulators of central nervous system homeostasis through their involvement in the metabolism of endogenous neuroactive compounds. While peripheral CYP function in drug metabolism is well-established, the role of brain-expressed CYP isoforms in neuropsychiatric pathophysiology remains poorly understood. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding brain CYP-mediated metabolism of endogenous substrates including neurosteroids, fatty acid derivatives, and biogenic amines, with particular emphasis on their potential contribution to mental disorders. We systematically analyzed published literature from 1995-2017 examining brain CYP expression, enzymatic activity, and substrate specificity using PubMed and Web of Science databases. Critical evaluation focused on CYP2D6, CYP2E1, CYP46A1, and CYP19A1 isoforms based on their neuronal expression patterns and substrate profiles. Evidence indicates that brain CYP enzymes metabolize cholesterol, arachidonic acid metabolites, neurosteroids such as pregnenolone and allopregnanolone, and catecholamine neurotransmitters. Dysregulation of these metabolic pathways correlates with altered levels of neuroactive metabolites in depression, schizophrenia, and anxiety disorders. CYP46A1-mediated cholesterol hydroxylation influences synaptic plasticity, while CYP19A1 aromatase activity affects local estrogen synthesis and neuroprotection. Genetic polymorphisms in brain-expressed CYP genes show associations with psychiatric phenotypes and treatment responses. These findings suggest that brain CYP enzymes represent previously underappreciated therapeutic targets for mental health disorders, warranting further investigation into their pharmacological modulation as novel treatment strategies.

---

Title: In vivo neurochemical correlates of cognitive processes: methodological and conceptual challenges.
Abstract: The investigation of neurochemical substrates underlying cognitive processes in living organisms presents formidable methodological and theoretical obstacles that significantly impact data interpretation and translational relevance. Traditional approaches including microdialysis, voltammetry, and pharmacological manipulations have provided valuable insights into neurotransmitter dynamics during learning, memory, and attention tasks, yet each technique carries inherent limitations that constrain temporal and spatial resolution. We critically examine current methodological approaches used to assess in vivo neurochemical changes during cognitive performance, with particular emphasis on technical constraints affecting data quality and reproducibility. Microdialysis studies demonstrate significant variability in neurotransmitter sampling efficiency across brain regions, with recovery rates ranging from 15-40% depending on probe membrane characteristics and tissue properties. Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry offers superior temporal resolution but remains limited to select neurotransmitters and restricted anatomical locations. Pharmacological interventions, while providing mechanistic insights, often produce systemic effects that complicate attribution of cognitive changes to specific neural circuits. Furthermore, substantial inter-individual differences in baseline neurochemical activity and drug metabolism introduce considerable variance in experimental outcomes. Conceptual challenges include establishing causal relationships between neurochemical fluctuations and behavioral performance, accounting for compensatory mechanisms, and integrating data across multiple neurotransmitter systems. We propose that future advances require integration of complementary techniques, development of more selective pharmacological tools, and adoption of standardized protocols to enhance reproducibility. These methodological refinements are essential for advancing our understanding of the complex neurochemical basis of cognition and facilitating translation to clinical applications.

---

Title: Incident psychopharmacological treatment and psychiatric hospital contact in individuals with newly developed type 2 diabetes - a register-based cohort study.
Abstract: To investigate the association between newly developed type 2 diabetes (T2D) and incident psychopharmacological treatment and psychiatric hospital contact. Via Danish registers, we identified all 56 640 individuals from the Central and Northern Denmark Regions with newly developed T2D (defined by the first HbA1c measurement ≥6.5%) in 2000-2016 as well as 315 694 age- and sex-matched controls (without T2D). Those having received psychopharmacological treatment or having had a psychiatric hospital contact in the 5 years prior to the onset of T2D were not included. For this cohort, we first assessed the 2-year incidence of psychopharmacological treatment and psychiatric hospital contact. Secondly, via Cox regression, we compared the incidence of psychopharmacological treatment/psychiatric hospital contact among individuals with T2D to propensity score-matched controls - taking a wide range of potential confounders into account. Finally, via Cox proportional hazards regression, we assessed which baseline (T2D onset) characteristics were associated with subsequent psychopharmacological treatment and psychiatric hospital contact. A total of 8.3% of the individuals with T2D initiated psychopharmacological treatment compared to 4.6% of the age- and sex-matched controls. Individuals with T2D were at increased risk of initiating psychopharmacological treatment compared to the propensity score-matched controls (HR = 1.51, 95% CI = 1.43-1.59), whereas their risk of psychiatric hospital contact was not increased to the same extent (HR = 1.14, 95% CI = 0.98-1.32). Older age, somatic comorbidity, and being divorced/widowed were associated with both psychopharmacological treatment and psychiatric hospital contact following T2D. Individuals with T2D are at elevated risk of requiring psychopharmacological treatment.

---

Title: Simultaneous real-time amperometric measurement of catecholamines and serotonin at carbon fibre 'dident' microelectrodes.
Abstract: Amperometry allows real-time measurement of in vivo electrochemical signals, albeit with no capacity to identify the constituents of the signal. In the present study, differential amperometry was used to monitor catecholamine and serotonin (5-HT) simultaneously at the same location. 'Dident' carbon fibre microelectrodes (microelectrodes with two working electrodes in a single assembly) were constructed and coated with Nafion to prevent poisoning on contact with brain tissue. One electrode (channel A) was held at +200 mV versus Ag/AgCl to monitor catecholamines selectively. This potential is too low to oxidise 5-HT. The second electrode (channel B), recording faradaic current at +500 mV versus Ag/AgCl, measured the sum of catecholamine and 5-HT oxidation. The 5-HT current component was the difference of channel B minus channel A. With appropriate balancing of the two channels, it is possible to record catecholamines and 5-HT simultaneously at the same dident microelectrode. Examples of measurements in striatum, cortex and locus coeruleus are shown.

---

Title: In vivo voltammetry: from wire to wireless measurements.
Abstract: In vivo voltammetry has emerged as a powerful technique for real-time monitoring of neurotransmitter dynamics in the living brain, yet traditional wired recording systems impose significant limitations on behavioral studies and chronic measurements. Current cable-tethered approaches restrict animal movement, introduce electrical artifacts, and preclude naturalistic behavioral paradigms. To address these constraints, we developed and validated a novel wireless voltammetry system for continuous monitoring of dopamine release in freely moving rats. The system incorporates a miniaturized potentiostat (2.1 g) with integrated radio frequency transmission capabilities, coupled with carbon fiber microelectrodes implanted in the nucleus accumbens. We compared the performance of wireless recordings with conventional wired fast-scan cyclic voltammetry during pharmacological challenges and behavioral tasks over 14 days post-implantation. Wireless measurements demonstrated equivalent sensitivity (detection limit: 50 nM dopamine) and temporal resolution (100 ms sampling) compared to wired controls, with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 15:1 for stimulated dopamine release. Chronic recordings revealed stable baseline measurements and preserved responses to amphetamine administration (2 mg/kg, i.p.) and electrical stimulation of the ventral tegmental area. The wireless system eliminated movement artifacts present in tethered recordings and enabled dopamine monitoring during complex social behaviors previously inaccessible with conventional approaches. This wireless voltammetry platform represents a significant methodological advancement for neuropharmacological research, facilitating investigation of neurotransmitter function in naturalistic settings while maintaining the high temporal and chemical resolution essential for mechanistic studies of brain neurochemistry.

---

Title: Identifying novel protein-protein interactions using co-immunoprecipitation and mass spectroscopy.
Abstract: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are fundamental to neuronal signaling cascades and synaptic plasticity, yet many critical interactions remain uncharacterized. Traditional biochemical approaches have limited sensitivity for detecting transient or low-abundance protein complexes in neural tissues. We developed an optimized protocol combining co-immunoprecipitation (co-IP) with liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) to systematically identify novel PPIs in mouse brain tissue. Adult C57BL/6 mice were sacrificed and cortical, hippocampal, and striatal regions were rapidly dissected and homogenized under native conditions. Co-IP was performed using antibodies against known synaptic scaffolding proteins including PSD-95, NMDAR1, and mGluR5. Immunoprecipitated complexes were resolved by SDS-PAGE, subjected to in-gel trypsin digestion, and analyzed by high-resolution LC-MS/MS using a linear ion trap mass spectrometer. Database searches identified peptides with >95% confidence scores. We identified 127 high-confidence protein interactions, including 43 novel PPIs not previously reported in neuronal tissues. Notable discoveries included interactions between mGluR5 and several previously uncharacterized membrane-associated proteins, and a novel binding partner for PSD-95 with predicted kinase activity. Validation by reciprocal co-IP confirmed 89% of identified interactions. Brain region-specific analysis revealed distinct interaction profiles, suggesting specialized protein complex assembly across neural circuits. This optimized co-IP/MS protocol provides a robust platform for discovering functionally relevant protein interactions in neural tissues and may facilitate identification of new therapeutic targets for neurological disorders.

---

Title: Smart drugs and synthetic androgens for cognitive and physical enhancement: revolving doors of cosmetic neurology.
Abstract: Cognitive enhancement can be defined as the use of drugs and/or other means with the aim to improve the cognitive functions of healthy subjects in particular memory, attention, creativity and intelligence in the absence of any medical indication. Currently, it represents one of the most debated topics in the neuroscience community. Human beings always wanted to use substances to improve their cognitive functions, from the use of hallucinogens in ancient civilizations in an attempt to allow them to better communicate with their gods, to the widespread use of caffeine under various forms (energy drinks, tablets, etc.), to the more recent development of drugs such as stimulants and glutamate activators. In the last ten years, increasing attention has been given to the use of cognitive enhancers, but up to now there is still only a limited amount of information concerning the use, effect and functioning of cognitive enhancement in daily life on healthy subjects. The first aim of this paper was to review current trends in the misuse of smart drugs (also known as Nootropics) presently available on the market focusing in detail on methylphenidate, trying to evaluate the potential risk in healthy individuals, especially teenagers and young adults. Moreover, the authors have explored the issue of cognitive enhancement compared to the use of Anabolic Androgenic Steroids (AAS) in sports. Finally, a brief overview of the ethical considerations surrounding human enhancement has been examined.

Title: A Pressure Injection System for Investigating the Neuropharmacology of Information Processing in Awake Behaving Macaque Monkey Cortex.
Abstract: The top-down modulation of feed-forward cortical information processing is functionally important for many cognitive processes, including the modulation of sensory information processing by attention. However, little is known about which neurotransmitter systems are involved in such modulations. A practical way to address this question is to combine single-cell recording with local and temporary neuropharmacological manipulation in a suitable animal model. Here we demonstrate a technique combining acute single-cell recordings with the injection of neuropharmacological agents in the direct vicinity of the recording electrode. The video shows the preparation of the pressure injection/recording system, including preparation of the substance to be injected. We show a rhesus monkey performing a visual attention task and the procedure of single-unit recording with block-wise pharmacological manipulations.

---

Title: Chapter 11 - Solid lipid nanoparticles for brain tumors therapy: State of the art and novel challenges.
Abstract: The treatment of brain tumors remains one of the most challenging aspects of oncology due to the blood-brain barrier (BBB) limiting drug penetration and the need for targeted delivery to minimize systemic toxicity. Solid lipid nanoparticles (SLNs) have emerged as promising drug delivery systems that can enhance brain bioavailability of therapeutic agents while reducing adverse effects. This review examines current developments in SLN-based drug delivery systems for brain tumor therapy and identifies key challenges for clinical translation. We analyzed recent literature on SLN formulations, surface modifications, and targeting strategies for brain tumor treatment, focusing on studies published between 2005-2009. Various approaches for BBB penetration have been investigated, including receptor-mediated transcytosis using transferrin and lactoferrin conjugation, adsorptive-mediated transcytosis through cationic surface modifications, and disruption of tight junctions. SLNs loaded with chemotherapeutic agents such as doxorubicin, paclitaxel, and temozolomide have demonstrated enhanced brain accumulation and improved therapeutic efficacy in preclinical models compared to conventional formulations. Surface functionalization with polyethylene glycol has shown to prolong circulation time and reduce recognition by the reticuloendothelial system. However, several challenges remain, including optimization of particle size and surface properties for maximum BBB permeability, prevention of drug leakage during storage and circulation, and scale-up manufacturing processes. Additionally, limited understanding of SLN biodistribution and long-term safety profiles necessitates comprehensive toxicological evaluation. Future research should focus on developing multifunctional SLNs combining therapeutic and diagnostic capabilities for personalized brain tumor therapy.

---

Title: Is it possible to improve memory with drugs in health people?
Abstract: The development of general medication for memory loss in healthy people has proven difficult. The brain harbors a number of parallel memory systems, which further involve different and separate stages of processing. The action of these stages is modified partly by the same mediators having, however, different optimum levels in distinct processes. Thus for instance stress hormones, powerfully supporting storage in long-term memory, will in similar concentrations impair implicit memory. A number of memory-enhancing drugs are available, but they are usually effective only individually and in tailored doses.

Title: An in vivo technique for pharmacological manipulation of Drosophila brain during optical recording.
Abstract: The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster has emerged as a powerful model system for investigating neural circuits and behavior, yet current methods for simultaneous pharmacological manipulation and optical monitoring of brain activity in vivo remain limited. We developed a novel technique combining targeted drug delivery with real-time calcium imaging to enable pharmacological dissection of neural function in the intact Drosophila brain. Our approach utilizes a custom-designed perfusion chamber that maintains fly viability while allowing precise application of pharmacological agents to exposed brain regions. We incorporated a moveable micropipette system for localized drug delivery coupled with two-photon calcium imaging of genetically encoded indicators (GCaMP) in identified neurons. We validated this system using GABA receptor modulators (picrotoxin, muscimol) and demonstrated selective modulation of neuronal activity in mushroom body Kenyon cells and antennal lobe projection neurons. Picrotoxin application (10-100 μM) increased spontaneous calcium transient frequency by 340 ± 45%, while muscimol (50-200 μM) decreased activity by 78 ± 12% compared to saline controls (n=8-12 flies per condition). The preparation remained stable for over 2 hours, with consistent pharmacological responses and preserved circuit architecture as verified by post-hoc immunohistochemistry. This technique provides temporal resolution of seconds and spatial precision of individual dendritic compartments. Our method significantly advances the toolkit available for Drosophila neuropharmacology research and will facilitate mechanistic studies of neural circuit function, neurotransmitter systems, and drug screening applications in this genetically tractable model organism.

---

Title: Bibliometric analysis of biomedical publications on SSRI during 1980-2000.
Abstract: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have emerged as first-line pharmacotherapy for depression and anxiety disorders since the introduction of fluoxetine in the late 1980s. However, a comprehensive analysis of the scientific literature documenting SSRI research development has not been conducted. This bibliometric study examined patterns of biomedical publications on SSRIs from 1980 to 2000 to characterize research trends and identify key areas of investigation. We searched MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases using standardized search terms for fluoxetine, sertraline, paroxetine, fluvoxamine, and citalopram. Publications were categorized by year, journal, study type, therapeutic indication, and research focus. A total of 4,267 SSRI-related publications were identified, with exponential growth from 12 papers in 1980 to 587 in 2000. The greatest publication volume occurred during 1995-1998, coinciding with widespread clinical adoption. Major depression represented 42% of therapeutic focus, followed by obsessive-compulsive disorder (18%) and panic disorder (14%). Clinical trials comprised 35% of publications, while basic pharmacology and mechanism studies accounted for 28%. The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry, Archives of General Psychiatry, and Biological Psychiatry published the highest number of SSRI studies. International collaboration increased significantly after 1992, with European contributions rising from 15% to 38% by 2000. This analysis demonstrates the rapid evolution of SSRI research parallel to clinical implementation, with sustained focus on efficacy, safety, and mechanism of action studies that established the foundation for evidence-based psychopharmacology.

---

Title: The neuropharmacological basis for the use of memantine in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) represents a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by cognitive decline, memory impairment, and eventual loss of functional independence. Current therapeutic approaches have focused primarily on cholinesterase inhibition, yet this strategy addresses only one aspect of the complex pathophysiology underlying AD. Emerging evidence suggests that excitotoxicity mediated by excessive N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor activation contributes significantly to neuronal death in AD. Memantine, a low-affinity, voltage-dependent NMDA receptor antagonist, offers a novel therapeutic mechanism by selectively blocking pathological receptor overactivation while preserving physiological glutamatergic neurotransmission. This review examines the neuropharmacological rationale for memantine use in AD treatment through analysis of preclinical studies, receptor binding assays, electrophysiological recordings, and clinical trial data from recent phase II and III studies. Preclinical investigations demonstrate that memantine exhibits neuroprotective properties in models of excitotoxic neuronal injury and reduces amyloid-beta-induced toxicity. Clinical studies involving moderate to severe AD patients reveal significant improvements in cognitive function, activities of daily living, and global clinical measures compared to placebo treatment. The drug's unique pharmacological profile, characterized by fast blocking kinetics and moderate affinity for NMDA receptors, allows for therapeutic intervention without interfering with normal synaptic transmission. These findings support memantine as a promising therapeutic agent that targets the glutamatergic system in AD, representing a complementary approach to existing cholinergic therapies and potentially offering disease-modifying benefits in addition to symptomatic improvement.

---

Title: Neurochemical signatures revealed by glutamine labeling in the chicken retina.
Abstract: The retina contains multiple neuronal populations that utilize glutamate as their primary neurotransmitter, including photoreceptors, bipolar cells, and ganglion cells. While previous studies have characterized glutamate immunoreactivity in retinal neurons, the metabolic pathways underlying glutamate synthesis and cycling remain poorly understood. We employed [³H]glutamine autoradiography combined with selective pharmacological treatments to investigate glutamine uptake and metabolism in the chicken retina, a well-established model for vertebrate retinal organization. Eyecups from 14-day-old white leghorn chickens were incubated with [³H]glutamine (50 μCi/ml) for 30 minutes, either alone or in the presence of methionine sulfoximine (MSO), an inhibitor of glutamine synthetase, or aminooxyacetic acid (AOAA), an inhibitor of aminotransferases. Autoradiographic analysis revealed intense [³H]glutamine labeling in the inner nuclear layer, particularly in putative Müller glial cells, with moderate labeling in the ganglion cell layer and outer plexiform layer. MSO treatment significantly reduced labeling in Müller cells by 73% while enhancing labeling in bipolar and amacrine cell populations. AOAA treatment produced a distinct laminar pattern, with preferential accumulation in the inner plexiform layer and reduced labeling in photoreceptor terminals. Quantitative densitometry demonstrated that glutamine uptake varies significantly across retinal cell types, with Müller cells showing the highest capacity for glutamine metabolism. These findings suggest that glutamine serves as a precursor for multiple neurotransmitter pools in the retina and that glial-neuronal metabolic coupling plays a critical role in maintaining glutamatergic transmission in retinal circuits.

---

Title: In vivo voltammetry: from wire to wireless measurements.
Abstract: A novel telemetric system based on either differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) or direct current amperometry (DCA) by using a diffused infrared transmission channel is presented. Unlike similar pre-existing instruments based on infrared transmission, the present system works on a single-way communication, thus avoiding problems related to cross-talking between two-way channels. The infrared channel is also immune from electromagnetic interferences from the surrounding environment. Further advancement is the development of an original miniaturised system (dimension 1cm x 1.2 cm x 0.5 cm) with reduced weight (5-6 g), suitable for affixing to the rat head and allowing real time telemetric monitoring using DCA sampling of neurotransmitters such as dopamine or serotonin every 100 ms. The set-up is based on a transmitter (TX) circuit mounted on the animal's head and connected to the electrodes inserted into its brain. The TX circuit generates the proper electrical signals for DPV or DCA, collects the electrical response of the brain and transmits it, via an infrared channel, to a receiving station (RX) interfaced with a personal computer. The PC performs the sampling and elaboration of polarographic traces in a flexible and programmable way.

---

Title: Can voltammetry measure nitrogen monoxide (NO) and/or nitrites?
Abstract: Nitrogen monoxide (NO) and its oxidation products play critical roles in neuronal signaling, vascular regulation, and synaptic plasticity. Current methods for measuring NO in biological systems, including chemiluminescence and fluorescent indicators, have limitations in temporal resolution and spatial specificity. This study evaluated the feasibility of using fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) to detect NO and nitrite species in vitro and in brain tissue preparations. Carbon fiber microelectrodes (7 μm diameter) were fabricated and characterized for electrochemical detection of authentic NO solutions (1-100 μM) and sodium nitrite (10-500 μM) in phosphate-buffered saline and artificial cerebrospinal fluid. Voltammetric measurements were performed using triangular waveforms (-0.4 to +1.3 V, 400 V/s) and compared with established amperometric techniques. FSCV detected NO with an oxidation peak at approximately +0.9 V versus Ag/AgCl, showing linear responses from 5-75 μM (r² = 0.94) with detection limits of 2.3 μM. Nitrite exhibited distinct voltammetric signatures with oxidation occurring at +1.1 V. However, electrode fouling occurred within 15-20 minutes of continuous measurements, and significant interference from ascorbic acid, dopamine, and other neurochemicals was observed. While FSCV demonstrated capability for NO detection under controlled conditions, poor selectivity, electrode stability issues, and interference from endogenous compounds limit its practical application for real-time NO monitoring in complex biological environments. Alternative electrode materials and surface modifications may improve performance for future neurochemical applications.

---

Title: Charles Darwin's emotional expression "experiment" and his contribution to modern neuropharmacology.
Abstract: In the late 1860s and early 1870s, Darwin had corresponded with the French physician and physiologist, G. B. A. Duchenne, regarding Duchenne's experimental manipulation of human facial expression of emotion, by applying Galvanic electrical stimulation directly to facial muscles. Duchenne had produced a set of over 60 photographic plates to illustrate his view that there are different muscles in the human face that are separately responsible for each individual emotion. Darwin studied this material very carefully and he received permission from Duchenne in 1871 to reproduce several of these images in The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals (1872). Darwin had doubted Duchenne's view that there were individual muscle groups that mediate the expression of dozens of separable emotions, and he wondered whether there might instead be a fewer set of core emotions that are expressed with great stability worldwide and across cultures. Prompted by his doubts regarding the veracity of Duchenne's model, Darwin conducted what may have been the first-ever single-blind study of the recognition of human facial expression of emotion. This single experiment was a little-known forerunner for an entire modern field of study with contemporary clinical relevance. Moreover, his specific question about cross-cultural recognition of the cardinal emotions in faces is a topic that is being actively studied (in the twenty-first century) with the hope of developing novel biomarkers to aid the discovery of new therapies for the treatment of schizophrenia, autism, and other neuropsychiatric diseases.

---

Title: The tree shrew cerebellum atlas: Systematic nomenclature, neurochemical characterization, and afferent projections.
Abstract: The cerebellum is involved in the control of movement, emotional responses, and reward processing. The tree shrew is the closest living relative of primates. However, little is known not only about the systematic nomenclature for the tree shrew cerebellum but also about the detailed neurochemical characterization and afferent projections. In this study, Nissl staining and acetylcholinesterase histochemistry were used to reveal anatomical features of the cerebellum of tree shrews (Tupaia belangeri chinensis). The cerebellar cortex presented a laminar structure. The morphological characteristics of the cerebellum were comprehensively described in the coronal, sagittal, and horizontal sections. Moreover, distributive maps of calbindin-immunoreactive (-ir) cells in the Purkinje cell layer of the cerebellum of tree shrews were depicted using coronal, sagittal, and horizontal schematics. In addition, 5th cerebellar lobule (5Cb)-projecting neurons were present in the pontine nuclei, reticular nucleus, spinal vestibular nucleus, ventral spinocerebellar tract, and inferior olive of the tree shrew brain. The anterior part of the paramedian lobule of the cerebellum (PMa) received mainly strong innervation from the lateral reticular nucleus, inferior olive, pontine reticular nucleus, spinal trigeminal nucleus, pontine nuclei, and reticulotegmental nucleus of the pons. The present results provide the first systematic nomenclature, detailed atlas of the whole cerebellum, and whole-brain mapping of afferent projections to the 5Cb and PMa in tree shrews. Our findings provide morphological support for tree shrews as an alternative model for studies of human cerebellar pathologies.

---

Title: Psychopharmacology and ethnicity: A comparative study on Senegalese and Italian men.
Abstract: Ethnic variations in drug metabolism and response represent critical considerations in psychopharmacological treatment, yet remain understudied in African populations. This study examined pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic differences in psychotropic medication response between Senegalese and Italian men. We conducted a randomized, double-blind crossover trial with 84 healthy male volunteers (42 Senegalese, 42 Italian) aged 18-35 years. Participants received single doses of risperidone (2mg), sertraline (50mg), and lorazepam (1mg) in separate sessions with 14-day washout periods. Blood samples were collected at predetermined intervals for 48 hours post-administration to assess plasma concentrations via LC-MS/MS. Cognitive and psychomotor assessments were performed using standardized batteries. Genotyping for CYP2D6, CYP2C19, and CYP3A4 polymorphisms was conducted. Senegalese participants demonstrated significantly higher plasma concentrations of risperidone (AUC: 1.47-fold increase, p<0.001) and its active metabolite, with delayed clearance compared to Italian subjects. Sertraline metabolism showed similar patterns (AUC: 1.32-fold increase, p<0.01). CYP2D6 poor metabolizer phenotypes were more prevalent in Senegalese participants (18.2% vs 7.1%, p<0.05). Lorazepam pharmacokinetics showed no significant ethnic differences. Cognitive performance decrements correlated with higher drug exposures in both groups. These findings highlight substantial ethnic differences in psychotropic drug metabolism between West African and European populations, primarily attributed to genetic polymorphisms in cytochrome P450 enzymes. Results support the need for ethnicity-guided dosing strategies and personalized medicine approaches in global psychiatric practice.
