Title: Utilizing resources of neuropsychopharmacology to address the opioid overdose crisis.
Abstract: North America is facing a severe public health crisis in the form of excessive numbers of deaths due to overdose from self-administration of opioids by individuals who are dependent on these substances. There are many factors that must be addressed in order to gain control over this tragedy. Of particular relevance to neuropsychopharmacology is the fact that the problem is due in part to misuse of pharmaceuticals and especially the illicit production of the powerful synthetic opioids, fentanyl, and carfentanil. The development and adoption of appropriate pharmacotherapies are of critical importance. We discuss specific options to deal effectively with both withdrawal from opioid dependence and substitution of clinically approved drugs in place of illicit substances. Hopefully, this crisis will reinvigorate both basic and clinical neuropsychopharmacological research leading to the develop new and more effective options for dealing with the many and varied elements of the current opioid crisis as described in the present commentary.

---

Title: A history of the Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (1957-2004).
Abstract: Recognition that one of the essential prerequisites of successful neuro-psychopharmacological research is a continuous dialogue between clinicians and basic scientists led to the founding of the Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) in Zurich on September 2, 1957. At the time, CINP was the only association in neuro-psychopharmacology. It was hoped that by organizing biennial congresses, it would facilitate the interaction among the many disciplines involved in the new field. It was also envisaged that discussion of findings in translational research would provide guidance for developing more selective and thereby more effective psychotropic drugs. These expectations were not fulfilled. In the years that followed, major developments in neuropharmacology without parallel development in the methodology of clinical investigations, created a widening gap between neuro- and psychopharmacology. Clinical interpretations of neuropharmacological findings filled in the missing information from translational research. In the 1980s, to facilitate the dissemination of these interpretations, CINP established Travel Awards for Young Investigators to assist them in attending the biennial meetings. In the 1990s, the college extended its activities by organizing other meetings, including president's workshops, regional meetings, educational seminars and other programs. By the end of the 20th century CINP was a legal entity that was registered in Switzerland with domicile in Zurich. It had also become a financially secure organization with sufficient funds to support administration and coordination. In the early years of the 21st century CINP established a central office and a congress-organizing group. To meet the needs of its steadily growing membership from Asia, Australia and the Latin Americas, CINP has been regionalized. In order to regain its relevance to all its members it is hoped that CINP will return to its roots.

---

Title: Developmental exposure to a serotonin agonist produces subsequent behavioral and neurochemical changes in the adult male prairie vole.
Abstract: Autistic spectrum disorders (ASDs) are classified as pervasive developmental disorders characterized by abnormalities in various cognitive and behavioral functions. Although exact underlying causes are still unknown, nearly 30% of autistic patients show elevated blood levels of serotonin (5-HT) and, therefore, various genetic and environmental factors that are known to elevate 5-HT levels may play a role in the development of ASDs. In the present study, we used the socially monogamous male prairie vole (Microtus ochrogaster) as an animal model to examine the effects of perinatal exposure to 5-methoxytryptamine (5-MT), a non-selective serotonin agonist, on subsequent behavioral and neurochemical changes in the brain. 5-MT treated males showed a decrease in affiliation and an increase in anxiety-related behavior, as well as a decrease in the density of 5-HT immunoreactive (ir) fibers in the amygdala and oxytocin-ir and vasopressin-ir cells in the paraventricular nucleus of the hypothalamus, compared to saline treated controls. These data indicate that exposure to 5-HT during early development can induce abnormalities in various neurochemical systems which, in turn, may underlie deficits in social and anxiety-related behaviors. In addition, these data will help to establish the prairie vole model to study the neurobiological underpinnings of complex neuropsychiatric disorders such as ASDs.

---

Title: Rapid, stimulation-induced reduction of C12-resorufin in motor nerve terminals: linkage to mitochondrial metabolism.
Abstract: The Alamar blue (resazurin) assay of cell viability monitors the irreversible reduction of non-fluorescent resazurin to fluorescent resorufin. This study focused on the reversible reduction of C12-resorufin to non-fluorescent C12-dihydroresorufin in motor nerve terminals innervating lizard intercostal muscles. Resting C12-resorufin fluorescence decreased when the activity of the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC) was accelerated with carbonyl cyanide m-chloro phenyl hydrazone, and increased when ETC activity was inhibited with cyanide. Trains of action potentials (50 Hz for 20-50 s), which reversibly decreased NADH fluorescence and partially depolarized the mitochondrial membrane potential, produced a reversible decrease in C12-resorufin fluorescence which had a similar time course. The stimulation-induced decrease in C12-resorufin fluorescence was blocked by inhibitors of ETC complexes I, III, and IV and by carbonyl cyanide m-chloro phenyl hydrazone, but not by inhibiting mitochondrial ATP synthesis with oligomycin. Mitochondrial depolarization and the decreases in C12-resorufin and NADH fluorescence depended on Ca2+ influx into the terminal, but not on vesicular transmitter release. These results suggest that the reversible reduction of C12-resorufin in stimulated motor nerve terminals is linked, directly or indirectly, to the reversible oxidation of NADH and to Ca(2+) influx into mitochondria, and provides an assay for rapid changes in motor terminal metabolism.

---

Title: Neurochemistry of neurons in the ventrolateral medulla activated by hypotension: Are the same neurons activated by glucoprivation?
Abstract: Previous studies have demonstrated that a range of stimuli activate neurons, including catecholaminergic neurons, in the ventrolateral medulla. Not all catecholaminergic neurons are activated and other neurochemical content is largely unknown hence whether stimulus specific populations exist is unclear. Here we determine the neurochemistry (using in situ hybridization) of catecholaminergic and noncatecholaminergic neurons which express c-Fos immunoreactivity throughout the rostrocaudal extent of the ventrolateral medulla, in Sprague Dawley rats treated with hydralazine or saline. Distinct neuronal populations containing PPCART, PPPACAP, and PPNPY mRNAs, which were largely catecholaminergic, were activated by hydralazine but not saline. Both catecholaminergic and noncatecholaminergic neurons containing preprotachykinin and prepro-enkephalin (PPE) mRNAs were also activated, with the noncatecholaminergic population located in the rostral C1 region. Few GlyT2 neurons were activated. A subset of these data was then used to compare the neuronal populations activated by 2-deoxyglucose evoked glucoprivation (Brain Structure and Function (2015) 220:117). Hydralazine activated more neurons than 2-deoxyglucose but similar numbers of catecholaminergic neurons. Commonly activated populations expressing PPNPY and PPE mRNAs were defined. These likely include PPNPY expressing catecholaminergic neurons projecting to vasopressinergic and corticotrophin releasing factor neurons in the paraventricular nucleus, which when activated result in elevated plasma vasopressin and corticosterone. Stimulus specific neurons included noncatecholaminergic neurons and a few PPE positive catecholaminergic neuron but neurochemical codes were largely unidentified. Reasons for the lack of identification of stimulus specific neurons, readily detectable using electrophysiology in anaesthetized preparations and for which neural circuits can be defined, are discussed.

---

Title: Pharmacotherapy of alcohol dependence: a review of the clinical data.
Abstract: Alcohol dependence represents a major public health concern affecting millions worldwide, yet therapeutic options remain limited and show variable efficacy. This review examines the current state of pharmacological interventions for alcohol dependence based on available clinical trial data through 2003. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of randomized controlled trials evaluating FDA-approved medications and emerging therapeutic agents for alcohol dependence treatment. Three medications currently hold FDA approval: disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate. Disulfiram demonstrates efficacy primarily through supervised administration, with studies showing 60-80% abstinence rates under monitored conditions versus 20-30% with unsupervised use. Naltrexone, an opioid receptor antagonist, consistently reduces heavy drinking days by 15-25% and craving scores by 20-40% across multiple placebo-controlled trials involving over 2,000 patients. Acamprosate shows promise in European studies, with abstinence rates of 35-45% versus 15-25% for placebo at 6-12 months, though US trial results have been mixed. Emerging agents including topiramate, ondansetron, and baclofen show preliminary efficacy in small-scale studies. Combination therapy approaches demonstrate enhanced outcomes compared to monotherapy in several trials. Patient factors including genetic polymorphisms, particularly in ALDH2 and OPRM1 genes, appear to influence treatment response. Current pharmacotherapies represent important advances but show modest effect sizes, highlighting the need for personalized treatment approaches and continued development of novel therapeutic targets including glutamate and GABA systems.

---

Title: Neuropharmacology of cognition and memory: a unifying theory of neuromodulator imbalance in psychiatry and amnesia.
Abstract: The case of HM, a man with intractable epilepsy who became amnesic following bilateral medial temporal lobe surgery nearly half a century ago has instigated ongoing research and theoretical speculation on the nature of memory and the role of the hippocampus. Neuropsychological testing showed that although HM had extensive anterograde memory loss he could still acquire motor and cognitive skills implicitly, but could not remember the context of this learning. This has lead to declarative and procedural descriptions of the memory process. Cholinergic and monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems have also been implicated in the memory process and anticholinergic drugs traditionally have been associated with impairment of declarative memory. The cholinergic hypothesis of Alzheimer's disease is a classic example of an application of these neuropharmacological findings. In schizophrenia, preattentive deficits have been amply demonstrated by unconscious priming studies. Memory processes are also impaired in these patients. Dopamine, glutamate and even cholinergic dysfunction has been implicated in the clinical picture of schizophrenia. The present paper will attempt to bring together both the anatomical and pharmacological data from these disparate fields of research under a cohesive theory of cognition and memory. A hypothesis is presented for an inverse relationship between monoaminergic and cholinergic systems in the modulation of implicit (unconscious) and explicit (conscious) cognitive processes. It is postulated that muscarinic cholinergic receptors and monoaminergic systems facilitate unconscious and conscious processes, respectively, and they disfacilitate conscious and unconscious processes, respectively (the purported inverse relationship). In fact, the muscarinic and monoaminergic modulations of a neural network are proposed to be finely balanced such that, if, the activity of one receptor system is modified then this by necessity has effects on the other system. It takes into account receptor subtypes and their effects mediated through excitatory and inhibitory G-protein complexes. For example, m1/D2 and D1/m4 paired receptor subtypes, colocalized on separate neurons would have opposing functional effects. A theory is then presented that the critical underlying pathophysiology of schizophrenia involves a hypofunctional muscarinic cholinergic system, which induces abnormal facilitation of monoaminergic subsystems such as dopamine (e.g., a decrease in m1R function would potentiate D2R function). This extends the idea of an inverted U function for optimal monoaminergic concentrations. Not only would this impair unconscious preattentive processes, but according to the hypothesis, explicit cognition as well including memory deficits and would underlie the mechanism of psychosis. Contrary to current thinking a different view is also presented for the role of the hippocampus in the memory process. It is postulated that long-term explicit memory traces in the neocortex are laid down by phasic coactivation of forebrain projecting monoaminergic systems above some basal firing rate, such as the rostral serotonergic raphe, which projects diffusely to the cortex and according to a modified Hebbian principle. This is the proposed principal function of the hippocampal theta rhythm. The phasic activation of the cholinergic basal forebrain is mediated by projections from a separate cortical structure, possibly the lateral prefrontal cortex. Phasic muscarinic receptor activation is proposed to strengthen implicit memory traces (at a synaptic level) in the neocortex. Thus, the latter are spared by medial temporal surgery explaining the dissociation of explicit from implicit memory.

---

Title: Measurement of plasmalemmal dopamine transport, vesicular dopamine transport, and K(+)-stimulated dopamine release in frozen rat brain tissue.
Abstract: Dopamine neurotransmission plays a critical role in motor function, reward processing, and cognition, with dysfunction implicated in Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and addiction. Traditional methods for measuring dopaminergic function require fresh tissue preparations, limiting experimental flexibility and multicenter studies. We developed and validated a novel protocol for simultaneous assessment of three key dopaminergic parameters—plasmalemmal dopamine transporter (DAT) activity, vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) function, and potassium-stimulated dopamine release—using frozen rat brain tissue. Striatal tissue from adult Sprague-Dawley rats was sectioned at 10 μm thickness and stored at -80°C for up to 12 weeks. DAT activity was measured using [³H]dopamine uptake assays in the presence and absence of GBR12909. VMAT2 function was assessed via [³H]dopamine accumulation into synaptic vesicles isolated from thawed tissue homogenates, with reserpine as a selective inhibitor. K⁺-stimulated dopamine release was evaluated using high-performance liquid chromatography following depolarization with 30 mM KCl. Frozen tissue retained 82±5% of DAT activity, 76±7% of VMAT2 function, and 71±9% of K⁺-stimulated release capacity compared to fresh preparations (n=8-12 per group). All three measures remained stable for at least 8 weeks of storage and showed excellent correlation with fresh tissue values (r>0.85, p<0.001). This methodology provides a practical approach for comprehensive dopaminergic assessment in archived samples, facilitating retrospective studies and enabling standardized protocols across multiple laboratories investigating dopamine-related neurological and psychiatric disorders.

---

Title: NMDA antagonists: antiepileptic-neuroprotective drugs with diversified neuropharmacological profiles.
Abstract: N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor antagonists have emerged as promising therapeutic agents for epilepsy and neuroprotection, though their clinical utility has been limited by psychotomimetic side effects. This study investigated the neuropharmacological profiles of five structurally distinct NMDA antagonists: MK-801 (dizocilpine), CPP, APV, ketamine, and memantine. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) were subjected to pentylenetetrazol-induced seizures and focal cerebral ischemia models. Anticonvulsant efficacy was assessed using electroencephalographic monitoring and behavioral seizure scoring. Neuroprotective effects were evaluated through histological analysis of hippocampal CA1 pyramidal cell survival 72 hours post-ischemia. Cognitive and motor function were assessed using Morris water maze and rotarod tests. All NMDA antagonists demonstrated significant anticonvulsant activity, with MK-801 and ketamine showing the greatest potency (ED50: 0.12 and 0.8 mg/kg, respectively). Neuroprotective efficacy varied considerably, with competitive antagonists (CPP, APV) providing 60-75% neuronal preservation compared to 40-50% for channel blockers. However, the degree of behavioral impairment correlated inversely with therapeutic index. Memantine exhibited the most favorable profile, showing moderate anticonvulsant and neuroprotective effects (ED50: 5.2 mg/kg) with minimal cognitive disruption. These findings demonstrate that NMDA receptor antagonists possess diversified neuropharmacological profiles that may be exploited therapeutically. The differential effects observed suggest that receptor subtype selectivity and binding kinetics significantly influence both therapeutic efficacy and adverse effect profiles, supporting continued development of subtype-selective NMDA antagonists for clinical applications.

Title: 5-HT in the enteric nervous system: gut function and neuropharmacology.
Abstract: In recent times, the perception of functional gastrointestinal disorders such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) has shifted fundamentally. Such disorders are now thought of as serious diseases characterized by perturbations in the neuronal regulation of gastrointestinal function. The concept of visceral hypersensitivity, the characterization of neuronal networks in the 'brain-gut axis' and the identification of several novel 5-HT-mediated mechanisms have contributed to this shift. Here, we review how some of the more promising of these new mechanisms (e.g. those involving 5-HT transporters and the 5-HT(2B), 5-HT(7) and putative 5-HT(1p) receptors) might lead to a range of second-generation therapies that could revolutionize the treatment of functional gastrointestinal disorders, particularly IBS.

Title: Expression and comparative characterization of Gq-coupled invertebrate visual pigments and melanopsin.
Abstract: A non-visual pigment melanopsin, which is localized in photosensitive retinal ganglion cells and is involved in the circadian photoentrainment and pupillary responses in mammals, is phylogenetically close to the visual pigments of invertebrates, such as insects and cephalopods. Recent studies suggested that melanopsin is a bistable pigment and drives a Gq-mediated signal transduction cascade, like the invertebrate visual pigments. Because detailed electrophysiological properties are somewhat different between the visual cells and the photosensitive ganglion cells, we here expressed and purified the invertebrate visual pigment and melanopsin to comparatively investigate their Gq-activation abilities. We successfully expressed and purified UV and blue light-sensitive visual pigments of the honeybee as well as the amphioxus melanopsin. Although the purified UV-sensitive pigment and the melanopsin lost their bistable nature during purification, reconstitution of the pigments in lipid vesicles resulted in return of the bistable nature. The light-dependent Gq-activation abilities among these reconstituted pigments are similar, suggesting that the electrophysiological differences do not depend on the Gq-activation step but rather on the other signal transduction steps and/or on cell properties. Our findings are also important in that this is the first report describes a heterologous large-scale expression of the Gq-coupled invertebrate visual pigments in cultured cells.

---

Title: Reduced computational models of serotonin synthesis, release, and reuptake.
Abstract: Serotonergic dysfunction underlies numerous psychiatric and neurological disorders, yet computational modeling of serotonin (5-HT) dynamics remains limited by model complexity and computational cost. We developed reduced-order mathematical models of 5-HT synthesis, release, and reuptake to enable efficient simulation of serotonergic neurotransmission while preserving biological fidelity. Starting from detailed biophysical models incorporating tryptophan hydroxylase kinetics, vesicular packaging, calcium-dependent exocytosis, and serotonin transporter (SERT) function, we applied model reduction techniques including quasi-steady-state approximations and singular perturbation analysis. The resulting reduced models were validated against experimental data from rat dorsal raphe nucleus recordings and compared to full-order simulations. Our simplified models captured 5-HT synthesis dynamics with 85% accuracy while reducing computational time by two orders of magnitude. The reduced release model accurately reproduced stimulus-evoked 5-HT transients measured by fast-scan cyclic voltammetry, including both peak amplitudes and decay kinetics. Reuptake dynamics were well-preserved across physiological and pharmacologically-relevant SERT inhibition conditions, with less than 10% deviation from full models. Parameter sensitivity analysis revealed that synthesis rate and SERT density were the most critical determinants of steady-state 5-HT levels. These computationally efficient models enable real-time simulation of serotonergic circuits and provide a foundation for closed-loop neuromodulation systems targeting 5-HT dysfunction. The reduced-order framework presented here can be readily extended to other monoaminergic systems and integrated into larger network models of brain function.

Title: Effects of zona incerta lesions on striatal neurochemistry and behavioral asymmetry in 6-hydroxydopamine-lesioned rats.
Abstract: Analysis of optimal sites for neurosurgical interventions in patients with Parkinson's disease (PD) suggests that significant clinical benefits may be achieved by involvement of the zona incerta (ZI). Unilateral electrolytic ZI lesions were made in intact and ipsilaterally 6-hydroxydopamine (6OHDA)-lesioned rats. Extracellular levels of glutamate, dopamine, and its metabolites in the ipsilateral striatum of awake rats were measured by using microdialysis, and tests of behavioral asymmetry were performed. In intact rats, ZI lesions had no effect on striatal extracellular glutamate or absolute levels of dopamine or metabolites, but dopamine metabolism decreased. After ZI lesions, contralateral forepaw use decreased in the forepaw adjusting steps test, but there was no change in response to vibrissa stimulation or cylinder exploration. There was no development of rotational asymmetry with amphetamine. In 6OHDA-lesioned rats, striatal extracellular glutamate levels were elevated compared with controls. ZI lesions reduced the increased levels of glutamate back to normal values. ZI lesions reduced dopamine and homovanillic acid levels and showed a trend toward a decrease in dopamine metabolism. 6OHDA-lesioned rats demonstrated the expected asymmetry of motor behaviors. After ZI lesions, ipsilateral turns following amphetamine injection were reduced, and there was a trend toward improved symmetry of forepaw use as determined with the forepaw adjusting steps test. There was no change in forepaw use with vibrissa stimulation or cylinder exploration. These data indicate that lesions of the ZI can affect striatal neurochemistry and motor behavioral asymmetry and suggest potential mechanisms by which ZI lesions may improve symptoms in PD.

Title: Optofluidic control of rodent learning using cloaked caged glutamate.
Abstract: Glutamate is the major excitatory neurotransmitter in the brain, and photochemical release of glutamate (or uncaging) is a chemical technique widely used by biologists to interrogate its physiology. A basic prerequisite of these optical probes is bio-inertness before photolysis. However, all caged glutamates are known to have strong antagonism toward receptors of γ-aminobutyric acid, the major inhibitory transmitter. We have developed a caged glutamate probe that is inert toward these receptors at concentrations that are effective for photolysis with violet light. Pharmacological tests in vitro revealed that attachment of a fifth-generation (G5) dendrimer (i.e., cloaking) to the widely used 4-methoxy-7-nitro-indolinyl(MNI)-Glu probe prevented such off-target effects while not changing the photochemical properties of MNI-Glu significantly. G5-MNI-Glu was used with optofluidic delivery to stimulate dopamine neurons of the ventral tegmental area of freely moving mice in a conditioned place-preference protocol so as to mediate Pavlovian conditioning.

Title: Localization of L-DOPA uptake and decarboxylating neuronal structures in the cat brain using dopamine immunohistochemistry.
Abstract: The present study examined dopamine-immunoreactive neuronal structures using immunohistochemistry in conjunction with an anti-dopamine antiserum, following injection of l-3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (L-DOPA) with or without an inhibitor of monoamine oxidase (Pargyline) in the cat brain. L-DOPA injection made it possible to detect dopamine immunoreactivity in presumptive serotonergic and noradrenergic cell bodies and axons. Weak to moderate dopamine immunoreactivity was observed in non-aminergic cells (possibly so-called "D" cells containing aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (AADC)) in several hypothalamic, midbrain, pontine and medullary nuclei. Intense dopamine immunoreactivity became visible in a large number of cells and axons (possibly containing AADC) with wide distribution in the brain following administration of L-DOPA with Pargyline. AADC is most likely active in cells and axons that take up L-DOPA, where it decarboxylates the L-DOPA to dopamine. However, newly synthesized dopamine in such cells is rapidly oxidized by monoamine oxidase.

---

Title: Capillary depletion method for quantification of blood-brain barrier transport of circulating peptides and plasma proteins.
Abstract: Recent studies indicate that circulating peptides or plasma proteins, such as insulin or transferrin, or modified proteins, such as cationized albumin, undergo receptor-mediated or absorptive-mediated transport through the brain capillary wall, i.e., the blood-brain barrier (BBB). Although morphologic studies such as autoradiography or immunoperoxidase labeling can demonstrate transport of blood-borne protein into brain, there is a need for a rapid, sensitive, and quantifiable physiology-based technique for comparing the relative rates of transport of several different blood-borne peptides or proteins into brain. Therefore, the present investigations describe a carotid arterial infusion technique coupled with a capillary depletion method for quantifying transport of blood-borne cationized albumin, cationized IgG, and acetylated low-density lipoprotein (LDL). Because differentiation of true transcytosis into the postcapillary compartment of brain parenchyma from binding and/or endocytosis to the brain microvasculature is important, the present studies use a dextran density centrifugation step to deplete brain homogenate of the vasculature. In addition, 3H-labeled native albumin is used as a vascular space marker to account for release of capillary contents into the postcapillary supernatant following homogenization of brain. This study demonstrates rapid transport of cationized IgG or cationized albumin into brain, as these compounds achieve a volume of distribution of 20-30 microliters/g within 10 min of arterial perfusion. Conversely, acetylated LDL, although rapidly bound by cerebral microvasculature, is shown not to undergo transport into the post-capillary compartment of brain parenchyma. These studies provide the basis for a sensitive, quantifiable technique for studying transport of radiolabeled blood-borne peptides and proteins across the BBB of anesthetized animals.

---

Title: The pediatric psychopharmacology of autism spectrum disorder: A systematic review - Part I: The past and the present.
Abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1 in 54 children, yet evidence-based pharmacological interventions remain limited. While no medications are approved for core ASD symptoms, psychotropic drugs are frequently prescribed off-label to manage associated behavioral symptoms. This systematic review synthesizes the historical evolution and current state of pediatric psychopharmacological approaches in ASD. We conducted a comprehensive search of PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane databases from inception through December 2020, including randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series examining pharmacological interventions in children and adolescents with ASD. Two independent reviewers extracted data on study design, participant characteristics, interventions, outcome measures, and safety profiles. Of 2,847 initially identified studies, 156 met inclusion criteria, encompassing 8,923 pediatric participants. Antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, demonstrated the strongest evidence for reducing irritability and aggressive behaviors, with FDA approval for these indications. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors showed mixed results for repetitive behaviors and anxiety, with concerns regarding behavioral activation in younger children. Stimulants effectively addressed comorbid attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms but required careful monitoring for adverse effects. Alpha-2 agonists and anticonvulsants showed promise in preliminary studies. However, significant heterogeneity in outcome measures, small sample sizes, and limited long-term safety data constrain clinical recommendations. Current pediatric psychopharmacology in ASD remains symptom-focused rather than addressing core deficits. Future research should prioritize standardized outcome measures, longer-term studies, and investigation of novel therapeutic targets to advance evidence-based treatment approaches for this vulnerable population.

Title: Neuroglialpharmacology: white matter pathophysiologies and psychiatric treatments.
Abstract: Emerging evidence suggests that white matter abnormalities and glial dysfunction play critical roles in the pathophysiology of major psychiatric disorders, yet current therapeutic approaches primarily target neuronal mechanisms. Recent neuroimaging studies have revealed widespread white matter microstructural alterations in schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and major depression, while post-mortem analyses demonstrate significant oligodendrocyte pathology and altered myelin gene expression. This review synthesizes current understanding of neuroglial pharmacology and its therapeutic implications for psychiatric treatment. We examined preclinical and clinical literature from 1995-2011 focusing on white matter pathophysiology, glial cell biology, and emerging therapeutic targets. Database searches identified 247 relevant studies examining oligodendrocyte function, myelin dynamics, and glial-neuronal interactions in psychiatric contexts. Converging evidence indicates that psychiatric medications exert significant effects on glial cells beyond their primary neuronal targets. Lithium promotes oligodendrocyte survival and myelin repair through GSK-3β inhibition, while quetiapine enhances oligodendrocyte progenitor cell differentiation. Antidepressants modulate microglial activation and promote remyelination via BDNF-dependent mechanisms. Novel therapeutic approaches targeting white matter pathology show promise, including clemastine for myelin repair and minocycline for microglial modulation. Importantly, genetic variants affecting oligodendrocyte function predict treatment response to conventional psychiatric medications. These findings support a paradigm shift toward "neuroglialpharmacology" that considers both neuronal and glial targets. Future psychiatric drug development should incorporate white matter endpoints and glial biomarkers to optimize therapeutic efficacy. Understanding neuroglial mechanisms may explain individual treatment responses and guide personalized psychiatric medicine approaches.

---

Title: Getting a Handle on Neuropharmacology by Targeting Receptor-Associated Proteins.
Abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches have focused primarily on direct modulation of neurotransmitter receptors, yet mounting evidence suggests that receptor-associated proteins (RAPs) represent underexplored therapeutic targets with potentially superior selectivity and reduced side effects. Here, we investigated the druggability of RAPs using a comprehensive screen of 147 small molecules against key scaffolding and regulatory proteins associated with NMDA, AMPA, and GABA_A receptors in primary hippocampal neurons. We employed a combination of high-content imaging, electrophysiology, and proximity ligation assays to assess compound effects on receptor trafficking, synaptic localization, and functional modulation. Our screen identified 12 compounds that selectively disrupted specific protein-protein interactions without affecting receptor pharmacology directly. Notably, compound SN-2847, which targets the PSD-95/NMDA receptor interaction, produced antidepressant-like effects in behavioral assays while avoiding the psychotomimetic side effects associated with direct NMDA receptor antagonists. Mechanistic studies revealed that SN-2847 enhanced AMPA receptor surface expression through compensatory trafficking mechanisms. Similarly, compound GB-1203, targeting gephyrin-GABA_A receptor clustering, demonstrated anxiolytic properties with improved therapeutic windows compared to benzodiazepines. Proteomic analysis confirmed target specificity and revealed minimal off-target effects. These findings establish proof-of-principle that pharmacological targeting of receptor-associated proteins can achieve therapeutic efficacy with enhanced selectivity, opening new avenues for neuropsychiatric drug development. Our approach provides a framework for systematic exploration of the "RAP-ome" as a source of next-generation neuropharmacological targets.

Title: Two novel combined drug treatments for women with hypoactive sexual desire disorder.
Abstract: Hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) affects approximately 10% of women and represents the most common form of female sexual dysfunction, yet effective pharmacological interventions remain limited. Current monotherapies targeting dopaminergic, noradrenergic, or melanocortin pathways have shown modest efficacy with significant side effects. This study investigated two novel combination therapies designed to enhance sexual motivation through synergistic neurotransmitter modulation. A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial was conducted in 180 premenopausal women diagnosed with HSDD. Participants were randomized to receive either bupropion (300mg/day) combined with buspirone (30mg/day), flibanserin (100mg/day) combined with low-dose testosterone gel (300μg/day), or placebo for 12 weeks. Primary outcomes included changes in sexual desire as measured by the Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) desire domain and Sexual Desire Inventory-2 (SDI-2). Secondary measures assessed sexual satisfaction, distress levels, and adverse events. Both combination treatments demonstrated significantly greater improvements in sexual desire compared to placebo (p<0.001). The bupropion-buspirone combination increased mean FSFI desire scores by 2.4 points (95% CI: 1.8-3.0), while flibanserin-testosterone improved scores by 2.1 points (95% CI: 1.5-2.7). Sexual satisfaction improved in both active treatment groups with minimal adverse effects, primarily mild nausea and dizziness. These findings suggest that combination pharmacotherapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems may offer superior efficacy over monotherapy approaches for treating HSDD, warranting larger phase III trials to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term safety profiles.

Title: PET Neurochemical Imaging Modes.
Abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating neurochemical processes in vivo, offering unprecedented insights into neurotransmitter systems and their alterations in neurological and psychiatric disorders. This comprehensive review examines current PET neurochemical imaging approaches and their clinical applications. We analyzed literature from 2010-2015 encompassing major neurotransmitter systems including dopaminergic, serotonergic, cholinergic, and GABAergic pathways, evaluating radiotracer development, imaging protocols, and quantitative methodologies. Key dopaminergic tracers such as [11C]raclopride and [18F]DOPA continue to provide robust measures of receptor availability and presynaptic function, while newer compounds like [11C]PE2I offer improved specificity for dopamine transporter imaging. Serotonergic imaging has advanced significantly with [11C]DASB for serotonin transporter quantification and [18F]altanserin for 5-HT2A receptor studies. Cholinergic system evaluation benefits from [18F]fluorobenzovesamicol for vesicular acetylcholine transporter imaging, particularly valuable in dementia research. GABAergic imaging remains challenging, though [11C]flumazenil provides reliable benzodiazepine receptor measurements. Quantitative analysis methods have evolved from simple standardized uptake values to sophisticated kinetic modeling approaches using arterial input functions and reference tissue methods. Recent developments in partial volume correction and motion compensation have enhanced accuracy. PET neurochemical imaging continues to expand our understanding of brain chemistry in health and disease, with emerging tracers for glutamatergic, histaminergic, and cannabinoid systems promising further advances in translational neuroscience research and drug development.

Title: High-throughput brain activity mapping and machine learning as a foundation for systems neuropharmacology.
Abstract: Technologies for mapping the spatial and temporal patterns of neural activity have advanced our understanding of brain function in both health and disease. An important application of these technologies is the discovery of next-generation neurotherapeutics for neurological and psychiatric disorders. Here, we describe an in vivo drug screening strategy that combines high-throughput technology to generate large-scale brain activity maps (BAMs) with machine learning for predictive analysis. This platform enables evaluation of compounds' mechanisms of action and potential therapeutic uses based on information-rich BAMs derived from drug-treated zebrafish larvae. From a screen of clinically used drugs, we found intrinsically coherent drug clusters that are associated with known therapeutic categories. Using BAM-based clusters as a functional classifier, we identify anti-seizure-like drug leads from non-clinical compounds and validate their therapeutic effects in the pentylenetetrazole zebrafish seizure model. Collectively, this study provides a framework to advance the field of systems neuropharmacology.

Title: Transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS).
Abstract: tDCS appears to be a promising tool in neuroplasticity research with some tentative perspectives in clinical neurophysiology. The next steps to be carried out encompass better histological safety data. In order to preclude the possibility of neuronal damage, extending tDCS duration should be limited until more direct safety criteria are available than those derived from Agnew and McCreery (1987) (cf. Nitsche et al, this volume). Safe stimulation protocols have to be developed which allow an extension of the duration of after-effects towards a somewhat permanent state, supposing a beneficial effect can be found in neurological diseases or in neurorehabilitation.

Title: Principles of motivation revealed by the diverse functions of neuropharmacological and neuroanatomical substrates underlying feeding behavior.
Abstract: The neural mechanisms underlying feeding behavior represent a complex integration of homeostatic, hedonic, and cognitive processes that provide fundamental insights into motivational systems. While hypothalamic circuits have long been recognized as primary regulators of energy balance, emerging evidence demonstrates that feeding behavior engages distributed neural networks involving dopaminergic reward pathways, limbic structures, and prefrontal regulatory regions. This review synthesizes current understanding of the neuropharmacological and neuroanatomical substrates controlling food intake, with particular emphasis on how these systems reveal broader principles of motivated behavior. We examine the roles of key neurotransmitter systems, including dopamine, serotonin, GABA, and neuropeptides such as orexin and NPY, in mediating different aspects of feeding motivation. The hypothalamic arcuate nucleus emerges as a critical integration hub, receiving peripheral metabolic signals and coordinating responses through projections to brainstem and forebrain regions. Dopaminergic circuits in the ventral tegmental area and nucleus accumbens process food reward and incentive salience, while prefrontal cortical areas exert top-down control over feeding decisions. Dysregulation of these interconnected systems contributes to obesity and eating disorders, highlighting their clinical relevance. The multifaceted nature of feeding control illuminates general principles governing motivated behavior, including the integration of homeostatic need states with reward processing, the role of learning and memory in shaping behavioral responses, and the hierarchical organization of neural control systems. Understanding these principles advances both basic neuroscience and therapeutic approaches to metabolic disorders.

Title: Corticosterone urinalysis and nicotinic receptor modulation in rats.
Abstract: A routine method of measuring circulating corticosterone (CORT) levels in rats involves sampling of plasma from cannulated animals. However, being somewhat invasive, this method can potentially be confounded by its inherently stressful nature. This study investigated the feasibility of measuring corticosterone using a non-invasive sampling method from voided urine of male rats. Reliability was assessed pharmacologically with nicotinic compounds previously demonstrated to modulate plasma glucocorticoid levels. Nicotine (0.1-1mg/kg sc) dose-dependently increased corticosterone levels in rat urine at 30-70 min following administration. The short-lived nature of this elevation was confirmed as CORT levels measured 6 and 24h later were shown to have returned to basal levels. Both basal and nicotine-induced (0.5mg/kg sc) elevations in urinary CORT were consistent between groups of animals with weights ranging from 200 to 400 g. The magnitude of urinary CORT elevation induced by nicotine (0.5mg/kg sc) was found to be similar to that induced by a forced swim stressor in male Lister ) antagonist mecamylamine (0.05-0.5mg/kg sc) dose-dependently reversed the effects of nicotine (0.5mg/kg sc) on urinary CORT. Finally, the alpha(4)beta(2)-subunit preferring agonist TC-2559 induced a dose-dependent increase in CORT, whereas alpha(7)- and beta(4)-subunit preferring ligands had no effect, suggestive of the potential for differential involvement of nicotinic receptor subtypes in the mediation of this response. In conclusion, urinary corticosterone sampling in rats represents a robust assay sensitive to experimental manipulations of both pharmacological and behavioural relevances.

Title: The fly as a model for neurodegenerative diseases: is it worth the jump?
Abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases are responsible for agonizing symptoms that take their toll on the fragile human life. Aberrant protein processing and accumulation are considered to be the culprits of many classical neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, tauopathies, Parkinson's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, hereditary spastic paraplegia and various polyglutamine diseases. However, recently it has been shown that toxic RNA species or disruption of RNA processing and metabolism may be partly to blame as clearly illustrated in spinal muscular atrophy, spinocerebellar ataxia 8 and fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome. At the dawn of the twenty-first century, the fruit fly or Drosophila melanogaster has taken its place at the forefront of an uphill struggle to unveil the molecular and cellular pathophysiology of both protein- and RNA-induced neurodegeneration, as well as discovery of novel drug targets. We review here the various fly models of neurodegenerative conditions, and summarise the novel insights that the fly has contributed to the field of neuroprotection and neurodegeneration.

Title: Affinity labeling the dopamine transporter ligand binding site.
Abstract: The dopamine transporter (DAT) plays a crucial role in terminating dopaminergic neurotransmission by removing dopamine from the synaptic cleft, and represents a primary target for psychostimulants such as cocaine and amphetamine. Despite extensive pharmacological characterization, the precise molecular architecture of the DAT ligand binding site remains poorly understood. We employed photoaffinity labeling techniques to identify amino acid residues within the cocaine and dopamine binding domains of the human DAT. A series of photoreactive analogues of cocaine and dopamine were synthesized, including [³H]3β-[4-(4-azido-3-iodophenyl)methyl]tropane-2β-carboxylic acid methyl ester (RTI-82) and [³H]2-azido-dopamine. Membrane preparations from HEK293 cells stably expressing human DAT were incubated with photolabels under UV irradiation conditions. Covalently modified transporters were isolated by immunoprecipitation and subjected to proteolytic digestion followed by peptide mapping and Edman degradation sequencing. RTI-82 specifically labeled Asp79 in transmembrane domain 1 and Phe320 in transmembrane domain 6, while 2-azido-dopamine primarily modified Ser422 in transmembrane domain 8. Competition experiments with unlabeled ligands confirmed specificity of labeling. Mutational analysis revealed that substitution of labeled residues significantly reduced binding affinity for both substrate and inhibitor ligands. These results provide direct biochemical evidence for a multi-domain binding pocket involving transmembrane segments 1, 6, and 8, and suggest overlapping but distinct binding sites for dopamine and cocaine within the DAT structure. This photoaffinity mapping approach offers valuable insights into transporter structure-function relationships and may facilitate rational drug design efforts.

Title: Taurine Protects from Pentylenetetrazole-Induced Behavioral and Neurochemical Changes in Zebrafish.
Abstract: Epilepsy is a common neurological disorder characterized by recurrent unprovoked seizures, which culminate in various neurobehavioral and neurochemical changes. Taurine (TAU) is an amino sulfonic acid which acts an endogenous inhibitory neuromodulator. Moreover, TAU displays intrinsic antioxidant activity, contributing to its beneficial actions in the CNS. Here, we evaluated whether TAU pretreatment protects from pentylenetetrazole (PTZ)-induced behavioral alterations and oxidative stress-related parameters in zebrafish brain tissue. Fish were pretreated with 42, 150, and 400 mg/L TAU (40 min) and further exposed to 10 mM PTZ (20 min) to analyze the seizure-like behaviors. As a positive control, another group was previously treated with 75 μM diazepam (DZP). Afterwards, biochemical experiments were performed. All TAU concentrations tested decreased seizure intensity in the first 150 s. Importantly, 150 mg/L TAU attenuated seizure-like behavioral scores, decreased seizure intensity, reduced the frequency of clonic-like seizures (score 4), and increased the latency to score 4. TAU (150 mg/L) also prevented oxidative stress in PTZ-challenged fish by decreasing lipid peroxidation and protein carbonylation and preventing changes on nonprotein thiol levels. No significant changes were observed in MTT assay and LDH activity. Differently than observed in DZP group, TAU did not affect the overall swimming activity of fish, suggesting different mechanisms of action. Collectively, we show that TAU attenuates PTZ-induced seizure-like behaviors and brain oxidative stress in zebrafish, suggesting the involvement of antioxidant mechanisms in neuroprotection.

Title: The neuropharmacology of L-theanine(N-ethyl-L-glutamine): a possible neuroprotective and cognitive enhancing agent.
Abstract: L-theanine (N-ethyl-L-glutamine) or theanine is a major amino acid uniquely found in green tea. L-theanine has been historically reported as a relaxing agent, prompting scientific research on its pharmacology. Animal neurochemistry studies suggest that L-theanine increases brain serotonin, dopamine, GABA levels and has micromolar affinities for AMPA, Kainate and NMDA receptors. In addition has been shown to exert neuroprotective effects in animal models possibly through its antagonistic effects on group 1 metabotrophic glutamate receptors. Behavioural studies in animals suggest improvement in learning and memory. Overall, L-theanine displays a neuropharmacology suggestive of a possible neuroprotective and cognitive enhancing agent and warrants further investigation in animals and humans.

Title: Tobacco psychopharmacology and public health policy: it takes a community.
Abstract: Public health policies targeting tobacco use have achieved significant population-level reductions in smoking prevalence, yet individual neurobiological mechanisms underlying nicotine dependence remain critical barriers to cessation success. This review synthesizes current psychopharmacological evidence with community-based intervention strategies to examine how multilevel approaches may optimize tobacco control outcomes. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of randomized controlled trials published between 2005-2010 examining nicotine replacement therapies, bupropion, and varenicline in community settings, alongside population-based studies evaluating policy interventions including taxation, smoke-free laws, and media campaigns. Neurobiological studies demonstrate that chronic nicotine exposure produces persistent alterations in dopaminergic and cholinergic signaling pathways, particularly within mesocorticolimbic reward circuits, contributing to sustained craving and relapse vulnerability. Pharmacotherapies targeting these systems show 1.5-3.0 fold increases in quit rates compared to placebo in clinical trials. However, real-world effectiveness varies substantially across populations, with lower success rates observed in socioeconomically disadvantaged communities. Community-wide interventions combining pharmacotherapy access with environmental policy changes demonstrate enhanced outcomes, with population quit rates increasing 15-25% beyond individual treatment approaches alone. Smoke-free policies amplify pharmacotherapy effectiveness by reducing environmental cue exposure and social reinforcement of smoking behavior. These findings suggest that optimal tobacco control requires integrated strategies addressing both neurobiological dependence mechanisms through evidence-based pharmacotherapies and environmental factors through comprehensive community-level policies. Future research should prioritize implementation science approaches examining how to effectively deploy combined psychopharmacological and policy interventions across diverse populations.

Title: Metabolic changes detected by proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy in vivo and in vitro in a murin model of Parkinson's disease, the MPTP-intoxicated mouse.
Abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is characterized by selective dopaminergic neuronal loss in the substantia nigra, yet the underlying metabolic alterations remain poorly understood. The 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP) mouse model reproduces key features of PD pathology and provides an opportunity to investigate neurochemical changes associated with dopaminergic degeneration. We employed proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (¹H-MRS) to characterize metabolic profiles in MPTP-treated C57BL/6 mice both in vivo and in vitro. Adult male mice received four subcutaneous injections of MPTP (20 mg/kg) at 2-hour intervals. In vivo ¹H-MRS was performed at 9.4T focusing on striatal regions at 1, 7, and 21 days post-treatment. Ex vivo high-resolution magic angle spinning ¹H-MRS was conducted on striatal and nigral tissue extracts. MPTP treatment resulted in significant reductions in N-acetylaspartate (NAA) levels in the striatum by day 7 (23% decrease, p<0.01), which persisted through day 21. Lactate concentrations were elevated 2-fold at day 1 but normalized by day 7. In vitro analysis revealed decreased glutamate and increased taurine levels in both striatal and nigral regions. Creatine and choline-containing compounds showed region-specific alterations, with striatal creatine decreasing by 18% while nigral levels remained unchanged. These findings demonstrate that MPTP-induced dopaminergic toxicity produces distinct metabolic signatures detectable by ¹H-MRS, with NAA reduction likely reflecting neuronal dysfunction or loss. The metabolic profiles provide insights into the temporal progression of MPTP toxicity and may serve as biomarkers for monitoring disease progression and therapeutic interventions in experimental PD.

---

Title: The psychopharmacology of Huntington disease.
Abstract: Huntington disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by motor dysfunction, cognitive decline, and psychiatric manifestations that significantly impact patient quality of life. While no disease-modifying treatments exist, psychopharmacological interventions remain crucial for managing the complex neuropsychiatric symptoms. This comprehensive review examines current evidence for pharmacological management of psychiatric comorbidities in HD, including depression, anxiety, psychosis, and behavioral disturbances. We analyzed randomized controlled trials, observational studies, and case series published between 2000-2019, focusing on efficacy and safety profiles of psychotropic medications in HD populations. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, particularly sertraline and citalopram, demonstrate modest efficacy for depression with acceptable tolerability profiles. Atypical antipsychotics, especially olanzapine and quetiapine, show benefit for psychotic symptoms and agitation, though careful monitoring for extrapyramidal side effects is essential given underlying motor pathology. Benzodiazepines provide short-term anxiety relief but carry risks of cognitive impairment and falls. Novel approaches including lithium and antiepileptic drugs show preliminary promise for mood stabilization and neuroprotection, though larger controlled trials are needed. The unique pathophysiology of HD, involving progressive striatal degeneration and altered neurotransmitter systems, necessitates modified prescribing approaches compared to primary psychiatric disorders. Drug-drug interactions, altered pharmacokinetics in advanced disease stages, and heightened sensitivity to adverse effects require careful consideration. Future research should prioritize developing HD-specific treatment guidelines and investigating neuroprotective agents that may address both psychiatric symptoms and underlying disease progression.

---

Title: The history of neurochemistry as revealed by the Journal of Neurochemistry.
Abstract: Analyses of samples of articles in the Journal of Neurochemistry between 1956 (the year of its foundation) and 1990 were used to obtain numerical indices of the history of neurochemistry. Data suggest that the acceleration of neurochemical research did not merely reflect the increase of biochemical research in general and that it involved progressive decreases and increases of interest in major constituents and transmitters, respectively, as indicated by both numbers and citations of papers. Papers on all classes of transmitters increased steadily and in the order of amines > amino acids, acetylcholine > peptides. Within the field of brain metabolism, papers on energy metabolism decreased markedly. Use of techniques other than those of biochemistry/neurochemistry altered strikingly with decreases of histological, electrophysiological, and pharmacological methods and increases of chemical, immunological, and tissue culture methods. Citations by neuroscience core journals between 1975 and 1988 suggest that the relative prominence of neurochemistry within neuroscience has remained constant. Analyses indicate that the influence of the U.S.A. relative to that of other regions has remained fairly steady between 1956 and 1990, but that number of papers from the U.K. has declined, whereas the influences of Western Europe and other areas appear to have recently increased substantially. Sociological changes have been the virtual disappearance of single-author papers, an increase of multiauthorship (> 3), and a recent striking increase of assertive sentence titles.

---

Title: From the search for a molecular code of memory to the role of neurotransmitters: a historical perspective.
Abstract: The molecular basis of memory formation has been a central pursuit in neuroscience for over five decades, evolving from early theories of protein synthesis to our current understanding of neurotransmitter-mediated synaptic plasticity. This historical review examines the paradigmatic shifts in memory research from 1950 to 2004, analyzing key experimental findings and theoretical frameworks that shaped the field. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of landmark studies, tracing the progression from Hyden's early RNA hypothesis through Kandel's work on Aplysia to contemporary investigations of NMDA receptor-dependent long-term potentiation. The search for a discrete "molecular code" of memory initially focused on stable protein modifications and RNA templates as information storage mechanisms. However, accumulating evidence revealed that memory consolidation involves dynamic, network-based processes mediated by neurotransmitter systems rather than static molecular engrams. Critical discoveries include the identification of CREB as a transcriptional switch for memory formation, the role of protein kinase A in synaptic strengthening, and the requirement for de novo protein synthesis during memory consolidation. The field has transitioned from reductionist models seeking single molecular determinants to systems-level approaches emphasizing neurotransmitter-receptor interactions, particularly glutamatergic and cholinergic signaling cascades. These findings demonstrate that memory formation emerges from coordinated neurotransmitter-mediated changes in synaptic efficacy rather than discrete molecular codes, fundamentally reshaping our understanding of neural plasticity mechanisms and providing new therapeutic targets for memory-related disorders.

---

Title: Memory-sparing cognitive deterioration in the elderly: what is the diagnosis?
Abstract: The case presented highlights the difficult differential diagnosis of memory-sparing cognitive decline in an elderly patient with previously stable bipolar illness. Many disorders can contribute to cognitive and behavioral deterioration in this population, including reversible causes, particularly delirium and psychiatric illness, and irreversible structural or progressive processes including vascular disease, dementia with Lewy bodies, normal-pressure hydrocephalus, and frontotemporal dementia.

---

Title: Psychopharmacology of tic disorders in children and adolescents.
Abstract: Tic disorders, including Tourette syndrome, affect 0.5-3% of children and adolescents, causing significant functional impairment and psychosocial distress. Current pharmacological interventions demonstrate variable efficacy and tolerability profiles, necessitating evidence-based treatment guidelines. This comprehensive review synthesizes data from randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series published between 1990-2010 examining psychopharmacological treatments for pediatric tic disorders. We systematically analyzed 47 studies encompassing 2,847 patients aged 6-18 years with chronic tic disorders or Tourette syndrome. Traditional antipsychotics (haloperidol, pimozide) demonstrated robust tic reduction (40-60% improvement) but showed high rates of extrapyramidal side effects (28-45%) and cognitive dulling. Atypical antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, exhibited comparable efficacy (35-55% tic reduction) with improved tolerability profiles, though metabolic side effects remained concerning. Alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, guanfacine) provided moderate tic improvement (25-40%) with favorable side effect profiles, particularly beneficial for patients with comorbid ADHD. Emerging treatments including topiramate and botulinum toxin injections showed promise in preliminary studies but require further investigation. Treatment selection should consider tic severity, functional impairment, comorbid conditions, and individual risk-benefit profiles. Non-pharmacological interventions including behavioral therapy warrant consideration as first-line treatments for mild-moderate tics. Future research should focus on personalized treatment approaches, long-term safety data, and novel therapeutic targets to optimize outcomes while minimizing adverse effects in this vulnerable population.

---

Title: "Novel Psychopharmacology for Depressive Disorders".
Abstract: Depression affects over 300 million individuals worldwide, yet current antidepressant therapies demonstrate limited efficacy and significant latency to therapeutic response. Traditional monoaminergic approaches fail in approximately 30-40% of patients, necessitating exploration of novel pharmacological targets. This study investigated the therapeutic potential of selective modulation of AMPA receptor positive allosteric modulators (AMPAkines) combined with low-dose ketamine in treatment-resistant depression models. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were subjected to chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) for 6 weeks to induce depression-like behaviors. Animals were randomized into four groups: vehicle control, ketamine alone (2.5 mg/kg), AMPAkine CX717 alone (10 mg/kg), and combination therapy. Behavioral assessments included forced swim test, sucrose preference test, and elevated plus maze. Neurochemical analyses measured hippocampal BDNF, synaptic plasticity markers, and glutamate receptor expression via Western blot and RT-PCR. Combination therapy significantly reduced immobility time in forced swim test (p<0.001) and restored sucrose preference (p<0.01) compared to monotherapies. Hippocampal BDNF expression increased 2.3-fold with combination treatment, while GluA1 and PSD-95 protein levels were significantly elevated. Synergistic effects were observed in long-term potentiation enhancement and dendritic spine density restoration. The combination demonstrated rapid-acting antidepressant properties with sustained effects lasting 14 days post-treatment. These findings suggest that AMPAkine-ketamine combination therapy represents a promising novel approach for treatment-resistant depression, potentially offering superior efficacy through complementary glutamatergic mechanisms promoting synaptic plasticity and neuroplasticity.

---

Title: The neuropharmacology of sleep paralysis hallucinations: serotonin 2A activation and a novel therapeutic drug.
Abstract: Sleep paralysis (SP) is a parasomnia characterized by temporary inability to move accompanied by vivid and often terrifying hallucinations, affecting 7-8% of the general population. While the cholinergic mechanisms underlying SP atonia are well-established, the neuropharmacological basis of SP hallucinations remains poorly understood. Recent evidence suggests involvement of serotonergic pathways, particularly 5-HT2A receptors, which mediate hallucinogenic effects of psychedelics. This study investigated the role of 5-HT2A receptor activation in SP hallucinations and evaluated a novel therapeutic intervention. We administered the 5-HT2A agonist 25I-NBOMe (0.3 mg/kg) to sleep-deprived C57BL/6 mice during REM sleep rebound periods and measured behavioral correlates of hallucination-like states using the head-twitch response (HTR) paradigm modified for paralyzed animals. SP episodes were induced using the REM sleep fragmentation protocol combined with physostigmine administration. 5-HT2A activation significantly increased HTR frequency during SP episodes (p<0.001) and enhanced c-Fos expression in visual and auditory cortices. The selective 5-HT2A antagonist MDL 11939 (2 mg/kg) reduced both HTR responses and hallucination-associated cortical activity by 68% compared to vehicle controls. Chronic MDL 11939 treatment over 14 days decreased SP episode frequency from 4.2±0.8 to 1.6±0.4 episodes per week in a clinical pilot study of 24 recurrent SP patients. These findings demonstrate that 5-HT2A receptor hyperactivation contributes to SP hallucinations and suggest that selective 5-HT2A antagonists may represent a promising therapeutic approach for managing this distressing parasomnia.

Title: On the prescribing analyst.
Abstract: The integration of psychopharmacological interventions within psychoanalytic practice has generated considerable debate regarding therapeutic boundaries and analytic neutrality. This study examined the clinical outcomes and transference dynamics when psychoanalysts directly prescribe psychotropic medications to their analysands, compared to traditional referral models. We conducted a retrospective analysis of 127 patients in psychoanalysis over a 5-year period (2008-2013), comparing three groups: patients whose analysts prescribed medications directly (n=41), patients referred to psychiatrists for medication management (n=43), and medication-naive controls (n=43). Treatment outcomes were assessed using standardized measures including the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale, Global Assessment of Functioning, and detailed process notes analyzing transference manifestations. Patients with prescribing analysts demonstrated significantly greater treatment retention (89% vs. 67%, p<0.01) and showed equivalent symptomatic improvement compared to the referral group. However, distinct transference patterns emerged, including heightened idealization (43% vs. 18%) and increased somatic preoccupations during sessions (62% vs. 31%). Process analysis revealed that the dual role created unique opportunities for exploring the patient's relationship to dependency and authority, though it also generated more complex countertransference reactions in analysts. These findings suggest that the prescribing analyst model may enhance therapeutic alliance and retention while introducing novel transference configurations that require careful analytic attention. The integration of prescribing responsibilities appears clinically viable when accompanied by rigorous self-analysis and appropriate training in psychopharmacology.

Title: On a Very Basic Behavior in Science: Importance and Issues with Quotations.
Abstract: This journal, founded in 1968, not only has a publication history of more than 5 decades but also stands for a distinct scientific focus in psychiatric pharmacology or psychopharmacology: the research on medication treatments for mental disorders. At some point, it is worth taking a broader look at what we are doing in psychiatry or in medicine in general. That is why, in this issue, we are publishing a study by Christopher Baethge 1 that typically may not fit into the scope of Pharmacopsychiatry. As a psychiatrist and an editor, Baethge carried out an investigation in an underresearched area: the accuracy and significance of citations in psychiatric journals.

Title: Behavioral barcoding in the cloud: embracing data-intensive digital phenotyping in neuropharmacology.
Abstract: For decades, studying the behavioral effects of individual drugs and genetic mutations has been at the heart of efforts to understand and treat nervous system disorders. High-throughput technologies adapted from other disciplines (e.g., high-throughput chemical screening, genomics) are changing the scale of data acquisition in behavioral neuroscience. Massive behavioral datasets are beginning to emerge, particularly from zebrafish labs, where behavioral assays can be performed rapidly and reproducibly in 96-well, high-throughput format. Mining these datasets and making comparisons across different assays are major challenges for the field. Here, we review behavioral barcoding, a process by which complex behavioral assays are reduced to a string of numeric features, facilitating analysis and comparison within and across datasets.

Title: Neuroethics and psychiatry.
Abstract: The rapid advancement of neuroscience technologies and their clinical applications in psychiatry has generated unprecedented ethical challenges that require systematic examination. This review synthesizes current literature on neuroethical considerations in psychiatric practice, focusing on emerging diagnostic and therapeutic modalities. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of peer-reviewed publications from 2000-2008, examining ethical frameworks applied to neuroimaging-based diagnosis, deep brain stimulation, transcranial magnetic stimulation, and pharmacogenomic approaches in psychiatric treatment. Key databases including PubMed, PsycINFO, and Cochrane Library were searched using terms related to neuroethics, psychiatric treatment, and moral considerations in neuroscience applications. Our analysis reveals five primary ethical domains: patient autonomy and informed consent in the context of altered mental states, privacy and confidentiality of neurobiological data, justice in access to expensive neurotechnologies, potential for coercion in involuntary treatment scenarios, and the fundamental question of personal identity when directly modulating brain function. Neuroimaging presents particular challenges regarding predictive information and its implications for insurance coverage and employment. Deep brain stimulation and other invasive procedures raise concerns about personality changes and authentic selfhood. The integration of pharmacogenomics introduces questions about genetic discrimination and therapeutic obligation. Current regulatory frameworks appear insufficient to address these evolving challenges. We conclude that psychiatry requires specialized neuroethical guidelines that balance therapeutic innovation with protection of vulnerable populations, emphasizing the need for interdisciplinary collaboration between clinicians, ethicists, and policymakers to establish comprehensive governance structures.

Title: In vivo functional neurochemistry of human cortical cholinergic function during visuospatial attention.
Abstract: Cortical acetylcholine is involved in key cognitive processes such as visuospatial attention. Dysfunction in the cholinergic system has been described in a number of neuropsychiatric disorders. Levels of brain acetylcholine can be pharmacologically manipulated, but it is not possible to directly measure it in vivo in humans. However, key parts of its biochemical cascade in neural tissue, such as choline, can be measured using magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS). There is evidence that levels of choline may be an indirect but proportional measure of acetylcholine availability in brain tissue. In this study, we measured relative choline levels in the parietal cortex using functional (event-related) MRS (fMRS) during performance of a visuospatial attention task, with a modelling approach verified using simulated data. We describe a task-driven interaction effect on choline concentration, specifically driven by contralateral attention shifts. Our results suggest that choline MRS has the potential to serve as a proxy of brain acetylcholine function in humans.

Title: Understanding schizoaffective disorder: from psychobiology to psychosocial functioning.
Abstract: Psychobiologic evidence and psychosocial functioning in patients with schizoaffective disorder suggest that the disease may be a distinct disorder, a variant of schizophrenia or affective disorders, the comorbidity of schizophrenia and a mood disorder, or an intermediate disorder on a spectrum that ranges from schizophrenia to mood disorders. These data, although inconclusive, contribute to clinicians' understanding of the etiology of the disorder. Further research may lead to an increased understanding of the disorder, improved treatment, and, ultimately, better outcomes.

Title: A review of the neuropharmacological properties of khat.
Abstract: The psychostimulant khat (Catha edulis Forsk), is a herbal drug cultivated and chewed as a recreational and socializing drug in East Africa and the Arabian Peninsula for centuries. Due to increasing air transportation and the loosening of customs restrictions, it is now readily available in the Western Countries mainly used by immigrants from khat growing areas causing a concern to policy-makers. We conducted this review to further gain an insight to the neuropharmacological effects of khat. PubMed search engine with key terms 'khat' or 'qat' or 'mirra' or'qaad/jaad' or 'cathinone' was used to obtain articles relevant to khat chewing. In total 284 English written articles published from 1959 to 2007 were screened. Most of the studies focused on cathinone, the postulated active psychostimulant alkaloid in khat. There were few studies which investigated the entire plant extract in either in vitro or animal studies. In the majority of the studies it was reported that both cathinone and cathine, another psychoactive constituent, have actions that are similar to those of amphetamine. It seems that the well investigated khat alkaloids have many features similar to amphetamines; however there is a need for a more thorough examination of khat itself in well designed in vitro, animal and human studies with a range of comparator drugs before confirming the claim that khat is a "natural amphetamine".

Title: Neurophamacology of yawning.
Abstract: Yawning is a stereotyped behavioral pattern observed across vertebrate species, yet its underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. While traditionally associated with fatigue or boredom, emerging evidence suggests yawning serves important physiological functions including thermoregulation and arousal state transitions. This study investigated the neurotransmitter systems mediating yawning behavior using pharmacological manipulation in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-12 per group). Yawning frequency was quantified over 60-minute observation periods following systemic administration of selective receptor agonists and antagonists. Dopaminergic modulation was examined using apomorphine (0.05-0.8 mg/kg), haloperidol (0.1-1.0 mg/kg), and quinpirole (0.01-0.1 mg/kg). Cholinergic effects were assessed with physostigmine (0.1-0.5 mg/kg) and atropine (1-10 mg/kg). Additional experiments examined GABAergic, serotonergic, and opioidergic contributions. Low-dose apomorphine significantly increased yawning frequency (312% of control, p<0.001), an effect blocked by haloperidol pretreatment. Quinpirole produced a biphasic response with maximal yawning at 0.05 mg/kg. Physostigmine dose-dependently enhanced yawning behavior, while atropine produced modest inhibition. GABAA receptor activation with muscimol suppressed spontaneous yawning by 67% (p<0.01). Naloxone pretreatment potentiated apomorphine-induced yawning, suggesting tonic opioidergic inhibition. These findings demonstrate that yawning is regulated by complex interactions between dopaminergic D2/D3 receptors, cholinergic muscarinic pathways, and modulatory GABAergic and opioidergic systems. Understanding yawning neuropharmacology may provide insights into arousal disorders and serve as a behavioral biomarker for dopaminergic function in neurological conditions.

Title: Discovery of novel tetrahydro-pyrazolo [4,3-c] pyridines for the treatment of neuropathic pain: synthesis and neuropharmacology.
Abstract: Neuropathic pain represents a significant therapeutic challenge with limited treatment options displaying both efficacy and tolerability. Voltage-gated sodium channels, particularly Nav1.7 and Nav1.8, have emerged as promising targets for neuropathic pain management due to their preferential expression in nociceptive neurons. In this study, we report the discovery and characterization of a novel series of tetrahydro-pyrazolo[4,3-c]pyridine derivatives as selective sodium channel modulators. A library of 47 compounds was synthesized through a convergent approach involving cyclization of appropriately substituted hydrazines with β-ketoesters, followed by subsequent reduction and functionalization. Lead optimization focused on substitutions at the N1 and C6 positions to enhance potency and selectivity. Electrophysiological evaluation using whole-cell patch-clamp techniques on recombinant Nav channels revealed that compound 23c exhibited potent inhibition of Nav1.7 (IC₅₀ = 180 nM) and Nav1.8 (IC₅₀ = 95 nM) with >50-fold selectivity over cardiac Nav1.5. Structure-activity relationships indicated that electron-withdrawing groups at the C6 position and lipophilic substituents at N1 were crucial for activity. In vivo studies using the chronic constriction injury model in rats demonstrated significant anti-allodynic effects for compound 23c (ED₅₀ = 12 mg/kg, p.o.) without motor impairment or cardiovascular effects at therapeutic doses. These results establish tetrahydro-pyrazolo[4,3-c]pyridines as a promising new chemotype for developing next-generation analgesics targeting peripheral sodium channels in neuropathic pain conditions.
