Title: Pathological laughing and crying : epidemiology, pathophysiology and treatment.
Abstract: Pathological laughing and crying (PLC), also known as pseudobulbar affect, is a neuropsychiatric syndrome characterized by involuntary, inappropriate emotional outbursts that are incongruent with underlying mood. This condition significantly impacts quality of life in patients with various neurological disorders, yet remains underdiagnosed and undertreated. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of epidemiological studies, neuroimaging investigations, and clinical trials published between 1990-2007 to synthesize current understanding of PLC pathophysiology and therapeutic approaches. Epidemiological data indicate PLC prevalence ranges from 10-53% in stroke patients, 10-15% in traumatic brain injury, 23-49% in multiple sclerosis, and up to 95% in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Neuroanatomical studies demonstrate that disruption of cortico-pontine-cerebellar pathways, particularly involving the anterior limb of the internal capsule and basis pontis, underlies PLC pathophysiology. Lesions affecting serotonergic and glutamatergic neurotransmission appear critical in symptom development. Treatment efficacy has been demonstrated with tricyclic antidepressants, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and combination dextromethorphan/quinidine therapy. Double-blind, placebo-controlled trials show significant symptom reduction with amitriptyline (50-75mg daily), citalopram (20-30mg daily), and dextromethorphan/quinidine (20/10mg twice daily). Response rates typically range from 60-85% across different therapeutic agents. Recognition of PLC as a distinct neurological entity with specific anatomical substrates and effective pharmacological treatments should encourage clinicians to screen for this treatable condition in at-risk neurological populations.

---

Title: Astra Award Lecture. The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
Abstract: 5-HT3 receptors have an exclusive neuronal location and evidence is presented of their involvement in behaviour. 5-HT3 receptor antagonists such as ondansetron, tropisetron and zacopride have provided the critical pharmacological tools to reveal a potent and efficacious ability to regulate disturbed behaviour. Thus the 5-HT3 receptor antagonists will restore to normal rodent and primate behaviour disturbed by increasing limbic dopamine function, aversive situations, cognitive impairments and drug abuse. The remarkable feature of their action is a failure to modify normal behaviour. This unique pharmacological signature has ensured a wide interest in the potential role of the 5-HT3 receptor antagonists in the treatment of schizophrenia, anxiety, age related memory impairment and the problems of withdrawal from drugs of abuse. The preclinical data and preliminary clinical observations are presented.

---

Title: Drug development in neuropsychopharmacology.
Abstract: Personalized medicine is still in its infancy concerning drug development in neuropsychopharmacology. Adequate biomarkers with clinical relevance to drug response and/or tolerability and safety largely remain to be identified. Possibly, this kind of personalized medicine will first gain clinical relevance in the dementias. The clinical relevance of the genotyping of drug-metabolizing enzymes as suggested by drug licensing authorities for the pharmacokinetic evaluation of medicinal products needs to be proven in sound clinical trials.

---

Title: Reduced computational models of serotonin synthesis, release, and reuptake.
Abstract: Multiscale computational models can provide systemic evaluation and prediction of neuropharmacological drug effects. To date, little computational modeling work has been done to bridge from intracellular to neuronal circuit level. A complex model that describes the intracellular dynamics of the presynaptic terminal of a serotonergic neuron has been previously proposed. By systematically perturbing the model's components, we identify the slow and fast dynamical components of the model, and the reduced slow or fast mode of the model is computationally significantly more efficient with accuracy not deviating much from the original model. The reduced fast-mode model is particularly suitable for incorporating into neurobiologically realistic spiking neuronal models, and hence for large-scale realistic computational simulations. We also develop user-friendly software based on the reduced models to allow scientists to rapidly test and predict neuropharmacological drug effects at a systems level.

---

Title: Understanding the effects of stimulant medications on cognition in individuals with attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder: a decade of progress.
Abstract: The use of stimulant drugs for the treatment of children with attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is one of the most widespread pharmacological interventions in child psychiatry and behavioral pediatrics. This treatment is well grounded on controlled studies showing efficacy of low oral doses of methylphenidate and amphetamine in reducing the behavioral symptoms of the disorder as reported by parents and teachers, both for the cognitive (inattention and impulsivity) and non-cognitive (hyperactivity) domains. Our main aim is to review the objectively measured cognitive effects that accompany the subjectively assessed clinical responses to stimulant medications. Recently, methods from the cognitive neurosciences have been used to provide information about brain processes that underlie the cognitive deficits of ADHD and the cognitive effects of stimulant medications. We will review some key findings from the recent literature, and then offer interpretations of the progress that has been made over the past decade in understanding the cognitive effects of stimulant medication on individuals with ADHD.

---

Title: Pharmacological MRI in animal models: a useful tool for 5-HT research?
Abstract: Pharmacological magnetic resonance imaging (phMRI) offers the potential to provide novel insights into the functioning of neurotransmitter systems and drug action in the central nervous system. To date, much of the neuropharmacological research that has applied phMRI techniques has focused on the dopaminergic system with relatively few studies into serotonergic function. In this article, we discuss the current capabilities of, and future potential for phMRI to address fundamental questions in serotonergic research using animal models. Firstly we review existing literature on the application of phMRI to the serotonergic system by exploring 3 broad research themes: (i) the functional anatomy of the serotonergic system; (ii) drug-receptor targeting and distribution; and (iii) disease models and drug development. Subsequently, we discuss the interpretation of phMRI data in terms of neuropharmacological action with a focus on issues specific to neuroimaging studies of the serotonergic system. Unlike other neuroimaging approaches such as positron emission tomography, phMRI methods do not currently offer sensitivity to markers of specific pharmacological action. However, they can provide in vivo markers of the neuropharmacological modulation of neuronal activity across the whole brain with unparalleled spatial and temporal resolution. Furthermore, due to the non-invasive nature of MRI, these markers are readily translatable to human studies. Whilst there are a number of constraints and limitations to phMRI methods that necessitate careful data interpretation, we argue that phMRI could become a valuable research tool in neuropharmacological studies of the serotonergic system.

---

Title: Differential effects of acute administration of SCH-23390, a D₁ receptor antagonist, and of ethanol on swimming activity, anxiety-related responses, and neurochemistry of zebrafish.
Abstract: **Background:** Dopaminergic neurotransmission plays a crucial role in motor behavior and anxiety-related responses across vertebrate species. The zebrafish (Danio rerio) has emerged as a valuable model for studying neuropsychopharmacology due to its conserved neurotransmitter systems and behavioral repertoire. While ethanol's effects on zebrafish behavior are well-documented, the specific contribution of D₁ dopamine receptors to locomotor and anxiety-like behaviors remains poorly understood. **Objective:** This study examined the comparative effects of D₁ receptor blockade and ethanol exposure on zebrafish swimming behavior, anxiety-related responses, and brain neurochemistry. **Methods:** Adult zebrafish were acutely exposed to SCH-23390 (10, 50, 100 μg/L), ethanol (0.25%, 0.5%, 1.0% v/v), or vehicle control for 60 minutes. Swimming activity was assessed using automated video tracking, while anxiety-related behaviors were evaluated in a novel tank diving test. Whole-brain levels of dopamine, serotonin, and their metabolites were quantified using HPLC-ED. **Results:** SCH-23390 dose-dependently reduced total swimming distance (F₂,₄₅=12.4, p<0.001) and average velocity, while increasing bottom-dwelling time, indicative of heightened anxiety-like behavior. Ethanol produced biphasic effects, with low concentrations increasing activity and reducing anxiety-related responses, while high concentrations decreased locomotion. Neurochemically, SCH-23390 significantly elevated dopamine levels (142% of control, p<0.01) and reduced DOPAC/DA ratios, whereas ethanol decreased serotonin turnover at all concentrations tested. **Conclusions:** These findings demonstrate that D₁ receptor signaling is essential for normal locomotor activity and anxiety regulation in zebrafish, with distinct neurochemical profiles compared to ethanol-induced behavioral changes.

---

Title: Partial response, nonresponse, and relapse with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in major depression: a survey of current "next-step" practices.
Abstract: Despite the widespread use of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) as first-line treatment for major depressive disorder, substantial numbers of patients experience partial response, nonresponse, or relapse during treatment. However, limited data exist regarding clinicians' approaches to these treatment challenges in routine clinical practice. We conducted a nationwide survey of 847 psychiatrists and primary care physicians to examine current "next-step" strategies when patients fail to achieve or maintain adequate response to SSRI therapy. Participants completed a structured questionnaire detailing their management approaches for three clinical scenarios: partial response after 8-12 weeks of adequate SSRI dosing, complete nonresponse after 8-12 weeks, and relapse during maintenance therapy. Response rate was 73% (n=618). For partial responders, 54% of clinicians reported dose optimization as their preferred strategy, followed by augmentation with bupropion (23%) or lithium (18%). In cases of nonresponse, 67% favored switching to another SSRI, while 28% preferred switching to a non-SSRI antidepressant. For relapse scenarios, 71% increased the SSRI dose, 19% added augmentation therapy, and 10% switched medications. Significant differences emerged between psychiatrists and primary care physicians, with psychiatrists more likely to utilize augmentation strategies and combination therapies. Geographic variations were also observed, with urban practitioners showing greater willingness to employ complex regimens. These findings highlight substantial heterogeneity in clinical decision-making for SSRI treatment failures and underscore the need for evidence-based guidelines to optimize outcomes in this challenging patient population.

---

Title: Changes in striatal electroencephalography and neurochemistry induced by kainic acid seizures are modified by dopamine receptor antagonists.
Abstract: The excitotoxic effects of kainic acid (KA) involve complex interactions between glutamatergic and dopaminergic systems, particularly in the striatum where these neurotransmitter pathways extensively converge. This study investigated whether dopamine receptor antagonists could modulate KA-induced electrophysiological and neurochemical alterations in the rat striatum. Adult male Wistar rats (n=8 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of KA (10 mg/kg) alone or in combination with the D1 receptor antagonist SCH23390 (0.5 mg/kg) or the D2 receptor antagonist haloperidol (1.0 mg/kg). Striatal electroencephalographic activity was continuously monitored for 6 hours post-injection, and neurochemical analyses were performed using high-performance liquid chromatography to measure dopamine, DOPAC, HVA, and GABA concentrations at 24 hours. KA administration produced characteristic epileptiform discharges in the striatum within 30 minutes, accompanied by significant depletion of striatal dopamine (-67%, p<0.001) and GABA (-43%, p<0.01) levels. Pretreatment with SCH23390 significantly attenuated the duration and amplitude of KA-induced seizure activity and partially protected against dopamine depletion (-31%, p<0.05 vs KA alone). Haloperidol pretreatment showed less pronounced protective effects on both electrophysiological and neurochemical parameters. These findings suggest that D1 receptor-mediated mechanisms contribute to KA-induced striatal excitotoxicity, and that selective D1 antagonism may offer neuroprotection against glutamate-mediated seizure damage in this brain region.

---

Title: Role of glutamate and dopamine receptors in the psychopharmacological profile of the indole alkaloid psychollatine.
Abstract: The indole alkaloid psychollatine, isolated from Psychotria species, has demonstrated psychoactive properties in traditional ethnobotanical preparations, yet its mechanism of action remains poorly characterized. This study investigated the neuropharmacological profile of psychollatine with particular emphasis on glutamate and dopamine receptor interactions. Psychollatine was purified from Psychotria carthaginensis using standard chromatographic techniques. Receptor binding assays were performed using rat brain membrane preparations to evaluate affinity for NMDA, AMPA, kainate, D1, and D2 receptor subtypes. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity, prepulse inhibition, and conditioned avoidance response in male Sprague-Dawley rats following intraperitoneal administration of psychollatine (1-30 mg/kg). Electrophysiological recordings from hippocampal slices examined effects on glutamatergic neurotransmission. Psychollatine exhibited moderate binding affinity for NMDA receptors (Ki = 8.2 μM) and D2 receptors (Ki = 12.4 μM), with minimal affinity for other receptor subtypes tested. Behaviorally, psychollatine (10-30 mg/kg) produced dose-dependent hyperlocomotion and disrupted prepulse inhibition, effects that were attenuated by co-administration of the D2 antagonist haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg) but not the NMDA antagonist MK-801. Electrophysiological studies revealed psychollatine significantly enhanced glutamate-induced excitatory postsynaptic potentials in CA1 pyramidal neurons. These findings suggest that psychollatine's psychoactive effects involve complex interactions between glutamatergic and dopaminergic systems, with primary behavioral effects mediated through dopamine D2 receptor activation. Further investigation of this novel indole alkaloid may provide insights into glutamate-dopamine interactions underlying psychosis-related behaviors.

---

Title: Current and Future Neuropharmacological Options for the Treatment of Essential Tremor.
Abstract: Essential tremor (ET) is the most prevalent movement disorder, affecting approximately 1% of the global population and up to 5% of individuals over 65 years of age. Despite its high prevalence, current pharmacological treatments remain limited and often provide suboptimal symptom control. This comprehensive review examines the current neuropharmacological landscape for ET management and explores emerging therapeutic targets. Traditional first-line treatments include propranolol, a non-selective β-adrenergic antagonist, and primidone, an anticonvulsant, which demonstrate moderate efficacy in 50-70% of patients but are associated with significant side effects limiting long-term adherence. Second-line options such as topiramate, gabapentin, and benzodiazepines show variable effectiveness with considerable inter-individual variability. Recent mechanistic insights into ET pathophysiology have identified cerebellar dysfunction and altered GABAergic/glutamatergic neurotransmission as key therapeutic targets. Novel approaches under investigation include T-type calcium channel modulators, particularly zonisamide and 3,4-diaminopyridine, which have shown promising results in early-phase clinical trials. GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulators and metabotropic glutamate receptor antagonists represent additional emerging strategies. Furthermore, personalized medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenomic profiling may optimize treatment selection and dosing strategies. Deep brain stimulation remains the most effective intervention for medication-refractory cases, though surgical risks limit widespread application. The integration of precision medicine principles with novel pharmacological targets offers considerable promise for improving therapeutic outcomes in ET patients, potentially transforming the treatment paradigm for this common yet undertreated neurological condition.

---

Title: Predicting therapeutic efficacy - experimental pain in human subjects.
Abstract: The pharmaceutical industry faces tough times. Despite tremendous advances in the science and technology of new lead identification and optimization, attrition rates for novel drug candidates making it into the clinic remain unacceptably high. A seamless boundary between basic preclinical and clinical arms of the discovery process, embodying the concept of 'translational research' is viewed by many as the way forward. The rational application of human experimental pain models in early clinical development is reviewed. Capsaicin, UV-irradiation and electrical stimulation methods have each been used to establish experimental hyperalgesia in Phase-I human volunteers and the application of these approaches is discussed in the context of several pharmacological examples. However, data generated from such studies must be integrated into a well-conceived and executed series of Phase-II efficacy trials in patients in order to derive maximal benefit. The challenges involved in optimal Phase-II/III trial design are reviewed with specific attention to the issues of sample size and placebo response. Finally, the application and potential of cortical EEG studies are discussed as an objective alternative to more conventional pain assessment tools with specific examples of how this technique has been applied to the study of NSAID and opiate-based therapeutics.

---

Title: The golden years: a tribute to Erminio Costa.
Abstract: In June of 1968 the NIMH established the Laboratory of Preclinical Pharmacology (LPP) under the leadership of Erminio Costa at St. Elizabeths Hospital in Washington, DC. From its inception until 1985 more than 170 scientists produced over 800 scientific publications that largely influenced the direction of neuroscience research. Under Dr. Costa's scientific guidance LPP made numerous discoveries that contributed to the understanding of neuronal function and push the fledging field of neuroscience forward. Methods were developed to measure the turnover rate of catecholamines, serotonin, acetylcholine and GABA, and to assess the dynamic state of neuronal stores of peptides; the regulation of tyrosine hydroxylase activity was revealed; the molecular mechanisms underlying neurotransmitter receptor signaling and regulation were explored; the concept that opioid peptides function as neuromodulators in structures that are not involved in pain threshold regulation was proposed; the role of GABAergic transmission for the action of benzodiazepines was pioneered; the endogenous mechanisms operative in opiate tolerance were elucidated; and approaches to measure mRNA and evaluate its regulation were introduced. As a tribute to Dr. Costa, this article presents the initial journey and the many contributions to teaching and research that he made from 1963 to 1985, a period that I worked with him and call it "the golden years".

---

Title: The psychopharmacology of brain vascular disease/poststroke depression.
Abstract: Poststroke depression (PSD) affects approximately 30% of stroke survivors and represents a complex neuropsychiatric condition involving disrupted neural circuits, neurotransmitter imbalances, and inflammatory processes. While traditional antidepressants show variable efficacy in PSD, the underlying cerebrovascular pathology may alter pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties of psychotropic medications. This review examines the current evidence for psychopharmacological interventions in PSD and their mechanisms of action in the context of compromised cerebral vasculature. We conducted a systematic analysis of randomized controlled trials and observational studies published between 2010-2018, focusing on selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), tricyclic antidepressants, and novel therapeutic approaches in stroke patients with depression. Seventeen studies encompassing 2,847 patients were analyzed. SSRIs, particularly escitalopram and sertraline, demonstrated superior efficacy compared to placebo (effect size 0.62, 95% CI 0.44-0.80, p<0.001) with response rates of 65-72% versus 35-42% for placebo. Importantly, early initiation within 3 months poststroke showed enhanced treatment response and potential neuroprotective effects. Tricyclic antidepressants showed comparable efficacy but increased cardiovascular risk in this vulnerable population. Emerging evidence suggests that antidepressants may promote neuroplasticity through BDNF upregulation and reduce neuroinflammation via cytokine modulation. Blood-brain barrier disruption following stroke may enhance drug penetration but also increase susceptibility to adverse effects. These findings support early, targeted psychopharmacological intervention in PSD, with SSRIs as first-line therapy, while highlighting the need for personalized treatment approaches considering individual cerebrovascular risk profiles.

---

Title: Early phytocannabinoid chemistry to endocannabinoids and beyond.
Abstract: Isolation and structure elucidation of most of the major cannabinoid constituents--including Δ(9)-tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ(9)-THC), which is the principal psychoactive molecule in Cannabis sativa--was achieved in the 1960s and 1970s. It was followed by the identification of two cannabinoid receptors in the 1980s and the early 1990s and by the identification of the endocannabinoids shortly thereafter. There have since been considerable advances in our understanding of the endocannabinoid system and its function in the brain, which reveal potential therapeutic targets for a wide range of brain disorders.

---

Title: The nature of dopamine dysfunction in schizophrenia and what this means for treatment.
Abstract: Dopamine dysfunction has long been implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology, yet the precise nature of these alterations and their therapeutic implications remain incompletely understood. We conducted a comprehensive meta-analysis of neuroimaging studies examining dopamine function in schizophrenia patients and investigated the relationship between dopamine dysfunction patterns and treatment response. We analyzed data from 847 patients with schizophrenia and 623 healthy controls across 34 positron emission tomography and single-photon emission computed tomography studies measuring dopamine synthesis capacity, receptor availability, and synaptic dopamine levels in striatal and extrastriatal regions. Additionally, we examined treatment response data from 312 patients receiving antipsychotic medications over 12 weeks. Results demonstrated significant elevations in presynaptic dopamine synthesis capacity in the associative striatum (effect size = 0.79, p < 0.001) and reduced dopamine D1 receptor availability in prefrontal cortex (effect size = -0.65, p < 0.001) in patients versus controls. Dopamine dysfunction was most pronounced in treatment-resistant patients, who showed 23% greater striatal dopamine synthesis compared to treatment-responsive patients (p = 0.008). Prefrontal D1 receptor deficits correlated significantly with negative symptom severity (r = -0.42, p < 0.001) and cognitive impairment (r = 0.38, p = 0.003). These findings suggest schizophrenia involves a dual dopamine dysfunction pattern characterized by striatal hyperactivity and prefrontal hypoactivity. The magnitude of these alterations predicts treatment resistance and symptom domains, supporting the development of regionally-targeted therapeutic approaches that address both striatal dopamine excess and prefrontal dopamine deficiency in schizophrenia treatment.

---

Title: Love and attachment: the psychobiology of social bonding.
Abstract: Social bonding represents a fundamental aspect of human behavior with significant implications for mental health and neuropsychiatric disorders. While the neurobiological mechanisms underlying attachment have been extensively studied in animal models, translational research examining the psychobiology of human social bonding remains limited. This review synthesizes current understanding of the neurochemical and neuroanatomical substrates of love and attachment, integrating findings from preclinical studies with emerging human neuroimaging data. We examined peer-reviewed literature from 1990-2008 focusing on the roles of oxytocin, vasopressin, dopamine, and endogenous opioids in pair bonding and attachment behaviors. Prairie vole studies demonstrate that oxytocin and vasopressin receptor expression in the nucleus accumbens and ventral pallidum critically regulate pair bond formation, while dopaminergic signaling in mesolimbic reward circuits mediates the reinforcing aspects of social contact. Human neuroimaging studies reveal activation of similar reward pathways during romantic attachment, including the ventral tegmental area, caudate nucleus, and anterior cingulate cortex. Oxytocin administration enhances trust, empathy, and social recognition in healthy volunteers, supporting its role in human bonding. Dysregulation of these systems may contribute to attachment disorders, depression, and autism spectrum disorders. The neurochemistry of love involves complex interactions between neuropeptide, monoamine, and opioid systems that evolved to promote selective social bonds essential for reproductive success and offspring care. Understanding these mechanisms provides insight into the biological basis of human relationships and offers potential therapeutic targets for disorders characterized by impaired social functioning.

---

Title: Neurochemical substrates linked to impulsive and compulsive phenotypes in addiction: A preclinical perspective.
Abstract: Drug compulsion manifests in some but not all individuals and implicates multifaceted processes including failures in top-down cognitive control as drivers for the hazardous pursuit of drug use in some individuals. As a closely related construct, impulsivity encompasses rash or risky behaviour without foresight and underlies most forms of drug taking behaviour, including drug use during adverse emotional states (i.e., negative urgency). While impulsive behavioural dimensions emerge from drug-induced brain plasticity, burgeoning evidence suggests that impulsivity also predates the emergence of compulsive drug use. Although the neural substrates underlying the apparently causal relationship between trait impulsivity and drug compulsion are poorly understood, significant advances have come from the interrogation of defined limbic cortico-striatal circuits involved in motivated behaviour and response inhibition, together with chemical neuromodulatory influences from the ascending neurotransmitter systems. We review what is presently known about the neurochemical mediation of impulsivity, in its various forms, and ask whether commonalities exist in the neurochemistry of compulsive drug-motivated behaviours that might explain individual risk for addiction.

---

Title: From the search for a molecular code of memory to the role of neurotransmitters: a historical perspective.
Abstract: The history of the neurochemistry of mnesic processes can be divided into two main periods: the first (1946-1978) was inspired by the results of molecular genetics, providing evidence for storage of hereditary information in the DNA of genes. Therefore, the chemical bases for memory were investigated in the macromolecules of the brain. Such attempts were relatively unsuccessful, which led to a second period (starting in 1978) with the research emphasizing, in a less ambitious way, the role of the molecular correlates of mnesic processes, in particular in the main transmitter systems of the brain.

---

Title: The footfault test as a screening tool in the 6-hydroxydopamine rat model of Parkinson's disease.
Abstract: The 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) rat model is widely used to study Parkinson's disease (PD), but current behavioral assessments often lack sensitivity to detect mild-to-moderate dopaminergic deficits. The footfault test, which measures limb placement errors during grid walking, may provide a more sensitive screening tool for motor impairments in this model. We evaluated the footfault test as a behavioral screening method in unilaterally 6-OHDA-lesioned rats and compared its sensitivity to established tests including cylinder test and amphetamine-induced rotations. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) received unilateral 6-OHDA injections into the medial forebrain bundle or sham surgery. Behavioral testing was performed at 2, 4, and 6 weeks post-lesion using the footfault test (errors per 50 steps on wire grid), cylinder test (forelimb asymmetry), and amphetamine-induced rotations. Tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry confirmed lesion extent in the substantia nigra and striatum. The footfault test detected significant contralateral forelimb deficits as early as 2 weeks post-lesion in 6-OHDA rats compared to controls (p<0.01), with deficits correlating strongly with striatal dopamine depletion (r=0.78, p<0.001). Footfault sensitivity exceeded that of the cylinder test, detecting impairments in rats with 40-60% striatal dopamine loss, while cylinder test required >70% depletion. Amphetamine rotations showed comparable sensitivity but greater variability. The footfault test provides a simple, sensitive, and quantitative behavioral screening tool for detecting motor deficits in the 6-OHDA rat model, particularly useful for assessing mild-to-moderate dopaminergic lesions that may be missed by conventional tests.

---

Title: A flexible system for hands-free intracranial microinjection.
Abstract: Neuroscience research projects often use intracranial (IC) microinfusions to target drug delivery to specific brain areas during behavioral testing. These experiments require accurate and precisely-timed delivery of small volumes. We present here a stepper motor-powered micropump assembly for such delivery. This system is hands-free, does not use a potentially leaky fluid swivel or use long delivery tubes that are subject to peristaltic forces during animal movements, and has been applied in combination with other paradigms. This micropump system reliably delivers a wide range of fluid volumes (e.g. 50 nL to 1 microL in tissue or greater for intraventricular injections) bilaterally from two independent, commercially available microsyringes through standard surgically implanted guide cannulae. It is easy to build and disassemble for cleaning or changing microsyringes. This system can also be used for a variety of purposes, e.g. intracranial self-administration, place conditioning, and many more, with the advantage that it provides a way to gather important data in the seconds and minutes following IC microinfusion without disruption of the animal's behavior by handling.

Title: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptor redux: Discovery of accessories opens therapeutic vistas.
Abstract: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) have long been considered promising therapeutic targets for neurological and psychiatric disorders, yet clinical translation has been hampered by limited selectivity and off-target effects. Recent structural and functional studies have revealed that nAChRs exist in dynamic complexes with auxiliary proteins that modulate their pharmacology, trafficking, and signaling properties. Here, we systematically characterized the composition and functional impact of nAChR-associated protein complexes in human brain tissue and heterologous systems using proximity labeling proteomics, electrophysiology, and pharmacological profiling. We identified 47 high-confidence nAChR-interacting proteins, including previously uncharacterized chaperones, scaffolding proteins, and regulatory enzymes. Notably, the auxiliary protein NACHO (neuronal acetylcholine receptor-associated protein) enhanced α7 nAChR surface expression 3.2-fold and shifted sensitivity to the positive allosteric modulator PNU-120596 by 15-fold in hippocampal neurons. Co-expression of the scaffolding protein PSD-95 with α4β2 nAChRs increased receptor clustering and altered desensitization kinetics, reducing IC50 values for novel selective antagonists by 8-fold. Viral-mediated knockdown of key auxiliary proteins in mouse prefrontal cortex significantly impaired working memory performance and altered nicotine reward behaviors. These findings demonstrate that nAChR auxiliary proteins represent unexplored pharmacological targets that could enable development of more selective therapeutic interventions. Our results suggest that targeting nAChR-accessory protein interactions, rather than the receptors themselves, may overcome historical limitations in nAChR-based drug discovery and open new therapeutic avenues for addiction, cognitive disorders, and neuropsychiatric diseases.

---

Title: Expression of zinc transporter ZnT7 in mouse superior cervical ganglion.
Abstract: The superior cervical ganglion (SCG) neurons contain a considerable amount of zinc ions, but little is known about the zinc homeostasis in the SCG. It is known that zinc transporter 7 (ZnT7, Slc30a7), a member of the Slc30 ZnT family, is involved in mobilizing zinc ions from the cytoplasm into the Golgi apparatus. In the present study, we examined the expression and localization of ZnT7 and labile zinc ions in the mouse SCG using immunohistochemistry, Western blot and in vivo zinc selenium autometallography (AMG). Our immunohistochemical analysis revealed that the ZnT7 immunoreactivity in the SCG neurons was predominately present in the perinuclear region of the neurons, suggesting an affiliation to the Golgi apparatus. The Western blot results verified that ZnT7 protein was expressed in the mouse SCGs. The AMG reaction product was shown to have a similar distribution as ZnT7 immunoreactivity. These observations support the notion that ZnT7 may participate in zinc transport, storage, and incorporation of zinc into zinc-binding proteins in the Golgi apparatus of mouse SCG neurons.

Title: Neuronal and glial cell lines as model systems for studying P2Y receptor pharmacology.
Abstract: Investigation of the role of extracellular nucleotides in nervous system has been one of the main topics of the P2Y receptor research throughout the years. In parallel to numerous studies on primary culture systems, various neuronal and non-neuronal cell lines have been used to model in vitro the processes mediated by extracellular nucleotides. In this review article, a survey of expression profiles of G protein-coupled P2Y receptor subtypes in nervous-system-derived cell lines is presented, by analysing the receptor expression at the mRNA, protein, and functional level. The variability of receptor expression profiles in established cell lines is further discussed, bringing forward some general properties for neuronal and glial malignant cell lines.

Title: Lobeline inhibits the neurochemical and behavioral effects of amphetamine.
Abstract: Lobeline interacts with the dopamine transporter and vesicular monoamine transporter, presynaptic proteins involved in dopamine storage and release. This study used rodent models to assess lobeline-induced inhibition of the neurochemical and behavioral effects of amphetamine. Rat striatal slices were preloaded with [(3)H]dopamine and superfused with lobeline for 30 min, and then with d-amphetamine (0.03-3.00 microM) plus lobeline for 60 min. As predicted, lobeline (1-3 microM) intrinsically increased (3)H overflow but did not inhibit d-amphetamine-evoked (3)H overflow. Consequently, the effect of lobeline on d-amphetamine-evoked endogenous dopamine and dihydroxyphenylacetic acid overflow was assessed. Lobeline (0.1-1 microM) inhibited d-amphetamine (1 microM)-evoked dopamine overflow but did not inhibit electrically evoked (3)H overflow, indicating a selective inhibition of this effect of d-amphetamine. To determine whether the in vitro results translated into in vivo inhibition, the effect of lobeline (0.3-10.0 mg/kg) pretreatment on d-amphetamine (0.1-1.0 mg/kg)-induced hyperactivity in rats and on d-methamphetamine (0.1-3.0 mg/kg)-induced hyperactivity in mice was determined. Doses of lobeline that produced no effect alone attenuated the stimulant-induced hyperactivity. Lobeline also attenuated the discriminative stimulus properties of d-methamphetamine in rats. Acute, intermittent, or continuous in vivo administration of lobeline (1-30 mg/kg) did not deplete striatal dopamine content. Thus, lobeline inhibits amphetamine-induced neurochemical and behavioral effects, and is not toxic to dopamine neurons. These results support the hypothesis that lobeline redistributes dopamine pools within the presynaptic terminal, reducing pools available for amphetamine-induced release. Collectively, the results support a role for lobeline as a potential pharmacotherapy for psychostimulant abuse.

---

Title: Considerations in the choice of an antiepileptic drug in the treatment of epilepsy.
Abstract: The advent of multiple new antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) in the past 15 years has provided new options for clinicians treating patients with epilepsy, but has also created a need for guidelines in AED use. For selection of the most appropriate AED, the neurologist must know the pharmacological properties of the AEDs and has to balance their benefits and potential risks for the individual patient. A key consideration in AED selection is spectrum of efficacy for seizure types and epileptic syndrome. Other factors in AED selection include safety and tolerability, ease of use and titration rate, potential interactions, and efficacy in coexisting nonepileptic conditions. Comorbid conditions may favor or exclude the use of certain AEDs in a particular patient. Special considerations apply in specific epilepsy populations, such as women of childbearing age, children, and the elderly. This article analyzes the various factors involved in choosing an AED.

---

Title: The neuropsychopharmacology of fronto-executive function: monoaminergic modulation.
Abstract: We review the modulatory effects of the catecholamine neurotransmitters noradrenaline and dopamine on prefrontal cortical function. The effects of pharmacologic manipulations of these systems, sometimes in comparison with the indoleamine serotonin (5-HT), on performance on a variety of tasks that tap working memory, attentional-set formation and shifting, reversal learning, and response inhibition are compared in rodents, nonhuman primates, and humans using, in a behavioral context, several techniques ranging from microiontophoresis and single-cell electrophysiological recording to pharmacologic functional magnetic resonance imaging. Dissociable effects of drugs and neurotoxins affecting these monoamine systems suggest new ways of conceptualizing state-dependent fronto-executive functions, with implications for understanding the molecular genetic basis of mental illness and its treatment.

Title: Dr. Eugene Roberts.
Abstract: This is an obituary for Eugene Roberts (1920-2016), an outstanding neurobiochemist who was the first to report on the discovery of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) in the brain, and whose research focused on the important role of GABA in human health and disease.

Title: Neuropsychopharmacology: are we ready for a paradigm shift?
Abstract: The "paradigm shift" in the title of this article refers to molecular biology. The real challenge to molecular biologists interested in applying their methods to an understanding of psychiatric disorders involves the development of models that account for gene/environment/development interaction. Because of the existence of homologous animal models of addictive disorders, as well as a rich body of clinical observations and clinical and behavioral research, and recent data suggesting that chronic exposure to addictive drugs results in changes in gene expression in neurons from behaviorally relevant brain regions, addictive disorders offer a serendipitous model (to molecular biologists) that may have relevance to subsequent application in other areas of psychiatry and psychology.

---

Title: Abnormal neuronal morphology and neurochemistry in the auditory brainstem of Fmr1 knockout rats.
Abstract: Fragile X syndrome (FXS) is an inherited neurodevelopmental disorder affecting nearly one in 5000 newborn males and is a leading genetic cause of autism spectrum disorder. In addition to developmental delays and intellectual impairment, FXS is characterized by seizures, attention deficit, and hypersensitivity to visual, tactile and auditory stimuli. The Fmr1 gene encodes Fragile X mental retardation protein (FMRP), which is abundant in neurons, binds select mRNAs and functions as a negative regulator of mRNA translation. A deficiency in FMRP, as in FXS and Fmr1 knockout (KO) animals, results in neuronal dysmorphology and altered synaptic function. Additionally, there is evidence for disruption of GABAergic circuits in subjects lacking FMRP. Our previous studies demonstrated widespread expression of FMRP in human auditory brainstem neurons. Given this observation, we hypothesized that FMRP is highly expressed in rat auditory brainstem neurons and that the auditory hypersensitivity characteristic of FXS results from dysfunction of brainstem networks secondary to decreased expression of FMRP. In our investigation of postnatal day 50 (P50) control rats, we found that FMRP was widely expressed in neurons of the superior olivary complex (SOC). In P50 Fmr1 KO rats, many SOC neurons had a smaller soma when compared to controls, indicative of abnormal neuronal morphology. Additionally, neurons in the medial superior olive (MSO) were more round in Fmr1 KO rats. There was also reduced expression of glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD67) in neurons of the superior paraolivary nucleus (SPON) and a reduction in the number of calretinin-immunoreactive terminals associated with neurons of the medial nucleus of the trapezoid body (MNTB). Together, these findings support the conclusion that the auditory dysfunction characteristic of FXS arises, at least in part, from defective brainstem networks.

---

Title: NMDA antagonists: antiepileptic-neuroprotective drugs with diversified neuropharmacological profiles.
Abstract: In conclusion, NMDA antagonists as anticonvulsants are especially active in preventing the generalization of the behavioural and electrical seizures and display a typical spectrum of in vitro antiepileptiform activities. In addition, based on in vitro and in vivo limbic kindled studies, the drugs should be regarded more as an antiepileptiform than as an anticonvulsant drugs. As neuroprotective drugs, NMDA antagonists are effective against many types of neuronal injury and show a window of activity which does not exceed 1-2 h, thus suggesting an influence of NMDA receptors in the 'early' or 'acute' mechanisms of brain damage. Among NMDA antagonists, glycine antagonists or the morphinans dextromethorphan and dextrorphan showed a spectrum of antiepileptiform and neuroprotective activities broader than other NMDA antagonists. The primary pharmacological activities of NMDA antagonists are accompanied by some effects including perturbation of many sensory, psychological or motor processes. Typical behavioural and EEG changes were also induced by the drugs. In spite of the side-effects elicited by the drugs, differential effects detected among the various classes of NMDA antagonists (i.e. lack of induction of typical EEG-behavioural effects and of typical cortical neurotoxicity) might render some of these suitable for full clinical application as anticonvulsant-neuroprotective drugs.

---

Title: Achieving synaptically relevant pulses of neurotransmitter using PDMS microfluidics.
Abstract: Fast synaptic transmission is mediated by post-synaptic ligand-gated ion channels (LGICs) transiently activated by neurotransmitter released from pre-synaptic vesicles. Although disruption of synaptic transmission has been implicated in numerous neurological and psychiatric disorders, effective and practical methods for studying LGICs in vitro under synaptically relevant conditions are unavailable. Here, we describe a novel microfluidic approach to solution switching that allows for precise temporal control over the neurotransmitter transient while substantially increasing experimental throughput, flexibility, reproducibility, and cost-effectiveness. When this system was used to apply ultra-brief ( approximately 400micros) GABA pulses to recombinant GABA(A) receptors, members of the cys-loop family of LGICs, the resulting currents resembled hippocampal inhibitory post-synaptic currents (IPSCs) and differed from currents evoked by longer, conventional pulses, illustrating the importance of evaluating LGICs on a synaptic timescale. This methodology should therefore allow the effects of disease-causing mutations and allosteric modulators to be evaluated in vitro under physiologically relevant conditions.

---

Title: Neuropharmacology, pharmacogenetics and pharmacogenomics of aggression: The zebrafish model.
Abstract: The zebrafish (Danio rerio) is increasingly utilized as a powerful new model organism in neurobehavioral research. Aggression is a common symptom of many CNS disorders, has some genetic determinants and can be modulated pharmacologically in humans and animal model species. Mounting evidence suggests zebrafish as a useful tool to study neurobiology of aggression, and its pharmacological and genetic regulation. Here, we discuss mechanisms of zebrafish aggression and their pharmacological, pharmacogenetic and pharmacogenomic models, as well as recent developments and existing challenges in this field. We also emphasize the growing utility of zebrafish models in translational neuropharmacological research of aggression, fostering future discoveries of potential therapeutic agents for aggressive behavior.

---

Title: [Therapeutic drug monitoring in neuropsychopharmacology : Summary of the consensus guidelines 2017 of the TDM task force of the AGNP].
Abstract: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) has gained increasing importance in neuropsychopharmacology to optimize treatment outcomes and minimize adverse effects. The AGNP TDM task force developed updated consensus guidelines in 2017 to provide evidence-based recommendations for clinical practice. This summary reviews the key recommendations and their implementation in psychiatric treatment settings. The guidelines were developed through systematic literature review and expert consensus involving pharmacologists, psychiatrists, and laboratory specialists across German-speaking countries. Recommendations were categorized into four levels based on available evidence and clinical utility: strongly recommended, recommended, useful, and potentially useful. The 2017 guidelines provide specific recommendations for 60 psychotropic medications, including antidepressants, antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and anxiolytics. TDM is strongly recommended for tricyclic antidepressants, several atypical antipsychotics including clozapine and olanzapine, lithium, and anticonvulsant mood stabilizers. For newer antidepressants such as SSRIs and SNRIs, TDM is recommended in specific clinical situations including treatment-resistant cases, suspected non-adherence, drug interactions, and vulnerable populations. The guidelines emphasize proper timing of blood sampling, consideration of active metabolites, and interpretation within clinical context. Implementation of these evidence-based TDM recommendations can significantly improve therapeutic outcomes in psychiatric patients by enabling personalized dosing strategies, enhancing medication adherence monitoring, and reducing the risk of adverse drug reactions. Regular application of TDM principles represents a crucial step toward precision medicine in neuropsychopharmacology.

---

Title: High temporal resolution for in vivo monitoring of neurotransmitters in awake epileptic rats using brain microdialysis and capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection.
Abstract: Epilepsy is characterized by complex alterations in neurotransmitter dynamics during seizure events, yet conventional analytical methods lack the temporal resolution necessary to capture rapid neurochemical changes in vivo. We developed an integrated system combining brain microdialysis with capillary electrophoresis and laser-induced fluorescence detection (CE-LIF) to monitor glutamate, GABA, and glycine concentrations with unprecedented temporal resolution in awake, freely-moving epileptic rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with chronic hippocampal electrodes and microdialysis probes following pilocarpine-induced status epilepticus. Microdialysate samples were collected at 30-second intervals and analyzed using a custom-built CE-LIF system with 488-nm argon laser excitation and fluorescein isothiocyanate derivatization. The analytical system achieved detection limits of 0.8 nM for glutamate, 1.2 nM for GABA, and 0.6 nM for glycine, with separation completed within 2 minutes. During spontaneous seizures, glutamate concentrations increased 340±45% above baseline within 60 seconds of electrographic seizure onset, while GABA levels showed a delayed increase of 180±30% at 2-3 minutes post-ictal. Glycine remained relatively stable throughout seizure events. The glutamate/GABA ratio peaked at 4.2±0.8 during ictal periods compared to baseline values of 1.8±0.2. This high-resolution monitoring system provides critical insights into the temporal sequence of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitter release during epileptic seizures and represents a significant advancement in real-time neurochemical analysis for epilepsy research.

---

Title: A rodent model of appetitive discrimination with concomitant evaluation of anxiety-like behavior.
Abstract: The development of animal models that simultaneously assess multiple behavioral domains is crucial for understanding the complex interactions between cognitive function and emotional state in neuropsychiatric disorders. Existing paradigms typically evaluate appetitive learning and anxiety-related behaviors separately, limiting translational insights. We developed a novel behavioral apparatus combining elements of operant conditioning chambers with anxiety-testing environments to concurrently measure appetitive discrimination learning and anxiety-like behavior in rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were trained to discriminate between two auditory cues (2 kHz vs 8 kHz tones) for sucrose reinforcement while navigating an elevated platform with open and enclosed compartments. Discrimination performance was quantified by response accuracy and latency to lever press, while anxiety-like behavior was assessed through time spent in open versus enclosed areas and locomotor patterns. Animals successfully acquired the discrimination task over 14 days, reaching 85±7% accuracy by day 10. Rats with superior discrimination performance (>90% accuracy) showed significantly reduced anxiety-like behavior compared to poor performers (<70% accuracy), spending 23±4% versus 12±3% time in open areas, respectively (p<0.01). Administration of diazepam (1.0 mg/kg, i.p.) enhanced both discrimination learning and reduced anxiety measures, while amphetamine (1.5 mg/kg, i.p.) improved discrimination but increased anxiety-related behaviors. This paradigm provides a valuable tool for investigating the neurobiological substrates underlying cognition-emotion interactions and screening compounds with potential therapeutic effects on comorbid cognitive and anxiety disorders.

---

Title: Opinion on monoaminergic contributions to traits and temperament.
Abstract: Monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems have long been implicated in individual differences in personality traits and temperamental characteristics, yet their precise contributions remain contentious. This perspective synthesizes current evidence regarding dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic influences on dimensional models of personality, particularly focusing on extraversion, neuroticism, and conscientiousness within the five-factor framework. We critically evaluate findings from pharmacological challenge studies, neuroimaging investigations of monoamine transporter and receptor densities, and genetic association studies of polymorphisms affecting monoaminergic function. Converging evidence suggests that dopaminergic pathways, particularly within mesolimbic and mesocortical circuits, contribute substantially to reward sensitivity and approach-related behaviors underlying extraversion. Serotonergic function appears most robustly linked to emotional regulation and stress reactivity associated with neuroticism, with 5-HT1A receptor availability and serotonin transporter expression showing consistent associations across methodologies. Noradrenergic contributions appear more complex, with evidence for involvement in both arousal-related aspects of extraversion and anxiety-related components of neuroticism. However, effect sizes remain modest, and replication challenges persist, particularly for candidate gene associations. We propose that monoaminergic systems likely exert their influence on temperament through dynamic interactions rather than simple one-to-one mappings between neurotransmitter function and personality dimensions. Future research should prioritize polygenic approaches, longitudinal designs examining neurotransmitter-environment interactions, and integration of multiple levels of analysis to advance understanding of neurochemical contributions to individual differences in behavior and temperament.

---

Title: Traumatic brain injury: a review of practice management and recent advances.
Abstract: This review of traumatic brain injury encompasses its impact on society, pathophysiology, and rehabilitative management. Topics include mild traumatic brain injury, outcomes, prognosis, cognitive rehabilitation, behavioral management, and neurologic and medical complications. Emphasis is placed on clinically relevant topics that have had recent developments or have been historically difficult to treat. Neurologic complications discussed include seizure, balance, visual dysfunction, and spasticity. Medical complications discussed include neuroendocrine and pain issues.

---

Title: The psychopharmacology algorithm project at the Harvard South Shore Program: An algorithm for core symptoms of autism spectrum disorder in adults.
Abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is characterized by impaired social communication and restricted repetitive behaviors and interests. There are no FDA-approved medications for these core symptoms, and there are limited data regarding pharmacological management of ASD in adults. Here, the literature was reviewed in an effort to develop an algorithm for pharmacological management of core symptoms of ASD in adults. The literature search was conducted using PubMed. It was very difficult to distil a plausible algorithm from these data. Not included in this review are behavioral strategies, which are first-line. For instances when medication is being considered for management of core ASD symptoms in adults, the authors suggest starting with fluvoxamine as first-line, with possible consideration of a second SSRI trial if there is an inadequate or no response to fluvoxamine. The next step, if there is comorbid irritability, is to consider a second-generation antipsychotic. If there is no comorbid irritability, in the final step of the tentative algorithm, there are possible augmenting agents: propranolol, memantine, d-cycloserine, and oxytocin. Management of the symptoms of ASD requires a comprehensive treatment approach, and treatment planning must be individualized. Treatment of core ASD symptoms is not always desired. Further studies are needed to develop a stronger evidence base to support pharmacological management of core symptoms.

---

Title: Psychopharmacological possibilities in the acute disaster setting.
Abstract: The September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks and recent natural disasters have highlighted the urgent need for evidence-based psychopharmacological interventions in acute disaster settings. While psychological first aid remains the primary intervention, certain populations may benefit from targeted pharmacological support to prevent the development of chronic psychiatric sequelae. This review examines the current literature on acute pharmacological interventions following traumatic exposure and their potential applications in mass casualty events. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases from 1990-2004, focusing on randomized controlled trials and case series examining pharmacological interventions within 30 days of trauma exposure. Studies were evaluated for methodological quality and clinical applicability in disaster scenarios. Limited but promising evidence supports the use of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, particularly sertraline and paroxetine, in preventing post-traumatic stress disorder when initiated within 72 hours of exposure in high-risk individuals. Beta-blockers, specifically propranolol, show potential for disrupting traumatic memory consolidation when administered within 6 hours of the event, though replication studies are needed. Short-term benzodiazepine use may provide symptomatic relief but should be limited to 2-3 days due to concerns about dependence and potential interference with natural recovery processes. Atypical antipsychotics may be beneficial for individuals experiencing acute psychotic symptoms or severe agitation. While pharmacological interventions should not replace psychological support, targeted use of specific agents in carefully selected populations may reduce the risk of chronic psychiatric morbidity following mass traumatic events.

---

Title: Mass spectrometry imaging, an emerging technology in neuropsychopharmacology.
Abstract: Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry imaging (MALDI-MSI) represents a transformative analytical approach for investigating drug distribution and neurochemical changes in brain tissue with unprecedented spatial resolution. Traditional neuropsychopharmacological studies rely on tissue homogenization techniques that obscure regional drug localization and metabolite distribution patterns critical for understanding therapeutic mechanisms. This review synthesizes current applications of MALDI-MSI in neuropsychopharmacology research, examining methodological advances and their implications for drug discovery and development. We analyzed peer-reviewed publications from 2008-2014 utilizing MSI techniques for central nervous system drug studies, focusing on spatial resolution capabilities, quantitative accuracy, and biological relevance. MALDI-MSI enables direct detection and mapping of pharmaceuticals, neurotransmitters, and their metabolites at cellular-level resolution (10-50 μm) while preserving tissue architecture. Recent studies demonstrate successful imaging of antipsychotics, antidepressants, and anxiolytics across discrete brain regions, revealing heterogeneous distribution patterns not detectable through conventional methods. Quantitative improvements through internal standard incorporation and matrix optimization have enhanced analytical reliability, with detection limits reaching femtomolar concentrations for select compounds. Temporal pharmacokinetic studies show dynamic drug accumulation in target regions, providing mechanistic insights into therapeutic onset and duration. These findings establish MALDI-MSI as a powerful complement to traditional pharmacological approaches, offering unique capabilities for understanding drug action in complex neural networks. Integration of MSI technology with existing neuropsychopharmacological methodologies promises to accelerate drug development through improved target validation and biomarker identification, ultimately enhancing therapeutic strategies for psychiatric and neurological disorders.

---

Title: Trends in utilization of antiepileptic drugs in Denmark.
Abstract: To describe utilization of antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) in Denmark during 1993-2002, with special emphasis on oxcarbazepine, and to assess probable indications for AED use. We retrieved prescription data from Odense University Pharmacoepidemiological Database, in Funen County, Denmark (population in 2002: 472,869). Within each calendar year we estimated period prevalence, incidence rate and monotherapy rate. Based on co-medication we defined 'epilepsy' when only AEDs were prescribed, 'pain' with co-prescription of opioids, and 'mood disorder' with co-prescription of antipsychotics or antidepressants. We identified 15,604 AED users. The prevalence of using AED increased from 9.3 (95% CI, 9-9.5) to 12.1 (11.8-12.4)/1000 persons. The incidence rate increased from 1.4 (1.3-1.6) to 1.7 (1.6-1.9)/1000 personyears. The monotherapy rate was 79-82%. AED use for 'epilepsy' declined by 19.7%, whereas the proportion of 'pain' and 'mood disorder' treatment increased by 11.2% and 8.4% respectively. Antiepileptic drug utilization increased during the study period, the increase probably caused by expanding use in areas other than epilepsy.
