Title: Emerging targets for addiction neuropharmacology: From mechanisms to therapeutics.
Abstract: Drug abuse represents a considerable burden of disease and has enormous economic impacts on societies. Over the years, few medications have been developed for clinical use. Their utilization is endowed with several limitations, including partial efficacy or significant side effects. On the other hand, the successful advancement of these compounds provides an important proof of concept for the feasibility of drug development programs in addiction. In recent years, a wealth of information has been generated on the psychological mechanisms, genetic or epigenetic predisposing factors, and neurobiological adaptations induced by drug consumption that interact with each other to contribute to disease progression. It is now clear that addiction develops through phases, from initial recreational use to excessive consumption and compulsive drug seeking, with a shift from positive to negative reinforcement driving motivated behaviors. A greater understanding of these mechanisms has opened new vistas in drug development programs. Researchers' attention has been shifted from investigation of classical targets associated with reward to biological substrates responsible for negative reinforcement, impulse loss of control, and maladaptive mechanisms resulting from protracted drug use. From this research, several new biological targets for the development of innovative therapies have started to emerge. This chapter offers an overview of targets currently under scrutiny for the development of new medications for addiction. This work is not exhaustive but rather it provides a few examples of how this research has advanced in recent years by virtue of studies carried out in our laboratory.

---

Title: Impairments in reflective cognitive functions in alcoholics: a neuropharmacological model.
Abstract: The ability to make use of reflective cognitive operations in monitoring and evaluating remembered events is impaired in subgroups of nominally cognitively unimpaired, detoxified alcoholics. Alcoholics, relative to controls, make more errors in identifying the source of remembered information (i.e. whether a remembered word was self-generated or was a stimulus word presented by the experimenter), and are impaired in their ability to inhibit confabulatory errors (intrusions). The cognitive-memory impairment expressed in benzodiazepine-treated normal volunteers mimics this impairment in alcoholics. Disturbances in prefrontal and frontal lobe functions may be involved in this selective impairment in cognition in many alcoholics and may also contribute to what accounts for the failures in reflective cognitive operations observed in amnestic patients.

---

Title: Recent Updates in Psychopharmacology for the Core and Associated Symptoms of Autism Spectrum Disorder.
Abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by core deficits in social communication and restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior. This article aims to review the recent literature pertaining to psychopharmacology for the core and associated symptoms of ASD including social impairment, repetitive behaviors, irritability, and language impairment. Recent medication trials targeting social impairment in ASD have focused on neuropeptides (oxytocin and vasopressin) and memantine. None of these three medications has demonstrated consistent benefit for social impairment in ASD; however, additional studies are underway. Two double-blind, placebo-controlled studies on selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) provide evidence against the use of SSRIs for repetitive behaviors in youth with ASD. Preliminary studies have investigated cannabidiol (CBD) for irritability in ASD but further studies are needed to demonstrate safety and efficacy. Finally, three double-blind, placebo-controlled studies provide preliminary evidence for folinic acid for the treatment of verbal language deficits in children with ASD. The identification of safe and effective pharmacological treatments to ameliorate the core and associated symptoms of ASD has proven difficult.

---

Title: Proteomics and peptidomics in neuroscience. Experience of capabilities and limitations in a neurochemical laboratory.
Abstract: The increasing use of proteomics has created a basis for new strategies to develop methodologies for rapid identification of protein patterns in living organisms. It has also become evident that proteomics has other potential applications than protein and peptide identification, e.g. protein characterization, with the aim of revealing their structure, function(s) and interactions of proteins. In comparative proteomics studies, the protein expression of a certain biological system is compared with another system or the same system under perturbed conditions. Global identification of proteins in neuroscience is extremely complex, owing to the limited availability of biological material and very low concentrations of the molecules. Moreover, in addition to proteins, there are number of peptides that must also be considered in global studies on the central nervous system. In this overview, we focus on and discuss problems related to the different sources of biological material and sample handling, which are part of all preparatory and analytical steps. Straightforward protocols are desirable to avoid excessive purification steps, since loss of material at each step is inevitable. We would like to merge the two worlds of proteomics/peptidomics and neuroscience, and finally we consider different practical and technical aspects, illustrated with examples from our laboratory.

---

Title: Neurochemical phenotypes of cardiorespiratory neurons.
Abstract: Interactions between the cardiovascular and respiratory systems have been known for many years but the functional significance of the interactions is still widely debated. Here I discuss the possible role of metabotropic receptors in regulating cardiorespiratory neurons in the brainstem and spinal cord. It is clear that, although much has been discovered, cardiorespiratory regulation is certainly one area that still has a long way to go before its secrets are fully divulged and their function in controlling circulatory and respiratory function is revealed.

---

Title: Maintenance of eupnea and gasping following alterations in potassium ion concentration of perfusates of in situ rat preparation.
Abstract: Levels of extracellular potassium ion, above those in vivo, are required for the generation of rhythmic activities of in vitro preparations. Our purpose was to define whether hyperkalemia in the perfusate of an in situ preparation was likewise necessary for the expression of eupnea and gasping. Studies were performed using the in situ preparation of the juvenile rat, in which activity of the phrenic nerve was recorded as an index of the respiratory rhythm. Eupnea and gasping were impervious to modifications of potassium ion of the perfusate. Eupnea was maintained uninterrupted for more than 60 min whether the total concentration of potassium was hypokalemic (2.75 mM), normokalemic (4.0 mM), or hyperkalemic (6.25 and 7.75 mM). Gasping, with identical characteristics, was elicited at any concentration of potassium ion. We conclude that both eupnea and gasping are unaffected by modest changes in the concentration of potassium ion in the perfusate with which the in situ rat preparation is maintained. These results add further support to the conclusion that the in situ preparation represents a model that accurately reproduces mechanisms of rhythm generation of both eupnea and gasping in vivo.

---

Title: Pharmacological regulation of gene expression.
Abstract: The pharmacological regulation of gene expression represents a critical mechanism underlying therapeutic drug action and adverse effects in the central nervous system. While traditional approaches have focused on receptor binding and enzymatic inhibition, emerging evidence suggests that many psychoactive compounds exert their effects through direct modulation of transcriptional machinery. We investigated the capacity of classical neuropharmacological agents to regulate gene expression in primary cortical neurons and assessed the temporal dynamics of these responses. Primary rat cortical neurons were treated with clinically relevant concentrations of fluoxetine (10 μM), haloperidol (1 μM), diazepam (5 μM), or vehicle control for 6, 12, and 24 hours. Gene expression profiles were analyzed using quantitative RT-PCR arrays targeting 84 genes involved in synaptic plasticity, neurotransmitter synthesis, and cellular stress responses. Chromatin immunoprecipitation assays examined drug-induced changes in histone modifications at target gene promoters. Fluoxetine treatment resulted in significant upregulation of BDNF (2.3-fold, p<0.01) and CREB1 (1.8-fold, p<0.05) within 6 hours, accompanied by increased H3K4me3 marks at their promoter regions. Haloperidol induced delayed but sustained changes in dopamine receptor gene expression and immediate early genes. Diazepam primarily affected GABAergic signaling genes with minimal transcriptional effects. Time-course analysis revealed distinct temporal patterns for each compound class. These findings demonstrate that neuropharmacological agents possess intrinsic capacity to reshape neuronal gene expression programs through epigenetic mechanisms, providing novel insights into their therapeutic mechanisms and suggesting new targets for drug development in psychiatric and neurological disorders.

---

Title: Twenty-Year Trends in the Psychopharmacological Treatment of Outpatients with Borderline Personality Disorder: A Cross-Sectional Naturalistic Study in Spain.
Abstract: Although no psychotropic drugs have been officially approved for the treatment of borderline personality disorder (BPD), medications are routinely prescribed for these patients. The primary aim of this study was to evaluate changes in the pharmacological management of patients with BPD treated in an outpatient specific unit in Spain over the past 20 years, while a secondary aim was to identify the factors associated with the prescription. Observational and cross-sectional study of all patients with a primary diagnosis of BPD (n = 620) consecutively admitted to a BPD outpatient program in Barcelona, Spain, from 2001 through 2020. We examined trends in the prescription of antidepressants, benzodiazepines, mood stabilizers, and antipsychotics. For the analysis, prescription data were grouped into four 5-year periods (2001-2005, 2006-2010, 2011-2015, and 2016-2020). Logistic regression models were performed to identify sociodemographic and clinical variables associated with pharmacological prescription and polypharmacy. The percentage of patients receiving pharmacotherapy decreased over time. Antidepressant prescription rates remained high and stable over time (74% of patients), while benzodiazepine use decreased significantly during the study period (from 77 to 36%) and second-generation antipsychotic (SGA) use increased from 15 to 32%. Psychiatric comorbidity was the main factor associated with pharmacological treatment (odds ratio 2.5, 95% confidence interval 1.5-4.2) and polypharmacy, although a high percentage of patients without comorbidity were also taking medications. Over the past 20 years, the pharmacological treatment of BPD outpatients has undergone important changes, most notably the decrease in benzodiazepines and increase in SGAs. The findings of this study demonstrate that pharmacotherapy is much more prevalent in patients with BPD than recommended in most clinical guidelines.

---

Title: Effects of zona incerta lesions on striatal neurochemistry and behavioral asymmetry in 6-hydroxydopamine-lesioned rats.
Abstract: The zona incerta (ZI) has emerged as a potential modulator of basal ganglia function, yet its role in Parkinson's disease pathophysiology remains poorly understood. This study investigated whether ZI lesions influence striatal neurochemistry and motor asymmetry in the unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) rat model of Parkinson's disease. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received unilateral 6-OHDA lesions of the medial forebrain bundle, followed by either sham surgery or electrolytic ZI lesions ipsilateral to the dopaminergic lesion. Behavioral asymmetry was assessed using amphetamine-induced rotation testing at 2, 4, and 6 weeks post-lesion. Striatal tissue was analyzed for dopamine, DOPAC, HVA, and serotonin content using high-performance liquid chromatography. Immunohistochemistry was performed to quantify tyrosine hydroxylase-positive neurons in the substantia nigra. ZI lesions significantly reduced amphetamine-induced rotational behavior compared to 6-OHDA-only controls (327 ± 45 vs. 612 ± 78 rotations/hour at 6 weeks, p<0.001). Striatal dopamine depletion was similar between groups (>90% reduction), but ZI-lesioned animals showed increased striatal serotonin levels (142% of control vs. 98% in 6-OHDA-only group, p<0.05) and elevated serotonin turnover ratios. No differences in nigral cell loss were observed between groups. These findings suggest that ZI lesions ameliorate parkinsonian motor asymmetry through mechanisms independent of dopamine restoration, potentially involving serotonergic compensation. The ZI may represent a novel therapeutic target for modulating basal ganglia output in movement disorders.

---

Title: Comparative analysis of inhibitory effects of caged ligands for the NMDA receptor.
Abstract: Photolytic release of neurotransmitters from caged precursors is a useful method to study synaptic processes with high temporal and spatial resolution. At present, the two most widely used classes of caged precursors for studies on glutamate receptors are based on derivatives of the 2-nitrobenzyl caging group (alpha-carboxy-2-nitrobenzyl, CNB) and the nitroindoline caging group (7-nitroindoline, NI, and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindoline, MNI). Besides NI- and MNI-caged amino acids being thermally more stable than the CNB-caged amino acids, there have been no other major advantages reported of using compounds from either of these two classes. Here, we show inhibitory effects of CNB-glutamate and a number of other CNB-caged agonists on N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors at non-saturating concentrations of the co-agonist glycine. In contrast, NI- and MNI-glutamate and most other NI-/MNI-caged agonists that we tested were inert under these conditions. Furthermore, we demonstrate that carboxynitroindoline-caged glycine (CNI-glycine), which was previously found to inhibit glycine receptors, has no such effect on NMDA receptors. Together, these findings underline the usefulness of NI- and MNI-caged ligands and show that CNB-caged compounds should be avoided in studies involving NMDA receptors.

---

Title: Effects of dopamine D2/D3 receptor antagonism on human planning and spatial working memory.
Abstract: Dopaminergic signaling in the prefrontal cortex and striatum plays a crucial role in executive functions, yet the specific contribution of D2/D3 receptors to higher-order cognitive processes remains poorly understood. This study investigated the effects of selective D2/D3 receptor antagonism on planning abilities and spatial working memory in healthy volunteers. We conducted a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study with 24 healthy adults (mean age 24.3±3.1 years, 12 female). Participants received either sulpiride (400mg) or placebo and completed the Tower of London task to assess planning performance and a spatial n-back task (1-back, 2-back, 3-back) to evaluate working memory capacity. Cognitive testing occurred 2-3 hours post-administration during peak plasma concentrations. Compared to placebo, sulpiride significantly impaired planning performance, increasing both solution time (p=0.012) and number of moves required (p=0.031) on complex Tower of London problems. Spatial working memory showed a differential pattern, with sulpiride selectively impairing 3-back performance accuracy (p=0.018) while leaving 1-back and 2-back performance intact. Reaction times were prolonged across all working memory conditions under sulpiride (p=0.007). These findings demonstrate that D2/D3 receptors are critical for optimal planning and high-demand spatial working memory in humans. The selective impairment of complex cognitive operations suggests that D2/D3 antagonism disrupts prefrontal-striatal circuits specifically when cognitive load is high, providing insight into the neurochemical mechanisms underlying executive dysfunction in psychiatric disorders characterized by altered dopaminergic transmission.

---

Title: Differential dynamics of amino acid release in the amygdala and olfactory cortex during odor fear acquisition as revealed with simultaneous high temporal resolution microdialysis.
Abstract: Although the amygdala seems to be essential to the formation and storage of fear memories, it might store only some aspects of the aversive event and facilitate the storage of more specific sensory aspects in cortical areas. We addressed the time course of amygdala and cortical activation in the context of odor fear conditioning in rats. Using high temporal resolution (1-min sampling) intracerebral microdialysis, we investigated the dynamics of glutamate and GABA fluctuations simultaneously in basolateral amygdala (BLA) and posterior piriform cortex (pPCx) during the course of the acquisition session, which consisted of six odor (conditioned stimulus)-footshock (unconditioned stimulus) pairings. In BLA, we observed a transient increase in amino acid concentrations following the first odor-shock pairing, after which concentrations returned to baseline levels or slightly below. In pPCx, transient increases were seen after each pairing and were also observed after the last odor-shock pairing, corresponding to the predicted times of anticipated trials. Furthermore, we observed that for the first pairing, the increase in BLA occurred earlier than the increase in pPCx. These data suggest that the amygdala is engaged early during acquisition and precedes the activation of the olfactory cortex, which is maintained until the end of the session. In addition, our data raise the challenging idea that the olfactory cortex might store certain aspects of fear conditioning related to the timing of the associations.

---

Title: The rotational model and microdialysis: Significance for dopamine signalling, clinical studies, and beyond.
Abstract: The rotational model of Parkinson's disease, first described by Ungerstedt in the 1970s, remains a cornerstone for investigating dopaminergic dysfunction and therapeutic interventions. When combined with in vivo microdialysis, this model provides unprecedented insights into real-time neurotransmitter dynamics within the basal ganglia circuitry. This review synthesizes three decades of research utilizing the rotational model-microdialysis paradigm to examine dopamine signalling mechanisms, evaluate therapeutic strategies, and translate findings to clinical applications. The unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine lesion creates a reproducible model of asymmetric dopaminergic denervation, enabling quantitative assessment of rotational behavior while simultaneously monitoring extracellular dopamine, its metabolites, and other neurotransmitters through microdialysis probes implanted in the striatum. Studies demonstrate that amphetamine-induced rotation correlates inversely with residual dopamine content, while L-DOPA-induced rotation reflects the degree of denervation supersensitivity. Microdialysis reveals that effective therapeutic interventions, including L-DOPA, dopamine agonists, and deep brain stimulation, normalize aberrant dopamine release patterns and reduce rotational asymmetry. Furthermore, this combined approach has elucidated the roles of dopamine transporters, vesicular storage, and glial uptake in parkinsonian pathophysiology. The model's predictive validity for clinical outcomes has been demonstrated through successful translation of dopamine replacement strategies, continuous drug delivery systems, and neuroprotective agents from preclinical studies to human trials. Contemporary applications extend beyond Parkinson's disease to include investigations of addiction, schizophrenia, and neurodevelopmental disorders, establishing this methodology as an indispensable tool for advancing our understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission and developing targeted therapeutic interventions.

---

Title: Evaluation of metaquant microdialysis for measurement of absolute concentrations of amphetamine and dopamine in brain: a viable method for assessing pharmacokinetic profile of drugs in the brain.
Abstract: Accurate measurement of absolute drug concentrations in brain tissue remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional microdialysis techniques provide relative concentration changes but yield recovery-dependent values that complicate pharmacokinetic interpretation. This study evaluated the metaquant microdialysis approach for determining absolute concentrations of amphetamine and dopamine in rat striatum. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with CMA/12 probes in the dorsal striatum. Following amphetamine administration (2.5 mg/kg i.p.), metaquant calibration was performed using retrodialysis of known concentrations of amphetamine (10-1000 nM) and dopamine (1-100 nM) through the probe membrane. Extraction fraction was determined by measuring the difference between perfusate input and output concentrations. Dialysate samples were analyzed using HPLC-ED with detection limits of 0.5 nM for amphetamine and 0.2 nM for dopamine. The metaquant method yielded extraction fractions of 0.18 ± 0.03 for amphetamine and 0.22 ± 0.04 for dopamine in vivo. Peak absolute amphetamine concentrations reached 285 ± 45 nM at 45 minutes post-injection, while dopamine concentrations increased from baseline 12 ± 3 nM to 89 ± 18 nM at 30 minutes. Pharmacokinetic analysis revealed amphetamine half-life of 78 ± 12 minutes in striatal extracellular fluid. These results demonstrate that metaquant microdialysis provides reliable absolute concentration measurements, enabling accurate assessment of drug pharmacokinetics in discrete brain regions and offering significant advantages over conventional relative microdialysis approaches for neuropharmacological investigations.

---

Title: Participation of women in neurochemistry societies.
Abstract: Women have made important scientific contributions to the field of neurochemistry, and they have also been leaders in neurochemical societies throughout the world. Here I discuss women's involvement and leadership in six neurochemistry societies: American Society for Neurochemistry, Argentine Society for Neurochemistry, International Society for Neurochemistry, European Society for Neurochemistry, Japanese Society for Neurochemistry, and Asian-Pacific Society for Neurochemistry. The number of women who have been active in these societies and the level of their activity vary considerably. Neurochemical societies in the Western hemisphere, i.e., the American and the Argentine Society for Neurochemistry, have much greater numbers of women who have held office, been on council, or engaged in other leadership activities than in the rest of the world. The limited participation of women in the Japanese Neurochemistry Society relates to Japanese cultural views and was not unexpected. However, the relatively few women leaders in the International Society for Neurochemistry was a surprise. The European Society had a somewhat better record of female participation than did the International Society. The reasons for these differences are partly cultural, but factors related to when each society was formed, how it is organized, and how elections are structured undoubtedly play a role. Further analysis of these observations would be of interest from a sociological and a women's studies point of view.

---

Title: Synthesis and pharmacological evaluation of 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines.
Abstract: The synthesis of novel 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinoline derivatives was undertaken to explore their potential as central nervous system active compounds. Tetrahydroisoquinoline scaffolds have demonstrated significant pharmacological activity at dopaminergic and adrenergic receptors, warranting investigation of structurally related analogues. A series of twenty-three compounds was synthesized via Pictet-Spengler cyclization reactions starting from appropriately substituted phenethylamines and aldehydes, followed by phenolic coupling reactions to generate the chromeno-fused derivatives. All compounds were characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and mass spectrometry. Pharmacological evaluation included radioligand binding assays at dopamine D1 and D2 receptors, α1- and α2-adrenergic receptors, and serotonin 5-HT1A and 5-HT2A receptors using rat brain membranes. Functional activity was assessed using cAMP accumulation assays and calcium mobilization studies. Several 4-phenoxy-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives exhibited moderate to high affinity for dopamine D2 receptors (Ki = 45-180 nM), with compound 7c showing selectivity over D1 receptors (>50-fold). The chromeno-fused analogues demonstrated preferential binding to α1-adrenergic receptors, with compound 12b displaying nanomolar affinity (Ki = 32 nM). Functional studies revealed that most active compounds behaved as partial agonists or antagonists at their respective targets. These results suggest that 4-phenoxy-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives represent promising lead compounds for the development of selective dopaminergic agents, while the chromeno-fused derivatives may serve as α1-adrenergic receptor modulators with potential cardiovascular applications.

---

Title: Anxiolytics antagonize yohimbine-induced central noradrenergic activity: a concomitant in vivo voltammetry-electrophysiology model of anxiety.
Abstract: Anxiety disorders involve complex interactions between noradrenergic and GABAergic neurotransmitter systems, yet the real-time neurochemical mechanisms underlying anxiolytic drug action remain poorly characterized. We developed a novel dual-modality approach combining in vivo voltammetry with simultaneous electrophysiology to examine how clinically relevant anxiolytics modulate yohimbine-induced noradrenergic activation in the locus coeruleus (LC) and its projection targets. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were implanted with carbon fiber electrodes for voltammetric detection of norepinephrine in the LC, hippocampus, and prefrontal cortex, alongside tungsten microelectrodes for single-unit recordings of LC neuronal activity. Yohimbine (2 mg/kg, i.p.) significantly increased extracellular norepinephrine levels by 340±45% in the LC, 280±38% in hippocampus, and 195±32% in prefrontal cortex, concurrent with increased LC firing rates (baseline: 1.2±0.3 Hz; post-yohimbine: 4.8±0.7 Hz). These neurochemical and electrophysiological changes correlated with elevated anxiety-like behaviors in the elevated plus maze. Pretreatment with diazepam (1 mg/kg) or buspirone (5 mg/kg) significantly attenuated yohimbine-induced norepinephrine release by 65-75% across all brain regions and normalized LC firing patterns. The temporal correlation between neurochemical and electrophysiological measurements (r=0.87, p<0.001) validated our methodological approach. These findings demonstrate that anxiolytics act, in part, by restraining excessive noradrenergic activity and provide a robust experimental model for real-time assessment of anxiety-related neurochemical processes. This dual-modality technique offers enhanced temporal and spatial resolution for investigating anxiolytic mechanisms and may facilitate development of novel anti-anxiety therapeutics.

---

Title: [The history of CINP between 1958 and 2000].
Abstract: Collegium Internationale Neuropsychopharmacologicum (CINP) is an international society which was established to facilitate academic exchange of basic and clinical studies related to psychotropic drugs. The CINP has been devoted to the problems of methodology and to the analysis of the pharmacological and therapeutic results with psychotropic drugs under normal and pathological conditions as well to the medico-social implications of neuropsychopharmacology. The CINP was organized by Prof. Trabucchi (Milan) in collaboration with an international team of neuropsychopharmacologists from 15 countries. The first International CINP Congress was held on September of 1958 in Rome. Thereafter, CINP world congresses were held every two years until 2012. The number of Japanese neuropsycho-pharmacologists who are members has been increasing since the 1970s. The 17th CINP Congress was held in Kyoto in 1990, and the CINP Hiroshima regional meeting was held, combined the annual meeting of the Japanese Society of Neuropsychopharmacology and Korean Society of Psychopharmacology in 2001. The early history of CINP between 1958 and 2000 is briefly described with special reference to the participation of the Japanese Society of Neuropsychopharmacology.

---

Title: High-field proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy reveals metabolic effects of normal brain aging.
Abstract: Altered brain metabolism is likely to be an important contributor to normal cognitive decline and brain pathology in elderly individuals. To characterize the metabolic changes associated with normal brain aging, we used high-field proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy in vivo to quantify 20 neurochemicals in the hippocampus and sensorimotor cortex of young adult and aged rats. We found significant differences in the neurochemical profile of the aged brain when compared with younger adults, including lower aspartate, ascorbate, glutamate, and macromolecules, and higher glucose, myo-inositol, N-acetylaspartylglutamate, total choline, and glutamine. These neurochemical biomarkers point to specific cellular mechanisms that are altered in brain aging, such as bioenergetics, oxidative stress, inflammation, cell membrane turnover, and endogenous neuroprotection. Proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy may be a valuable translational approach for studying mechanisms of brain aging and pathology, and for investigating treatments to preserve or enhance cognitive function in aging.

---

Title: Extracting the basal extracellular dopamine concentrations from the evoked responses: re-analysis of the dopamine kinetics.
Abstract: Accurate determination of basal extracellular dopamine concentrations in vivo remains challenging due to the rapid uptake kinetics and low steady-state levels of this neurotransmitter. Traditional microdialysis methods suffer from temporal resolution limitations, while fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) primarily measures evoked dopamine release. Here we present a novel analytical approach to extract basal dopamine concentrations from electrically-evoked dopamine responses recorded using FSCV in the striatum of anesthetized rats. Our method employs a modified Michaelis-Menten kinetic model that incorporates both uptake and release parameters to back-calculate baseline concentrations from the decay phase of evoked dopamine transients. We validated this approach using simultaneous FSCV and microdialysis recordings (n=8 rats) and compared results with conventional no-net-flux microdialysis measurements. Mathematical modeling of 127 evoked dopamine responses revealed basal concentrations of 18.3 ± 4.7 nM, which correlated strongly with microdialysis values (r=0.82, p<0.001). The method showed high reproducibility (CV=12.4%) and was sensitive to pharmacological manipulations, including dopamine transporter blockade with nomifensine (10 mg/kg i.p.) and tyrosine hydroxylase inhibition with α-methyl-p-tyrosine (200 mg/kg i.p.). Re-analysis of existing datasets using this approach suggests that previous estimates of dopamine uptake parameters may have been confounded by inaccurate baseline assumptions. This methodology provides a valuable tool for determining physiological dopamine concentrations with high temporal resolution and may improve our understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission in health and disease states.

---

Title: Neurochemical dementia diagnostics: a simple algorithm for interpretation of the CSF biomarkers.
Abstract: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)-based neurochemical dementia diagnostics (NDD) is a well-established diagnostic tool for neurodegenerative disorders, including Alzheimer's disease (AD). However, the direct comparison of the concentrations of the biomarkers between laboratories is often very misleading, due to relatively high inter-laboratory discrepancies of normal/abnormal ranges (cutoff values). Therefore, an interpretation tool might be useful for centers performing NDD to facilitate a standardized, diagnostic-oriented reporting of the data on biomarkers. In this study, we present a simple, easy-to-implement algorithm allowing diagnostic-relevant categorization of patients according to the outcome of the CSF NDD results and, correspondingly, enabling reporting of the data to clinicians in a clear and easy-to-follow form. The algorithm is flexible and cutoff values independent, meaning each laboratory can easily supplement it with the cutoff values and normal/abnormal ranges according to the needs; the only prerequisite is to perform the standard CSF NDD assays (amyloid beta peptides and Tau/pTau).

---

Title: From systems biology to dynamical neuropharmacology: proposal for a new methodology.
Abstract: The concepts and methods of systems biology are extended to neuropharmacology in order to test and design drugs for the treatment of neurological and psychiatric disorders. Computational modelling by integrating compartmental neural modelling techniques and detailed kinetic descriptions of pharmacological modulation of transmitter-receptor interaction is offered as a method to test the electrophysiological and behavioural effects of putative drugs. Even more, an inverse method is suggested as a method for controlling a neural system to realise a prescribed temporal pattern. In particular, as an application of the proposed new methodology, a computational platform is offered to analyse the generation and pharmacological modulation of theta rhythm related to anxiety.

---

Title: Subcellular probes for neurochemical recording from multiple brain sites.
Abstract: Spatially resolved neurochemical measurements are essential for understanding complex brain signaling networks, yet current approaches are limited by probe dimensions and multiplexing capabilities. Traditional microelectrodes lack the spatial resolution to distinguish subcellular compartments, while existing microsensor arrays cannot simultaneously monitor neurochemicals across distant brain regions. Here we report the development of ultrasmall carbon fiber microelectrodes (2-5 μm diameter) integrated with multiplexed recording capabilities for real-time neurochemical detection from multiple brain sites. We fabricated 16-channel arrays using photolithographic patterning of carbon fiber bundles coupled to miniaturized potentiostat circuits. The devices were characterized using cyclic voltammetry and demonstrated selective detection of dopamine, serotonin, and adenosine with sub-second temporal resolution. In vivo validation was performed in anesthetized rats (n=8) with simultaneous recordings from striatum, nucleus accumbens, and prefrontal cortex during electrical stimulation and behavioral paradigms. Our microsensor arrays detected stimulus-evoked dopamine release with 50 nM sensitivity and spatial resolution sufficient to distinguish synaptic versus extrasynaptic signaling within individual brain nuclei. Cross-correlation analysis revealed coordinated neurochemical dynamics between brain regions that were previously undetectable with single-site measurements. The multiplexed recordings demonstrated region-specific differences in neurotransmitter kinetics and revealed novel patterns of inter-regional neurochemical communication. This technology enables unprecedented spatial and temporal mapping of brain neurochemistry, providing new insights into distributed neural network function and offering significant advantages for neuropharmacological research and therapeutic monitoring applications.

---

Title: Pharmacological imaging as a tool to visualise dopaminergic neurotoxicity.
Abstract: Dopaminergic neurotoxicity underlies several neurodegenerative disorders, yet current methods for visualizing and quantifying neuronal damage in vivo remain limited. This study evaluated the utility of pharmacological imaging approaches combining positron emission tomography (PET) with selective radioligands to assess dopaminergic neurotoxicity in real-time. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received unilateral striatal injections of 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA, 8 μg) or vehicle. Animals underwent longitudinal PET imaging using [¹¹C]raclopride for dopamine D2 receptor availability and [¹¹C]PE2I for dopamine transporter density at 1, 3, 7, and 28 days post-lesion. Behavioral assessment via amphetamine-induced rotation and post-mortem tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry served as validation measures. 6-OHDA lesioning produced progressive decreases in striatal [¹¹C]PE2I binding, with 45±8% reduction at day 3 and 78±6% reduction at day 28 compared to contralateral striatum (p<0.001). [¹¹C]Raclopride binding showed initial increases (125±12% at day 3) followed by normalization by day 28, consistent with denervation supersensitivity. Strong correlations were observed between [¹¹C]PE2I binding and both rotational behavior (r=-0.84, p<0.001) and tyrosine hydroxylase-positive cell counts (r=0.79, p<0.001). These findings demonstrate that pharmacological PET imaging provides sensitive, quantitative assessment of dopaminergic neurotoxicity progression. The temporal resolution and non-invasive nature of this approach offers significant advantages for monitoring disease progression and evaluating neuroprotective interventions in preclinical models of Parkinson's disease and related disorders.

---

Title: Psychoneuroimmunology meets neuropsychopharmacology: translational implications of the impact of inflammation on behavior.
Abstract: The bidirectional communication between the immune system and central nervous system has emerged as a critical pathway linking peripheral inflammation to neuropsychiatric disorders. Growing evidence indicates that pro-inflammatory cytokines can access the brain and alter neurotransmitter metabolism, neuroendocrine function, and synaptic plasticity, ultimately leading to behavioral changes reminiscent of depression and anxiety. This review synthesizes current understanding of inflammatory mechanisms underlying mood disorders and examines therapeutic implications for neuropsychopharmacology. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies investigating cytokine-induced behavioral alterations and anti-inflammatory treatment strategies. Animal models utilizing lipopolysaccharide, cytokine administration, and chronic stress paradigms consistently demonstrate that peripheral immune activation produces depressive-like behaviors including anhedonia, social withdrawal, and cognitive impairment. Mechanistically, inflammatory mediators disrupt monoaminergic signaling through enhanced tryptophan catabolism via indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase, reduced tetrahydrobiopterin availability, and increased monoamine transporter expression. Clinical studies reveal elevated inflammatory markers in subsets of patients with major depression, particularly those with treatment-resistant illness. Importantly, anti-inflammatory interventions including cyclooxygenase-2 inhibitors, tumor necrosis factor-α antagonists, and minocycline show therapeutic efficacy in both preclinical models and preliminary clinical trials. These findings suggest that inflammatory biomarkers may identify patient subgroups most likely to respond to anti-inflammatory augmentation strategies. The convergence of psychoneuroimmunology and neuropsychopharmacology offers promising avenues for developing personalized medicine approaches targeting immune-mediated mechanisms in psychiatric disorders, potentially improving outcomes for treatment-refractory patients.

---

Title: Glia and immune cell signaling in bipolar disorder: insights from neuropharmacology and molecular imaging to clinical application.
Abstract: Bipolar disorder (BD) is a debilitating mental illness characterized by severe fluctuations in mood, sleep, energy and executive functioning. Pharmacological studies of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and the monoamine system have helped us to clinically understand bipolar depression. Mood stabilizers such as lithium and valproic acid, the first-line treatments for bipolar mania and depression, inhibit glycogen synthase kinase-3 beta (GSK-3β) and regulate the Wnt pathway. Recent investigations suggest that microglia, the resident immune cells of the brain, provide a physiological link between the serotonin system and the GSK-3β/Wnt pathway through neuroinflammation. We review the pharmacological, translational and brain imaging studies that support a role for microglia in regulating neurotransmitter synthesis and immune cell activation. These investigations provide a model for microglia involvement in the pathophysiology and phenotype of BD that may translate into improved therapies.

---

Title: The golden years: a tribute to Erminio Costa.
Abstract: The field of neuropharmacology has been profoundly shaped by the pioneering contributions of Erminio Costa, whose groundbreaking research spanning five decades fundamentally transformed our understanding of GABAergic neurotransmission and neuropsychiatric disorders. This tribute examines Costa's seminal discoveries and their lasting impact on contemporary neuropharmacology. We conducted a comprehensive review of Costa's published works from 1955-2009, analyzing his major contributions to GABA receptor pharmacology, benzodiazepine mechanisms, and schizophrenia research. Additionally, we surveyed current literature citing his work to assess ongoing influence on modern neuropharmacological research. Costa's laboratory identified the crucial role of GABA as the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter and elucidated the molecular mechanisms underlying benzodiazepine anxiolytic effects through allosteric modulation of GABAA receptors. His team discovered that benzodiazepines enhance GABA-mediated chloride conductance without directly activating the receptor, establishing the conceptual framework for positive allosteric modulation that remains central to modern drug development. Furthermore, Costa's investigations into GABA dysfunction in schizophrenia revealed reduced GAD67 expression and altered parvalbumin-positive interneuron function, providing neurobiological foundations for current theories of psychiatric pathophysiology. Analysis reveals over 15,000 citations of Costa's work in the past decade, with particular influence on GABA receptor subunit research, interneuron development, and therapeutic target identification. Costa's legacy continues to guide contemporary neuropharmacological research, from basic GABA receptor biology to translational studies of anxiety disorders and schizophrenia, cementing his position as one of neuropharmacology's most influential pioneers.

---

Title: Proton magnetic resonance spectroscopic creatine correlates with creatine transporter protein density in rat brain.
Abstract: Creatine (Cr) is an amino acid, which upon phosphorylation is utilized as an energy reservoir in cells with high-energy demand. The ongoing catabolism of creatine to creatinine requires a permanent creatine replenishment into the cells. Because neurons themselves cannot synthesize creatine, they have to take it up via the creatine transporter (CrT). Thus, the concentration of intracellular Cr available for the Cr/PCr shuttle system depends on the expression level of CrT protein. The proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) creatine peak (total creatine=tCr) constitutes of two metabolites, namely Cr and phosphocreatine (PCr). We have quantified the level of CrT protein expression with western blotting and compared it to tCr content as estimated by in vitro MRS in Sprague-Dawley rats. Under the assumption of hemispheric symmetry, we took identical samples from left and right hemisphere, which were used for in vitro MRS (tCr) and for western blotting (CrT), respectively. Altogether, it was possible to take 90 corresponding brain samples from 31 animals. A Pearson linear regression analysis for CrT and tCr revealed p<0.0001, explaining 14% of the variance. Since MR-detectable alterations of tCr in the human brain are widespread (e.g. in most major psychiatric disorders proton MRS detectable tCr alterations have been described as regionally and usually state dependent) it is stringent to elucidate their meaning. An influence of tCr on the brain's energy regulating system seems plausible.

---

Title: Attention: oscillations and neuropharmacology.
Abstract: Attention-related neural oscillations in the gamma frequency range (30-100 Hz) have been implicated in selective attention and conscious perception, yet their underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. We investigated the effects of GABAergic and cholinergic modulation on gamma oscillations during visual attention tasks in awake, behaving macaque monkeys. Local field potentials were recorded from area V4 while animals performed a cued spatial attention task. Iontophoretic application of the GABA_A antagonist bicuculline (10-50 nA) significantly increased gamma power (147 ± 23% of baseline, p < 0.001) and enhanced attentional modulation of neuronal responses by 34 ± 12%. Conversely, muscimol (GABA_A agonist, 20-80 nA) suppressed gamma oscillations (62 ± 18% of baseline, p < 0.01) and reduced attention-related firing rate modulation. The acetylcholinesterase inhibitor physostigmine (15-40 nA) enhanced gamma synchronization during attended conditions (128 ± 19% of baseline, p < 0.05) and improved behavioral performance on attention-demanding trials. The nicotinic receptor antagonist mecamylamine (25-60 nA) disrupted attention-related gamma enhancement and impaired discrimination accuracy. Cross-correlation analysis revealed that pharmacologically induced changes in gamma power correlated strongly with attentional performance (r = 0.73, p < 0.001). These findings demonstrate that gamma oscillations underlying visual attention depend critically on the balance between GABAergic inhibition and cholinergic excitation. Our results suggest that attention-related gamma rhythms emerge from local GABAergic interneuron networks modulated by ascending cholinergic projections, providing a neurochemical framework for understanding attentional dysfunction in neuropsychiatric disorders.

---

Title: Extracellular trypsin increases ASIC1a selectivity for monovalent versus divalent cations.
Abstract: Acid-sensing ion channels (ASICs) are proton-gated cation channels widely distributed throughout the nervous system and implicated in synaptic transmission, mechanosensation, and pathological conditions including ischemic brain injury. ASIC1a, the predominant brain isoform, exhibits relatively low selectivity between monovalent and divalent cations under physiological conditions. Previous studies have shown that extracellular proteases can modulate ASIC function, though the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. Here we investigated whether trypsin treatment affects the ionic selectivity of recombinant human ASIC1a channels expressed in Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) cells using whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology. Cells were treated with bovine pancreatic trypsin (10-100 μg/ml) for 2-15 minutes before recording acid-evoked currents (pH 6.0) in solutions containing various concentrations of Na+, K+, Ca2+, or Mg2+. Relative permeability ratios were calculated from reversal potential measurements using the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation. Trypsin treatment (50 μg/ml, 5 min) significantly increased PNa+/PCa2+ from 2.1 ± 0.3 to 8.7 ± 1.2 (n=12, p<0.001) and PK+/PMg2+ from 1.8 ± 0.4 to 6.9 ± 0.8 (n=10, p<0.001). The effect was dose- and time-dependent, reversible upon trypsin removal, and blocked by the serine protease inhibitor PMSF. Current amplitude and pH sensitivity remained unchanged. These findings demonstrate that extracellular trypsin selectively enhances ASIC1a permeability to monovalent cations while reducing divalent cation flux, suggesting proteolytic modification of channel selectivity filter regions. This mechanism may be physiologically relevant during tissue injury when both acidosis and protease release occur simultaneously.

---

Title: Dorsal raphe vs. median raphe serotonergic antagonism. Anatomical, physiological, behavioral, neuroendocrinological, neuropharmacological and clinical evidences: relevance for neuropharmacological therapy.
Abstract: The serotonergic system comprises anatomically and functionally distinct dorsal raphe (DR) and median raphe (MR) nuclei that exhibit differential responses to pharmacological interventions and may contribute differently to psychiatric disorders. This study examined the contrasting effects of selective serotonergic modulation in DR versus MR regions using combined anatomical, electrophysiological, behavioral, and neuroendocrine approaches. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received microinjections of the 5-HT1A receptor antagonist WAY-100635 (0.1-1.0 μg) into either DR or MR nuclei, followed by assessment of anxiety-related behaviors in elevated plus maze and open field tests, measurement of plasma corticosterone and ACTH levels, and single-unit recordings from hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed distinct 5-HT1A receptor distribution patterns between DR and MR regions. DR antagonism produced anxiogenic effects, elevated stress hormone levels, and increased hippocampal theta rhythm power, while MR antagonism resulted in anxiolytic responses, reduced HPA axis activation, and decreased theta activity. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated that DR manipulation primarily affected dorsal hippocampal neuronal firing, whereas MR modulation influenced ventral hippocampal activity. These findings provide compelling evidence for functional antagonism between DR and MR serotonergic systems, with DR primarily mediating stress responses and anxiety, while MR appears to regulate emotional processing and behavioral inhibition. The differential therapeutic targets identified suggest that anatomically-selective serotonergic interventions may offer improved treatment strategies for anxiety disorders and depression, potentially explaining variable clinical responses to current serotonergic medications.

Title: Should we or shouldn't we?: Preface to the Special Issue "Neurochemistry of Reward-Seeking".
Abstract: Reward-seeking is critical for survival. Learning about the relationship between actions and outcomes helps us to make decisions and select behaviours that result in a specific outcome. This special issue entitled "The Neurochemistry of Reward-Seeking" addresses this crucial facet of behaviour and brings together a number of key thought-leaders to provide a timely update on the circuitry, chemistry and mechanisms underlying different aspects of reward-seeking. The reviews in this issue canvass unanswered questions in the field and provide a degree of forethought about how we may advance our understanding of reward-seeking by embracing novel technology alongside existing scholarship. This issue also highlights the neurochemical complexity of reward-seeking, and the reader will uncover both distinct and shared circuits and transmitters driving various forms of reward-seeking. Accordingly, we hope that this special issue will provide a valuable resource for the field and trigger future research on this topic.

---

Title: A short historical review of SCNP with special reference to the UKU.
Abstract: The development of systematic approaches to assess side effects of psychotropic medications has been crucial for advancing neuropsychopharmacological research and clinical practice. The Scandinavian Committee on Neuropsychopharmacology (SCNP), established in the 1970s, played a pivotal role in standardizing adverse event reporting through the development of the UKU Side Effect Rating Scale (Udvalg for Kliniske Undersøgelser). This historical review examines the evolution of the SCNP and its contribution to systematic side effect assessment in psychiatric treatment. We conducted a comprehensive literature review spanning 1970-2014, analyzing key publications, meeting proceedings, and collaborative research initiatives involving SCNP members. Historical documents and personal communications with founding members were also examined to trace the development of the UKU scale. The SCNP emerged from the need for standardized clinical trial methodologies in Nordic countries, with early focus on antipsychotic and antidepressant efficacy studies. The UKU scale, developed between 1983-1987, represented a breakthrough in systematic side effect documentation, incorporating 48 items across four domains: psychological, neurological, autonomic, and other symptoms. The scale demonstrated excellent inter-rater reliability (κ=0.74-0.89) and became widely adopted internationally, translated into over 20 languages. Validation studies confirmed its utility across diverse psychiatric populations and treatment modalities. The SCNP's collaborative model influenced subsequent international research networks, while the UKU scale remains a gold standard for comprehensive side effect assessment. This historical perspective highlights how Nordic cooperation in neuropsychopharmacology contributed significantly to evidence-based psychiatric treatment and patient safety monitoring worldwide.

---

Title: [The way out from crisis--from the closing of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI) to Semmelweis University].
Abstract: The closure of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI) in 2007 represented a critical juncture in Hungarian psychiatric care, necessitating comprehensive evaluation of patient outcomes and treatment continuity during the institutional transition to Semmelweis University. This retrospective cohort study analyzed clinical data from 847 patients with severe psychiatric disorders who were transferred from OPNI to the newly established Department of Psychiatry and Psychotherapy at Semmelweis University between January 2007 and December 2008. Primary outcomes included treatment adherence rates, hospitalization frequency, and clinical symptom severity assessed using the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS) and Global Assessment of Functioning (GAF). Secondary measures examined medication compliance, outpatient follow-up rates, and patient satisfaction scores. Despite initial concerns regarding service disruption, 78.3% of patients maintained stable treatment engagement within six months of transfer. Mean BPRS scores showed no significant deterioration (pre-transfer: 42.1±12.4 vs. post-transfer: 43.7±13.1, p=0.23), while GAF scores demonstrated modest improvement (51.2±15.8 vs. 54.3±16.2, p=0.04) at 12-month follow-up. Medication adherence rates remained stable at 71%, comparable to pre-closure levels. However, emergency department visits increased by 23% during the initial transition period before returning to baseline. The successful integration of OPNI services into Semmelweus University demonstrates that institutional transitions in psychiatric care can be achieved without compromising patient outcomes when implemented with adequate planning, resource allocation, and continuity protocols. These findings provide valuable insights for future healthcare system reorganizations in psychiatric services.

---

Title: Neuropharmacological basis of combining antidepressants.
Abstract: To review the neuropharmacological basis of antidepressant combination therapy. Literature searches and other relevant material were obtained and reviewed. The overall clinical aim of combining antidepressants is to increase the efficacy whilst minimizing the side effects. Although such prescriptions are frequently based on the previous experience and knowledge, a sound neuropharmacological basis to support these combinations is desirable. When combining antidepressants, it is important to combine mechanisms of action, rather than simply one drug with another, and to aim for synergistic effects. The possibilities of combining mechanisms of action should also be exploited to the full if necessary, and the potential exists for combining two independent actions that have synergistic effects on the serotonergic, noradrenergic and even the dopaminergic systems. Unfortunately, there are still, as yet, insufficient data to categorically justify choosing one or other combination based only on the neuropharmacological evidence.

Title: Wireless transmission of fast-scan cyclic voltammetry at a carbon-fiber microelectrode: proof of principle.
Abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) at a carbon-fiber microelectrode (CFM) provides exquisite temporal and spatial resolution for monitoring brain chemistry. The utility of this approach has recently been demonstrated by measuring sub-second dopamine changes associated with behavior. However, one drawback is the cable link between animal and recording equipment that restricts behavior and precludes monitoring in complex environments. As a first step towards developing new instrumentation to overcome this technical limitation, the goal of the present study was to establish proof of principle for the wireless transmission of FSCV at a CFM. Proof of principle was evaluated in terms of measurement stability, fidelity, and susceptibility to ambient electrical noise. Bluetooth digital telemetry provided bi-directional communication between remote and home-base units and stable, high-fidelity data transfer comparable to conventional, wired systems when tested using a dummy cell (i.e., a resistor and capacitor in series simulating electrical properties of a CFM), and dopamine measurements with flow injection analysis and in the anesthetized rat with electrical stimulation. The wireless system was also less susceptible to interference from ambient electrical noise. Taken together, the present findings establish proof of principle for the wireless transmission of FSCV at a CFM.

Title: Analysis of neurotransmitter tissue content of Drosophila melanogaster in different life stages.
Abstract: The neurotransmitter systems of Drosophila melanogaster undergo extensive remodeling during metamorphosis, yet comprehensive quantitative analysis across developmental stages remains limited. This study employed high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) to quantify dopamine, serotonin, octopamine, tyramine, and GABA content in whole-body extracts of D. melanogaster across four distinct life stages: third-instar larvae, pupae (48-hour), newly eclosed adults (0-24 hours), and mature adults (7-day). Twenty replicate samples of each stage were analyzed, with neurotransmitter content normalized to total protein concentration. Dopamine levels showed a biphasic pattern, decreasing 3.2-fold from larvae to pupae (p<0.001), then increasing 4.7-fold in newly eclosed adults before stabilizing in mature adults. Serotonin content remained relatively constant through larval and pupal stages but increased 2.8-fold in adults (p<0.001). Octopamine exhibited the most dramatic developmental regulation, with barely detectable levels in larvae and pupae, followed by a 15-fold increase in adults (p<0.001). Tyramine showed similar but less pronounced changes, increasing 6.2-fold from pupae to adults. GABA content gradually declined throughout development, decreasing by 40% from larvae to mature adults (p<0.05). These findings reveal stage-specific neurotransmitter profiles that likely reflect the distinct behavioral and physiological requirements of each developmental phase, providing a quantitative foundation for future studies of neurotransmitter function during Drosophila development and metamorphosis.

Title: Experimental neuropsychopharmacology, yesterday, today and tomorrow. A conversation with Michel Hamon.
Abstract: For this issue, Luc Zimmer, professor of pharmacology and chair of the Neuropsychopharmacology Committee of the French Society of Pharmacology and Therapeutics, talked with Michel Hamon, honorary director of research at the French National Institute of Health and Medical Research (INSERM) and honorary professor of neuropharmacology at Paris-Sorbonne (Pierre et Marie Curie) University. Some of the leading names in neuropsychopharmacology research are mentioned, pointing to significant conceptual advances that founded this discipline. The links between psychopharmacology and neuropharmacology are also discussed in the light of past collaborations. Finally, priorities are proposed for the emergence of the psychopharmacology of the future.

Title: Generalized anxiety disorder. An important clinical concern.
Abstract: GAD is common, often follows a chronic course, and usually is associated with extensive psychiatric and medical comorbidity. This disorder often presents in a primary care setting, commonly with somatic symptoms; it is important for primary care physicians to be aware of its existence, forms of presentation, and different treatments. GAD appears to be twice as common in women than men. There is some evidence that the neurobiologic basis of GAD may involve abnormalities in neurochemical, neuroendocrine, neurophysiologic, and neuroanatomic factors. Research on psychosocial treatment of GAD has favored the combination of cognitive therapy and relaxation techniques or anxiety management training. It also appears that relapse rates after termination of cognitive-behavioral therapy are low. Taking into consideration that GAD generally is chronic and associated frequently with depressive symptoms, the ideal pharmacotherapy may be a drug that can treat these comorbid disorders adequately. New antidepressants, especially those with action in the serotonin system and possibly noradrenergic, may be the appropriate option: They not only treat anxiety, but also treat or prevent the development of comorbid depression; they should be effective ideally during prolonged periods without risks related to addiction or withdrawal, such as may happen with benzodiazepines and some antidepressants. The role of newly emerging drugs, such as some anticonvulsants, in GAD needs to be defined more clearly. More research is warranted to address issues such as (1) whether pharmacotherapy is as effective, less effective, or more effective than cognitive-behavioral therapy; (2) whether antidepressants improve the rate of wellness or remission; and (3) whether prolonged antidepressant therapy for GAD protects against later emergent depression.

---

Title: Structure of the catalytic pore of gamma-secretase probed by the accessibility of substituted cysteines.
Abstract: Several single-span membrane proteins are cleaved within their transmembrane domains (TMDs) by intramembrane-cleaving proteases, although the structure of the active site executing intramembrane cleavage remains unknown. Here we use the substituted cysteine accessibility method to examine the structure of presenilin-1, a catalytic subunit of gamma-secretase, involved in amyloid beta protein generation in Alzheimer's disease and Notch signaling. We show that TMD6 and TMD7 of presenilin-1 contribute to the formation of a hydrophilic pore within the membrane. Residues at the luminal portion of TMD6 are predicted to form a subsite for substrate or inhibitor binding on the alpha-helix facing a hydrophilic milieu, whereas those around the GxGD catalytic motif within TMD7 are highly water accessible, suggesting formation of a hydrophilic structure within the pore. Collectively, our data suggest that the active site of gamma-secretase resides in a catalytic pore filled with water within the lipid bilayer and is tapered around the catalytic aspartates.

Title: Power spectra reveal distinct BOLD resting-state time courses in white matter.
Abstract: Accurate characterization of the time courses of blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD) signal changes is crucial for the analysis and interpretation of functional MRI data. While several studies have shown that white matter (WM) exhibits distinct BOLD responses evoked by tasks, there have been no comprehensive investigations into the time courses of spontaneous signal fluctuations in WM. We measured the power spectra of the resting-state time courses in a set of regions within WM identified as showing synchronous signals using independent components analysis. In each component, a clear separation between voxels into two categories was evident, based on their power spectra: one group exhibited a single peak, and the other had an additional peak at a higher frequency. Their groupings are location specific, and their distributions reflect unique neurovascular and anatomical configurations. Importantly, the two categories of voxels differed in their engagement in functional integration, revealed by differences in the number of interregional connections based on the two categories separately. Taken together, these findings suggest WM signals are heterogeneous in nature and depend on local structural-vascular-functional associations.

Title: In vivo autofluorescence spectrofluorometry of central serotonin.
Abstract: Serotonin (5-HT) plays crucial roles in mood regulation, sleep-wake cycles, and cognitive function, yet real-time monitoring of central serotonergic neurotransmission in living brain tissue remains technically challenging. Traditional microdialysis approaches suffer from poor temporal resolution and tissue damage, while existing fluorometric methods require exogenous fluorophores that may alter normal physiology. We developed a novel in vivo autofluorescence spectrofluorometry technique to directly monitor endogenous serotonin dynamics in the rat brain without chemical modification. Using a custom-built fiber-optic spectrofluorometer with excitation at 295 nm and emission detection at 340 nm, we implanted 200-μm diameter optical fibers into the dorsal raphe nucleus and hippocampus of anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rats (n=12). The system achieved 1-second temporal resolution with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 15:1. Pharmacological validation demonstrated that autofluorescence signals increased 180±25% following administration of the serotonin reuptake inhibitor fluoxetine (10 mg/kg, i.p.) and decreased 65±12% after depletion with para-chlorophenylalanine (300 mg/kg, i.p., 48h pretreatment). Electrical stimulation of the median raphe nucleus (20 Hz, 1 min) produced rapid, reproducible increases in hippocampal autofluorescence (peak amplitude 45±8% above baseline). Control experiments using 5,7-dihydroxytryptamine lesions confirmed signal specificity for serotonergic transmission. This minimally invasive technique provides unprecedented temporal resolution for monitoring central serotonin dynamics and offers significant advantages for investigating serotonergic mechanisms underlying behavior and psychopathology in real-time experimental paradigms.

Title: Advances in paediatric neuropsychopharmacology: an overview.
Abstract: The field of paediatric neuropsychopharmacology has experienced remarkable growth over the past decade, driven by increased recognition of childhood psychiatric disorders and the unique pharmacological challenges they present. This comprehensive review examines recent advances in the use of psychotropic medications in children and adolescents, focusing on developmental considerations, efficacy data, and safety profiles. A systematic literature review was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases from 1990-2001, supplemented by recent conference proceedings and regulatory documents. Key therapeutic areas examined include attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), childhood depression, anxiety disorders, and early-onset psychotic disorders. Significant progress has been made in understanding the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of psychotropic agents in developing populations. Stimulant medications for ADHD demonstrate robust efficacy with well-characterized safety profiles, while selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show promise for childhood anxiety and depression, though with important caveats regarding suicidal ideation monitoring. Atypical antipsychotics have emerged as valuable treatments for severe behavioural disturbances and early-onset schizophrenia, despite concerns about metabolic side effects. Critical gaps remain in our understanding of long-term developmental effects of psychotropic medications, particularly regarding growth, sexual maturation, and cognitive development. The paucity of controlled trials in paediatric populations continues to challenge evidence-based prescribing. Future research priorities include dose-finding studies, long-term safety surveillance, and investigation of pharmacogenomic factors affecting drug response in children. Enhanced regulatory frameworks and industry incentives are essential to advance this critical field.

Title: Simple behavioral methods to assess the effect of drugs or toxins on sensory experience.
Abstract: Behavioral assessment of sensory function represents a critical component in neuropharmacological research, yet many current methods require extensive training, sophisticated equipment, or invasive procedures. We developed and validated a battery of simple, non-invasive behavioral assays to evaluate drug and toxin effects on visual, auditory, tactile, and olfactory sensory systems in laboratory rats. The protocol consists of four standardized tests: (1) visual cliff avoidance for depth perception, (2) acoustic startle response amplitude for auditory sensitivity, (3) adhesive tape removal latency for somatosensory function, and (4) buried food pellet discovery time for olfactory detection. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8 per group) were tested following administration of known sensory-impairing agents: atropine (1-10 mg/kg, i.p.) for visual impairment, kanamycin (400 mg/kg, i.p.) for ototoxicity, capsaicin (50 mg/kg, s.c.) for nociceptive dysfunction, and zinc sulfate (intranasal) for anosmia. All compounds produced dose-dependent sensory deficits detectable within 30-60 minutes post-administration. Atropine significantly increased visual cliff crossing latency (p<0.01), kanamycin reduced startle amplitude by 65% (p<0.001), capsaicin doubled tape removal time (p<0.01), and zinc sulfate increased food-finding latency 4-fold (p<0.001). Inter-rater reliability exceeded 0.85 for all measures, and test-retest correlation was >0.90 across a two-week interval. These simple behavioral methods provide researchers with accessible, quantitative tools for screening sensory toxicity and monitoring therapeutic interventions without requiring specialized training or equipment, making them particularly suitable for laboratories with limited resources.

Title: Role of Ratings of Perceived Exertion during Self-Paced Exercise: What are We Actually Measuring?
Abstract: Ratings of perceived exertion (RPE) and effort are considered extremely important in the regulation of intensity during self-paced physical activity. While effort and exertion are slightly different constructs, these terms are often used interchangeably within the literature. The development of perceptions of both effort and exertion is a complicated process involving numerous neural processes occurring in various regions within the brain. It is widely accepted that perceptions of effort are highly dependent on efferent copies of central drive which are sent from motor to sensory regions of the brain. Additionally, it has been suggested that perceptions of effort and exertion are integrated based on the balance between corollary discharge and actual afferent feedback; however, the involvement of peripheral afferent sensory feedback in the development of such perceptions has been debated. As such, this review examines the possible difference between effort and exertion, and the implications of such differences in understanding the role of such perceptions in the regulation of pace during exercise.

Title: Pathological gambling: a comprehensive review of biobehavioral findings.
Abstract: In this review, findings of biobehavioral research into pathological gambling (PG) are discussed, focusing on neuropsychological, psychophysiological, neuroimaging, neurochemical and genetic studies. Neuropsychological studies indicate deficiencies in certain executive functions. Psychophysiological studies indicate that arousal in PG is of importance when reward is present. Neuroimaging studies point to abnormalities in brain functioning. Recent research into the neurochemistry of PG indicates that abnormalities exist in different neurotransmitter systems. Finally, genetic studies indicate the existence of abnormal dopamine receptor genes in PG. Methodological and theoretical factors that may explain discrepancies between studies include differences in screening and assessment, heterogeneity of gambling problems and different underlying cognitive or motivational mechanisms. Results from the PG studies fit in with recent theoretical models of addiction and PG, which stress the involvement of brain reward pathways, neurotransmitter abnormalities, the frontal cortex and the psychophysiological stress system. A framework for future studies is suggested, indicating the need for studies that integrate knowledge from different research areas, and that employ stricter diagnostic screening methods and inclusion of clinical control groups.

Title: A review of Neuropharmacology Effects of Nigella sativa and Its Main Component, Thymoquinone.
Abstract: Neuropharmacology is the scientific study of drug effect on nervous system. In the last few years, different natural plants and their active constituents have been used in neurological therapy. The availability, lower price, and less toxic effects of herbal medicines compared with synthetic agents make them as simple and excellent choice in the treatment of nervous diseases. Nigella sativa, which belongs to the botanical family of Ranunculaceae, is a widely used medicinal plant all over the world. In traditional and modern medicines several beneficial properties have been attributed to N. sativa and its main component, thymoquinone (TQ). In this review, various studies in scientific databases regarding the neuropharmacological aspects of N. sativa and TQ have been introduced. Results of these studies showed that N. sativa and TQ have several properties including anticonvulsant, antidepressant, anxiolytic, anti-ischemic, analgesic, antipsychotic, and memory enhancer. Furthermore, its protective effects against neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer, Parkinson and multiple sclerosis have been discussed. Although there are many studies indicating the beneficial actions of this plant in nervous system, the number of research projects relating to the human reports is rare. Copyright © 2016 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

Title: Nesfatin-1 in the neurochemistry of eating disorders.
Abstract: The vast majority of new neuropeptides feature unique biochemical properties as well as awide spectrum of physiological activity applied in numerous neuronal pathways, including hypothalamus and the limbic system. Special interest should be paid to nesfatin-1 - the relatively recently discovered and still intensively studied regulatory factor and a potential modulator of eating behaviors. New information about it now allows to consider this neuropeptide as a potentially important factor involved in the pathogenesis of many different mental disorders. The considered pharmacomodulation of nesfatinergic signaling may be potentially helpful in the future treatment of some neuropsychiatric and metabolic disorders including anorexia nervosa. Although the results of some basic and clinical tests seem to be promising, all possible applications of the aforementioned neuropeptides, together with their agonists and antagonists still remain in the area of speculation. The intensive search of selective modulators of their known receptors may facilitate the opening of a promising chapter in the eating disorders therapy. This paper provides a review of recent scientific reports regarding the hypothetical role of nesfatin-1 in the neuronal pathways related to pathophysiology of anorexia nervosa.

Title: Modelling of the blood-brain barrier in drug discovery and development.
Abstract: The market for neuropharmaceuticals is potentially one of the largest sectors of the global pharmaceutical market owing to the increase in average life expectancy and the fact that many neurological disorders have been largely refractory to pharmacotherapy. The brain is a delicate organ that can efficiently protect itself from harmful compounds and precisely regulate its microenvironment. Unfortunately, the same mechanisms can also prove to be formidable hurdles in drug development. An improved understanding of the regulatory interfaces that exist between blood and brain may provide novel and more effective strategies to treat neurological disorders.

Title: Clinical neuropharmacology of drugs of abuse: a comparison of drug-discrimination and subject-report measures.
Abstract: Advances in molecular pharmacology and behavioral science have helped elucidate the structure and function of the central nervous system and its relationship to behavior and has sparked the development of pharmacological agents that have increasingly selective and potent effects with fewer adverse side effects. The sensitivity and predictive validity of the two most commonly used methodologies for assessing the neuropharmacological effects of centrally active drugs, subject report of drug effects and drug discrimination, were examined. The sensitivity of the measures was comparable across stimulant, sedative, and opioid drugs. Results with drug-discrimination methodologies were generally consistent with hypothesized neuropharmacological mechanisms across all drug classes, whereas subject reports conformed under more limited testing conditions. Firm conclusions regarding the relative utility of drug-discrimination and subject-report measures for clinical studies of neuropharmacological mechanisms are limited by the small number of studies in which the two methodologies have been tested using identical pharmacological pretreatment manipulations.
