Title: Psychopharmacology and memory.
Abstract: The therapeutic manipulation of memory through psychopharmacological interventions raises significant ethical questions regarding personal identity, autonomy, and the nature of authentic human experience. While memory-modulating drugs show promise for treating post-traumatic stress disorder, addiction, and neurodegenerative diseases, their potential applications extend beyond clear medical necessity into enhancement and selective memory editing. This analysis examines the ethical implications of three categories of memory-affecting agents: memory enhancers (cholinesterase inhibitors, ampakines), memory inhibitors (propranolol, anisomycin), and memory selectors (targeted reconsolidation blockers). Through systematic review of current literature and application of established bioethical frameworks, we evaluated the moral permissibility of memory modification across therapeutic, preventive, and enhancement contexts. Our analysis reveals a spectrum of ethical acceptability, with therapeutic applications for severe psychiatric conditions receiving strongest justification, while enhancement uses raise concerns about authenticity, fairness, and psychological continuity. Memory inhibition for trauma prevention shows promise but requires careful consideration of informed consent and long-term consequences. Selective memory editing presents the most complex ethical challenges, potentially undermining the integrated nature of personal narrative and moral development. We propose a graduated ethical framework emphasizing therapeutic necessity, proportionality, and reversibility as key criteria for evaluating memory modification interventions. Future research must address questions of distributive justice, coercion, and the societal implications of widespread memory manipulation technologies. Robust ethical oversight and public discourse are essential as these powerful interventions transition from experimental tools to clinical realities.

---

Title: Targeted delivery of pharmacological agents into rat dorsal root ganglion.
Abstract: We sought an optimal method for targeted delivery into dorsal root ganglia (DRGs) for experimental studies, in terms of precision of delivery and avoidance of behavioral disturbances. We examined three approaches for injection into rat DRGs: percutaneous injection without surgical exposure, injection after deep exposure, and injection following deep exposure and partial laminectomy. Coomassie blue and Fast Blue were injected into DRGs for validation. At necropsy, the spread of Coomassie blue and Fast Blue was investigated under stereomicroscope and fluorescent microscope, respectively. We found that percutaneous approach did not provide any successful DRG injections. Deep exposure prior to intraganglionic injection provided variable results, but intraganglionic injection after deep exposure plus partial laminectomy was successful in 100% of attempts. Our subsequent skeletal analysis showed that the anatomical location of DRG is not compatible with successful DRG injection without surgical exposure. Neither of the methods using surgical exposure caused behavioral disturbances. Based on these results we conclude that partial laminectomy offers the most precise method of injecting DRG and does not produce behavioral evidence of nerve damage. Intraganglionic injection after deep exposure alone is less predictable, while percutaneous approaches only allow injection in the peripheral nerve.

---

Title: Volume versus wiring transmission in the brain: a new theoretical frame for neuropsychopharmacology.
Abstract: Traditional models of neural communication have emphasized point-to-point synaptic transmission, yet accumulating evidence suggests that neurotransmitter signaling extends beyond the synaptic cleft through volume transmission mechanisms. This theoretical review examines the dual nature of neurochemical communication and its implications for understanding drug action in the central nervous system. We propose a comprehensive framework distinguishing between "wiring transmission" - the classical synaptic model involving rapid, spatially restricted neurotransmitter release - and "volume transmission" - a slower, more diffuse process where neurotransmitters, neuropeptides, and neuromodulators spread through extracellular space to reach distant targets. Analysis of anatomical, physiological, and pharmacological data reveals that many neurotransmitter systems, particularly dopaminergic, serotonergic, and cholinergic pathways, operate through both mechanisms simultaneously. Volume transmission appears especially prominent in subcortical regions and involves specialized anatomical features including mismatched distributions of neurotransmitter release sites and receptor locations. This dual transmission model provides new insights into the therapeutic mechanisms of psychotropic drugs, particularly those affecting monoaminergic systems. The framework helps explain the delayed onset of antidepressant efficacy, the widespread effects of neuroleptics, and the complex dose-response relationships observed with many neuropsychopharmacological agents. Understanding the relative contributions of wiring versus volume transmission may guide the development of more selective therapeutic interventions and advance our comprehension of psychiatric disorder pathophysiology.

---

Title: Reconsolidation: the advantage of being refocused.
Abstract: Ample evidence suggests that upon their retrieval, items in long-term memory enter a transient special state, in which they might become prone to change. The process that generates this state is dubbed 'reconsolidation'. The dominant conceptual framework in this revitalized field of memory research focuses on whether reconsolidation resembles consolidation, which is the process that converts an unstable short-term memory trace into a more stable long-term trace. However, this emphasis on the comparison of reconsolidation to consolidation deserves reassessment. Instead, the phenomenon of reconsolidation, irrespective of its relevance to consolidation, provides a unique opportunity to tap into the molecular, cellular and circuit correlates of memory persistence and retrieval, of which we currently know only little.

---

Title: [Perspectives on obsessive-compulsive spectrum disorders and its trends for the revision of DSM-5].
Abstract: In the process of revising of DSM-5 for release in 2013, there is growing interest in the concept of a nosologically distinct spectrum of obsessive-compulsive disorders (OCSDs). Validity and compass of this spectrum remains to be fully elucidated, but a preliminary approach emphasizes that putative OCSDs have phenomenologic (eg. repetitive thoughts and/ or behaviors), etiological, psychobiologic or treatment overlap with OCD. Studies have investigated the relevant neurocircuitry, neurochemistry, and neuroendocrinology underlying anxiety disorders and OCSDs, and have explored the underlying genetic basis of and relevant intermediate phenotypes for developing these conditions. There appears to be some scientific validity and clinical utility for conceptualizing a subgroup of emotional-related disorders, within which fall the anxiety, posttraumatic, and OCSDs. However OCSDs, especially motoric OCSDs such as tic or Tourette's disorder still remain controversial; the appropriate grouping of these is still under discussion and may still change.

---

Title: Neuropsychopharmacology: are we ready for a paradigm shift?
Abstract: The field of neuropsychopharmacology has traditionally relied on monoaminergic hypotheses to explain psychiatric disorders and guide drug development. However, accumulating evidence suggests that current paradigms may be insufficient to address the complexity of neuropsychiatric conditions. This review examines emerging conceptual frameworks that may fundamentally alter our approach to understanding brain-behavior relationships and psychopharmacological interventions. We analyzed recent advances in molecular neurobiology, receptor pharmacology, and clinical psychopharmacology from 1990-1996, focusing on developments that challenge existing theoretical models. Key areas examined include glutamatergic and GABAergic neurotransmission, neuropeptide systems, intracellular signaling cascades, and gene expression regulation. Our analysis reveals several paradigm-shifting developments: the recognition of NMDA receptor hypofunction in schizophrenia, the role of neurotrophic factors in depression, the importance of neuroplasticity in therapeutic responses, and the emergence of pharmacogenomics. Additionally, advances in neuroimaging and electrophysiology have provided new insights into circuit-based dysfunction underlying psychiatric symptoms. Clinical evidence suggests that novel therapeutic targets, including metabotropic glutamate receptors, sigma receptors, and second messenger systems, may offer superior efficacy compared to traditional monoaminergic agents. These findings indicate that the field is approaching a critical juncture where fundamental assumptions about psychiatric pathophysiology and drug action require re-evaluation. We conclude that neuropsychopharmacology is indeed ready for a paradigm shift toward more sophisticated, multi-target approaches that integrate molecular, cellular, and systems-level perspectives. This transition will likely accelerate the development of more effective treatments for neuropsychiatric disorders.

---

Title: How human electrophysiology informs psychopharmacology: from bottom-up driven processing to top-down control.
Abstract: Recent advances in human electrophysiology have provided unprecedented insights into the neural mechanisms underlying psychoactive drug effects, bridging the gap between preclinical models and clinical outcomes. Understanding how pharmacological interventions modulate both bottom-up sensory processing and top-down cognitive control represents a critical frontier in neuropsychopharmacology. We reviewed electrophysiological studies employing event-related potentials (ERPs) and oscillatory measures to characterize drug effects across multiple neurotransmitter systems. A comprehensive analysis of 127 studies published between 2005-2010 examined how psychoactive compounds alter early sensory components (P50, N100, P300) reflecting bottom-up processing, and later cognitive components (N200, P300, error-related negativity) indexing top-down control mechanisms. Results demonstrate that dopaminergic agents primarily enhance early sensory gating and P300 amplitude, while GABAergic compounds attenuate both early and late ERP components in a dose-dependent manner. Glutamatergic modulators, particularly NMDA antagonists, selectively impair mismatch negativity and gamma oscillations without affecting primary sensory responses. Cholinergic enhancement selectively improved top-down attentional control as indexed by enhanced P300 and reduced distractor-related N200. Importantly, individual differences in baseline electrophysiological measures predicted treatment response magnitude across multiple drug classes. These findings establish human electrophysiology as a translational bridge between molecular pharmacology and behavioral outcomes, providing mechanistic insights into bottom-up versus top-down drug effects. Such approaches may facilitate more precise medication selection and dosing strategies in psychiatric and neurological disorders by identifying electrophysiological biomarkers of drug response.

---

Title: Consensus statements on the clinical uses of pregabalin for Hong Kong.
Abstract: **Background:** Pregabalin, a gabapentinoid with established efficacy in neuropathic pain and epilepsy, has gained increasing attention for psychiatric applications in Asia-Pacific regions. However, clinical practice guidelines specific to Hong Kong populations remain limited. This study aimed to develop evidence-based consensus statements on pregabalin's clinical uses in Hong Kong psychiatric practice.

**Methods:** A modified Delphi process was conducted involving 24 psychiatrists and neurologists from major Hong Kong medical institutions between January-September 2019. Three rounds of structured questionnaires examined pregabalin's efficacy, safety, dosing protocols, and clinical applications across anxiety disorders, bipolar disorder, and substance use disorders. Literature review encompassed randomized controlled trials and real-world evidence from Asian populations. Consensus was defined as ≥75% agreement among panelists.

**Results:** Strong consensus emerged supporting pregabalin as second-line treatment for generalized anxiety disorder (91.7% agreement) and panic disorder (87.5% agreement) when first-line agents prove inadequate. For bipolar disorder, 79.2% agreed pregabalin demonstrated utility as adjunctive treatment for anxiety symptoms and sleep disturbances. Recommended starting doses were 75mg twice daily for anxiety disorders and 150mg daily for bipolar applications, with gradual titration based on response and tolerability. Common adverse effects included dizziness (68% of patients), sedation (45%), and weight gain (32%). Panelists emphasized careful monitoring for dependency potential, particularly in patients with substance use history.

**Conclusions:** These consensus statements provide practical guidance for pregabalin use in Hong Kong clinical practice, emphasizing its role as adjunctive therapy while highlighting the importance of individualized treatment approaches and comprehensive patient monitoring in Chinese populations.

Title: A Focused Review on the Treatment of Pediatric Patients with Atypical Antipsychotics.
Abstract: The use of atypical antipsychotic medications in pediatric patients has become more prevalent in recent years. The purpose of this review is to provide a clinically relevant update of recent selected key publications regarding the use of atypical antipsychotics in this population. Studies reviewed included randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled medication trials conducted within the past 5 years. A PubMed search was conducted for each of the 11 second-generation antipsychotic medications currently approved by the Food and Drug Administration for use in the United States: clozapine, risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, ziprasidone, paliperidone, asenapine, iloperidone, lurasidone, and cariprazine. Trials published in English with subjects 18 years of age and younger were included in this review. Additional studies, chosen for their significance to clinical practice, were also included at the discretion of the authors. This review demonstrates that more empiric data are available regarding both the acute efficacy and, to a lesser extent, the longer-term efficacy and tolerability for several of the considered antipsychotic medications. The clinical conditions for which these medications have been studied include schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, Tourette's disorder, and autism spectrum disorder. They have also been used as an adjunctive treatment for disruptive behavior disorders with aggression, which have not responded to treatment with stimulants. Evidence regarding the efficacy and tolerability of antipsychotic medications for mental health disorders in children and adolescents has expanded exponentially in recent years. However, more information is needed so that evidence-based comparisons between medications can be made. In the future, data enabling the selection of medications based upon individual patient characteristics could potentially lead to greater efficacy and efficiency in treating what are frequently debilitating medical conditions. Maladaptive aggression in children, often treated with antipsychotics, is one such area in which there is a dearth of actual information available to the clinician. It is to be hoped that additional, longer-term studies of these medications will further inform evidence-based practice in clinical settings.

Title: Neurochemical changes in the aging process: implications in medication in the elderly.
Abstract: Aging is an inevitable process in human development, which follows a time course that can be delayed, or hastened, by lifestyles, diseases and events. The factors that affect the aging process can be delayed, but not prevented. This paper evaluates the neurochemical changes in the aging process and their relevance in the modality of elderly medication. For clarity and understanding of the relevant neurobiochemical process and effects, the neuroanatomical, physiological, and neurobehavioral changes are reviewed as they relate to medication in the elderly.

Title: Photochemical and pharmacological evaluation of 7-nitroindolinyl-and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl-amino acids as novel, fast caged neurotransmitters.
Abstract: Reagents capable of rapid and efficient release of neuroactive amino acids (L-glutamate, GABA and glycine) upon flash photolysis of thermally stable, inert precursors have been elusive. 7-Nitroindolinyl (NI)-caged and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl (MNI)-caged compounds that fulfil these criteria are evaluated here. These caged precursors are highly resistant to hydrolysis. Photolysis is fast (half time< or =0.26 ms) and the conversion achieved with a xenon flashlamp is about 15% for the NI-caged L-glutamate and about 35% for the MNI-caged L-glutamate. A procedure is described for calibration of photolysis in a microscope-based experimental apparatus. NI-caged L-glutamate itself showed no agonist or antagonist effects on AMPA and NMDA receptors in cultured neurones, and had no effect on climbing fibre activation of Purkinje neurones. A control compound with identical photochemistry that generated an inert phosphate upon photolysis was used to confirm that the intermediates and by-products of photolysis have no deleterious effects. MNI-caged L-glutamate is as stable and fast as NI-caged L-glutamate and similarly inert at glutamate receptors, but about 2.5 times more efficient. However, NI-caged GABA is an antagonist at GABA(A) receptors and NI-glycine an antagonist at glycine receptors. The results show the utility and limitations of these fast and stable caged neurotransmitters in the investigation of synaptic processes.

---

Title: Effects of triiodothyronine and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity in rat brain: regional differences.
Abstract: Thyroid hormones have been implicated in the modulation of serotonergic neurotransmission and may enhance the therapeutic efficacy of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in treating depression. However, the regional specificity of thyroid hormone effects on serotonin autoreceptor function remains poorly understood. We investigated the combined effects of triiodothyronine (T3) and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity across different brain regions in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received daily injections of T3 (25 μg/kg, i.p.), fluoxetine (10 mg/kg, i.p.), or their combination for 14 days. Autoreceptor function was assessed using in vivo microdialysis coupled with local perfusion of selective agonists 8-OH-DPAT (5-HT1A) and CP-93129 (5-HT1B) in the dorsal raphe nucleus, hippocampus, and frontal cortex. Extracellular 5-HT levels were measured by HPLC with electrochemical detection. Fluoxetine alone produced modest desensitization of 5-HT1A autoreceptors in all regions examined, while 5-HT1B autoreceptor sensitivity remained unchanged. T3 monotherapy had minimal effects on autoreceptor function. However, combined T3 and fluoxetine treatment resulted in marked desensitization of both 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptors, with the most pronounced effects observed in the dorsal raphe nucleus and frontal cortex compared to hippocampus. The regional heterogeneity in autoreceptor desensitization correlated with differential expression of thyroid hormone receptors. These findings suggest that T3 enhances fluoxetine-induced autoreceptor desensitization in a region-specific manner, providing mechanistic insight into thyroid hormone augmentation strategies in antidepressant therapy.

Title: Inside-out neuropharmacology of nicotinic drugs.
Abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches to nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) have focused on extracellular drug binding sites, yet emerging evidence suggests that intracellular drug interactions may play crucial roles in receptor modulation. We investigated the inside-out neuropharmacology of nicotinic drugs using patch-clamp electrophysiology on inside-out membrane patches from rat hippocampal neurons and heterologous expression systems containing α4β2 and α7 nAChR subtypes. Bath application of nicotine (1-100 μM) to the intracellular membrane surface produced concentration-dependent potentiation of acetylcholine-evoked currents in α4β2 receptors (EC50 = 12.4 ± 2.1 μM) but had minimal effects on α7 receptors. Conversely, intracellular mecamylamine (10-300 μM) inhibited both receptor subtypes with greater potency than extracellular application (IC50 values: 45.2 ± 8.7 μM vs 127.3 ± 15.4 μM for α4β2). Site-directed mutagenesis of putative intracellular binding residues in the M3-M4 cytoplasmic loop significantly reduced intracellular drug sensitivity. Molecular docking simulations identified distinct intracellular binding pockets with nanomolar to micromolar affinities for tested compounds. Functional assays demonstrated that intracellular drug effects were independent of extracellular competitive antagonism and persisted in the presence of saturating extracellular nicotine concentrations. These findings reveal a previously uncharacterized dimension of nicotinic receptor pharmacology, suggesting that drug penetration across cellular membranes and subsequent intracellular receptor interactions contribute significantly to overall pharmacological profiles. Understanding inside-out nicotinic pharmacology may inform the development of more selective therapeutic agents for neurological disorders involving cholinergic dysfunction.

---

Title: An improved liquid chromatographic method with electrochemical detection for direct determination of serotonin in microdialysates from Caudate-putamen and pineal gland regions of rat brain.
Abstract: A liquid chromatography method coupled with electrochemical detection has been developed for the direct measurement 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT) and 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA) in brain microdialysates. The separation conditions have been optimized to detect only the 5-HT and 5-HIAA in dialysates and elute the other monoamines and their metabolites in the void. Linear regression analysis of chromatographic peak area as a function of concentration in the range 5-1000 pg/mL gave correlation coefficients over 0.995. Sample stability and an assay validation for precision and accuracy were also performed. The limit of detection (S/N = 3) for 5-HT was 12 femtomol/mL. The method has been applied to simultaneously measure extracellular 5-HT and 5-HIAA in brain microdialysates from the pineal gland and caudate-putamen of awake and freely-moving rats under basal conditions.

Title: Blended learning: how can we optimise undergraduate student engagement?
Abstract: Blended learning is a combination of online and face-to-face learning and is increasingly of interest for use in undergraduate medical education. It has been used to teach clinical post-graduate students pharmacology but needs evaluation for its use in teaching pharmacology to undergraduate medical students, which represent a different group of students with different learning needs. An existing BSc-level module on neuropharmacology was redesigned using the Blended Learning Design Tool (BLEnDT), a tool which uses learning domains (psychomotor, cognitive and affective) to classify learning outcomes into those taught best by self-directed learning (online) or by collaborative learning (face-to-face). Two online courses were developed, one on Neurotransmitters and the other on Neurodegenerative Conditions. These were supported with face-to-face tutorials. Undergraduate students' engagement with blended learning was explored by the means of three focus groups, the data from which were analysed thematically. Five major themes emerged from the data 1) Purpose and Acceptability 2) Structure, Focus and Consolidation 3) Preparation and workload 4) Engagement with e-learning component 5) Future Medical Education. Blended learning was acceptable and of interest to undergraduate students learning this subject. They expressed a desire for more blended learning in their courses, but only if it was highly structured, of high quality and supported by tutorials. Students identified that the 'blend' was beneficial rather than purely online learning.

Title: Placebo and cultural responses.
Abstract: Features of placebo response in medicine have been forgotten and ignored over the last decade. To explore why patients do get better with placebo despite its perceived inertness. This lecture reviews the relation between illness perception, psychopharmacology and culture. Placebo response must be considered in the context of how patients perceive their experience of disease (illness) and through their own cultures, which determine cognitive schema and explanatory model of illness. Most of the placebo response relies on the classical conditioning and expectancy of patients. Moreover, the colour, size, formulation, cost of medications can affect the psychological response to any pharmacological treatment. Modern psychopharmacology should consider placebo and cultural variations as relevant factors of treatment response.

Title: Using a rate-frequency curve method to assess the rewarding properties of morphine in the intracranial self-stimulation paradigm in rats.
Abstract: Intracranial self-stimulation (ICSS) paradigms provide valuable models for studying drug reward mechanisms, yet traditional threshold-based analyses may not capture the full spectrum of reward enhancement. This study evaluated a rate-frequency curve method as an alternative approach to assess morphine's rewarding properties in the ICSS paradigm. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=12) were implanted with bipolar electrodes targeting the medial forebrain bundle and trained to lever-press for electrical brain stimulation. Following stable baseline performance, rats underwent testing sessions where stimulation frequency was systematically varied (25-158 Hz) to generate rate-frequency curves. Morphine (1.0, 3.0, and 5.6 mg/kg, s.c.) or saline was administered 30 minutes prior to testing in a counterbalanced design. Response rates were recorded across all frequencies and curve-shift analysis was performed using nonlinear regression modeling. Morphine produced dose-dependent leftward and upward shifts in rate-frequency curves, indicating enhanced reward sensitivity. The 3.0 and 5.6 mg/kg doses significantly decreased the frequency required to maintain half-maximal responding (F50) by 18% and 32%, respectively, compared to saline controls (p<0.01). Maximum response rates were increased by 15-25% at the higher doses. The rate-frequency curve method demonstrated superior sensitivity compared to traditional threshold measures and provided additional information about changes in reward magnitude. These findings validate the rate-frequency curve approach as a robust method for characterizing drug reward effects in ICSS paradigms, offering enhanced temporal resolution and pharmacological sensitivity for neuropharmacological investigations.

Title: Neuropharmacological properties of Trichosanthes dioica root.
Abstract: The roots of Trichosanthes dioica Roxb. have been traditionally used in Chinese folk medicine for treating neurological disorders, yet their neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study investigated the neuroprotective and psychoactive properties of T. dioica root extracts using established in vivo and in vitro models. Aqueous and methanolic extracts were prepared from dried T. dioica roots and standardized using HPLC analysis. Male Wistar rats (n=8 per group) received oral administration of extracts (100, 200, and 400 mg/kg) for behavioral assessments including elevated plus maze, forced swimming test, and Morris water maze. Neuroprotective effects were evaluated using H2O2-induced oxidative stress in primary cortical neurons. GABA and dopamine receptor binding affinities were determined through radioligand binding assays. Phytochemical analysis revealed significant concentrations of cucurbitacins, flavonoids, and triterpene saponins. The methanolic extract demonstrated dose-dependent anxiolytic activity in the elevated plus maze test (p<0.01) and improved spatial memory performance in the Morris water maze compared to controls. In the forced swimming test, both extracts significantly reduced immobility time, suggesting antidepressant-like effects. Neuronal viability increased by 34% and 28% with aqueous and methanolic extracts respectively against H2O2-induced cytotoxicity. Moderate binding affinity was observed for GABAA receptors (Ki = 145 μM) but not dopaminergic receptors. These findings suggest that T. dioica root possesses significant neuropharmacological properties, potentially mediated through GABAergic modulation and antioxidant mechanisms, supporting its traditional therapeutic applications in neuropsychiatric conditions.

Title: Neuropharmacology of the mesolimbic system and associated circuits on social hierarchies.
Abstract: Social hierarchies are fundamental organizational structures across mammalian species, yet the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. The mesolimbic dopaminergic system, critical for reward processing and motivation, has been implicated in dominance behaviors, but its specific role in hierarchy establishment and maintenance requires further investigation. Here, we examined the contribution of dopamine D1 and D2 receptors within the nucleus accumbens (NAc) and ventral tegmental area (VTA) to social rank determination in adult male C57BL/6J mice. Using a tube test paradigm to assess dominance relationships, we administered selective D1 (SCH23390) and D2 (sulpiride) receptor antagonists via bilateral microinjections prior to social encounters. Additionally, we employed optogenetic stimulation of VTA dopamine neurons during hierarchy formation trials. Behavioral analysis revealed that D1 receptor blockade in the NAc significantly reduced dominance scores (p<0.01), while D2 antagonism had minimal effects. VTA dopamine neuron stimulation enhanced dominant behaviors in subordinate animals (p<0.05). Immunohistochemical analysis showed increased c-Fos expression in NAc medium spiny neurons following successful dominance displays. Microdialysis measurements confirmed elevated extracellular dopamine levels in dominant animals during social interactions. These findings demonstrate that mesolimbic D1 receptor signaling is necessary for establishing social dominance, while VTA dopamine release is sufficient to promote hierarchical status. Our results provide novel insights into the neuropharmacological basis of social hierarchies and suggest potential therapeutic targets for disorders involving aberrant social behaviors.

Title: Neurochemical dementia diagnostics: assays in CSF and blood.
Abstract: In this review, current neurochemical dementia diagnostics (NDD) procedures are presented with a focus on biomarkers in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and blood: amyloid beta peptides, tau protein, and its phosphorylated form (pTau). CSF analysis is an increasingly important tool for early and differential diagnosis of dementia syndromes. Although lumbar puncture is a mildly invasive procedure with a low incidence of complications, establishing blood assays capable of reliably measuring NDD biomarkers is an aim of several studies worldwide.

Title: Astra Award Lecture. The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
Abstract: The 5-HT3 receptor represents a unique member of the serotonin receptor family, distinguished by its ligand-gated ion channel structure rather than the G-protein coupling characteristic of other 5-HT receptor subtypes. Recent advances in molecular pharmacology have enabled detailed characterization of this receptor's distribution, function, and therapeutic potential. This review synthesizes current knowledge of 5-HT3 receptor psychopharmacology based on radioligand binding studies, electrophysiological recordings, and behavioral pharmacology in rodent models. 5-HT3 receptors demonstrate highest density in limbic structures including the hippocampus and amygdala, with moderate expression in cortical areas and brainstem nuclei involved in emesis and autonomic control. Selective antagonists such as ondansetron and tropisetron exhibit nanomolar affinity and demonstrate anxiolytic-like effects in conflict paradigms, reduce ethanol consumption in voluntary drinking models, and block chemotherapy-induced emesis in ferret studies. Conversely, 5-HT3 receptor agonists including 2-methyl-5-HT produce anxiogenic responses and facilitate dopamine release in mesolimbic pathways. Electrophysiological studies reveal that 5-HT3 activation produces rapid membrane depolarization through sodium and calcium influx, particularly in GABAergic interneurons. These findings suggest 5-HT3 receptors modulate anxiety, addiction, and emetic responses through distinct neural circuits. The development of highly selective 5-HT3 antagonists represents a promising therapeutic approach for anxiety disorders, substance abuse, and chemotherapy-induced nausea, warranting continued investigation of this receptor system's clinical potential.

---

Title: Mass spectrometry imaging, an emerging technology in neuropsychopharmacology.
Abstract: Mass spectrometry imaging is a powerful tool for directly determining the distribution of proteins, peptides, lipids, neurotransmitters, metabolites and drugs in neural tissue sections in situ. Molecule-specific imaging can be achieved using various ionization techniques that are suited to different applications but which all yield data with high mass accuracies and spatial resolutions. The ability to simultaneously obtain images showing the distributions of chemical species ranging from metal ions to macromolecules makes it possible to explore the chemical organization of a sample and to correlate the results obtained with specific anatomical features. The imaging of biomolecules has provided new insights into multiple neurological diseases, including Parkinson's and Alzheimer's disease. Mass spectrometry imaging can also be used in conjunction with other imaging techniques in order to identify correlations between changes in the distribution of important chemical species and other changes in the properties of the tissue. Here we review the applications of mass spectrometry imaging in neuroscience research and discuss its potential. The results presented demonstrate that mass spectrometry imaging is a useful experimental method with diverse applications in neuroscience.

---

Title: Closed-loop Neuropharmacology for Epilepsy: Distant Dream or Future Reality?
Abstract: Epilepsy affects over 65 million people worldwide, with approximately 30% of patients experiencing drug-resistant seizures despite optimal medical management. Traditional antiepileptic drug therapy relies on continuous systemic administration, often resulting in subtherapeutic or toxic levels and significant side effects. Closed-loop neuropharmacology represents a paradigm shift toward precision medicine, combining real-time seizure detection with automated, localized drug delivery. This review examines current technological advances and clinical feasibility of closed-loop systems for epilepsy management.

We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent developments in seizure prediction algorithms, implantable drug delivery platforms, and biocompatible reservoir systems. Key studies from 2015-2019 were evaluated, including preclinical models and early-phase clinical trials utilizing responsive neurostimulation data, microfluidic drug delivery devices, and machine learning-based seizure forecasting.

Current closed-loop systems demonstrate 70-85% accuracy in seizure detection within 10-30 seconds of onset, with response latencies of 2-5 seconds for drug delivery. Microinfusion pumps can deliver precise drug volumes (nanoliters to microliters) directly to epileptogenic zones, achieving local concentrations 10-100 times higher than systemic administration while minimizing off-target effects. Battery life and biocompatibility remain technical challenges, with current devices requiring replacement every 3-5 years. Clinical pilot studies show 40-60% seizure reduction in drug-resistant patients with minimal systemic side effects.

Closed-loop neuropharmacology for epilepsy represents a promising therapeutic frontier that addresses limitations of conventional treatment. While technical hurdles remain, rapid advances in biosensors, drug delivery systems, and artificial intelligence suggest clinical implementation within the next decade is increasingly realistic rather than aspirational.

---

Title: Past and future of neuropsychopharmacology.
Abstract: The field of neuropsychopharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation since the serendipitous discovery of chlorpromazine's antipsychotic properties in the 1950s. This review examines the historical development of psychotropic medications and projects future directions based on emerging neuroscientific understanding. We analyzed the evolution of major drug classes including antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, and mood stabilizers through comprehensive literature review spanning 1950-1989, evaluating therapeutic efficacy, mechanism elucidation, and adverse effect profiles. Additionally, we assessed contemporary research trends in receptor pharmacology, neurotransmitter systems, and molecular biology techniques. Historical analysis reveals three distinct phases: the empirical discovery period (1950s-1960s), characterized by phenothiazines and tricyclic antidepressants; the mechanistic understanding era (1970s-1980s), marked by dopamine hypothesis development and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor introduction; and the emerging molecular phase (late 1980s), featuring receptor subtype characterization. Current limitations include incomplete understanding of psychiatric disease etiology, significant adverse effects, and variable therapeutic response. Future developments likely include: selective receptor subtype modulators based on advancing molecular pharmacology; neuropeptide-targeted therapeutics; drugs addressing specific neurotransmitter pathway dysfunctions; and personalized medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenetic principles. The integration of modern neuroscience methodologies with traditional pharmacological approaches promises more effective, better-tolerated treatments. Success will depend upon continued collaboration between clinical psychiatry, basic neuroscience, and pharmaceutical research, potentially revolutionizing psychiatric therapeutics within the next two decades.

---

Title: Blended learning: how can we optimise undergraduate student engagement?
Abstract: **Background:** Traditional lecture-based teaching in undergraduate neuroscience education often results in passive learning and suboptimal knowledge retention. Blended learning approaches, combining face-to-face instruction with digital technologies, have emerged as promising pedagogical strategies. However, optimal methods for maximizing student engagement in neuropharmacology courses remain poorly defined. This study investigated the effectiveness of a structured blended learning framework on undergraduate student engagement and academic performance in neuropharmacology education.

**Methods:** A randomized controlled trial was conducted with 156 third-year undergraduate students enrolled in a neuropharmacology course at two medical schools. Students were randomly assigned to either traditional lecture-based instruction (control, n=78) or a blended learning intervention (n=78). The blended approach incorporated pre-class online modules, interactive case-based discussions, virtual laboratory simulations, and post-class digital assessments. Student engagement was measured using the Student Engagement Questionnaire and behavioral analytics from the learning management system. Academic performance was assessed through standardized examinations and practical assessments.

**Results:** Students in the blended learning group demonstrated significantly higher engagement scores (7.2±1.1 vs 5.8±1.3, p<0.001) and increased online participation rates (85% vs 45%, p<0.001). Examination scores improved by 12.3% compared to controls (78.6±8.2 vs 70.1±9.4, p<0.001). Time spent on course materials increased by 34% in the intervention group. Student satisfaction ratings were significantly higher for blended learning participants (4.6±0.5 vs 3.8±0.7, p<0.001).

**Conclusions:** Structured blended learning significantly enhances undergraduate student engagement and academic performance in neuropharmacology education. These findings support the integration of digital technologies with traditional teaching methods to optimize learning outcomes in medical education.

Title: Establishment of a neurochemistry track under the new ACS guidelines.
Abstract: The new guidelines put forth by the ACS for approved chemistry degrees provide departments with greater flexibility in designing their ACS majors. Under these guidelines, students receive foundational and in-depth chemistry training while allowing individual departments to use their creativity in developing a curriculum that best meets the needs of their students and plays to the strength of the department. The chemistry department at Concordia College has developed an ACS Neurochemistry track and shares how the program arose, some of the practical matters in developing it, and how it can be made to work well within a liberal arts college.

Title: Comparative Neuropharmacology and Pharmacokinetics of Methamphetamine and Its Thiophene Analog Methiopropamine in Rodents.
Abstract: The escalating prevalence of novel psychoactive substances (NPS) has created significant challenges for both clinical toxicology and drug policy. Methiopropamine (MPA), a thiophene analog of methamphetamine (METH), emerged as a "legal high" alternative with limited pharmacological characterization. This study investigated the comparative neuropharmacological profiles and pharmacokinetic properties of METH and MPA in rodent models. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of METH or MPA (1, 3, or 10 mg/kg). Locomotor activity was monitored using automated activity chambers, while monoamine transporter interactions were assessed through in vitro binding assays and ex vivo neurochemical analyses. Pharmacokinetic parameters were determined using LC-MS/MS analysis of plasma and brain samples collected at multiple time points. Both compounds demonstrated dose-dependent hyperlocomotion, with MPA producing 65-70% of METH's peak stimulant effects. In vitro binding studies revealed MPA exhibited moderate affinity for dopamine (DAT) and norepinephrine transporters (NET) with Ki values of 245 ± 32 nM and 189 ± 28 nM, respectively, compared to METH (Ki = 125 ± 18 nM for DAT, 89 ± 15 nM for NET). MPA demonstrated slower brain penetration (Tmax = 45 min vs. 20 min for METH) but prolonged elimination half-life (t½ = 4.2 ± 0.6 h vs. 2.8 ± 0.4 h). These findings suggest MPA functions as a weaker but longer-acting psychostimulant compared to METH, providing crucial pharmacological data for forensic identification and clinical management of MPA intoxication cases.

---

Title: History of Psychopharmacology.
Abstract: We live in an age of psychopharmacology. One in six persons currently takes a psychotropic drug. These drugs have profoundly shaped our scientific and cultural understanding of psychiatric disease. By way of a historical review, we try to make sense of psychiatry's dependency on psychiatric drugs in the care of patients. Modern psychopharmacology began in 1950 with the synthesis of chlorpromazine. Over the course of the next 50 years, the psychiatric understanding and treatment of mental illness radically changed. Psychotropic drugs played a major part in these changes as state hospitals closed and psychotherapy gave way to drug prescriptions. Our review suggests that the success of psychopharmacology was not the consequence of increasingly more effective drugs for discrete psychiatric diseases. Instead, a complex mix of political economic realities, pharmaceutical marketing, basic science advances, and changes in the mental health-care system have led to our current infatuation with psychopharmacology.

---

Title: Drug-gut microbiota interactions: implications for neuropharmacology.
Abstract: The fate and activity of drugs are frequently dictated not only by the host per se but also by the microorganisms present in the gastrointestinal tract. The gut microbiome is known to, both directly and indirectly, affect drug metabolism. More evidence now hints at the effects that drugs can have on the function and composition of the gut microbiome. Both microbiota-mediated alterations in drug metabolism and drug-mediated alterations in the gut microbiome can have beneficial or detrimental effects on the host. Greater insights into the mechanisms driving these reciprocal drug-gut microbiota interactions are needed to guide the development of microbiome-targeted dietary or pharmacological interventions, which may have the potential to enhance drug efficacy or reduce drug side effects. In this review, we explore the relationship between drugs and the gut microbiome, with a specific focus on potential mechanisms underpinning the drug-mediated alterations on the gut microbiome and the potential implications for psychoactive drugs. LINKED ARTICLES: This article is part of a themed section on When Pharmacology Meets the Microbiome: New Targets for Therapeutics? To view the other articles in this section visit http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bph.v175.24/issuetoc.

Title: Investigation of Potential Antioxidant, Thrombolytic and Neuropharmacological Activities of Homalomena aromatica Leaves Using Experimental and In Silico Approaches.
Abstract: Homalomena aromatica (Schott) Hook.f. is a traditional medicinal plant widely used in Southeast Asian folk medicine for treating neurological disorders, but its pharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study aimed to evaluate the antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological properties of H. aromatica leaf extracts through combined experimental and computational approaches. Methanol and aqueous extracts were prepared and subjected to phytochemical screening, DPPH radical scavenging assays, and thrombolytic activity evaluation using human blood clots. Neuropharmacological activities were assessed using hole-board, open-field, and forced swimming tests in Swiss albino mice (n=6 per group). Molecular docking studies were performed against key neurological targets including acetylcholinesterase, GABA receptors, and monoamine oxidases. Phytochemical analysis revealed the presence of flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic compounds. The methanol extract demonstrated significant antioxidant activity (IC₅₀ = 47.32 ± 2.1 μg/mL) compared to ascorbic acid standard. Thrombolytic activity showed 23.4% clot lysis at 200 μg/mL concentration. In behavioral studies, both extracts (200 and 400 mg/kg) exhibited anxiolytic effects in the hole-board test and antidepressant-like activity in the forced swimming test, with the methanol extract showing superior efficacy. Molecular docking revealed strong binding affinities of identified compounds to acetylcholinesterase (-8.2 kcal/mol) and GABA-A receptors (-7.6 kcal/mol). These findings suggest that H. aromatica possesses promising antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological activities, supporting its traditional use and warranting further investigation for developing novel neurotherapeutic agents.

Title: The neuropharmacological basis for the use of memantine in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Abstract: Memantine has been demonstrated to be safe and effective in the symptomatic treatment of Alzheimer's disease (AD). While the neurobiological basis for the therapeutic activity of memantine is not fully understood, the drug is not a cholinesterase inhibitor and, therefore, acts differently from current AD therapies. Memantine can interact with a variety of ligand-gated ion channels. However, NMDA receptors appear to be a key target of memantine at therapeutic concentrations. Memantine is an uncompetitive (channel blocking) NMDA receptor antagonist. Like other NMDA receptor antagonists, memantine at high concentrations can inhibit mechanisms of synaptic plasticity that are believed to underlie learning and memory. However, at lower, clinically relevant concentrations memantine can under some circumstances promote synaptic plasticity and preserve or enhance memory in animal models of AD. In addition, memantine can protect against the excitotoxic destruction of cholinergic neurons. Blockade of NMDA receptors by memantine could theoretically confer disease-modifying activity in AD by inhibiting the "weak" NMDA receptor-dependent excitotoxicity that has been hypothesized to play a role in the progressive neuronal loss that underlies the evolving dementia. Moreover, recent in vitro studies suggest that memantine abrogates beta-amyloid (Abeta) toxicity and possibly inhibits Abeta production. Considerable attention has focused on the investigation of theories to explain the better tolerability of memantine over other NMDA receptor antagonists, particularly those that act by a similar channel blocking mechanism such as dissociative anesthetic-like agents (phencyclidine, ketamine, MK-801). A variety of channel-level factors could be relevant, including fast channel-blocking kinetics and strong voltage-dependence (allowing rapid relief of block during synaptic activity), as well as reduced trapping (permitting egress from closed channels). These factors may allow memantine to block channel activity induced by low, tonic levels of glutamate--an action that might contribute to symptomatic improvement and could theoretically protect against weak excitotoxicity--while sparing synaptic responses required for normal behavioral functioning, cognition and memory.

Title: Investigation of Potential Antioxidant, Thrombolytic and Neuropharmacological Activities of Homalomena aromatica Leaves Using Experimental and In Silico Approaches.
Abstract: The leaves of Homalomena aromatica are traditionally used in Bangladesh for the treatment of different chronic ailments. The purpose of this study was to explore in vitro antioxidant, thrombolytic activities, and in vivo neuropharmacological effects of methanolic extract of Homalomena aromatica (MEHA) leaves. Antioxidant activity of MEHA was assessed by a DPPH free radical scavenging assay and total phenolics content, total flavonoids content were also measured. The thrombolytic activity was determined by percentage of clot lysis and neuropharmacological activities by hole board, tail suspension, forced swimming and elevated plus maze tests. The results showed that the IC50 value of the extract against DPPH was 199.51 μg/mL. Quantitative analysis displayed higher contents of phenolics and flavonoids (147.71 mg gallic acid equivalent/g & 66.65 mg quercetin equivalent/g dried extract, respectively). The extract also showed a significant clot lysis (33.31%) activity. In case of anxiolytic activity, the elevate plus maze (EPM) test demonstrated an increase in time spent in open arms, and in case of hole board test, the number of head dipping was also significantly increased (p < 0.05). All the test compared with control (1% Tween in water) and standard (diazepam 1 mg/kg), significant dose (200 & 400 mg/kg) dependent anxiolytic activity was found. In antidepressant activity, there was a significant decrease in period of immobility in both test models (tail suspension and forced swimming) (p < 0.05). Moreover, 13 compounds were identified as bioactive, showed good binding affinities to xanthine oxidoreductase, tissue plasminogen activator receptor, potassium channel receptor, human serotonin receptor targets in molecular docking experiments. Furthermore, ADME/T analysis revealed their drug-likeness, likely pharmacological actions and non-toxic upon consumption. Taken together, our finding support the traditional medicinal use of this plant, which may provide a potential source for future drug discovery.

Title: The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
Abstract: The 5-HT3 receptor represents a novel class of serotonin receptors that differs fundamentally from previously characterized 5-HT1 and 5-HT2 subtypes in its mechanism of action. Unlike other serotonin receptors which are coupled to second messenger systems, the 5-HT3 receptor functions as a ligand-gated ion channel, allowing rapid depolarization upon serotonin binding. We examined the pharmacological properties and behavioral effects of 5-HT3 receptor activation and blockade using selective compounds in male Sprague-Dawley rats. The selective 5-HT3 agonist 2-methyl-5-HT (0.1-10 mg/kg, i.p.) produced dose-dependent increases in spontaneous locomotor activity and induced a characteristic behavioral syndrome including head weaving and flat body posture. These effects were completely blocked by pretreatment with the selective 5-HT3 antagonist ICS 205-930 (0.01-1 mg/kg, i.p.) but were unaffected by 5-HT1 or 5-HT2 receptor antagonists. Electrophysiological recordings from hippocampal slices revealed that 5-HT3 receptor activation produced rapid membrane depolarization with a reversal potential near 0 mV, consistent with a non-selective cation channel. Radioligand binding studies using [3H]ICS 205-930 demonstrated high-affinity, saturable binding sites in brain regions including area postrema, nucleus tractus solitarius, and hippocampus. These findings establish the 5-HT3 receptor as a pharmacologically distinct serotonin receptor subtype with unique electrophysiological properties. The development of selective 5-HT3 receptor ligands provides new tools for investigating serotonergic neurotransmission and may offer therapeutic opportunities for treating psychiatric and gastrointestinal disorders involving serotonergic dysfunction.

Title: A rodent model of appetitive discrimination with concomitant evaluation of anxiety-like behavior.
Abstract: The plus-maze discriminative avoidance paradigm has been used to study the relationship between aversive memory and anxiety. The present study aims to verify if the elevated plus-maze can provide information about appetitive memory and anxiety-like behavior, through a task motivated by food reward. Animals were allowed to explore an elevated plus-maze and received reinforcement in one of the enclosed arms. In a test session performed 24h later, in the absence of reward, rats showed preference for the previously rewarded enclosed arm over the neutral enclosed arm. The administration of diazepam and pentylenetetrazole before training induced, respectively, anxiolytic and anxiogenic effects (as evaluated by open-arm exploration). Both drugs induced amnestic effects, i.e., lack of preference for the rewarded arm in the test session. The results suggest that appetitive memory can be influenced by anxiety levels as well. The plus-maze appetitive discrimination task seems to be a useful model to investigate the relationship between memory and anxiety.

Title: Method for in situ detection of the mitochondrial function in neurons.
Abstract: Conventional studies of neuronal mitochondria have been limited to the use of purified preparations of isolated mitochondria, neural cell homogenates, living neurons, or brain slices. However, each technique has several drawbacks. Here, we demonstrate that the neuronal cell's membrane can be effectively permeabilized by saponin-treatment and that these permeabilized neurons can be used for qualitative and quantitative assessments of oxygen consumption in combination with registration of mitochondrial membrane potential and free [Ca2+] in the matrix. Under these conditions, the mitochondrial function can be studied without removing the mitochondria from their natural milieu thus avoiding the damage of the associated cytoskeleton and outer membrane. At the same time, the method allows the estimation of the mitochondrial function independently of other processes in the cell, and the easy manipulation of the milieu surrounding the mitochondria. Thus, the presented method offers the opportunity to study the neuronal mitochondrial function in situ and can also be applied to examine the mitochondrial function by other commonly used methods.

Title: Advances in post mortem molecular neurochemistry and neuropathology: examples from schizophrenia research.
Abstract: Post mortem studies of psychiatric disorders have been revitalised by several developments. Molecular biology has provided tools for studying genes and their expression in post mortem brain tissue, an approach which facilitates integration of molecular genetics with neurochemistry and neuropathology. The techniques can be used quantitatively as well as qualitatively, applications which have been aided by developments in image analysis. Accompanying these advances has been an improvement in the robustness of the data as a result of greater attention to confounding variables and other methodological improvements. These issues are illustrated by two recent areas of interest in schizophrenia research: the expression of central dopamine D4 and 5-HT receptors, and synaptic pathology.

Title: Stereochemistry in clinical medicine: a neurological perspective.
Abstract: Stereochemistry plays a crucial role in neuropharmacology, yet its clinical implications remain incompletely understood in neurological practice. Many neurotropic medications exist as stereoisomers with potentially different pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties. We conducted a comprehensive review of stereochemical considerations in commonly prescribed neurological medications and analyzed clinical outcomes data from 847 patients treated at our tertiary neurology center between 1998-2002. We examined racemic versus enantiopure formulations of key drug classes including anticonvulsants, anti-parkinsonian agents, and psychotropic medications, correlating stereochemical properties with therapeutic efficacy and adverse event profiles. Our analysis revealed significant differences in clinical outcomes between stereoisomeric formulations. S-citalopram demonstrated superior efficacy compared to racemic citalopram in treating post-stroke depression (response rate 73% vs 58%, p<0.05). Levodopa combined with carbidopa showed enhanced bioavailability when the L-enantiomer was used exclusively versus racemic preparations. Among anticonvulsants, the S-enantiomer of phenytoin exhibited reduced cognitive side effects while maintaining seizure control in 89% of patients switched from racemic formulations. Pharmacokinetic modeling revealed that hepatic metabolism, protein binding, and blood-brain barrier penetration varied significantly between enantiomers of the same compound. These findings suggest that stereochemical optimization of neurotropic medications could improve therapeutic outcomes while minimizing adverse effects. Greater attention to stereochemistry in drug development and clinical practice may enhance precision in neurological therapeutics, though larger controlled trials are needed to establish definitive treatment guidelines.

Title: [Department of neurochemistry].
Abstract: By the beginning of 1980s the questions concerning the mechanism of neurosecretion, the mode of neurotoxin action on this process and the mechanism of protein insertion into biological membranes were the principal directions of studies. Two views on the process of insertion of membrane proteins into, and transport across the biological membranes have been proposed. One hypothesis on membrane biogenesis describes the mechanism of incorporation of protein molecules into phospholipid matrix as a co-translational process. It starts with the synthesis of a hydrophobic N-terminal signal sequence of the protein destined to span the bilayer. In this case the energy of elongation was supposed to be utilized for insertion of the protein into hydrophobic core of the bilayer. Using the model system of co-translational translocation that consisted of liposomes and cell-free translational system including wheat germ extract and poly(A) RNA obtained from the mammary gland it has been studied whether the liposomes could serve as acceptor of synthesized secretory proteins. It is shown that in the presence of casein mRNA the 14-C-labeled product of translation is accumulated inside liposomes. When mRNA for globin, a nonsecretory protein, is translated in this cell-free system, 14-C-label is not found in the internal volume of liposomes. The polypeptides extracted from liposomes after their incubation in the system of casein mRNA translation interact specifically with anti-casein antibodies (D. I. Balkov, A. V. El'skaya, V. K. Lishko et al., 1988). These data provide the evidence that the transfer of synthesizing casein through bilayer lipid membrane does not require a specific receptor. Insertion and transfer of this protein occur co-translationally, due to the interaction of "signal peptide" with membrane lipids. The second model of the assembly of protein into membrane supposes an ability of hydrophilic proteins to cooperate with the phospholipid bilayer. As a result of such cooperation the polypeptide changes its conformation adequately to the hydrophilic environment and is integrated with the bilayer. This second model suggests the existence of water soluble precursors of membrane proteins localized in the cell cytoplasm. The last idea was studied in experiment with a specific hydrophilic protein from the cytoplasmic fraction of excitable tissues. Tetrodotoxin-sensitive (TTX-sensitive) structures have been found in soluble fractions of the brain, heart and skeletal muscle homogenates (V. K. Lishko, M. K. Malysheva, A. V. Stefanov, 1977). Initially we prepared proteoliposomes by sonication of a phospholipid suspension with the supernatants of tissue homogenates.(ABSTRACT TRUNCATED AT 250 WORDS)

Title: Using positron emission tomography to investigate hormone-mediated neurochemical changes across the female lifespan: implications for depression.
Abstract: Ovarian hormones, particularly oestrogen and progesterone, undergo major fluctuations across the female lifespan. These hormone transition periods, such as the transition from pregnancy to postpartum, as well as the transition into menopause (perimenopause), are also known to be times of elevated susceptibility to depression. This study reviews how these transition periods likely influence neurochemical changes in the brain that result in disease vulnerability. While there are known associations between oestrogen/progesterone and different monoaminergic systems, the interactions and their potential implications for mood disorders are relatively unknown. Positron Emission Tomography (PET) allows for the in-vivo quantification of such neurochemical changes, and, thus, can provide valuable insight into how both subtle and dramatic shifts in hormones contribute to the elevated rates of depression during pre-menstrual, post-partum, and perimenopausal periods in a woman's life. As one better understands how to address the challenges of PET studies involving highly vulnerable populations, such as women who have recently given birth, one will gain the insight necessary to design and individualize treatment and therapy. Understanding the precise time-line in younger women when dramatic fluctuations in the hormonal milieu may contribute to brain changes may present a powerful opportunity to intervene before a vulnerable state develops into a diseased state in later life.

Title: Neurobiological mechanisms in the transition from drug use to drug dependence.
Abstract: Drug addiction is a chronic relapsing disorder characterized by compulsive drug intake, loss of control over intake, and impairment in social and occupational function. Animal models have been developed for various stages of the addiction cycle with a focus in our work on the motivational effects of drug dependence. A conceptual framework focused on allostatic changes in reward function that lead to excessive drug intake provides a heuristic framework with which to identify the neurobiologic mechanisms involved in the development of drug addiction. Neuropharmacologic studies in animal models have provided evidence for the dysregulation of specific neurochemical mechanisms in specific brain reward and stress circuits that provide the negative motivational state that drives addiction. The allostatic model integrates molecular, cellular and circuitry neuroadaptations in brain motivational systems produced by chronic drug ingestion with genetic vulnerability, and provides a new opportunity to translate advances in animal studies to the human condition.

Title: Advancing equity, diversity, and inclusion in the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP): advances, challenges, and opportunities to accelerate progress.
Abstract: The American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP) has historically faced challenges in achieving equitable representation across gender, racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic dimensions within its membership and leadership structure. To assess current diversity metrics and identify barriers to inclusion, we conducted a comprehensive analysis of ACNP membership demographics, leadership composition, and meeting participation patterns from 2015-2020. We surveyed 1,247 members regarding perceived barriers to advancement and collected demographic data on annual meeting speakers, award recipients, and council members. Additionally, we analyzed citation patterns and publication metrics across demographic groups to examine potential disparities in career advancement opportunities. Our analysis revealed significant underrepresentation of women (32% of membership), racial minorities (18% combined), and early-career investigators (23%) relative to the broader neuroscience workforce. Leadership positions showed even greater disparities, with women holding 28% of council positions and racial minorities comprising only 12% of committee chairs over the study period. Survey responses identified key barriers including limited mentorship opportunities, geographic constraints, financial barriers to meeting attendance, and perceived "old boys' network" dynamics. However, targeted initiatives implemented since 2018, including mentorship programs, travel awards, and structured networking events, demonstrated measurable improvements in diversity metrics. These findings highlight the need for sustained, multi-faceted approaches to advance equity in neuropsychopharmacology, including policy reforms, resource allocation, and cultural transformation to ensure the field benefits from diverse perspectives and talent.

Title: The role of 5-HT₁A receptors in fish oil-mediated increased BDNF expression in the rat hippocampus and cortex: a possible antidepressant mechanism.
Abstract: Omega-3 fatty acids have demonstrated antidepressant-like effects in both clinical and preclinical studies, yet the underlying neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. Brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) upregulation and serotonergic system modulation have been proposed as potential mediators of these effects. This study investigated whether fish oil-induced increases in hippocampal and cortical BDNF expression are mediated through 5-HT₁A receptor signaling pathways. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received daily oral administration of fish oil (300 mg/kg) or vehicle for 21 days. To examine 5-HT₁A receptor involvement, separate groups received co-treatment with the selective 5-HT₁A antagonist WAY-100635 (0.5 mg/kg, s.c.). BDNF mRNA and protein levels were quantified using qRT-PCR and Western blot analysis in hippocampal and prefrontal cortical tissues. Immunohistochemical analysis was performed to assess regional BDNF distribution. Fish oil treatment significantly increased BDNF mRNA expression in the hippocampus (2.3-fold, p<0.01) and prefrontal cortex (1.8-fold, p<0.05) compared to controls. Corresponding increases in BDNF protein levels were observed in both regions. Co-administration of WAY-100635 completely abolished fish oil-induced BDNF upregulation in the hippocampus and partially attenuated the response in the prefrontal cortex. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed that fish oil enhanced BDNF immunoreactivity primarily in CA1 and CA3 pyramidal neurons and dentate gyrus granule cells. These findings suggest that 5-HT₁A receptors play a critical role in mediating fish oil-induced BDNF expression, providing novel mechanistic insight into the antidepressant properties of omega-3 fatty acids.

Title: A review of monoaminergic neuropsychopharmacology in zebrafish.
Abstract: The zebrafish (Danio rerio) has emerged as a powerful vertebrate model for investigating monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems and their pharmacological modulation. This review synthesizes current knowledge of dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic pathways in zebrafish and their relevance to neuropsychiatric drug discovery. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published literature from 2000-2010 examining monoamine neurotransmitter distribution, receptor pharmacology, and behavioral responses to psychoactive compounds in zebrafish. Immunohistochemical and in situ hybridization studies reveal that zebrafish possess conserved monoaminergic nuclei analogous to mammalian systems, including dopaminergic clusters in the posterior tuberculum and ventral diencephalon, serotonergic raphe-like neurons, and noradrenergic locus coeruleus homologs. Pharmacological characterization demonstrates that zebrafish monoamine receptors exhibit similar ligand binding profiles and G-protein coupling patterns to their mammalian counterparts. Behavioral assays measuring locomotion, anxiety-like responses, and social behaviors show dose-dependent effects of classical psychotropic drugs including SSRIs, dopamine receptor agonists/antagonists, and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors that parallel mammalian responses. Importantly, genetic mutants affecting monoamine synthesis and metabolism display behavioral phenotypes consistent with human neuropsychiatric conditions. These findings establish zebrafish as a valuable translational model bridging molecular mechanisms and behavioral outcomes in monoaminergic neuropsychopharmacology. The conservation of monoamine systems across vertebrates, combined with zebrafish experimental advantages including genetic tractability, optical transparency, and high-throughput screening capabilities, positions this model organism as an important tool for understanding monoamine function and developing novel therapeutics for psychiatric disorders.

Title: New treatments for ocular hypertension.
Abstract: Ocular hypertension remains a significant risk factor for the development of primary open-angle glaucoma, necessitating effective therapeutic interventions to reduce intraocular pressure (IOP). Current first-line treatments, including topical beta-blockers and prostaglandin analogs, demonstrate limitations in efficacy and tolerability in certain patient populations. This study investigated the ocular hypotensive effects of a novel dual-action compound, NK-347, which combines selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonism with nitric oxide donation properties. Twenty-four New Zealand white rabbits were randomized to receive topical NK-347 (0.1%, 0.2%, or 0.5%), timolol 0.5%, or vehicle control twice daily for 14 days. IOP measurements were obtained using Goldmann applanation tonometry at baseline and at 2, 4, 6, and 8 hours post-dosing on days 1, 7, and 14. Aqueous humor dynamics were assessed using fluorophotometry, and autonomic innervation of ciliary tissues was evaluated through immunohistochemistry for tyrosine hydroxylase and neuronal nitric oxide synthase. NK-347 0.2% and 0.5% demonstrated significant IOP reduction compared to vehicle (p<0.001), with maximal effects observed at 4 hours post-dosing (mean reduction: 6.2±1.4 mmHg and 8.1±1.8 mmHg, respectively). The compound enhanced aqueous humor outflow facility by 34% (p<0.01) and reduced aqueous humor production by 18% (p<0.05). Immunohistochemical analysis revealed preserved sympathetic and nitrergic innervation patterns. These findings suggest NK-347 represents a promising therapeutic approach for ocular hypertension management through dual mechanisms targeting both aqueous humor dynamics and vascular regulation.

Title: [Current trends in clinical psychiatric research].
Abstract: The landscape of clinical psychiatric research has undergone significant transformation during the 1990s, driven by advances in neuropharmacology and neuroimaging technologies. This review examines emerging methodological approaches and therapeutic targets in contemporary psychiatric investigation. We analyzed 347 clinical trials published between 1995-1998 across major psychiatric journals, focusing on study design innovations, novel pharmacological agents, and integration of biological markers. Additionally, we surveyed 125 research centers across Europe and North America to assess current research priorities and methodological trends. Our analysis reveals a marked shift toward multi-modal treatment approaches, with 68% of recent studies incorporating both pharmacological and psychosocial interventions. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors remain the predominant focus (42% of trials), though research into atypical antipsychotics has increased substantially (23% vs. 8% in 1990-1994). Notably, 31% of contemporary studies now utilize neuroimaging techniques, compared to 12% in earlier investigations. Genetic polymorphism analysis is emerging as a significant research component, appearing in 19% of recent pharmacogenetic studies. Quality-of-life measures have become standard outcome assessments, present in 76% of trials. The integration of placebo-controlled designs with active comparator arms has become increasingly common, reflecting enhanced methodological rigor. These trends indicate a paradigm shift toward personalized medicine approaches and dimensional rather than categorical diagnostic frameworks. Future psychiatric research will likely emphasize biomarker-guided treatment selection and the development of mechanism-based therapeutic interventions targeting specific neurobiological pathways.

Title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning I: the degraded contingency effect is disrupted by atropine but reinstated by physostigmine.
Abstract: The cholinergic system has been shown to modulate contextual fear conditioning. However, with the exception of trace conditioning studies, most of the available data have focussed on independent context, i.e., context that do not compete with the conditioned stimulus to control for the conditioned response (interactive context). In the present series of experiments, the effects of the muscarinic antagonist, atropine, were assessed when contextual fear memory interacts with cued fear memory to regulate conditioned response, using a Pavlovian degraded contingency preparation in rats. This preparation not only afforded an insight into simple Pavlovian associations but also enabled us to test for the processes of competition that made use of these associations to make an appropriate response to a stimulus [degraded contingency effect (DCE)]. In experiment 1, three doses of atropine [2.5, 5.0, and 10.0 mg/kg, intraperitoneally (i.p.)] were evaluated on male Sprague-Dawley rats. In experiment 2, physostigmine (0.037-0.3 mg/kg, i.p.) was injected after the administration of 5 mg/kg of atropine. Experiment 1A and its partial replication (experiment 1B) showed that at asymptotic level of training, atropine did not alter contextual and cued fear memories when the subjects were directly tested for them, whereas it suppressed the DCE for a 5 mg/kg dose. Indeed, atropine-induced suppression of the DCE was found to be an inverted U-shaped dose-response curve. Experiment 2 showed that physostigmine caused a dose-dependent reversal of the atropine-induced alleviation of the DCE, without altering the expression of simple cued and contextual fear memories. These results evidence at asymptotic level of training a cholinergic modulation of the processing of interactive context, but not of independent ones. They are discussed in the framework of the mechanisms that are involved in both types of contextual processing.

Title: Neuropharmacology of performance monitoring.
Abstract: Performance monitoring, the ability to detect and respond to errors and conflicts during goal-directed behavior, represents a fundamental cognitive control mechanism mediated by the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) and associated neural networks. While extensive neuroimaging and electrophysiological studies have characterized the neural correlates of performance monitoring, including the error-related negativity (ERN) and conflict-related activity, the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding the neurochemical basis of performance monitoring, with particular emphasis on dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems. We examine evidence from pharmacological challenge studies in humans and animals, focusing on how acute manipulations of neurotransmitter systems affect behavioral and neural indices of error detection and conflict monitoring. Dopamine appears to play a critical role in signaling prediction errors and modulating ACC activity during performance monitoring tasks, with D2 receptor blockade consistently impairing error detection. GABAergic modulation through benzodiazepine administration reduces conflict-related ACC activation and impairs behavioral adjustments following errors. Glutamatergic NMDA receptor antagonism similarly disrupts performance monitoring, suggesting involvement of cortical excitatory networks. Additionally, we review emerging evidence for acetylcholine and norepinephrine contributions to attention-related aspects of performance monitoring. The convergent findings indicate that performance monitoring depends on complex interactions between multiple neurotransmitter systems, with dopamine and GABA playing particularly prominent roles. Understanding these neurochemical mechanisms has important implications for psychiatric disorders characterized by performance monitoring deficits, including schizophrenia, ADHD, and obsessive-compulsive disorder.
